The medial and lateral epicondyles are both parts of the distal humerus and are generally in the same coronal plane, though the medial epicondyle is larger and more prominent. The medial epicondyle is directed medially and slightly posteriorly in the anatomical position. They are not described as being anterior or posterior to one another in a way that defines the joint's surface landmarks.
citation: When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Axillary Artery Palpation
statement: To verify the position of the axillary sheath, a clinician palpates the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. Against which muscle is this artery most easily felt in the axilla?
options: Pectoralis minor
Teres major
Serratus anterior
Subscapularis
Coracobrachialis
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor muscle is used to divide the axillary artery into three parts, but the artery lies deep to it (second part) or superior to it (first part). It is not the muscle against which the third part is palpated. Palpation here is difficult due to the overlying pectoralis major.

The third part of the axillary artery extends from the inferior border of the pectoralis minor to the inferior border of the teres major. It is in the distal axilla, specifically in front of the teres major muscle, where its pulsations are most accessible for palpation. This landmark is crucial for identifying the neurovascular bundle within the axillary sheath.

The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla. While the axillary artery is lateral to this muscle, it is not the primary posterior landmark used for palpating the third part of the artery. The artery is separated from the serratus anterior by the axillary fat and lymph nodes.

The subscapularis forms the majority of the posterior wall of the axilla, but the third part of the axillary artery is specifically described as being palpable in front of the teres major, which forms the inferior boundary of the posterior wall. The subscapularis is more superior and deep.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery in the axilla. While it is a useful landmark for the neurovascular bundle, the artery is typically palpated against the posterior wall structures like the teres major rather than the lateral muscle. The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis nearby.
citation: The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65).

coreConcept: Subclavian Artery Compression
statement: In an emergency situation involving a catastrophic hemorrhage from the upper limb, a clinician decides to compress the subclavian artery. Where is the most effective site to apply pressure to compress this artery against a bony landmark?
options: Against the clavicle in the infraclavicular fossa
Against the first rib in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck
Against the coracoid process deep to the deltoid
Against the transverse process of the C7 vertebra
Against the medial border of the scapula
correctOption: Against the first rib in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck
optionExplanations: While the subclavian artery passes behind the clavicle, the clavicle is not the primary landmark for effective compression because the artery is deeper and protected by the bone. The infraclavicular fossa is more appropriate for the axillary artery. Compression against the first rib is more direct.

The subclavian artery passes over the superior surface of the first rib as it exits the root of the neck to enter the axilla. By applying deep pressure downward and backward in the lower part of the posterior triangle of the neck (supraclavicular fossa), the artery can be compressed against the first rib. This is a vital maneuver for controlling severe bleeding in the upper extremity.

The coracoid process is a landmark for the attachment of several muscles and is near the axillary artery, but it is not used for subclavian artery compression. The deltoid muscle is too thick to allow effective arterial compression against this process. Furthermore, the subclavian artery has already become the axillary artery by this point.

The transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae are landmarks for the vertebral artery, which is a branch of the subclavian artery. Compressing the subclavian artery itself against these processes is not anatomically feasible or effective for hemorrhage control. The first rib provides a much broader and more stable base for compression.

The medial border of the scapula is located on the posterior aspect of the thorax and is far removed from the course of the subclavian artery. No major arteries are compressed against this border for hemorrhage control. The subclavian artery is located anteriorly in the neck and superior thorax.
citation: The subclavian artery, as it crosses the first rib to become the axillary artery, can be palpated in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck (see Fig. 3.66). The artery can be compressed here against the first rib to stop a catastrophic hemorrhage.

coreConcept: Brachial Artery Palpation
statement: A medical student is practicing palpating the brachial artery in the mid-arm. Which muscle provides the lateral boundary that overlaps the artery at this level?
options: Triceps brachii
Biceps brachii
Coracobrachialis
Brachioradialis
Deltoid
correctOption: Biceps brachii
optionExplanations: The triceps brachii is located in the posterior compartment of the arm and is separated from the brachial artery by the humerus and the medial intermuscular septum. It does not overlap the artery anteriorly. Palpating the brachial pulse requires an anterior or medial approach.

In the mid-arm, the brachial artery travels in the medial bicipital groove. It lies on the anterior surface of the brachialis muscle and is partially overlapped on its lateral side by the belly of the biceps brachii. To palpate the pulse, the clinician usually displaces the biceps laterally or palpates just medial to its margin.

The coracobrachialis is located in the proximal arm and the artery lies medial to it, but it does not provide the characteristic lateral overlap seen in the mid-arm where the pulse is commonly sought. The biceps brachii is the more prominent landmark for the majority of the artery's course. The coracobrachialis ends at the mid-humerus.

The brachioradialis originates from the lateral supracondylar ridge of the humerus and is a muscle of the forearm. It does not overlap the brachial artery in the mid-arm. It only becomes a relevant landmark for the radial artery much further distal in the forearm.

The deltoid muscle covers the shoulder and the proximal part of the humerus. It does not extend far enough distally to overlap the brachial artery in the mid-arm. The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major, which is near the level where the deltoid insertion occurs.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis.

coreConcept: Ulnar Nerve Palpation
statement: Which palpable bony landmark of the humerus is used to locate the ulnar nerve at the elbow, where the nerve can be rolled against the bone?
options: Lateral epicondyle
Medial epicondyle
Olecranon process
Greater tuberosity
Coronoid process
correctOption: Medial epicondyle
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is the origin for the superficial extensor muscles of the forearm. The radial nerve passes anterior to it, not posterior. The ulnar nerve is located on the opposite side of the elbow joint.

The medial epicondyle of the humerus is a prominent, easily palpable landmark. The ulnar nerve passes through the ulnar sulcus (groove) on the posterior aspect of this epicondyle. Because the nerve is superficial here, it can be palpated and 'rolled' against the bone, and it is the site of the 'funny bone' sensation upon impact.

The olecranon process is part of the ulna, not the humerus, and forms the point of the elbow. While the ulnar nerve passes between the olecranon and the medial epicondyle, the epicondyle is the specific humeral landmark used for its palpation. The olecranon serves as the insertion for the triceps.

The greater tuberosity is located at the proximal end of the humerus, near the shoulder joint. It is a landmark for the rotator cuff muscles (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor). It is far from the elbow and the ulnar nerve's path.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior part of the proximal ulna. It receives the insertion of the brachialis muscle and forms the anterior part of the trochlear notch. It is not a humeral landmark and is not used for ulnar nerve palpation.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here.

coreConcept: Allen's Test
statement: During the performance of Allen's test to assess the patency of the ulnar artery, what is the physiological purpose of asking the patient to tightly clench their fist while the radial artery is compressed?
options: To increase the heart rate and force of contraction to better visualize the pulse
To empty the blood from the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches
To test the strength of the flexor digitorum profundus muscle
To compress the ulnar artery against the hook of the hamate
To stimulate the sympathetic nervous system to cause vasodilation
correctOption: To empty the blood from the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches
optionExplanations: Allen's test is a localized assessment of hand circulation and does not rely on systemic changes like heart rate. Increasing the heart rate would not help determine if a specific artery in the wrist is patent. The test focuses on the refill time of the palmar arches.

Clenching the fist forces blood out of the hand's small vessels and the palmar arches. When combined with manual compression of the radial and ulnar arteries at the wrist, the palm becomes pale (blanched). Releasing one artery (e.g., the ulnar) allows the clinician to see if that specific vessel can independently resupply the entire hand, confirming its patency.

While clenching the fist does involve the flexor digitorum profundus, this is not the purpose of the Allen's test. The test is purely vascular, not neuromuscular. Muscle strength is assessed through different physical examination maneuvers.

The goal of clenching the fist is to exsanguinate the palm, not to compress the ulnar artery. The clinician's fingers are responsible for compressing the arteries at the wrist. Compressing the ulnar artery against the hamate is not part of the standard Allen's test procedure.

Clenching the fist actually causes a temporary mechanical obstruction of blood flow and does not primarily act through sympathetic vasodilation. In fact, sympathetic stimulation usually causes vasoconstriction in the skin. The test relies on the mechanical release of arterial pressure.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches.

coreConcept: Median Cubital Vein and Bicipital Aponeurosis
statement: During venipuncture in the cubital fossa, which anatomical structure provides a protective barrier between the superficial median cubital vein and the deeper brachial artery?
options: Brachialis muscle
Bicipital aponeurosis
Pronator teres muscle
Lateral intermuscular septum
Deep fascia of the arm
correctOption: Bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle lies deep to the brachial artery, forming part of the floor of the cubital fossa. It does not sit between the vein and the artery. Therefore, it cannot protect the artery from a needle entering from the skin surface.

The bicipital aponeurosis (lacertus fibrosus) is a triangular band of fascia that runs from the biceps brachii tendon medially across the brachial artery and median nerve. It fuses with the deep fascia of the forearm. Its presence is clinically significant as it separates the superficial veins (like the median cubital vein) from the deeper vital structures, offering protection during medical procedures.

The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. While it is near the neurovascular bundle, it does not act as a horizontal barrier between the superficial veins and the brachial artery. The artery actually passes deep to the humeral head of the pronator teres.

The lateral intermuscular septum is located in the arm, separating the anterior and posterior compartments. It is not located in the cubital fossa and does not play a role in separating superficial veins from the brachial artery. It is a vertical partition.

While the bicipital aponeurosis is a specialized part of the deep fascia, 'deep fascia' is too general a term. The specific structure that provides the protection in the cubital fossa is the aponeurosis itself. The deep fascia elsewhere in the arm does not provide this specific barrier.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein.

coreConcept: Palpation of the ulnar artery at the wrist
statement: A clinician is attempting to assess the patency of the ulnar artery in a patient with suspected distal forearm trauma. Which of the following best describes the anatomical landmark and position for palpating the ulnar pulse at the wrist?
options: Medial to the pisiform bone, deep to the flexor retinaculum
Lateral to the pisiform bone, superficial to the flexor retinaculum
Medial to the ulnar styloid process, deep to the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon
Lateral to the tendon of the flexor carpi radialis
Within the anatomic snuffbox, medial to the extensor pollicis longus
correctOption: Lateral to the pisiform bone, superficial to the flexor retinaculum
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery does not lie medial to the pisiform bone; rather, it is positioned laterally. Furthermore, the artery passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum within Guyon's canal, not deep to it. Placing fingers medially would likely result in palpating the bone itself rather than the arterial pulse.

The ulnar artery is most accessible for palpation just lateral to the pisiform bone where it lies superficial to the flexor retinaculum. In this location, it is only covered by skin and fascia, allowing the pulse to be easily felt against the underlying structures. It is accompanied by the ulnar nerve, which typically lies between the artery and the pisiform bone.

The ulnar styloid process is a posterior landmark and is not the primary reference for the ulnar pulse on the anterior aspect of the wrist. While the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is nearby, the artery is specifically related to the pisiform bone and the flexor retinaculum. Palpating deep to the tendon would be obstructed by the muscle's insertion.

The area lateral to the flexor carpi radialis tendon is the standard site for palpating the radial pulse, not the ulnar pulse. The radial artery lies on the anterior surface of the distal radius in this region. Confusing these two sites would lead to an incorrect assessment of the specific vessel's patency.

The anatomic snuffbox is located on the posterolateral aspect of the wrist and contains the radial artery, not the ulnar artery. The ulnar artery remains on the anterior (palmar) side of the wrist as it enters the hand. The snuffbox is bounded by the extensor tendons of the thumb and is used to palpate the scaphoid.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Anatomic snuffbox boundaries and contents
statement: During a physical examination of the wrist, a student identifies the anatomic snuffbox. Which of the following correctly identifies the medial boundary and a primary clinical feature of this space?
options: Extensor pollicis brevis; palpation of the lunate bone
Abductor pollicis longus; palpation of the ulnar artery
Extensor pollicis longus; palpation of the scaphoid bone
Extensor carpi radialis longus; palpation of the radial nerve
Flexor carpi radialis; palpation of the trapezium
correctOption: Extensor pollicis longus; palpation of the scaphoid bone
optionExplanations: The extensor pollicis brevis actually forms part of the lateral boundary of the snuffbox, not the medial boundary. Additionally, the lunate bone is located more medially in the carpus and is not the primary bone palpated in the floor of the snuffbox. The scaphoid is the floor's main constituent.

The abductor pollicis longus is a component of the lateral boundary, along with the extensor pollicis brevis. The ulnar artery is located on the opposite side of the wrist and cannot be palpated in the snuffbox. This option incorrectly identifies both the boundary and the vascular content.

The extensor pollicis longus tendon forms the medial (ulnar) boundary of the anatomic snuffbox when the thumb is extended. The floor of this depression contains the scaphoid bone, making it the most common site to check for tenderness following a fall on an outstretched hand. The radial artery also crosses the floor of this space, providing a secondary site for pulse assessment.

The extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis tendons actually pass deep to the snuffbox tendons but do not form the boundaries of the skin depression itself. While the superficial branch of the radial nerve crosses over the snuffbox, it is not the primary structure palpated for clinical bone assessment. The scaphoid is the critical landmark here.

The flexor carpi radialis is an anterior muscle of the forearm and does not contribute to the boundaries of the posterolateral snuffbox. The trapezium is indeed part of the distal floor of the snuffbox, but the scaphoid is the more proximal and clinically significant bone for palpation in this space. This option misplaces the boundary entirely.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis (see Figs. 3.34 and 3.105B, C). Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here

coreConcept: Palpation of the brachial artery
statement: When assessing the blood pressure or pulse in the mid-arm region, where should the clinician place their fingers to palpate the brachial artery?
options: Lateral to the biceps brachii, resting on the humerus
Medial to the biceps brachii, resting on the brachialis muscle
Posterior to the triceps brachii, near the radial groove
Deep to the coracobrachialis muscle in the upper third of the arm
In the deltopectoral triangle, medial to the cephalic vein
correctOption: Medial to the biceps brachii, resting on the brachialis muscle
optionExplanations: The brachial artery is not found lateral to the biceps brachii; the lateral side of the arm is primarily occupied by the deltoid and the lateral head of the triceps. Palpating here would miss the neurovascular bundle entirely. The artery travels in the medial bicipital groove.

The brachial artery travels down the medial aspect of the arm in the groove between the biceps and triceps. It lies directly on the brachialis muscle and is partially overlapped by the medial border of the biceps brachii. This medial position allows the artery to be compressed against the humerus if necessary.

The posterior aspect of the arm contains the triceps brachii and the profunda brachii artery within the radial groove, but not the main brachial artery. The brachial artery is an anterior compartment structure. Palpating posteriorly would only allow assessment of the triceps muscle mass.

While the brachial artery begins near the coracobrachialis, it is not typically palpated 'deep' to it for a standard pulse check. It becomes more superficial and accessible as it moves distally along the medial side of the biceps. The mid-arm palpation site is specifically medial to the biceps muscle belly.

The deltopectoral triangle contains the cephalic vein and the deltoid branch of the thoracoacromial artery, but not the brachial artery. The brachial artery only begins distal to the lower border of the teres major, which is well below the deltopectoral triangle. This site is used for central venous access, not brachial pulse palpation.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Subclavian artery palpation and compression
statement: In an emergency situation involving a catastrophic hemorrhage from the upper limb, a clinician decides to compress the subclavian artery. What is the correct anatomical landmark for this compression?
options: Against the clavicle in the mid-clavicular line
Against the first rib in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck
Against the transverse process of the C7 vertebra
Against the coracoid process of the scapula
Against the surgical neck of the humerus
correctOption: Against the first rib in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck
optionExplanations: While the subclavian artery passes posterior to the clavicle, the bone is too thick and the artery too deep for effective compression against the clavicle itself. The first rib provides a much firmer and more direct surface for arterial occlusion. Attempting compression against the clavicle is usually ineffective in trauma.

The subclavian artery is most accessible where it arches over the first rib, located in the inferior portion of the posterior triangle of the neck, just superior to the clavicle. By applying deep pressure downward and backward in this region, the artery is pinned against the flat surface of the first rib. This maneuver is a critical life-saving technique to control distal bleeding.

The transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae are located deeper and more medially than the subclavian artery's accessible point. The vertebral artery travels through the foramina of these processes, but the subclavian artery itself is best compressed against the thoracic cage. C7 is not the standard landmark for this procedure.

The coracoid process is a landmark for the attachment of muscles like the pectoralis minor and short head of biceps, but it is not the site for subclavian compression. The axillary artery passes medial to the coracoid process, but the subclavian artery is more proximal. Compression here would be difficult due to overlying muscle mass.

The surgical neck of the humerus is the site where the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap around the bone. It is far distal to the subclavian artery. Compressing here might help with very distal humeral bleeding but would not address a hemorrhage requiring subclavian control.
citation: The subclavian artery, as it crosses the first rib to become the axillary artery, can be palpated in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck (see Fig. 3.66). The artery can be compressed here against the first rib to stop a catastrophic hemorrhage. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the elbow joint
statement: A physician is evaluating a patient for a suspected elbow dislocation. Which of the following describes the normal topographical relationship of the olecranon and the humeral epicondyles when the elbow is fully extended?
options: They form the vertices of an equilateral triangle
The olecranon is positioned significantly superior to the epicondyles
The three bony landmarks lie in a straight horizontal line
The medial epicondyle and olecranon are aligned, but the lateral epicondyle is higher
They form a right-angled triangle with the olecranon at the 90-degree vertex
correctOption: The three bony landmarks lie in a straight horizontal line
optionExplanations: The equilateral triangle configuration is the normal anatomical relationship when the elbow is in a state of flexion, not extension. In flexion, the olecranon moves distally and posteriorly relative to the epicondyles. Using this as a guide for extension would lead to a misdiagnosis of displacement.

In a normal extended elbow, the olecranon is not superior to the epicondyles; it is level with them. If the olecranon were significantly superior or displaced, it would suggest a fracture or a posterior dislocation of the ulna. The horizontal alignment is a key diagnostic feature of a healthy joint.

When the elbow joint is fully extended, the medial epicondyle, the lateral epicondyle, and the proximal tip of the olecranon process are aligned horizontally. This linear relationship is lost in many types of elbow fractures and dislocations. It is a fundamental surface marking used during physical examinations to assess joint integrity.

The medial and lateral epicondyles are generally at the same horizontal level on the distal humerus. There is no normal physiological state where one epicondyle is significantly 'higher' than the other in a way that aligns only with the olecranon. Such a finding would indicate a supracondylar or epicondylar fracture.

A right-angled triangle is not a standard description for the relationship between these three bony landmarks in any normal position. The transition is strictly from a straight line in extension to an equilateral triangle in flexion. Deviations from these two specific geometric patterns are clinically significant for trauma.
citation: When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Axillary lymph node palpation technique
statement: To properly palpate the anterior (pectoral) group of axillary lymph nodes during a breast examination, in which direction and against which structure should the examiner press?
options: Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle
Pressing laterally against the medial side of the humerus
Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Pressing medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
Pressing superiorly toward the apex of the axilla and the first rib
correctOption: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
optionExplanations: Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to palpate the posterior (subscapular) lymph nodes, not the anterior ones. These nodes are located along the posterior wall of the axilla. Confusing these would lead to an incomplete examination of the lymphatic drainage of the breast.

Pressing laterally against the humerus is the method for palpating the lateral axillary lymph nodes, which are situated along the medial side of the axillary vein. These nodes primarily drain the upper limb. They are distinct from the pectoral nodes that drain the majority of the breast tissue.

The anterior or pectoral lymph nodes are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor, behind the pectoralis major. To feel them, the examiner must reach into the axilla and press forward, using the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle as a firm backdrop. This is a standard part of a comprehensive clinical breast exam.

Pressing medially against the thoracic wall (serratus anterior and ribs) is the technique used to palpate the central lymph nodes. These nodes receive lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. While important, this does not specifically target the anterior pectoral group.

Pressing superiorly toward the apex of the axilla is the maneuver used to identify the apical lymph nodes. These are the most superior group and are located at the transition to the supraclavicular nodes. They are usually only palpable if significantly enlarged and require the patient to relax their shoulder completely.
citation: The anterior (pectoral) nodes may be palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall of the axilla. The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla.

coreConcept: Anatomy of the cubital fossa and bicipital aponeurosis
statement: During venipuncture in the cubital fossa, which anatomical structure provides a protective barrier between the superficial median cubital vein and the deeper brachial artery?
options: The tendon of the biceps brachii
The bicipital aponeurosis
The brachialis muscle fibers
The deep fascia of the forearm
The pronator teres muscle
correctOption: The bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The biceps brachii tendon is a cord-like structure that inserts into the radial tuberosity and lies lateral to the brachial artery. While it is a landmark in the cubital fossa, it does not form the broad, flat protective sheet that separates the vein from the artery. The aponeurosis is the specific structure providing this coverage.

The bicipital aponeurosis (lacertus fibrosus) is a triangular extension of the biceps tendon that passes medially across the cubital fossa. It fuses with the deep fascia of the forearm and sits directly between the median cubital vein and the brachial artery. Its presence is clinically significant as it helps prevent accidental intra-arterial injections during routine blood draws.

The brachialis muscle forms the floor of the cubital fossa and lies deep to the brachial artery. Therefore, it cannot separate the artery from the more superficial vein. The artery actually rests upon the brachialis muscle. This muscle provides the posterior boundary of the fossa's contents.

While the deep fascia of the forearm is present, the bicipital aponeurosis is a specialized thickening of this fascia that specifically reinforces the area over the neurovascular bundle. Simply identifying it as 'deep fascia' lacks the anatomical specificity required for this clinical landmark. The aponeurosis is the distinct structure mentioned in clinical texts.

The pronator teres muscle forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. It does not pass between the median cubital vein and the brachial artery. The artery typically passes deep to the humeral head of the pronator teres as it leaves the fossa, but the aponeurosis is the barrier within the fossa itself.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein.

coreConcept: Clinical significance of the anatomic snuffbox and scaphoid
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls on an outstretched hand and complains of wrist pain. On examination, there is localized tenderness in the triangular depression between the extensor pollicis longus and brevis. Which bone is most likely injured?
options: Lunate
Triquetrum
Scaphoid
Pisiform
Hamate
correctOption: Scaphoid
optionExplanations: The lunate is located in the center of the proximal carpal row and is more commonly associated with dislocations than isolated snuffbox tenderness. It is not easily palpated within the boundaries of the anatomic snuffbox. Tenderness for the lunate is usually found more medially on the dorsal aspect of the wrist.

The triquetrum is located on the ulnar side of the wrist, distal to the ulnar styloid process. It is far removed from the radial-sided anatomic snuffbox. Injuries to the triquetrum would present with pain on the medial aspect of the wrist, often after a different mechanism of injury.

The scaphoid bone forms the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. Because of its unique blood supply and position, it is the most frequently fractured carpal bone, typically following a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH). Tenderness in the snuffbox is considered pathognomonic for a scaphoid fracture until proven otherwise by imaging.

The pisiform is a sesamoid bone located on the palmar aspect of the wrist within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris. It is not palpable from the dorsal or lateral side where the snuffbox is located. It is a landmark for the ulnar nerve and artery, not for radial-sided falls.

The hamate is a distal carpal bone on the ulnar side, characterized by its palpable hook on the palmar surface. It does not contribute to the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. Fractures of the hamate are often associated with direct trauma to the palm, such as swinging a golf club or baseball bat.
citation: Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid. Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here

coreConcept: Palpation of the ulnar nerve at the elbow
statement: A clinician is performing a neurological exam and wishes to palpate the ulnar nerve at the elbow. Where is the nerve most easily felt?
options: Anterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
Posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
Directly over the lateral epicondyle of the humerus
In the cubital fossa, medial to the brachial artery
Within the radial groove of the humerus
correctOption: Posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
optionExplanations: The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is occupied by the origins of the flexor muscles of the forearm. The ulnar nerve does not pass through this anterior space; it enters the forearm by passing behind the epicondyle. Palpating anteriorly would only reveal muscle mass.

The ulnar nerve is highly superficial as it passes through the ulnar sulcus (groove) on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle. In this location, the nerve can be rolled against the bone, which often produces a tingling sensation (the 'funny bone' effect). This is the standard site for clinical palpation and assessment of nerve thickening or sensitivity.

The lateral epicondyle is the site of origin for the extensor muscles and is related to the radial nerve, but the radial nerve is not easily palpated here as it stays deeper and more anterior. The ulnar nerve is strictly a medial structure at the elbow. Palpating the lateral side would not allow assessment of the ulnar nerve.

In the cubital fossa, the median nerve is located medial to the brachial artery, not the ulnar nerve. The ulnar nerve bypasses the cubital fossa entirely by traveling posteriorly to the medial epicondyle. Confusing the median and ulnar nerves is a common error in identifying cubital fossa contents.

The radial groove is located on the posterior shaft of the humerus and contains the radial nerve and profunda brachii artery. This is much more proximal than the elbow joint and the nerve is covered by the triceps muscle. The ulnar nerve does not enter the radial groove at any point.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here

coreConcept: Allen's Test procedure and rationale
statement: When performing the first stage of Allen's test to specifically verify the patency of the ulnar artery, which of the following steps is correct?
options: Compress the ulnar artery and observe for palmar flushing
Compress the radial artery and ask the patient to clench their fist
Compress both the radial and ulnar arteries simultaneously while the hand is open
Compress the brachial artery in the mid-arm to check for collateral flow
Compress the radial artery and observe for immediate blanching of the thumb
correctOption: Compress the radial artery and ask the patient to clench their fist
optionExplanations: Compressing the ulnar artery would be the step used to test the patency of the radial artery, not the ulnar artery. The goal of the test is to see if the *uncompressed* artery can sufficiently supply the hand. If you compress the ulnar artery, you are testing the radial artery's ability to fill the arches.

To test the ulnar artery, the clinician must occlude the radial artery (the other main supply) and have the patient clench their fist to 'pump' the blood out of the hand. When the hand is opened (while the radial artery remains compressed), a rapid return of color (pinkness) indicates that the ulnar artery is patent and capable of supplying the palmar arches alone.

While the test involves compressing both arteries initially to blanch the hand, the specific question asks about the stage to verify ulnar patency. Simply compressing both with an open hand does not utilize the 'emptying' effect of clenching the fist. The clenching is vital to clear the blood from the superficial and deep arches.

The brachial artery is the parent vessel for both the radial and ulnar arteries. Compressing it would stop all flow to the forearm and hand, which does not help in determining the relative contribution or patency of the individual distal branches. Allen's test is specifically designed for the wrist-level vessels.

Immediate blanching of the thumb upon radial compression might occur, but it is not the diagnostic endpoint of Allen's test. The test looks for the 're-pinking' or flushing of the entire palm once the hand is opened, which demonstrates collateral flow. Thumb blanching alone is an incomplete and potentially misleading observation.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches.

coreConcept: Radial Artery Palpation Site
statement: A clinician is attempting to assess a patient's heart rate by palpating the radial pulse in the distal forearm. Between which two tendons should the clinician place their fingers to most effectively feel the pulsations?
options: Flexor carpi ulnaris and palmaris longus
Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
Flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus
Abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
Extensor pollicis longus and extensor carpi radialis brevis
correctOption: Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
optionExplanations: The flexor carpi ulnaris and palmaris longus are located on the medial and central aspects of the anterior forearm, respectively, and do not bound the radial artery. Palpating here would likely yield the ulnar pulse or no pulse at all depending on the depth. The radial artery is specifically located on the lateral side of the distal forearm.

The radial artery is most superficial at the distal end of the radius, where it is bounded laterally by the brachioradialis tendon and medially by the flexor carpi radialis tendon. This anatomical positioning allows the artery to be easily compressed against the underlying bone for pulse assessment. It is the standard site for clinical pulse monitoring due to its accessibility and consistent location.

The flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus tendons bound the median nerve in the distal forearm rather than the radial artery. While the flexor carpi radialis is a landmark for the radial artery, the artery lies lateral to it, not between it and the palmaris longus. Palpation in this area is more relevant for assessing median nerve pathology.

The abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis form the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox on the posterolateral aspect of the wrist. While the radial artery does pass through the snuffbox, the standard site for taking the radial pulse in the distal forearm is anterior, not posterolateral. These tendons are used to identify the snuffbox rather than the primary forearm pulse site.

The extensor pollicis longus and extensor carpi radialis brevis are located on the dorsal aspect of the wrist and forearm. The radial artery does not lie between these specific tendons for the purpose of pulse palpation. The extensor pollicis longus specifically forms the medial boundary of the anatomic snuffbox.
citation: The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse. If the pulse cannot be felt, try feeling for the radial artery on the other wrist.

coreConcept: Ulnar Artery Palpation Landmark
statement: During the performance of Allen's test, the clinician must locate the ulnar artery to ensure its patency. What is the primary bony landmark used to locate the ulnar pulse at the wrist?
options: The styloid process of the radius
The scaphoid tubercle
The pisiform bone
The hook of the hamate
The lunate bone
correctOption: The pisiform bone
optionExplanations: The styloid process of the radius is a lateral landmark used primarily for locating the radial artery and the attachment of the brachioradialis. It is on the opposite side of the wrist from the ulnar artery. Palpating here would not assist in identifying the ulnar pulse.

The scaphoid tubercle is a palpable prominence on the lateral side of the carpus, forming part of the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. It is associated with the radial side of the wrist and the radial artery's path. It is not a landmark for the ulnar artery.

The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum, where it lies immediately lateral to the pisiform bone. Because it is covered only by skin and fascia in this region, the pisiform serves as the essential palpable landmark for finding the ulnar pulse. This relationship is critical for performing Allen's test and assessing collateral circulation to the hand.

The hook of the hamate is located distal and slightly lateral to the pisiform and forms the lateral wall of Guyon's canal. While the ulnar artery and nerve pass near it, the pisiform is the more proximal and easily palpable landmark for initial pulse identification. The hook of the hamate is deeper and harder to palpate through the hypothenar eminence.

The lunate bone is located in the proximal row of carpals but is situated centrally and is not easily palpable from the surface. It does not serve as a landmark for any major arterial pulse. Its clinical significance is more related to carpal dislocations and Kienböck's disease.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Axillary Artery Palpation
statement: A medical student is asked to palpate the third part of the axillary artery. Against which muscle should the student feel for the pulsations in the axilla?
options: Pectoralis minor
Coracobrachialis
Teres major
Subscapularis
Serratus anterior
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor muscle is the landmark used to divide the axillary artery into its three parts, with the second part lying deep to it. It is not the muscle against which the third part is typically palpated. Palpation here would be difficult due to the overlying pectoralis major.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery in the axilla and serves as a guide for the neurovascular bundle. However, the third part of the artery is specifically described as lying in front of the teres major. The coracobrachialis is more of a lateral boundary than a posterior support for palpation.

The third part of the axillary artery is the most accessible portion for palpation within the axilla. It lies directly in front of the teres major muscle, which provides a firm surface against which the artery can be felt. Identifying these pulsations is also a clinical method to verify the position of the axillary sheath.

The subscapularis muscle forms the majority of the posterior wall of the axilla, but the third part of the axillary artery specifically relates to the teres major as it exits the axilla. The subscapularis is located more superiorly and deeply. It is a landmark for the posterior axillary lymph nodes rather than the arterial pulse.

The serratus anterior muscle forms the medial wall of the axilla and is not in direct contact with the third part of the axillary artery for palpation purposes. It is associated with the long thoracic nerve. Palpating against the medial wall would not reveal the axillary pulse.
citation: The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery.

coreConcept: Subclavian Artery Compression
statement: In an emergency situation involving a catastrophic hemorrhage from a deep wound in the upper arm, a clinician decides to compress the subclavian artery. Where is the most effective anatomical location to apply this pressure?
options: Against the clavicle in the supraclavicular fossa
Against the first rib in the posterior triangle of the neck
Against the coracoid process of the scapula
Against the humerus in the bicipital groove
Against the transverse process of the C7 vertebra
correctOption: Against the first rib in the posterior triangle of the neck
optionExplanations: While the subclavian artery is located near the clavicle, compressing it against the clavicle is less effective than using the first rib as a backstop. The clavicle is mobile and does not provide the same rigid resistance as the rib. The artery actually passes inferior to the clavicle, making the rib the primary compression point.

The subclavian artery crosses the superior surface of the first rib as it transitions into the axillary artery. By applying deep pressure in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck, the clinician can pin the artery against the rigid first rib. This maneuver is a life-saving technique used to control severe bleeding in the upper limb when distal pressure is insufficient.

The coracoid process is a bony projection of the scapula that serves as an attachment for muscles like the short head of the biceps. It is not in a position to allow effective compression of the subclavian artery. Pressure here would likely compress the pectoralis minor or deltoid instead.

The humerus in the bicipital groove is a site for palpating or compressing the brachial artery, not the subclavian artery. This site is too distal to control hemorrhage if the injury is in the proximal arm or axilla. The subclavian artery must be addressed more proximally at the root of the neck.

The transverse process of C7 is located deeply in the neck and is not the standard anatomical landmark for arterial compression. The vertebral artery passes through the transverse foramina of the cervical vertebrae, but the subclavian artery is best accessed over the first rib. Attempting to compress against C7 would be anatomically incorrect and ineffective.
citation: The subclavian artery, as it crosses the first rib to become the axillary artery, can be palpated in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck (see Fig. 3.66). The artery can be compressed here against the first rib to stop a catastrophic hemorrhage.

coreConcept: Anatomic Snuffbox Contents
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls on an outstretched hand and complains of pain on the lateral side of the wrist. The clinician palpates a triangular depression at the base of the thumb. Which two structures are most relevantly assessed in this specific area?
options: Trapezium and ulnar artery
Scaphoid and radial artery
Lunate and median nerve
Pisiform and ulnar nerve
Triquetrum and radial nerve
correctOption: Scaphoid and radial artery
optionExplanations: The trapezium is located distal to the scaphoid and forms part of the floor of the snuffbox, but the ulnar artery is on the opposite side of the wrist. The ulnar artery is never found in the anatomic snuffbox. This combination is anatomically impossible.

The anatomic snuffbox is a critical clinical landmark where the scaphoid bone forms the floor and the radial artery crosses through. Tenderness in this region after trauma is a classic sign of a scaphoid fracture, which carries a risk of avascular necrosis. Additionally, the radial pulse can be felt here, although it is less prominent than at the distal radius.

The lunate bone and median nerve are located centrally within the carpal tunnel and are not found in the anatomic snuffbox. The median nerve is typically assessed for compression (Carpal Tunnel Syndrome) rather than via snuffbox palpation. The lunate is rarely palpable from the surface in this manner.

The pisiform and ulnar nerve are located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist, far from the anatomic snuffbox. They are associated with Guyon's canal. Palpating the snuffbox would provide no information about these structures.

The triquetrum is a medial carpal bone, and while the superficial branch of the radial nerve does provide sensation over the snuffbox, the nerve itself is not the primary structure 'palpated' for injury in the floor of the snuffbox. The scaphoid is the bone of concern in this clinical context. The radial nerve's involvement here is sensory rather than a palpable deep structure.
citation: Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here (see Fig. 3.105B). Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox... after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Elbow Bony Landmarks in Flexion
statement: A clinician is evaluating a patient for a suspected elbow dislocation. When the elbow is placed in a position of flexion, what is the normal topographical relationship between the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the olecranon?
options: They form a straight horizontal line
They form an equilateral triangle
The olecranon is positioned directly between the epicondyles
They form a right-angled triangle
The epicondyles overlap the olecranon
correctOption: They form an equilateral triangle
optionExplanations: A straight horizontal line is the normal relationship of these three bony landmarks only when the elbow is in full extension. In flexion, the olecranon moves distally and posteriorly relative to the epicondyles. Using this landmark in flexion would lead to a misdiagnosis of displacement.

In a normal elbow, the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the tip of the olecranon form the vertices of an equilateral triangle when the joint is flexed. This geometric relationship is a key diagnostic tool; if the triangle is distorted, it suggests a fracture or dislocation. This is a fundamental part of the surface anatomy examination of the upper limb.

The olecranon is only positioned directly between the epicondyles (in a linear fashion) during extension. In flexion, it moves out of that plane to form the apex of a triangle. This description fails to account for the three-dimensional movement of the ulna during flexion.

The relationship is specifically described as an equilateral triangle, meaning all sides and angles are roughly equal, rather than a right-angled triangle. A right-angled configuration would imply an abnormal displacement of one of the landmarks. Clinical texts emphasize the equilateral nature of this landmark.

The epicondyles do not overlap the olecranon; they remain distinct palpable points on the distal humerus. The olecranon is the proximal part of the ulna and remains posterior. Overlapping would suggest a severe comminuted fracture or gross deformity.
citation: In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle. When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points.

coreConcept: Ulnar Nerve Palpation at the Elbow
statement: Where can the ulnar nerve be most easily palpated and rolled against a bony surface at the elbow?
options: Anterior to the lateral epicondyle
Posterior to the medial epicondyle
Superior to the olecranon process
Within the cubital fossa medial to the biceps tendon
Lateral to the radial head
correctOption: Posterior to the medial epicondyle
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is associated with the origin of the extensor muscles and the radial nerve (which passes anteriorly), not the ulnar nerve. Palpating here would not allow for assessment of the ulnar nerve. The ulnar nerve is strictly a medial structure at the elbow.

The ulnar nerve passes through the ulnar sulcus (groove) on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. In this superficial location, the nerve can be easily palpated and even 'rolled' against the bone. This site is also where the nerve is most vulnerable to blunt trauma, often referred to as hitting the 'funny bone.'

The area superior to the olecranon process is occupied by the triceps brachii tendon and the olecranon bursa. The ulnar nerve is located more medially than the midline of the olecranon. Palpating directly superior to the olecranon would not encounter the nerve.

The cubital fossa contains the median nerve and the brachial artery, but the ulnar nerve does not enter this space. The ulnar nerve stays posterior to the medial epicondyle as it enters the forearm. Therefore, it cannot be palpated within the cubital fossa.

The radial head is located on the lateral side of the elbow, where the radial nerve (specifically the posterior interosseous branch) is located. The ulnar nerve is on the opposite (medial) side of the joint. Palpating the radial head is useful for assessing radial fractures but not ulnar nerve integrity.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here.

coreConcept: Coracoid Process Palpation
statement: To identify the coracoid process during a physical examination, where should the clinician apply deep pressure?
options: Superior to the acromion
Through the posterior part of the deltoid
Through the anterior part of the deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
Medial to the sternoclavicular joint
Directly in the center of the axilla
correctOption: Through the anterior part of the deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
optionExplanations: The acromion is the most superior and lateral point of the shoulder; the coracoid process lies anterior and inferior to it. Palpating superior to the acromion would only encounter skin and the trapezius muscle. It is not the correct location for the coracoid.

The posterior deltoid covers the infraspinatus and teres minor muscles and the posterior aspect of the glenohumeral joint. The coracoid process is an anterior structure. Palpating posteriorly would never reach the coracoid process.

The coracoid process is situated deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle. By applying deep pressure just inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle, the clinician can feel this beak-like projection. It is a vital landmark for shoulder injections and identifying the origin of the short head of the biceps.

The sternoclavicular joint is at the medial end of the clavicle, near the midline of the body. The coracoid process is at the lateral end of the scapular neck. These two structures are at opposite ends of the clavicular region.

The center of the axilla contains the central axillary lymph nodes and the axillary neurovascular bundle, but not the coracoid process. The coracoid process forms part of the superior boundary of the axilla but is palpated through the deltoid, not from within the axillary pit. Palpating the center of the axilla is for lymph node assessment.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Bicipital Aponeurosis Function
statement: During venipuncture of the median cubital vein, what anatomical structure provides a protective barrier that prevents accidental needle entry into the underlying brachial artery?
options: The brachialis muscle
The bicipital aponeurosis
The deep fascia of the arm
The pronator teres muscle
The radial collateral ligament
correctOption: The bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle lies deep to the brachial artery, forming part of the floor of the cubital fossa. It does not sit between the vein and the artery and therefore cannot provide protection for the artery during venipuncture. Its role is primarily elbow flexion.

The bicipital aponeurosis (lacertus fibrosus) is a flat expansion of the biceps tendon that passes medially across the cubital fossa. It specifically separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deeper brachial artery and median nerve. This anatomical arrangement is clinically significant as it protects these deeper structures during blood draws or IV insertions.

While deep fascia covers the entire limb, the bicipital aponeurosis is a specialized, thickened structure in the cubital fossa that provides the specific barrier between the vein and artery. General deep fascia is not thick enough to be considered the primary protective landmark in this context. The aponeurosis is a distinct anatomical entity.

The pronator teres muscle forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. While it is in the vicinity, it does not lie between the median cubital vein and the brachial artery. It is involved in forearm pronation and is not a protective barrier for the artery.

The radial collateral ligament is located on the lateral side of the elbow joint, providing lateral stability. It is nowhere near the median cubital vein or the brachial artery, which are located anteriorly and medially in the cubital fossa. It has no role in venipuncture safety.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein.

coreConcept: Brachial Artery Palpation
statement: When palpating the brachial artery in the mid-arm, what is its relationship to the biceps brachii muscle?
options: It lies lateral to the biceps brachii
It is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii
It lies superficial to the biceps brachii
It is separated from the biceps brachii by the humerus
It lies posterior to the triceps brachii
correctOption: It is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii
optionExplanations: The brachial artery is located on the medial side of the arm, not the lateral side. The lateral side of the arm contains the cephalic vein and the deltoid muscle. Palpating laterally would not reveal the brachial pulse.

In the arm, the brachial artery travels along the medial aspect of the humerus, resting on the brachialis muscle. The biceps brachii muscle lies lateral to the artery and partially overlaps it. To palpate the pulse, a clinician often needs to displace the medial border of the biceps slightly.

The brachial artery is a deep structure relative to the biceps brachii; it is not superficial to it. The only superficial structures in this area are the skin, fascia, and the basilic vein. Palpating superficially would only reveal venous pulsations if any.

The humerus lies lateral to the brachial artery in the mid-arm; it does not separate the artery from the biceps. In fact, the artery is often compressed against the humerus to stop bleeding. The biceps and the artery are in the same anterior compartment.

The triceps brachii is located in the posterior compartment of the arm, separated from the brachial artery by the medial intermuscular septum and the humerus. The brachial artery is an anterior compartment structure. It has no direct relationship with the posterior surface of the triceps.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis.

coreConcept: Palpation of the ulnar artery at the wrist
statement: A clinician is attempting to palpate the ulnar pulse at the wrist. Which of the following anatomical landmarks is most useful for identifying the precise location of the ulnar artery as it crosses the flexor retinaculum?
options: Lateral to the pisiform bone
Medial to the pisiform bone
Lateral to the radial styloid process
Medial to the hook of the hamate
Deep to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
correctOption: Lateral to the pisiform bone
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery is located just lateral to the pisiform bone as it crosses the flexor retinaculum, where it is covered only by skin and fascia. This superficial position makes it an ideal site for pulse palpation, as the artery can be compressed against the underlying retinaculum. The ulnar nerve lies even more medially, situated between the artery and the pisiform bone itself.

The area medial to the pisiform bone consists of the medial edge of the wrist and does not contain the ulnar artery, which must pass lateral to this bone to enter the hand. Palpating medially would miss the arterial pulsations entirely. The pisiform serves as the medial boundary for the ulnar neurovascular bundle.

The radial styloid process is a landmark for the radial artery on the lateral (thumb) side of the wrist, not the ulnar side. The radial artery is palpated between the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis tendons. Using this landmark would lead to the assessment of the radial rather than the ulnar pulse.

The hook of the hamate is located distal and slightly lateral to the pisiform, forming part of the ulnar canal (Guyon's canal). While the ulnar artery passes near it, the standard and most accessible site for palpation is more proximal, specifically lateral to the pisiform. Palpating at the hamate is more difficult due to the overlying palmaris brevis and fascia.

The flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is a landmark for the ulnar nerve and artery, but the artery lies lateral to this tendon and superficial to the retinaculum. It is not deep to the tendon at the point of pulse palpation. Attempting to palpate deep to the tendon would be obstructed by the thick tendinous fibers and the pisiform bone.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Anatomic snuffbox boundaries and contents
statement: During a physical examination of the wrist, a physician identifies the anatomic snuffbox. Which of the following structures forms the medial boundary of this depression and what clinical finding is most associated with tenderness in this area?
options: Extensor pollicis longus; Scaphoid fracture
Extensor pollicis brevis; Radial nerve entrapment
Abductor pollicis longus; De Quervain's tenosynovitis
Extensor carpi radialis longus; Lunate dislocation
Extensor digitorum; Hamate fracture
correctOption: Extensor pollicis longus; Scaphoid fracture
optionExplanations: The medial boundary of the anatomic snuffbox is formed by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus, while the lateral boundary is formed by the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis. Deep tenderness within this triangular space after a fall on an outstretched hand is a classic clinical sign of a scaphoid fracture. The radial artery also passes through the floor of this space, allowing for pulse palpation.

The extensor pollicis brevis forms part of the lateral boundary of the snuffbox, not the medial boundary. While the superficial branch of the radial nerve crosses the roof of the snuffbox, tenderness in the floor is specifically indicative of scaphoid pathology rather than nerve entrapment. Nerve entrapment would typically present with sensory deficits rather than deep focal tenderness.

The abductor pollicis longus is a component of the lateral boundary of the snuffbox. De Quervain's tenosynovitis involves the first dorsal compartment (APL and EPB) and causes pain over the radial styloid, but the specific deep tenderness within the snuffbox floor is the hallmark of scaphoid injury. The medial boundary is strictly the EPL tendon.

The extensor carpi radialis longus tendon passes deep to the snuffbox boundaries but does not form the medial wall of the depression. Lunate dislocations typically present with pain and swelling in the central carpal region and potential median nerve compression. They are not primarily assessed via palpation of the anatomic snuffbox.

The extensor digitorum tendons are located in the fourth dorsal compartment, far medial to the anatomic snuffbox. The hamate bone is located on the ulnar side of the wrist and would not be palpable in the snuffbox. Tenderness in the snuffbox is unrelated to these structures and specifically points to the scaphoid or radial artery.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis (see Figs. 3.34 and 3.105B, C). Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here

coreConcept: Allen's test for arterial patency
statement: A patient requires radial artery catheterization. To ensure adequate collateral circulation, the clinician performs an Allen's test. After compressing both the radial and ulnar arteries and having the patient clench their fist, the ulnar artery pressure is released while radial compression is maintained. What specific physiological event is being confirmed if the palm turns pink within seconds?
options: Patency of the ulnar artery and the palmar arterial arches
Patency of the radial artery and the dorsal carpal arch
Integrity of the median nerve and its vascular supply
Competence of the valves in the deep venous system
Adequacy of the collateral flow from the interosseous arteries
correctOption: Patency of the ulnar artery and the palmar arterial arches
optionExplanations: The return of color to the palm upon releasing the ulnar artery while maintaining radial compression confirms that the ulnar artery is patent and capable of supplying the palmar arches. This ensures that if the radial artery is compromised during catheterization, the hand will remain perfused. The test specifically evaluates the communication between the ulnar artery and the superficial/deep palmar arches.

The radial artery remains compressed during this phase of the Allen's test, so its patency is not being assessed at this moment. The dorsal carpal arch provides blood to the back of the hand, whereas the Allen's test specifically monitors the palmar skin color to assess the palmar arches. Releasing the ulnar artery tests the ulnar contribution, not the radial.

The Allen's test is a vascular assessment tool and does not directly test the integrity of the median nerve. While the median nerve is supplied by small vasa nervorum, the rapid change in skin color is a macrovascular phenomenon related to the ulnar and radial arteries. Nerve function is assessed through sensory and motor testing.

The Allen's test evaluates arterial inflow and capillary refill, not the venous system or the competence of venous valves. Venous insufficiency would present with congestion or edema rather than the pallor and subsequent flushing seen in this test. The clenching of the fist is designed to empty the arterial beds, not the veins.

While the interosseous arteries contribute to the overall forearm circulation, they are not the primary vessels being evaluated by the Allen's test. The test specifically isolates the radial and ulnar arteries at the wrist to determine which one can independently support the palmar arches. Interosseous flow is generally insufficient to rapidly reperfuse the palm in this clinical maneuver.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches. When the patient is asked to open the hands, the skin of the palms is at first white, and then normally the blood quickly flows into the arches through the ulnar arteries, causing the palms to promptly turn pink.

coreConcept: Palpation of the brachial artery
statement: A medical student is asked to locate the brachial pulse in the mid-arm of a healthy subject. According to standard anatomical relationships, where should the student apply pressure to most effectively feel the pulsations?
options: Medial to the biceps brachii muscle, pressing against the brachialis
Lateral to the biceps brachii muscle, pressing against the humerus
Posterior to the triceps brachii, pressing against the radial groove
Directly over the deltoid tuberosity of the humerus
In the center of the coracobrachialis muscle belly
correctOption: Medial to the biceps brachii muscle, pressing against the brachialis
optionExplanations: The brachial artery travels down the medial aspect of the arm in the medial bicipital groove. It lies on the brachialis muscle and is partially overlapped on its lateral side by the biceps brachii muscle. Palpating just medial to the biceps allows the clinician to compress the artery against the brachialis or the humerus to feel the pulse.

The lateral side of the biceps brachii does not contain the main trunk of the brachial artery; instead, the cephalic vein and the deltoid muscle are found in this region. The brachial artery is strictly a medial structure in the arm. Palpating laterally would result in missing the arterial pulsations entirely.

The posterior compartment of the arm contains the triceps brachii and the profunda brachii artery within the radial groove, but not the main brachial artery. The brachial artery is located in the anterior compartment. The radial groove is a landmark for the radial nerve and the deep artery of the arm, not the brachial pulse.

The deltoid tuberosity is located on the lateral aspect of the mid-humerus and serves as the insertion for the deltoid muscle. The brachial artery is located medially and is not associated with this bony landmark. Palpation here would be obstructed by the thick deltoid muscle and would not reveal a pulse.

The coracobrachialis muscle is located in the proximal arm and the brachial artery lies medial to it initially, but the artery does not pass through the muscle belly. The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis. Palpating the muscle belly itself is not the standard or effective method for finding the brachial pulse.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse. If the pulse cannot be felt, try feeling for the radial artery on the other wrist.

coreConcept: Compression of the subclavian artery
statement: In an emergency situation involving a severe hemorrhage of the upper limb, a clinician decides to compress the subclavian artery. At which anatomical location and against which structure should this compression be applied?
options: Root of the posterior triangle of the neck; First rib
Superior to the mid-clavicle; Coracoid process
Posterior to the sternocleidomastoid; Transverse process of C6
In the deltopectoral triangle; Surgical neck of the humerus
Inferior to the medial third of the clavicle; Second rib
correctOption: Root of the posterior triangle of the neck; First rib
optionExplanations: The subclavian artery is most accessible for compression as it passes over the first rib in the lower part of the posterior triangle of the neck. By pressing inferiorly and posteriorly in this region, the artery is pinned against the broad surface of the first rib, effectively occluding blood flow to the axillary artery. This is a critical maneuver for controlling proximal limb hemorrhage.

While the subclavian artery passes behind the clavicle, the coracoid process is located much more laterally and anteriorly on the scapula. It does not provide a suitable backing for compressing the subclavian artery. The first rib is the specific bony landmark required for effective occlusion in the neck.

The transverse process of C6 (Chassaignac's tubercle) is the landmark for compressing the common carotid artery, not the subclavian artery. The subclavian artery lies lower and more lateral in the root of the neck. Compressing at C6 would affect cerebral blood flow rather than limb blood flow.

The deltopectoral triangle contains the cephalic vein and the deltoid branch of the thoracoacromial artery, but the subclavian artery has already become the axillary artery by this point. The surgical neck of the humerus is a landmark for the axillary nerve and circumflex humeral arteries. It is too distal for subclavian compression.

The second rib is located inferior to the first rib and is covered by more soft tissue and the pectoralis muscles, making it an ineffective site for arterial compression. The subclavian artery specifically crosses the first rib before entering the axilla. The first rib's superior surface is the only viable platform for this emergency procedure.
citation: The subclavian artery, as it crosses the first rib to become the axillary artery, can be palpated in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck (see Fig. 3.66). The artery can be compressed here against the first rib to stop a catastrophic hemorrhage. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65).

coreConcept: Surface landmarks of the elbow in flexion and extension
statement: A clinician is evaluating a patient for a suspected elbow dislocation. Which of the following describes the normal topographical relationship of the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and olecranon process when the elbow is fully flexed?
options: They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
They are arranged in a straight horizontal line
The olecranon moves superior to the interepicondylar line
They form a right-angled triangle with the apex at the lateral epicondyle
The epicondyles overlap the olecranon in a single vertical plane
correctOption: They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
optionExplanations: In the flexed position of the elbow, the olecranon process moves distally relative to the epicondyles of the humerus. This change in position results in the three bony landmarks forming the vertices of an equilateral triangle. Disruption of this geometric relationship is a key clinical indicator of a fracture or dislocation.

The three bony points (medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and olecranon) are arranged in a straight line only when the elbow is in full extension. As the elbow flexes, the olecranon pulls away from this line. Therefore, a straight line in flexion would be an abnormal finding suggesting pathology.

During flexion, the olecranon moves distally and posteriorly, placing it 'below' the interepicondylar line if the arm is viewed from the back. It does not move superior to the line. The triangular configuration is formed by this distal migration of the olecranon relative to the humeral epicondyles.

The relationship in flexion is specifically described as an equilateral triangle, meaning all sides and angles are roughly equal. A right-angled triangle would imply a different anatomical displacement. The symmetry of the equilateral triangle is what clinicians look for during a physical exam.

The epicondyles and olecranon do not overlap in a single vertical plane during flexion; they remain distinct palpable landmarks.
