Its diameter increases as it moves proximally, which accommodates the catheter more easily than the cephalic vein. The cephalic vein often enters the axillary vein at a sharp angle, making catheter advancement difficult.

The bicipital aponeurosis actually separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery, not the basilic vein specifically. While this is an important anatomical relationship in the cubital fossa, it does not explain why the basilic vein is superior for central catheterization. The aponeurosis is a protective layer for the artery during routine venipuncture.

The basilic vein is actually closely associated with the medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm in the cubital region. Therefore, it is not 'less likely' to be accompanied by a nerve compared to the cephalic vein. The choice is purely based on the mechanical ease of passing a long catheter into the central circulation.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein (see Fig. 3.68). The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Palpation of posterior (subscapular) axillary lymph nodes
statement: When examining the axillary lymph nodes, how should the clinician palpate the posterior (subscapular) group?
options: By pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
By pressing laterally against the medial side of the humerus
By pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle
By pressing medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
By reaching high into the apex toward the first rib
correctOption: By pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle
optionExplanations: Pressing forward against the pectoralis major is the technique used to palpate the anterior or pectoral group of lymph nodes. These nodes lie along the lower border of the pectoralis minor. This maneuver assesses the anterior wall of the axilla, not the posterior wall.

Pressing laterally against the humerus or the medial side of the axillary vein is the method for palpating the lateral group of lymph nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. This group is located along the lateral wall of the axilla, distinct from the subscapular group.

The posterior (subscapular) nodes are located along the subscapular vessels on the posterior axillary wall. To palpate them, the clinician must press backward against the subscapularis muscle, which forms the posterior boundary of the axillary space. This is a standard part of a comprehensive breast and axillary examination.

Pressing medially against the thoracic wall (serratus anterior and ribs) is the technique for palpating the medial or central group of lymph nodes. These nodes are located deep in the axillary fat. They are not the subscapular nodes, which are specifically associated with the posterior wall.

Reaching high into the axilla toward the first rib is the technique for palpating the apical lymph nodes. This requires the patient to relax their shoulder muscles to allow the examiner's fingers to reach the highest point of the axilla. The apical nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage.
citation: The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery.

coreConcept: Radiographic angulation of the humerus
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anatomical angulation of the distal humerus relative to its shaft?
options: Angulated backward 15°
Angulated forward 45°
Angulated medially 30°
Perfectly aligned with the shaft (0°)
Angulated forward 90°
correctOption: Angulated forward 45°
optionExplanations: A backward angulation of the humerus would be highly abnormal and suggestive of a supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement. The normal anatomy of the distal humerus involves an anterior tilt to facilitate a full range of flexion. Radiologists look for this forward tilt to rule out subtle bony injuries.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at approximately 45° relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This angulation is clearly visible on a true lateral radiograph and is essential for the proper mechanics of the elbow joint. Loss of this angle often indicates a fracture, particularly in pediatric patients.

Medial angulation refers to the carrying angle in the frontal plane, not the sagittal angulation seen on a lateral radiograph. While the humerus does have a medial epicondyle, the 45° forward tilt is the key feature assessed in the lateral view. Medial angulation would be described as varus or valgus.

If the distal humerus were perfectly aligned with the shaft (0°), the range of elbow flexion would be significantly limited by bony impingement. The 45° forward tilt allows the coronoid process of the ulna to fit into the coronoid fossa more effectively. Perfect alignment on a lateral view is usually a sign of a displaced fracture.

A 90° forward angulation would represent a severe deformity or a completely displaced fracture. The normal physiological tilt is much more subtle at 45°. Such an extreme angle would be incompatible with normal joint function and would likely involve significant neurovascular compromise.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the greater tuberosity in deltoid wasting
statement: A patient with a chronic axillary nerve injury shows significant muscle wasting at the shoulder. Which bony landmark becomes much more prominent and easily palpable as a result of this wasting?
options: Lesser tuberosity of the humerus
Greater tuberosity of the humerus
Coracoid process
Surgical neck of the humerus
Bicipital groove
correctOption: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
optionExplanations: The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis muscle. It is generally not the most prominent landmark felt when the deltoid wastes, as it is situated more medially. The greater tuberosity forms the lateral contour of the shoulder.

The deltoid muscle normally provides the rounded contour of the shoulder by overlying the greater tuberosity of the humerus. When the deltoid undergoes atrophy (wasting) due to axillary nerve damage, the muscle bulk disappears, leaving the greater tuberosity as a sharp, easily palpable bony prominence. This change is a classic sign of chronic axillary nerve palsy.

The coracoid process is an anterior projection of the scapula and is not covered by the main bulk of the deltoid muscle in the same way as the greater tuberosity. While it is palpable, its prominence does not change as dramatically with deltoid wasting. It is located more medially, inferior to the clavicle.

The surgical neck of the humerus is the site where the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap around the bone. It is a common site for fractures but is not a surface landmark that becomes 'prominent' during muscle wasting. It is located inferior to the tuberosities and is deep to the remaining tissues.

The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) lies between the greater and lesser tuberosities and contains the long head of the biceps tendon. While it is an important landmark, it is a depression rather than a prominence. The greater tuberosity itself is what creates the bony protrusion felt after deltoid loss.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse.

coreConcept: Scapular Surface Landmarks and Vertebral Levels
statement: During a physical examination, a clinician palpates the inferior angle of the scapula to identify specific anatomical levels. Which of the following structures corresponds to the level of this landmark?
options: The spine of the fourth thoracic vertebra
The spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra
The spine of the second thoracic vertebra
The spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra
The spine of the twelfth thoracic vertebra
correctOption: The spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra
optionExplanations: The spine of the fourth thoracic vertebra is typically located superior to the inferior angle of the scapula, often corresponding more closely to the root of the scapular spine. It is not the standard landmark for the inferior angle. Palpation at this level would be too high for the seventh rib reference.

The spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7) and the seventh rib are the primary anatomical landmarks associated with the inferior angle of the scapula in a living subject. This relationship is vital for counting ribs and identifying vertebral levels during a physical exam. It provides a consistent reference point for posterior thoracic anatomy.

The spine of the second thoracic vertebra is located much higher in the thoracic region, near the superior angle of the scapula. Using this as a landmark for the inferior angle would result in significant clinical error. It is generally found at the level of the suprasternal notch anteriorly.

The spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra is located significantly inferior to the scapula. This level is closer to the lower margin of the thoracic cage and the kidneys. It does not serve as a landmark for any part of the scapular body.

The spine of the twelfth thoracic vertebra marks the transition between the thoracic and lumbar spine. It is far below the inferior angle of the scapula and is associated with the floating ribs. Palpating the scapula would not provide an accurate guide to this level.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Palpation of the Coracoid Process
statement: A medical student is attempting to palpate the coracoid process on a simulated model. Which of the following techniques is most appropriate to locate this structure?
options: Applying light pressure to the posterior aspect of the acromion
Applying deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
Palpating the medial border of the scapula at the level of the T3 spine
Applying pressure to the subcutaneous surface of the mid-clavicular line
Palpating the floor of the anatomic snuffbox during thumb extension
correctOption: Applying deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
optionExplanations: The posterior aspect of the acromion is the origin of the posterior deltoid and is easily palpable, but it is not the location of the coracoid process. The coracoid process is an anteriorly projecting structure. Palpating posteriorly would lead the clinician to the spine of the scapula instead.

The coracoid process is situated deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle and can be felt by applying deep pressure just inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle. It serves as an important attachment point for the short head of the biceps brachii and coracobrachialis. Its 'beaklike' projection is a key landmark in shoulder surgery and physical diagnosis.

The medial border of the scapula at the T3 level corresponds to the root of the scapular spine, not the coracoid process. This area is used to assess scapular winging or rhomboid function. It is located on the posterior thorax, whereas the coracoid is anterior.

The mid-clavicular line is a vertical reference line used for cardiac and pulmonary exams, but the coracoid process is located more laterally. The subcutaneous surface of the clavicle is easily felt, but the coracoid lies deeper and more inferiorly. Pressure here would only identify the clavicular shaft.

The anatomic snuffbox is located at the wrist and is used to palpate the scaphoid bone and radial artery. It has no anatomical relationship to the coracoid process of the scapula. This option describes a landmark for the distal upper limb rather than the shoulder girdle.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar Nerve Vulnerability at the Medial Epicondyle
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the small finger after striking the 'funny bone' against a table. Which anatomical landmark and associated structure are involved in this common clinical presentation?
options: The lateral epicondyle and the radial nerve
The medial epicondyle and the ulnar nerve
The olecranon process and the median nerve
The coracoid process and the musculocutaneous nerve
The radial styloid and the superficial radial nerve
correctOption: The medial epicondyle and the ulnar nerve
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is the site of origin for the common extensor tendon, and while the radial nerve is nearby, it does not pass through a vulnerable posterior sulcus here. Injury to the radial nerve usually occurs in the spiral groove of the humerus. Tingling in the small finger is not characteristic of radial nerve compression.

The ulnar nerve passes posteriorly to the medial epicondyle of the humerus within the ulnar sulcus. Because it is superficial and rests against the bone, it is highly susceptible to compression or blunt trauma, often referred to as hitting the 'funny bone.' This results in paresthesia along the ulnar distribution, including the fifth digit.

The olecranon process is the proximal end of the ulna and forms the point of the elbow, but the median nerve does not pass over it. The median nerve is located anteriorly in the cubital fossa, protected by the bicipital aponeurosis. Olecranon injuries typically involve the triceps tendon or bursa.

The coracoid process is located in the shoulder region and is the site of origin for the coracobrachialis, which is pierced by the musculocutaneous nerve. While trauma here could affect the nerve, it would not cause symptoms at the elbow or the small finger. This landmark is too proximal for the described scenario.

The radial styloid is a landmark at the wrist, and the superficial radial nerve passes nearby to provide sensation to the dorsum of the hand. However, it does not supply the small finger, which is ulnar territory. Trauma at the radial styloid would affect the thumb and index finger regions.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture).

coreConcept: Clinical Signs of Breast Carcinoma
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a physician observes nipple retraction and skin dimpling when the patient raises her arms. What is the underlying anatomical mechanism for these findings in the context of a suspected malignancy?
options: Hypertrophy of the pectoralis major muscle pulling on the skin
Tumor infiltration and shortening of the lactiferous ducts or suspensory ligaments
Obstruction of the cephalic vein causing localized edema
Rupture of the bicipital aponeurosis altering chest wall tension
Atrophy of the axillary lymph nodes leading to skin sagging
correctOption: Tumor infiltration and shortening of the lactiferous ducts or suspensory ligaments
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major hypertrophy would increase the bulk of the chest wall but does not typically cause localized skin dimpling or nipple retraction. These signs are specific to tethering of the breast tissue itself. Muscle hypertrophy is a physiological response to exercise, not a sign of malignancy.

Malignant tumors can invade and cause fibrotic shortening of the lactiferous ducts (leading to nipple retraction) or the suspensory ligaments of Cooper (leading to skin dimpling). When the patient raises her arms, the tension on these structures increases, making the retraction or dimpling more apparent. This is a classic clinical sign of invasive breast carcinoma.

Cephalic vein obstruction would lead to venous congestion and potentially 'peau d'orange' appearance due to lymphedema, but it does not cause the mechanical pulling required for nipple retraction. The cephalic vein is located in the deltopectoral groove, lateral to the primary breast tissue. Edema usually causes skin thickening rather than dimpling.

The bicipital aponeurosis is located in the cubital fossa of the elbow and has no anatomical role in the structure or support of the breast. Its rupture would affect elbow function and forearm tension. It is completely unrelated to breast cancer pathology or surface markings of the thorax.

Axillary lymph node involvement in cancer usually results in lymphadenopathy (enlargement), not atrophy. Enlarged nodes might be palpable in the axilla but do not cause nipple retraction. Skin sagging from atrophy would be a generalized age-related change, not a localized retraction sign.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Mammography Utility and Safety
statement: Which of the following statements correctly describes the clinical application and safety of mammography as a radiographic tool?
options: It uses high-frequency sound waves to visualize deep cysts
It uses extremely low doses of x-rays, allowing for frequent screening
It is only useful for identifying malignant tumors, not benign cysts
It requires the injection of radioactive isotopes into the axillary vein
It is contraindicated in patients with palpable axillary lymph nodes
correctOption: It uses extremely low doses of x-rays, allowing for frequent screening
optionExplanations: High-frequency sound waves are used in ultrasonography, not mammography. While ultrasound is a useful adjunct for breast imaging, especially to distinguish solid from cystic masses, mammography specifically refers to X-ray imaging. Mammography is the primary screening modality for microcalcifications.

Mammography utilizes low-dose ionizing radiation (X-rays) to produce detailed images of breast tissue. Because the dose is kept extremely low, the risk of radiation-induced harm is minimized, making it a safe and effective tool for regular population-based screening. This allows for the early detection of both benign and malignant lesions.

Mammography is effective at identifying a wide range of breast pathologies, including benign cysts, fibroadenomas, and malignant carcinomas. It is not limited to cancer detection. Radiologists look for specific patterns of density and calcification to differentiate these entities.

Mammography is a non-invasive radiographic technique and does not require the injection of radioactive isotopes. Isotope injection is used in sentinel lymph node biopsy or PET scans, which are different diagnostic procedures. Mammography involves mechanical compression of the breast and X-ray exposure.

Palpable axillary lymph nodes are actually an indication for thorough breast imaging, including mammography, to search for a primary tumor. It is not a contraindication. Mammography helps evaluate the extent of the disease when nodal involvement is suspected clinically.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: Upper Limb Dermatomes (C3-T1)
statement: A clinician is testing sensory perception to evaluate the C7 spinal segment. According to the standard dermatomal map of the upper limb, where should the stimulus be applied?
options: The lateral aspect of the arm (over the deltoid)
The middle finger
The medial aspect of the forearm
The skin over the fifth digit (small finger)
The skin of the axilla
correctOption: The middle finger
optionExplanations: The lateral aspect of the arm, specifically over the deltoid, is primarily supplied by the C5 dermatome (axillary nerve territory). Testing here would evaluate the C5 spinal segment, not C7. This area is proximal to the lateral margin distribution of C3-C6.

The C7 dermatome is consistently mapped to the middle finger of the hand. Testing sensation here is a standard clinical maneuver to assess the integrity of the seventh cervical spinal nerve root. It represents the central axis of the dermatomal distribution in the hand.

The medial aspect of the forearm is supplied by the T1 dermatome (via the medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve). This is part of the medial margin distribution of the lower cervical and upper thoracic segments. It is distinct from the C7 distribution which occupies the middle of the hand.

The skin over the fifth digit (small finger) is part of the C8 dermatome. This segment follows the medial margin of the limb along with T1. Loss of sensation here would suggest a lesion at the C8 nerve root or the ulnar nerve.

The skin of the axilla and the medial aspect of the upper arm are supplied by the T2 dermatome (intercostobrachial nerve). This is the most inferior segment of the upper limb dermatomal sequence. It is far removed from the C7 territory of the hand.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Surface Anatomy of Deltoid Wasting
statement: A patient presents with a flattened shoulder contour and loss of abduction following an axillary nerve injury. Which bony landmark becomes significantly more prominent and easily palpable due to the resulting muscle atrophy?
options: The lesser tuberosity of the humerus
The greater tuberosity of the humerus
The coracoid process
The medial epicondyle
The radial tuberosity
correctOption: The greater tuberosity of the humerus
optionExplanations: The lesser tuberosity is located anteriorly on the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis. While it may be slightly more accessible after deltoid wasting, it is not the primary landmark that defines the lateral shoulder contour. The greater tuberosity is the more lateral and prominent structure in this context.

The deltoid muscle normally provides the rounded contour of the shoulder by overlying the greater tuberosity of the humerus. When the deltoid undergoes atrophy (wasting) due to axillary nerve paralysis, the muscle bulk is lost, making the greater tuberosity much more prominent and easily palpable through the skin. This 'squaring' of the shoulder is a classic diagnostic sign.

The coracoid process is an anterior structure and its palpability is not as directly affected by the loss of the lateral deltoid mass as the greater tuberosity is. While the anterior deltoid covers it, the 'flattening' of the shoulder specifically refers to the lateral aspect. The coracoid remains deep to the pectoralis major and deltoid junction.

The medial epicondyle is located at the elbow, far from the deltoid muscle. Its prominence is related to the distal humerus and is unaffected by axillary nerve injury or deltoid wasting. It is a landmark for the ulnar nerve, not the shoulder contour.

The radial tuberosity is a deep structure in the proximal forearm where the biceps brachii inserts. It is not palpable on the surface and has no relationship to the deltoid muscle or the axillary nerve. Atrophy of the deltoid would not change the accessibility of the radial tuberosity.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated.

coreConcept: Surface Marking of the Cephalic Vein
statement: In an emergency situation where superficial veins are not visible due to shock, where can a clinician consistently locate the cephalic vein for venipuncture?
options: Immediately anterior to the medial epicondyle
Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Deep to the bicipital aponeurosis in the cubital fossa
Lateral to the pisiform bone at the wrist
In the center of the anatomic snuffbox
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is the typical location for the basilic vein as it ascends the forearm. While important, it is not the constant landmark described for the cephalic vein. The cephalic vein is located on the lateral (radial) side of the limb.

The cephalic vein has a very consistent anatomical position in the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process at the wrist. This makes it a reliable site for venous access even when other veins have collapsed due to hypotension or shock. It then continues up the lateral side of the forearm.

The bicipital aponeurosis actually separates the median cubital vein from the underlying brachial artery. It does not serve as a landmark for the cephalic vein's constant position. Placing a needle deep to the aponeurosis would risk arterial puncture rather than finding a superficial vein.

The area lateral to the pisiform bone is the site where the ulnar artery and ulnar nerve cross the flexor retinaculum. This is a site for taking the ulnar pulse, not for locating the cephalic vein. The cephalic vein is on the opposite (radial) side of the wrist.

The anatomic snuffbox contains the radial artery and the scaphoid bone. While the cephalic vein begins in the dorsal venous network nearby, its 'fairly constant' landmark for emergency access is specifically noted as being posterior to the radial styloid, rather than within the snuffbox itself.
citation: The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization. Every clinical professional, in an emergency, should know where to obtain blood from the arm. When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Anatomy of Central Venous Catheterization
statement: Why is the basilic vein preferred over the cephalic vein for the insertion of a central venous catheter from the cubital fossa?
options: The cephalic vein has a larger diameter than the basilic vein
The basilic vein increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
The cephalic vein is deeper and harder to palpate in the arm
The basilic vein is protected by the bicipital aponeurosis throughout its course
The cephalic vein contains more valves that obstruct catheter advancement
correctOption: The basilic vein increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
optionExplanations: In reality, the basilic vein typically becomes larger than the cephalic vein as it ascends toward the axilla. A larger diameter is preferable for catheterization to reduce the risk of venous thrombosis and ease the passage of the catheter. The cephalic vein often narrows or has sharp angles.

The basilic vein is the preferred route because it increases in caliber as it moves proximally and provides a relatively straight, direct path into the axillary vein. In contrast, the cephalic vein often enters the axillary vein at a sharp angle in the deltopectoral triangle, which can make advancing a long catheter difficult. This anatomical alignment reduces procedural complications.

The cephalic vein is actually more superficial and often easier to see in the deltopectoral groove than the basilic vein, which becomes deep in the mid-arm. However, ease of initial palpation does not outweigh the structural advantages of the basilic vein for long-term catheter placement. The basilic vein's internal path is superior.

The bicipital aponeurosis only covers the structures in the cubital fossa, not the entire course of the basilic vein. While it provides some protection at the elbow, it is not the reason the basilic vein is chosen for central access. The primary reason is the direct continuity with the axillary vein.

While both veins contain valves, the primary anatomical hurdle for the cephalic vein is its terminal junction with the axillary vein, not necessarily the number of valves. The basilic vein's increasing diameter and linear path are the decisive factors in clinical preference for central lines.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein (see Fig. 3.68).

coreConcept: Radiological Landmarks of the Elbow
statement: When evaluating a lateral radiograph of the elbow following trauma, which normal anatomical angulation of the distal humerus must be maintained to rule out a supracondylar fracture?
options: A 90° posterior angulation of the trochlea
A 45° forward angulation of the lower end of the humerus on the shaft
A 15° lateral deviation of the olecranon
A 30° medial tilt of the radial head
A 180° straight alignment between the humeral shaft and the capitulum
correctOption: A 45° forward angulation of the lower end of the humerus on the shaft
optionExplanations: A 90° posterior angulation would be a gross deformity and is not a normal anatomical feature. The distal humerus is naturally tilted anteriorly, not posteriorly. Such a finding on a radiograph would indicate a significant fracture-dislocation.

On a normal lateral X-ray of the elbow, the distal articular piece of the humerus (containing the capitulum and trochlea) is angulated anteriorly (forward) at approximately 45 degrees relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. Loss of this angulation is a critical radiographic sign of a supracondylar fracture, especially in pediatric patients. Maintaining this relationship is essential for normal elbow flexion range.

The olecranon fits into the olecranon fossa during extension and does not have a normal 15° lateral deviation on a lateral radiograph. Lateral deviations are better assessed on an AP (anteroposterior) view to check the carrying angle. This option does not describe the standard lateral radiographic landmark.

The radial head should align with the capitulum in all views, but a '30° medial tilt' is not a standard normal measurement for the radial head on a lateral film. The radial head's orientation is primarily checked using the radiocapitellar line. Misalignment here suggests a radial head dislocation.

A straight 180° alignment would mean there is no anterior tilt of the distal humerus, which is anatomically incorrect. The absence of the normal 45° forward tilt would suggest a fracture where the distal fragment has been displaced or tilted posteriorly. This would severely limit the functional range of motion of the elbow joint.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Clinical signs of breast carcinoma and nipple retraction
statement: A 55-year-old woman presents for a routine breast examination. Upon asking the patient to raise her arms above her head, the clinician observes a noticeable retraction of the right nipple. Which of the following anatomical structures is most likely being pulled by an underlying malignancy to cause this specific physical finding?
options: Pectoralis major fascia
Lactiferous ducts
Retromammary space
Pectoralis minor tendon
Serratus anterior fascia
correctOption: Lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major fascia is the deep fascia upon which the breast rests, and while a tumor can become fixed to it, it is not the primary structure responsible for nipple retraction. Fixation to this fascia typically results in decreased mobility of the breast mass rather than specific nipple inversion. The lactiferous ducts are the direct conduits to the nipple, and their involvement is required for the retraction seen here.

The lactiferous ducts are the structures that, when shortened or pulled by an infiltrating carcinoma, cause the nipple to retract or invert. This occurs because the tumor's fibrotic response tethers these ducts, pulling the nipple inward toward the mass. This is a classic clinical sign of breast cancer that may be accentuated by maneuvers that stretch the breast tissue, such as raising the arms.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue between the breast and the pectoralis major fascia that allows for breast mobility. While a tumor can obliterate this space, leading to fixation of the breast to the chest wall, it does not directly cause nipple retraction. Nipple retraction specifically involves the ductal system or the suspensory ligaments.

The pectoralis minor tendon is located deep to the pectoralis major and inserts on the coracoid process of the scapula. It is not anatomically connected to the breast tissue or the nipple in a way that would cause retraction during a breast malignancy. Its primary role is in stabilizing the scapula and assisting in respiration.

The serratus anterior fascia covers the muscle on the lateral thoracic wall and forms part of the bed of the breast. Similar to the pectoralis fascia, involvement of this layer might lead to mass fixation but does not explain the specific pulling of the nipple. Nipple retraction is a localized phenomenon involving the internal ductal architecture of the mammary gland.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatome distribution (C7)
statement: During a neurological examination of the upper limb, a clinician tests for sensory integrity. If a patient reports a complete loss of sensation specifically localized to the middle finger, which spinal cord segment is most likely compromised?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C5 dermatome is typically located on the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid region and the lateral epicondyle. It does not extend to the digits of the hand. Sensory loss here would suggest a lesion in the upper trunk of the brachial plexus or the C5 nerve root.

The C6 dermatome covers the lateral forearm and the thumb (first digit). While it is adjacent to the middle finger, it does not provide the primary sensory innervation for the third digit. C6 is also commonly tested via the brachioradialis reflex.

The C7 dermatome is specifically situated on the middle finger, making it the primary segment responsible for sensation in this area. Testing the middle finger is the standard clinical method for assessing the integrity of the C7 spinal segment. This segment also provides motor innervation to the triceps muscle and the wrist extensors.

The C8 dermatome is located on the medial margin of the hand, specifically involving the little finger (fifth digit) and sometimes the ring finger. It does not cover the middle finger. C8 lesions often present with weakness in the intrinsic muscles of the hand.

The T1 dermatome is found along the medial aspect of the forearm and the distal arm. It does not extend into the hand or the fingers. T1 is crucial for the innervation of the intrinsic hand muscles via the ulnar and median nerves.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Deltoid wasting and greater tuberosity prominence
statement: A patient suffers a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus, resulting in axillary nerve injury. Several weeks later, the shoulder appears flattened, and a bony landmark on the lateral aspect of the proximal humerus has become significantly more prominent and easily palpable. Which landmark is this?
options: Lesser tuberosity
Greater tuberosity
Coracoid process
Acromion
Deltoid tuberosity
correctOption: Greater tuberosity
optionExplanations: The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and serves as the insertion for the subscapularis muscle. It is generally not the landmark that becomes prominent laterally when the deltoid wastes. Its position is more medial and deep compared to the greater tuberosity.

The greater tuberosity is the large, lateral projection of the proximal humerus that is normally covered by the thick deltoid muscle. When the deltoid undergoes rapid atrophy due to axillary nerve paralysis, the muscle bulk is lost, making the underlying greater tuberosity much more palpable and visible. This contributes to the 'squared-off' appearance of the shoulder in such patients.

The coracoid process is a projection of the scapula located anteriorly, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. While it is a palpable landmark, its prominence does not change significantly with deltoid wasting, as it is not the primary structure covered by the deltoid's lateral mass. It is felt through the anterior fibers of the deltoid.

The acromion is the lateral extension of the scapular spine and forms the tip of the shoulder. While it may appear more distinct when the deltoid wastes, the question specifically asks for the landmark on the lateral aspect of the proximal humerus. The acromion is a scapular feature, not a humeral one.

The deltoid tuberosity is a roughened area on the lateral surface of the mid-shaft of the humerus where the deltoid muscle inserts. It is not a proximal landmark and does not become a prominent 'point' of the shoulder following muscle atrophy. It is located much further down the humeral shaft.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. ... The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. ... The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the cephalic vein at the wrist
statement: In an emergency situation where a patient is in shock and superficial veins are collapsed, a clinician attempts to locate the cephalic vein for venous access. Based on surface anatomy, where is this vein most consistently found at the level of the wrist?
options: Anterior to the medial epicondyle
Posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Medial to the pisiform bone
Lateral to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
Directly over the scaphoid bone in the snuffbox
correctOption: Posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is associated with the basilic vein and the median cubital vein in the cubital fossa, not the cephalic vein at the wrist. This site is much more proximal than the wrist. The cephalic vein at the elbow is found in the lateral aspect of the cubital fossa.

The cephalic vein is known for its relatively constant position in the superficial fascia immediately posterior to the radial styloid process. This makes it a reliable site for venipuncture or cutdown procedures when other veins are not visible due to hypotension or shock. It then continues up the lateral side of the forearm.

The area medial to the pisiform bone is the site where the ulnar nerve and artery pass into the hand. There is no major superficial vein consistently located here for emergency access. The ulnar artery is deep to the fascia in this region, unlike the superficial cephalic vein.

The flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist. The cephalic vein is a lateral (radial) structure and would not be found in proximity to this tendon. The basilic vein is the primary superficial vein on the ulnar side of the forearm.

While the cephalic vein does cross the region of the anatomic snuffbox, its most 'constant' and clinically described landmark for emergency identification is its position relative to the radial styloid process. The scaphoid bone forms the floor of the snuffbox, but the vein is more superficial. The radial artery is also found in the snuffbox, which requires caution during venipuncture.
citation: The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization. Every clinical professional, in an emergency, should know where to obtain blood from the arm. When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Palpation of central axillary lymph nodes
statement: During a comprehensive axillary lymph node examination, the clinician palpates the center of the axillary base. Which two anatomical structures form the anterior and posterior boundaries between which the central lymph nodes are palpated?
options: Pectoralis minor and Teres major
Pectoralis major and Subscapularis
Clavicle and First rib
Latissimus dorsi and Serratus anterior
Biceps brachii and Coracobrachialis
correctOption: Pectoralis major and Subscapularis
optionExplanations: While the pectoralis minor and teres major contribute to the axillary walls, they are not the primary landmarks used to define the space for palpating the central nodes. The central nodes are located more superficially in the fat of the axillary base. The pectoralis major and subscapularis are the larger, more easily identifiable boundaries.

The central axillary lymph nodes are located deep to the base of the axilla and are palpated by pressing upward toward the center of the axillary cavity. They are bounded anteriorly by the pectoralis major muscle (the anterior wall) and posteriorly by the subscapularis muscle (the posterior wall). This group receives lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral node groups.

The clavicle and the first rib define the apex of the axilla, where the apical lymph nodes are located. This is much higher and more medial than the central group. Palpating the apical nodes requires the patient to relax the shoulder to allow the examiner's fingers to reach the outer border of the first rib.

The latissimus dorsi forms part of the posterior axillary fold along with the teres major, and the serratus anterior forms the medial wall. While these are important boundaries of the axilla, they do not define the specific anterior-posterior sandwiching used to isolate the central nodes during a clinical exam. The subscapularis is the more direct posterior boundary for the central group.

The biceps brachii and coracobrachialis muscles form the lateral wall of the axilla. The lateral lymph nodes are palpated against these structures, specifically along the medial side of the axillary vein. They are not the boundaries for the central node group, which lies more medially in the axillary fat.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib.

coreConcept: Radiographic angulation of the distal humerus
statement: When evaluating a lateral radiograph of the elbow joint to rule out a supracondylar fracture, what is the normal anterior angulation of the distal end of the humerus relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft?
options: 15 degrees
30 degrees
45 degrees
60 degrees
90 degrees
correctOption: 45 degrees
optionExplanations: An angulation of 15 degrees is insufficient and would be considered an abnormal finding on a lateral elbow radiograph. This might suggest a posteriorly displaced fracture or a malunion. The normal anatomy requires a much more significant forward tilt of the condyles.

While 30 degrees represents a forward tilt, it is less than the standard anatomical norm for the distal humerus. Clinicians look for a specific 45-degree angle to ensure the articular surfaces are correctly aligned. Deviations from this can indicate subtle fractures in pediatric patients.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at a 45-degree angle relative to the shaft. This angulation is critical for the normal range of flexion at the elbow joint and is a key landmark in radiology. If this angle is altered, it often indicates a supracondylar fracture, which is common in children.

A 60-degree angulation would be excessive and is not the standard anatomical measurement for the distal humerus. Such an extreme angle would likely interfere with the mechanical advantage of the elbow extensors and flexors. It would be viewed as a pathological deformity on imaging.

A 90-degree angulation would mean the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is not the case in human anatomy. The distal humerus is designed with a 45-degree anterior tilt to accommodate the coronoid process of the ulna during full flexion. A 90-degree angle would be a gross deformity.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the lateral epicondyle
statement: Which of the following best describes the anatomical position of the lateral epicondyle of the humerus relative to other distal humeral landmarks?
options: Distal to the capitulum
Proximal to the capitulum
Medial to the trochlea
Distal to the olecranon fossa
Proximal to the medial epicondyle
correctOption: Proximal to the capitulum
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is not distal to the capitulum; rather, the capitulum is part of the articular surface that lies distal to the epicondyle. The epicondyles serve as attachment points for ligaments and muscles, while the capitulum articulates with the radius. Therefore, the epicondyle must be proximal to the joint surface.

The lateral epicondyle is a palpable, roughened projection located on the lateral side of the distal humerus, situated immediately proximal to the capitulum. It serves as the primary origin for the supinator muscle and the common extensor tendon of the forearm. Its position is easily identified on the lateral aspect of the elbow.

The lateral epicondyle is located on the lateral side of the humerus, whereas the trochlea is the medial part of the distal articular surface. The structure medial to the trochlea is the medial epicondyle. These two epicondyles are on opposite sides of the distal humerus.

The olecranon fossa is a deep depression on the posterior surface of the distal humerus that receives the olecranon during extension. The lateral epicondyle is lateral to this fossa, not distal to it. The epicondyles and the fossa are roughly at the same horizontal level of the distal humeral expansion.

The lateral and medial epicondyles are generally at the same horizontal level on the distal humerus, although the medial epicondyle is significantly larger and more prominent. One is not proximal to the other in standard anatomical terms. They represent the widest points of the distal humerus.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. ... The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm.

coreConcept: Palpation of the posterior border of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: Anterior border
Interosseous border
Posterior aspect of the body
Coronoid process
Radial notch
correctOption: Posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be easily palpated along its length. Palpation of the anterior forearm reveals muscle mass rather than a distinct bony edge.

The interosseous border is the sharp lateral edge of the ulna where the interosseous membrane attaches. It is located deep within the forearm between the radius and ulna and is completely inaccessible to surface palpation. It serves as a partition between the anterior and posterior compartments.

The posterior aspect (or border) of the ulnar shaft is rounded and lies directly beneath the skin, with no intervening muscles. This allows it to be palpated easily from the olecranon all the way down to the ulnar styloid process at the wrist. This feature is often used by clinicians to assess for ulnar fractures.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior and proximal part of the ulna that forms part of the trochlear notch. It is located deep within the cubital fossa and is covered by the brachialis muscle and other soft tissues. It is not palpable from the surface.

The radial notch is a small depression on the lateral side of the coronoid process that articulates with the head of the radius. It is a deep articular feature of the proximal radioulnar joint and is not accessible for surface palpation. It is located deep to the extensor muscles of the forearm.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. ... The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. ... The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus.

coreConcept: Mammography and breast screening
statement: Which of the following statements regarding mammography is correct based on its clinical application and safety profile?
options: It uses high-dose gamma radiation to penetrate dense tissue.
It is primarily used only when a palpable mass is already present.
It utilizes extremely low doses of x-rays to allow for frequent screening.
It is an invasive procedure requiring contrast injection into the ducts.
It cannot distinguish between solid tumors and fluid-filled cysts.
correctOption: It utilizes extremely low doses of x-rays to allow for frequent screening.
optionExplanations: Mammography does not use gamma radiation; it uses x-rays. Furthermore, the doses are specifically kept extremely low to minimize the risk of radiation-induced tissue damage, especially since screening is performed periodically over many years. High-dose radiation is reserved for therapeutic purposes, not screening.

Mammography is extensively used as a screening tool, meaning it is performed on asymptomatic women to detect tumors before they become palpable. While it is also used to evaluate known masses, its greatest clinical value lies in early detection during routine screening programs.

Mammography is designed to use extremely low doses of x-rays, which minimizes the cumulative radiation risk to the patient. This safety profile allows the examination to be repeated at regular intervals (e.g., annually or biennially) for effective long-term monitoring and early cancer detection.

Mammography is a non-invasive radiographic technique. While a related procedure called a galactogram involves injecting contrast into the lactiferous ducts, a standard mammogram simply involves compressing the breast between two plates and taking x-ray images. It does not require any injections.

Mammography is highly effective at identifying various breast pathologies, including both benign and malignant tumors as well as cysts. While ultrasound is often used as a follow-up to better characterize a lesion as solid or cystic, mammography provides the initial evidence of these structures' presence.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the clavicle
statement: Which of the following bones of the pectoral girdle is characterized by being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length?
options: Scapula
Humerus
Clavicle
First rib
Coracoid process
correctOption: Clavicle
optionExplanations: The scapula is largely covered by thick muscles such as the trapezius, infraspinatus, and latissimus dorsi. Only certain parts, like the spine, acromion, and inferior angle, are easily palpable. The majority of the scapular body is protected by muscle and is not subcutaneous.

The humerus is almost entirely surrounded by large muscle groups, including the deltoid, biceps, and triceps. Only the epicondyles at the distal end and the greater tuberosity (if the deltoid is wasted) are easily palpable. The shaft of the humerus is not subcutaneous.

The clavicle is unique because it lies directly beneath the skin throughout its entire extent from the sternum to the acromion. This makes it very easy to palpate and also makes it highly susceptible to fractures, as there is little soft tissue to absorb impact. It serves as a key landmark for various clinical procedures.

The first rib is located deep in the root of the neck and is covered by the clavicle and the scalene muscles. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be palpated along its length. It is only accessible to palpation in the posterior triangle of the neck under specific conditions.

The coracoid process is a projection of the scapula that is not subcutaneous. It is located deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle and requires deep pressure to palpate. It is a localized point, not a long structure palpable along its 'entire length'.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. ... The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder. ... The coracoid process ... can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle.

coreConcept: Palpation of the coracoid process
statement: A clinician intends to palpate the coracoid process of the scapula during a physical examination. Where should the clinician apply deep pressure to locate this structure?
options: Inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
Superior to the acromion process
Medial to the sternoclavicular joint
Posterior to the spine of the scapula
Lateral to the bicipital groove of the humerus
correctOption: Inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a prominent beaklike projection that can be felt by applying deep pressure through the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle, specifically just inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle. This landmark is essential for identifying the origin of the short head of the biceps brachii and coracobrachialis muscles. It is situated at the junction of the scapular neck and superior border.

The acromion is the most lateral part of the scapular spine and forms the tip of the shoulder; palpating superior to it would not reach the coracoid process. The acromion is subcutaneous and easily felt, but it is distinct from the deeper, more anteriorly placed coracoid. This area is typically used to assess the acromioclavicular joint.

The area medial to the sternoclavicular joint corresponds to the manubrium of the sternum and the root of the neck. The coracoid process is located much further laterally on the appendicular skeleton. Palpation here would involve the jugular notch or the sternocleidomastoid attachments.

The posterior aspect of the scapular spine is where the infraspinatus and supraspinatus muscles are located. The coracoid process projects anteriorly and laterally, making it impossible to palpate from a posterior approach. Posterior palpation is better suited for the medial border or the inferior angle of the scapula.

The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) is located on the anterior humerus between the greater and lesser tubercles. While the coracoid is in the same general region, it is a scapular landmark located medial to the humerus rather than lateral to the groove. The groove itself contains the long head of the biceps tendon.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve relationship to the medial epicondyle
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the medial two fingers after striking the medial aspect of their elbow against a hard surface. Which bony landmark is the nerve most likely compressed against in this region?
options: Medial epicondyle
Lateral epicondyle
Olecranon process
Coronoid process
Radial head
correctOption: Medial epicondyle
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve passes through the ulnar sulcus on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Because the nerve is superficial at this site, it is highly vulnerable to compression or blunt trauma, often referred to as hitting the 'funny bone.' This results in paresthesia along the ulnar distribution of the hand.

The lateral epicondyle is located on the outer aspect of the distal humerus and serves as the origin for the common extensor tendon. The radial nerve passes anterior to the lateral epicondyle, not the ulnar nerve. Injury here is more commonly associated with lateral epicondylitis or radial nerve issues.

The olecranon process is the proximal end of the ulna that forms the point of the elbow. While it is near the ulnar nerve, the nerve specifically sits in the groove between the olecranon and the medial epicondyle. The olecranon itself is the insertion point for the triceps brachii muscle.

The coronoid process is an anterior projection of the proximal ulna that fits into the coronoid fossa of the humerus during flexion. It is not a site where the ulnar nerve is commonly palpated or injured by external trauma. It is located deep within the cubital fossa.

The radial head is located on the lateral side of the proximal forearm, articulating with the capitulum of the humerus. It is palpable distal to the lateral epicondyle, especially during pronation and supination. It is not anatomically related to the course of the ulnar nerve at the elbow.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture).

coreConcept: Breast examination and skin dimpling
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a patient is asked to raise both arms above her head. What is the primary anatomical reason this maneuver is performed?
options: To highlight skin dimpling caused by a tumor pulling on suspensory ligaments
To allow for easier palpation of the deep pectoral lymph nodes
To stretch the pectoralis minor and expose the axillary tail
To assess the symmetry of the inframammary fold during contraction
To facilitate the drainage of lymphatic fluid for better visualization
correctOption: To highlight skin dimpling caused by a tumor pulling on suspensory ligaments
optionExplanations: Raising the arms tensions the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) and the lactiferous ducts. If a carcinoma is present and attached to these structures, the tension causes the overlying skin to dimple or the nipple to retract. This is a classic clinical sign of an underlying malignancy tethering the breast tissue.

While arm elevation can help expose the axilla, the specific maneuver of raising arms to look for dimpling is focused on skin and ligamentous changes rather than lymph node palpation. Lymph node examination usually requires the patient to relax the pectoral muscles. Deep pectoral nodes are generally not palpable unless significantly enlarged.

The axillary tail (of Spence) is best palpated with the arm in a neutral or slightly abducted position to relax the overlying fascia. Stretching the pectoralis minor is not the primary goal of this specific visual inspection maneuver. The maneuver is designed to reveal architectural distortion of the breast contour.

The inframammary fold is a natural landmark, but its symmetry is usually assessed with the patient sitting with arms at the sides. While arm elevation might change its appearance, it is not the diagnostic focus for detecting tethered carcinomas. The focus remains on the skin of the upper quadrants and the nipple.

Lymphatic drainage is a physiological process and is not significantly altered by arm position in a way that improves visual inspection for tumors. Visual inspection relies on the mechanical pulling of fibrous structures by a mass. Mammography or ultrasound would be used for internal visualization rather than postural changes.
citation: Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple. ... A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts.

coreConcept: Mammography utility and safety
statement: Which of the following statements best describes the clinical application and safety of mammography?
options: It uses low-dose X-rays to screen for both benign and malignant lesions
It is only effective for detecting malignant carcinomas in older patients
It requires high-energy radiation that limits the frequency of testing
It is primarily used to distinguish between solid and cystic masses
It is a therapeutic procedure used to aspirate fluid from breast cysts
correctOption: It uses low-dose X-rays to screen for both benign and malignant lesions
optionExplanations: Mammography is a standard screening tool that utilizes low-dose ionizing radiation to identify various breast abnormalities, including cysts, benign tumors, and cancers. Because the radiation dose is kept extremely low, the risk-to-benefit ratio allows for regular, repeated screenings in at-risk populations. It is the primary modality for early detection of non-palpable masses.

Mammography is effective across a wide range of ages and can detect both benign conditions (like fibroadenomas) and malignant ones. While breast density in younger patients can sometimes limit its sensitivity, it is not restricted to older patients. It remains a cornerstone of preventative health for adult women.

Modern mammography is specifically designed to use the lowest possible dose of X-rays to achieve diagnostic quality. This minimizes the cumulative radiation risk, allowing for annual or biennial screenings as recommended by health guidelines. High-energy radiation is reserved for therapeutic purposes, such as radiotherapy.

While mammography can identify the presence of a mass, ultrasound is typically the preferred modality for distinguishing whether a mass is solid or fluid-filled (cystic). Mammography provides a 2D projection of tissue density and calcifications. It often works in conjunction with ultrasound for a complete diagnosis.

Mammography is strictly a diagnostic imaging technique and does not involve the removal of fluid. Procedures like fine-needle aspiration or core needle biopsy are used for tissue or fluid sampling. These are often performed under ultrasound or stereotactic (mammographic) guidance but are separate from the mammogram itself.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: C7 dermatome testing
statement: To specifically test the sensory integrity of the C7 spinal segment, a clinician should assess sensation in which of the following areas?
options: The middle finger
The thumb and lateral forearm
The little finger and medial hand
The medial aspect of the arm near the axilla
The skin over the point of the shoulder
correctOption: The middle finger
optionExplanations: The C7 dermatome is classically mapped to the middle finger of the hand. Testing sensation here provides a specific assessment of the C7 nerve root and its corresponding spinal cord segment. This is a standard part of a neurological exam for the upper limb.

The thumb and the lateral aspect of the forearm are primarily supplied by the C6 dermatome. C6 follows the lateral margin of the limb down to the first digit. Loss of sensation here would suggest a lesion at the C6 level rather than C7.

The little finger and the medial (ulnar) aspect of the hand are supplied by the C8 dermatome. C8 covers the medial margin of the hand and the distal medial forearm. It is distinct from the central position of the C7 dermatome.
The medial aspect of the arm near the axilla is supplied by the T1 and T2 dermatomes. These segments cover the medial side of the proximal upper limb. C7 is located much further distally in the hand.

The skin over the point of the shoulder (acromion area) is typically supplied by the C4 dermatome via the supraclavicular nerves. C3 and C4 cover the base of the neck and the superior aspect of the shoulder. C7 does not extend to this proximal region.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Deltoid wasting and greater tuberosity
statement: A patient with a chronic axillary nerve injury presents with significant atrophy of the shoulder musculature. Which bony landmark becomes abnormally prominent and easily palpable due to the loss of muscle bulk?
options: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
Lesser tuberosity of the humerus
Coracoid process of the scapula
Infraglenoid tubercle of the scapula
Surgical neck of the humerus
correctOption: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
optionExplanations: The deltoid muscle normally covers the greater tuberosity of the humerus, giving the shoulder its rounded contour. When the deltoid atrophies due to axillary nerve paralysis, the muscle bulk disappears, making the greater tuberosity much more prominent and easy to palpate. This leads to a 'squared-off' appearance of the shoulder.

The lesser tuberosity is located more medially and anteriorly on the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis muscle. It is generally deeper and less affected by the loss of the deltoid muscle bulk compared to the greater tuberosity. It is not the primary landmark that becomes prominent in deltoid wasting.

The coracoid process is an anterior scapular structure that is already palpable under the anterior deltoid. While it might be slightly easier to feel with muscle loss, the most dramatic change in shoulder contour and palpability occurs over the lateral aspect where the greater tuberosity resides. The coracoid is medial to the humeral head.

The infraglenoid tubercle is located on the lateral border of the scapula, inferior to the glenoid cavity, and serves as the origin for the long head of the triceps. It is located deep within the axilla and is not a surface landmark that becomes prominent with deltoid atrophy. It is obscured by the triceps and teres muscles.

The surgical neck of the humerus is the constricted region distal to the tubercles. While it is a common site for fractures and is related to the axillary nerve, it is a region rather than a specific bony 'knob' that becomes prominent. The greater tuberosity is the specific projection that loses its muscular covering.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. ... The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Cephalic vein location at the wrist
statement: When attempting to establish peripheral venous access at the level of the wrist, where is the cephalic vein most consistently located?
options: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Directly anterior to the flexor retinaculum
Medial to the pisiform bone in the ulnar canal
Deep to the tendons of the anatomic snuffbox
On the medial side of the olecranon process
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is a reliable site for venipuncture at the wrist because it consistently passes through the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process. From here, it continues up the lateral side of the forearm. Its predictable location makes it a 'go-to' vein when others are not visible.

The area anterior to the flexor retinaculum contains the palmaris longus tendon and the palmar cutaneous branches of the median and ulnar nerves. While some small superficial veins may be present, the main trunk of the cephalic vein is located laterally. The flexor retinaculum is on the anterior (palmar) aspect of the wrist.

The area medial to the pisiform bone corresponds to the ulnar canal (Guyon's canal), which houses the ulnar nerve and artery. This is a deep compartment relative to the superficial veins. The cephalic vein is a lateral structure, whereas the pisiform is on the medial side of the wrist.

The cephalic vein is superficial to the tendons that form the anatomic snuffbox (abductor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor pollicis longus). It is not deep to them; the radial artery and scaphoid bone are the structures found deep within the snuffbox. Superficial veins are always located in the subcutaneous fascia.

The olecranon process is at the elbow, not the wrist. The cephalic vein does pass near the lateral side of the elbow region, but the question specifically asks for its location at the wrist. The basilic vein is the primary superficial vein on the medial side of the forearm and elbow.
citation: The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization. ... The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis.

coreConcept: Basilic vein for central venous catheterization
statement: Why is the basilic vein often preferred over the cephalic vein for the insertion of a central venous catheter from the cubital fossa?
options: It increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
It is more superficial and easier to palpate in obese patients
It lacks valves, allowing for easier advancement of the catheter
It is located further away from the brachial artery in the cubital fossa
It has a thicker muscular wall that prevents vein collapse during shock
correctOption: It increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is anatomically advantageous for central access because it follows a relatively straight course into the axillary vein and increases in caliber as it ascends. This direct path reduces the risk of the catheter getting 'hung up' at junctions, which is a common issue with the cephalic vein's more acute angle of entry into the axillary vein. It provides a smoother transition for long catheters.

The cephalic vein is actually often more superficial and easier to see in the forearm than the basilic vein, which becomes deeper as it moves proximally in the arm. However, visibility does not equate to ease of central advancement. The basilic vein's deeper, straighter course is the deciding factor for catheterization.

All major superficial veins of the limbs contain valves to prevent the backflow of blood. The presence or absence of valves is not the reason the basilic vein is preferred. Catheters are designed to pass through valves, but they struggle with sharp anatomical turns.

In the cubital fossa, the basilic vein (or the median cubital vein leading to it) is actually quite close to the brachial artery, separated only by the bicipital aponeurosis. The cephalic vein is located laterally, further from the main brachial artery. Proximity to the artery is a risk factor, not a reason for preference.

Superficial veins generally have similar wall structures and are all prone to collapse in states of severe shock or dehydration. The basilic vein does not have a uniquely thick muscular wall compared to the cephalic vein. Its preference is based on its diameter and linear anatomy.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein (see Fig. 3.68).

coreConcept: Palpation of the lateral epicondyle
statement: Which of the following structures is located immediately distal to the lateral epicondyle of the humerus?
options: Capitulum
Trochlea
Coronoid fossa
Olecranon fossa
Radial tuberosity
correctOption: Capitulum
optionExplanations: The capitulum is the rounded articular surface on the lateral aspect of the distal humerus that articulates with the head of the radius. It is situated immediately distal and slightly medial to the lateral epicondyle. The lateral epicondyle itself serves as a non-articular attachment point for ligaments and muscles.

The trochlea is the pulley-shaped articular surface on the medial side of the distal humerus, articulating with the ulna. It is located distal to the medial epicondyle, not the lateral one. The trochlea and capitulum together form the condyle of the humerus.

The coronoid fossa is a depression on the anterior surface of the humerus, superior to the trochlea. It receives the coronoid process of the ulna during elbow flexion. It is a medial structure and is proximal to the articular surface, not distal to the lateral epicondyle.

The olecranon fossa is a large deep depression on the posterior surface of the humerus, superior to the trochlea. It accommodates the olecranon of the ulna during elbow extension. Like the coronoid fossa, it is centrally/medially located and proximal to the joint line.

The radial tuberosity is a projection on the medial side of the neck of the radius, serving as the insertion for the biceps brachii. While it is in the lateral region of the forearm, it is significantly distal to the humerus and the lateral epicondyle. It is not part of the humerus itself.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. ... The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea.

coreConcept: Palpation of the posterior border of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is subcutaneous and can be easily palpated along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: Posterior aspect of the body
Anterior aspect of the body
Interosseous border
Coronoid process
Radial notch
correctOption: Posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The posterior border (or aspect) of the ulnar shaft is subcutaneous throughout its length. This makes it an important landmark for clinical assessment and a common site for feeling fractures or bony irregularities. It separates the flexor and extensor compartments of the forearm.
