The anterior aspect of the ulnar body is covered by the deep flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. It is not directly palpable through the skin. Accessing the anterior surface would require displacing significant muscle mass.

The interosseous border is the sharp lateral edge of the ulna where the interosseous membrane attaches. It is located deep between the radius and ulna and is surrounded by muscles. It cannot be palpated from the surface of the limb.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior and proximal part of the ulna. It is located deep within the cubital fossa and is covered by the brachialis muscle and other soft tissues. It is not palpable along the length of the forearm.

The radial notch is a small depression on the lateral side of the coronoid process that articulates with the head of the radius. It is a deep articular feature of the proximal ulna and is not accessible for surface palpation. It is part of the proximal radioulnar joint.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. ... The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow.

coreConcept: Coracoid process palpation
statement: A clinician is performing a surface anatomy examination of the shoulder. To palpate the coracoid process, where should they apply deep pressure?
options: Through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
Directly over the subcutaneous tip of the shoulder at the acromion
In the center of the axilla against the medial side of the humerus
Along the posterior border of the scapular spine near the medial margin
Superior to the medial third of the clavicle in the posterior triangle
correctOption: Through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a prominent scapular landmark that projects anteriorly and serves as an attachment for the short head of the biceps brachii and coracobrachialis. It is located deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle and can be felt by pressing firmly just below the lateral portion of the clavicle. This landmark is essential for orienting the clinician to the anterior aspect of the glenohumeral joint.

The acromion is the lateral extension of the scapular spine and forms the most lateral, subcutaneous point of the shoulder. While it is easily palpable, it is distinct from the coracoid process, which lies more anteriorly and deeper. Palpating the acromion does not require deep pressure through the deltoid muscle.

The center of the axilla is the site for palpating central axillary lymph nodes, not the coracoid process. This area is bounded by the pectoralis major anteriorly and the subscapularis posteriorly. The coracoid process is located superior to this region, near the clavicular attachment.

The posterior border of the scapular spine is subcutaneous and leads to the acromion, but it is on the dorsal aspect of the body. The coracoid process is an anteriorly projecting structure and cannot be reached from the posterior scapular spine. Palpation here would identify the spine of the scapula or the supraspinatus muscle.

The area superior to the medial third of the clavicle corresponds to the root of the neck and the posterior triangle. This region contains the subclavian artery and the trunks of the brachial plexus. The coracoid process is located much further laterally and inferiorly relative to this position.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve palpation at the medial epicondyle
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the medial two fingers after a blunt trauma to the elbow. During the physical exam, the clinician attempts to palpate the nerve most likely involved. Where is this nerve normally felt?
options: In the cubital fossa, medial to the brachial artery
On the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle in the ulnar sulcus
Lateral to the tendon of the biceps brachii in the cubital fossa
Deep to the brachioradialis muscle in the proximal forearm
Anterior to the lateral epicondyle of the humerus
correctOption: On the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle in the ulnar sulcus
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle of the humerus in a specific groove known as the ulnar sulcus. In this location, the nerve is subcutaneous and can be easily rolled against the bone, making it highly vulnerable to compression or direct trauma. Injury here typically results in sensory loss or paresthesia in the ulnar distribution of the hand.

The median nerve is located in the cubital fossa, medial to the brachial artery and deep to the bicipital aponeurosis. While it is a major nerve of the upper limb, it does not pass behind the medial epicondyle. Median nerve injury would typically affect the lateral aspect of the palm and the first three and a half fingers.

The radial nerve or its superficial branch is found lateral to the biceps tendon in the cubital fossa. It provides sensory innervation to the radial side of the hand and motor control to the extensors. It is not associated with the medial epicondyle or the ulnar sulcus.

The superficial branch of the radial nerve runs deep to the brachioradialis muscle in the forearm. This nerve is primarily sensory and does not pass through the elbow's medial compartment. It would not be the primary nerve palpated behind the medial epicondyle.

The area anterior to the lateral epicondyle contains the radial nerve as it descends into the forearm. This is on the opposite side of the elbow from the ulnar nerve. Palpation here would not reveal the ulnar nerve or explain symptoms in the medial fingers.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here

coreConcept: Scapula and rib level landmarks
statement: During a physical examination, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a surface landmark. Which rib level is typically identified by this structure?
options: Second rib
Fourth rib
Seventh rib
Tenth rib
Twelfth rib
correctOption: Seventh rib
optionExplanations: The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that corresponds to the level of the seventh rib and the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7). This landmark is frequently used by clinicians to count ribs or locate specific vertebral levels during a physical exam. It is easily palpable when the patient's arm is at their side.

The second rib is located much higher on the thoracic cage and is typically identified by the sternal angle (Angle of Louis). The superior angle of the scapula is closer to the level of the second rib, but the inferior angle is significantly lower. Using the inferior angle to identify the second rib would be a significant anatomical error.

The fourth rib is generally located at the level of the nipples in males and corresponds roughly to the middle of the scapular body. It is superior to the inferior angle of the scapula. The inferior angle is specifically aligned with the seventh rib, not the fourth.

The tenth rib is part of the lower thoracic cage and is located well below the inferior angle of the scapula. The scapula typically covers the second through seventh ribs. Palpating the tenth rib would require moving significantly inferior to the scapular tip.

The twelfth rib is a floating rib located at the base of the thoracic cage, near the lumbar region. It is far inferior to the scapula and is used as a landmark for the kidneys or the T12/L1 vertebral level. It cannot be identified using the inferior angle of the scapula.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Breast examination maneuvers
statement: A 55-year-old woman is undergoing a clinical breast examination. The clinician asks her to sit up and raise both arms above her head. What is the primary anatomical reason for this specific maneuver?
options: To relax the pectoralis major muscle for deeper palpation of the axillary tail
To reveal skin dimpling or nipple retraction caused by a carcinoma tethered to suspensory ligaments
To allow the breast tissue to fall laterally for better visualization of the medial quadrants
To stretch the skin to make superficial cysts more visible to the naked eye
To facilitate the palpation of the apical group of axillary lymph nodes
correctOption: To reveal skin dimpling or nipple retraction caused by a carcinoma tethered to suspensory ligaments
optionExplanations: Raising the arms above the head tensions the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) and the lactiferous ducts. If a carcinoma is present and has invaded these structures, the tension will cause visible dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple. This maneuver is a critical part of the physical exam to detect underlying malignancies that may not be palpable.

Raising the arms actually tensions the pectoralis major muscle rather than relaxing it. To relax the pectoralis major for axillary palpation, the patient is typically asked to place their hands on their hips and press medially. Relaxation is necessary to feel deep into the axillary fossa, which is not the goal of the overhead arm maneuver.

While raising the arms does change the distribution of breast tissue, its primary clinical purpose is not to move tissue laterally for medial visualization. Medial quadrants are best examined while the patient is supine, allowing the tissue to flatten against the chest wall. The overhead maneuver specifically targets signs of tethering.

Superficial cysts may become more prominent with skin stretching, but this is not the primary diagnostic goal of the maneuver. The maneuver is specifically designed to identify signs of invasive carcinoma, such as dimpling or retraction. Cysts typically do not cause the same tethering effect as malignant tumors.

Palpation of the apical axillary lymph nodes requires the patient to relax their shoulder muscles and let the arm hang down. This allows the examiner to reach high into the apex of the axilla toward the first rib. Raising the arms would tighten the axillary fascia and muscles, making it impossible to palpate the apical nodes.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatomes
statement: A clinician is testing sensory perception to evaluate the integrity of the C7 spinal segment. Which specific area of the hand should be tested?
options: The skin over the lateral aspect of the shoulder (deltoid region)
The skin of the thumb and lateral forearm
The skin of the middle finger
The skin of the little finger and medial hand
The skin of the medial aspect of the arm near the axilla
correctOption: The skin of the middle finger
optionExplanations: The C7 dermatome is consistently mapped to the middle finger of the hand. Testing sensation in this specific area allows clinicians to isolate the C7 spinal nerve root during a neurological examination. It is a key landmark for distinguishing between different levels of cervical radiculopathy.

The lateral aspect of the shoulder is primarily supplied by the C5 dermatome, which corresponds to the axillary nerve's sensory distribution. Testing this area evaluates the C5 spinal segment, not C7. Loss of sensation here often accompanies deltoid muscle weakness.

The thumb and the lateral (radial) side of the forearm are typically associated with the C6 dermatome. This segment is tested to evaluate the C6 nerve root, often in conjunction with the biceps reflex. It is distinct from the C7 distribution found on the middle finger.

The little finger and the medial (ulnar) aspect of the hand are supplied by the C8 dermatome. This area is used to assess the C8 spinal segment, which is the lowest cervical nerve root. It is located on the opposite side of the hand from the C6 distribution.

The medial aspect of the arm near the axilla and the upper medial forearm are supplied by the T1 and T2 dermatomes. These segments represent the transition from the upper limb to the thoracic dermatomes. They are located along the medial margin of the limb, proximal to the C8 distribution.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Cephalic vein surface anatomy
statement: A patient in hypovolemic shock requires urgent peripheral venous access, but most superficial veins are collapsed and invisible. Based on anatomical landmarks, where is the cephalic vein most consistently located for a 'blind' cannulation attempt?
options: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Medial to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris at the wrist
Directly over the olecranon process of the ulna
In the groove between the brachialis and triceps muscles
Lateral to the medial epicondyle in the cubital fossa
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein has a very consistent anatomical position in the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process at the wrist. This consistency makes it a valuable site for venipuncture or cutdown procedures when other veins are not visible due to circulatory collapse. It then ascends along the lateral aspect of the forearm.

The area medial to the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon at the wrist contains the ulnar artery and nerve, not the cephalic vein. The ulnar vein is deep and usually accompanies the artery. This site is not used for routine peripheral venous access, especially in shock.

The olecranon process is a bony landmark at the elbow and is covered by a bursa and skin, but not by a major superficial vein suitable for cannulation. The superficial veins of the elbow, like the median cubital, are located anteriorly in the cubital fossa. Palpating the olecranon would not assist in finding the cephalic vein.

The groove between the brachialis and triceps muscles is on the lateral aspect of the arm and contains the radial nerve and profunda brachii artery. While the cephalic vein does travel in the deltopectoral groove higher up, it does not reside in this deep muscular groove. It remains superficial throughout its course in the arm.

The area lateral to the medial epicondyle is the cubital fossa, where the basilic vein and median cubital vein are found. The cephalic vein is located on the lateral side of the cubital fossa, not the medial side. The medial side is more closely associated with the basilic vein and the brachial artery.
citation: When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis.

coreConcept: Ulna palpation and scaphoid clinical signs
statement: Which of the following statements correctly describes the surface anatomy of the forearm and wrist bones?
options: The posterior border of the ulna is subcutaneous and palpable from the elbow to the wrist
The scaphoid bone is most easily palpated on the medial side of the wrist near the pisiform
The radius is subcutaneous along its entire posterior length, similar to the ulna
Tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox usually indicates a fracture of the triquetrum
The anterior surface of the ulna is the primary site for taking the radial pulse
correctOption: The posterior border of the ulna is subcutaneous and palpable from the elbow to the wrist
optionExplanations: The posterior border of the ulna is rounded and lies directly under the skin, making it palpable along its entire length from the olecranon to the styloid process. This makes the ulna a useful landmark for assessing forearm fractures or performing surgical approaches. Unlike the radius, it is not heavily covered by muscles posteriorly.

The scaphoid bone is located on the lateral (radial) side of the carpus and is most accessible for palpation within the anatomic snuffbox. The pisiform bone is located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris. These two bones are on opposite sides of the wrist joint.

The radius is largely covered by the extensor muscles of the forearm along its posterior aspect, making it much less palpable than the ulna. Only the distal end of the radius, near the styloid process, is truly subcutaneous. The ulna is unique in having a completely palpable posterior border.

Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox is a classic clinical sign of a scaphoid fracture, often following a fall on an outstretched hand. The triquetrum is located on the medial side of the wrist and would not cause tenderness in the lateral snuffbox. Scaphoid fractures are critical to identify due to the risk of avascular necrosis.

The radial pulse is taken on the anterior surface of the distal radius, not the ulna. The radial artery lies between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis. The ulnar pulse can be felt lateral to the pisiform bone on the ulnar side of the wrist.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Radiology of the elbow region
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anatomical angulation of the distal humerus relative to the humeral shaft?
options: Angulated forward 45°
Angulated backward 15°
Angulated forward 90°
Perfectly aligned with the shaft (0°)
Angulated medially 30°
correctOption: Angulated forward 45°
optionExplanations: The distal end of the humerus is normally tilted anteriorly (forward) at an angle of approximately 45 degrees relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This angulation is crucial for the normal range of motion of the elbow joint, particularly flexion. Recognizing this normal tilt on a lateral X-ray is essential for diagnosing supracondylar fractures.

An angulation of 15 degrees backward would be highly abnormal and likely indicate a displaced fracture or a significant congenital deformity. The normal anatomy requires an anterior tilt to accommodate the coronoid process of the ulna during flexion. A posterior tilt would severely limit the functional use of the arm.

A 90-degree forward angulation would represent an extreme deformity and is not a normal anatomical feature. Such a sharp angle would be inconsistent with the structural integrity of the humerus and the mechanics of the elbow joint. The standard physiological tilt is much more moderate at 45 degrees.

If the distal humerus were perfectly aligned (0 degrees) with the shaft, the elbow's range of motion would be significantly compromised. The 45-degree anterior tilt allows the forearm to clear the humerus during full flexion. This 'offset' is a fundamental feature of the hinge joint's design.

Medial angulation refers to a coronal plane deviation, whereas the 45-degree tilt is observed in the sagittal plane on a lateral radiograph. While the humerus does have a carrying angle (valgus), this is measured in the anterior-posterior view, not the lateral view. The lateral view specifically highlights the anterior-posterior angulation of the condyles.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Deltoid wasting and greater tuberosity palpation
statement: A patient presents with a loss of the rounded contour of the shoulder following an axillary nerve injury. Which bony landmark becomes significantly more palpable due to the resulting muscle atrophy?
options: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
Lesser tuberosity of the humerus
Coracoid process of the scapula
Infraglenoid tubercle
Surgical neck of the humerus
correctOption: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
optionExplanations: The deltoid muscle normally provides the rounded shape of the shoulder and covers the greater tuberosity of the humerus. When the axillary nerve is damaged, the deltoid undergoes rapid atrophy (wasting), causing the shoulder to appear 'squared off.' This loss of muscle bulk makes the greater tuberosity much more prominent and easy to palpate.

The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and is primarily covered by the subscapularis muscle and the pectoralis major. While deltoid wasting might slightly increase its accessibility, it is not the primary landmark that becomes prominent. The greater tuberosity is the most lateral bony point covered by the deltoid.

The coracoid process is a scapular landmark located anteriorly and is not covered by the main bulk of the deltoid muscle. It is palpated via deep pressure inferior to the lateral clavicle regardless of deltoid status. Its prominence does not change significantly with deltoid atrophy compared to the greater tuberosity.

The infraglenoid tubercle is located deep within the axilla at the superior end of the lateral border of the scapula. It serves as the origin for the long head of the triceps brachii. It is far too deep to be affected by the superficial wasting of the deltoid muscle.

The surgical neck of the humerus is the site where the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap around the bone. While it is a common site for fractures that cause axillary nerve injury, the neck itself is not a palpable 'landmark' that becomes prominent like the tuberosities. It remains deep to the remnants of the deltoid and other muscles.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse.

coreConcept: Basilic vein for central catheterization
statement: Why is the basilic vein often preferred over the cephalic vein for the insertion of a central venous catheter from the cubital fossa?
options: It increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
It is more superficial and easier to visualize than the cephalic vein
It lacks valves, allowing for easier advancement of the catheter
It is located further away from the brachial artery, reducing the risk of accidental puncture
It drains directly into the external jugular vein
correctOption: It increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is anatomically advantageous for central access because it follows a relatively straight path and increases in caliber as it moves proximally toward the axilla. This direct continuity with the axillary vein reduces the likelihood of the catheter getting 'stuck' or diverted. In contrast, the cephalic vein often enters the axillary vein at a sharp angle in the deltopectoral triangle.

The cephalic vein is often more superficial and easier to see in the forearm and arm than the basilic vein. However, ease of initial visualization does not make it superior for central catheterization. The internal path and diameter are the more critical factors for successful catheter placement.

Both the basilic and cephalic veins contain numerous valves, as do all major superficial veins of the limbs. The presence of valves is a challenge for any peripheral-to-central catheterization. The preference for the basilic vein is based on its diameter and straight course, not a lack of valves.

Actually, the basilic vein in the cubital fossa and arm lies in close proximity to the brachial artery and the median nerve. This proximity requires careful technique to avoid accidental arterial puncture. The cephalic vein is located laterally and is further from these major neurovascular structures.

The basilic vein drains into the axillary vein, which then becomes the subclavian vein and eventually the brachiocephalic vein. It does not drain into the external jugular vein. The external jugular vein is a superficial vein of the neck that drains into the subclavian vein separately.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia.

coreConcept: Clinical signs of breast carcinoma during physical examination
statement: A 55-year-old woman presents for a routine physical examination. Upon inspection of the breasts, the clinician notes that the right nipple is retracted compared to the left. Which of the following anatomical structures is most likely being pulled by an underlying malignancy to cause this specific finding?
options: Suspensory ligaments of Cooper
Lactiferous ducts
Pectoralis major fascia
Retromammary space
Deep cervical fascia
correctOption: Lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: The suspensory ligaments of Cooper connect the dermis to the deep fascia; when a tumor involves these ligaments, it typically results in skin dimpling rather than nipple retraction. This finding is often elicited by asking the patient to raise their arms, which increases the tension on the tethered skin.

Nipple retraction is specifically caused by a carcinoma within the breast substance pulling on and shortening the lactiferous ducts that lead to the nipple. This is a classic clinical sign of an underlying malignancy that has invaded the ductal system, necessitating further diagnostic imaging.

While a tumor may become fixed to the pectoralis major fascia, this usually results in decreased mobility of the breast mass against the chest wall rather than focal nipple retraction. Fixation is assessed by asking the patient to contract the pectoral muscles during palpation.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue between the breast and the pectoral fascia that allows for breast mobility. Involvement of this space by a tumor would lead to fixation of the breast to the thoracic wall but does not directly cause the nipple to retract.

The deep cervical fascia is located in the neck and is not anatomically related to the breast tissue or the mechanisms of nipple retraction. It does not play a role in the clinical presentation of breast carcinoma or the physical findings observed during a breast exam.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the cephalic vein for emergency venipuncture
statement: In a clinical emergency where a patient is in a state of shock and superficial veins are not readily visible, which anatomical landmark provides the most reliable guide for locating the cephalic vein for venipuncture?
options: Medial to the olecranon process
Anterior to the medial epicondyle
Posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Lateral to the bicipital aponeurosis
Deep to the flexor retinaculum
correctOption: Posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The area medial to the olecranon process is the site of the ulnar nerve and is not a standard location for superficial venous access. Palpating here would likely result in nerve irritation rather than successful venipuncture.

The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is where the basilic vein typically ascends, but it is less constant in position than the cephalic vein at the wrist. In shock, the basilic vein may be difficult to locate without visual cues.

The cephalic vein is known for its relatively constant anatomical position in the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process. This makes it an ideal 'fail-safe' site for venous access when more proximal veins in the cubital fossa are collapsed or invisible.

The area lateral to the bicipital aponeurosis in the cubital fossa contains the median cubital vein, which is often the first choice for venipuncture but frequently collapses during circulatory shock. The aponeurosis itself serves to protect the deeper brachial artery.

The flexor retinaculum is a deep structure at the wrist that forms the roof of the carpal tunnel; superficial veins like the cephalic vein lie in the subcutaneous tissue above such deep fascia. Accessing a vein deep to the retinaculum is not anatomically possible for standard venipuncture.
citation: The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization. Every clinical professional, in an emergency, should know where to obtain blood from the arm. When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Dermatomal mapping of the upper limb
statement: During a neurological examination of the upper limb to test the integrity of the spinal cord segments, which specific digit should be tested to evaluate the C7 dermatome?
options: Thumb
Index finger
Middle finger
Ring finger
Little finger
correctOption: Middle finger
optionExplanations: The thumb is primarily associated with the C6 dermatome, which also covers the lateral aspect of the forearm. Testing the thumb evaluates the sixth cervical spinal nerve rather than the seventh.

The index finger often receives sensory innervation from both C6 and C7, but it is not the classic, isolated landmark used for testing the C7 segment. The middle finger is the more definitive landmark for C7.

The C7 dermatome is classically situated on the middle finger, making it the primary site for sensory testing of the seventh cervical spinal nerve. This mapping is essential for localizing spinal cord or nerve root injuries in the cervical region.

The ring finger typically falls within the transition zone between the C7 and C8 dermatomes. It is not used as a primary landmark for isolating the C7 spinal segment during a standard neurological exam.
The little finger is the classic landmark for the C8 dermatome, which also extends along the medial margin of the hand. Testing the little finger evaluates the eighth cervical spinal nerve.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Radiological anatomy of the distal humerus
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow in a patient who sustained a fall, what is the normal anterior angulation of the distal humerus relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft?
optionExplanations: An angulation of 15° would be significantly less than the normal anatomical tilt, potentially indicating a supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement. Such a finding would require orthopedic intervention to restore normal joint mechanics.

While 30° is closer to the normal range, it still represents a deviation from the standard 45° anterior tilt seen in healthy individuals. Accurate assessment of this angle is vital for diagnosing subtle bony injuries in the elbow region.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at 45° relative to the shaft. This specific angulation is a critical radiological landmark; its loss or alteration is a primary indicator of supracondylar fractures, especially in pediatric patients.

An angulation of 60° would represent excessive anterior tilting, which is not typical of normal humeral anatomy. Such an exaggerated angle would likely be the result of a malunited fracture or a significant acute traumatic deformity.

A 90° angle would imply that the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is anatomically incorrect. This would represent a severe deformity or a complete disruption of the distal humeral architecture.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Surface landmarks of the elbow joint in extension
statement: During a physical examination of a fully extended elbow, what is the normal spatial relationship between the medial epicondyle, the lateral epicondyle, and the top of the olecranon process?
options: They form an equilateral triangle
They form a right-angled triangle
They form a straight horizontal line
The olecranon is significantly superior to the epicondyles
The epicondyles are obscured by the triceps tendon
correctOption: They form a straight horizontal line
optionExplanations: The formation of an equilateral triangle by these three bony landmarks occurs when the elbow is flexed, not when it is extended. This change in configuration is a key diagnostic feature used to assess joint alignment.

A right-angled triangle is not a standard anatomical configuration for these landmarks in either flexion or extension. Deviations into such shapes usually suggest a dislocation or a fracture of one of the bony components.

In full extension, the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the olecranon process normally align to form a straight horizontal line. This relationship is used by clinicians to quickly screen for gross abnormalities or displacements of the elbow joint.

The olecranon process is the 'point' of the elbow and aligns horizontally with the epicondyles in extension; it does not sit significantly superior to them unless there is a major structural disruption. Its position relative to the epicondyles changes predictably with flexion.

While the triceps tendon inserts onto the olecranon, the epicondyles remain easily palpable on the medial and lateral sides of the distal humerus. They are not obscured in a normal examination of the extended elbow.
citation: The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon and the pulley-shaped trochlea of the humerus; it also has strong medial and lateral ligaments. When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy and vertebral levels of the scapula
statement: A clinician is using surface landmarks to identify vertebral levels on a patient's back. The inferior angle of the scapula is palpated. This landmark corresponds to the level of which thoracic vertebral spine?
options: T2
T4
T7
L1
correctOption: T7
optionExplanations: The T2 vertebral level is generally associated with the superior angle of the scapula, not the inferior angle. Using T2 as a landmark for the inferior angle would result in an incorrect assessment of the patient's spinal anatomy.

The T4 vertebral level is roughly at the level of the root of the scapular spine. It is located superior to the inferior angle and is often used to identify the division between the superior and inferior mediastinum.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that corresponds to the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7). This is frequently used in clinical practice to count ribs or localize spinal levels during a physical exam.
The T10 vertebral level is located much lower on the back, near the level of the xiphoid process anteriorly. It is well below the inferior margin of the scapula in a person with normal posture.

The L1 vertebral level marks the beginning of the lumbar spine and is located in the small of the back. It is far inferior to the scapula and is associated with landmarks such as the transpyloric plane.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Palpation of the humerus in deltoid muscle wasting
statement: A patient presents with axillary nerve palsy, resulting in significant wasting of the deltoid muscle. Which bony landmark of the humerus becomes much more prominent and easily palpable as a result of this muscle atrophy?
options: Lesser tuberosity
Greater tuberosity
Deltoid tuberosity
Intertubercular sulcus
Anatomic neck
correctOption: Greater tuberosity
optionExplanations: The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis. While it may become slightly more accessible, it is not the primary landmark that becomes prominent with deltoid wasting.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus is normally covered by the thick deltoid muscle. When the deltoid wastes due to paralysis (such as in axillary nerve injury), the muscle bulk is lost, making the greater tuberosity readily palpable and giving the shoulder a 'squared-off' appearance.

The deltoid tuberosity is the site of insertion for the deltoid muscle on the lateral shaft of the humerus. While the muscle that attaches here wastes, the tuberosity itself is less prominent than the more proximal greater tuberosity at the shoulder tip.

The intertubercular sulcus (bicipital groove) lies between the greater and lesser tuberosities and contains the long head of the biceps tendon. It is a deep structure and does not become a prominent palpable landmark compared to the tuberosities themselves.

The anatomic neck of the humerus is the groove separating the head from the tuberosities. It is a deep structure and is not typically palpated on the surface, even in cases of significant muscular atrophy.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated.

coreConcept: Principles and safety of mammography
statement: Which of the following statements regarding the clinical use and safety of mammography is correct?
options: It uses high-frequency sound waves to detect cysts
It requires high doses of radiation to penetrate breast tissue
It uses extremely low doses of x-rays to minimize danger
It is only used for the detection of malignant tumors
It cannot be repeated more than once every five years
correctOption: It uses extremely low doses of x-rays to minimize danger
optionExplanations: The use of high-frequency sound waves describes ultrasonography, not mammography. While ultrasound is used for breast imaging (especially to distinguish cysts from solids), mammography is specifically a radiographic (X-ray) technique.

Mammography is designed to use the lowest possible dose of radiation that still provides diagnostic quality. High doses of radiation would increase the risk of induced malignancies, which is contrary to the goals of a screening program.

Mammography utilizes extremely low doses of x-rays, ensuring that the risks associated with radiation exposure are minimal. This safety profile allows the examination to be used as a frequent screening tool for the early detection of tumors and cysts.

Mammography is used to screen for and detect a wide range of breast abnormalities, including benign tumors, cysts, and calcifications, in addition to malignant carcinomas. It is a versatile tool for general breast health monitoring.

Because the radiation dose is extremely low, mammography can be repeated relatively often (typically annually or biennially for screening) without significant cumulative danger. A five-year restriction would be inappropriate for effective cancer screening.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: Anterior border
Interosseous border
Posterior aspect of the body
Coronoid process
Radial notch
correctOption: Posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be easily palpated along its length.

The interosseous border of the ulna is the sharp lateral edge where the interosseous membrane attaches. It is located deep between the muscles of the anterior and posterior compartments and is not accessible for palpation.

The posterior aspect (or border) of the body of the ulna is rounded, subcutaneous, and can be felt easily from the olecranon down to the ulnar styloid process. This makes it a useful landmark for assessing the bone for fractures or deformities.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior and proximal part of the ulna that fits into the coronoid fossa of the humerus during flexion. It is located deep within the cubital fossa and is not palpable on the surface.

The radial notch is a small depression on the lateral side of the coronoid process that articulates with the head of the radius. It is a deep articular surface and is not accessible to surface palpation.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length.

coreConcept: Palpation of the axillary artery and sheath
statement: To verify the position of the axillary sheath, a clinician palpates the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. Against which muscle is this part of the artery most easily felt in the axilla?
options: Pectoralis minor
Coracobrachialis
Teres major
Serratus anterior
Latissimus dorsi
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor muscle is used to divide the axillary artery into three parts; the second part lies behind it. The third part, which is used for palpation, is distal to the pectoralis minor.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery in the axilla. While it is a close neighbor, the artery is specifically described as lying in front of the teres major for the purpose of palpation.

The third part of the axillary artery is palpable in the axilla where it lies anterior to the teres major muscle. Feeling these pulsations allows the clinician to accurately locate the axillary sheath, which is important for procedures like brachial plexus blocks.
The serratus anterior muscle forms the medial wall of the axilla. The axillary artery travels along the lateral wall/apex area and is not typically palpated against the serratus anterior.

The latissimus dorsi forms the posterior axillary fold along with the teres major. While the artery is near both, the specific anatomical landmark for palpating the third part of the axillary artery is the teres major.
citation: The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the acromion
statement: A clinician is palpating the bony landmarks of the shoulder to assess for a suspected dislocation. Which structure forms the most lateral, flat extension of the scapular spine and serves as the palpable 'tip' of the shoulder?
options: Coracoid process
Acromion
Greater tuberosity
Lesser tuberosity
Sternal end of the clavicle
correctOption: Acromion
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is located anterolaterally and inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle, rather than forming the lateral tip of the shoulder. It is palpated through the deltoid muscle and serves as an attachment for the short head of the biceps and coracobrachialis. It does not represent the most lateral extension of the scapular spine.

The acromion is the correct anatomical landmark as it is the direct lateral continuation of the scapular spine. It is a broad, flat, and subcutaneous structure that defines the superior-lateral contour of the shoulder. Its palpability makes it a critical reference point for shoulder examinations and injections.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus is located lateral to the acromion but is covered by the thick deltoid muscle. While it becomes more prominent in deltoid wasting, it is not a part of the scapula. It serves as the insertion site for the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles.

The lesser tuberosity is situated on the anterior aspect of the proximal humerus and is not the lateral tip of the shoulder. It provides the insertion point for the subscapularis muscle. It is generally less palpable than the greater tuberosity due to its anterior position and overlying soft tissues.

The sternal end of the clavicle is located medially at the root of the neck where it articulates with the manubrium. It is far from the lateral tip of the shoulder. This landmark is used to identify the sternoclavicular joint rather than the acromial region.
citation: The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder. The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the olecranon
statement: During a physical examination of the elbow, which bony landmark is identified as the proximal end of the ulna that constitutes the 'point' of the elbow?
options: Lateral epicondyle
Medial epicondyle
Olecranon
Coronoid process
Radial head
correctOption: Olecranon
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is a projection on the distal humerus, not the ulna, and is located on the lateral side of the elbow. It serves as the common origin for the extensor muscles of the forearm. While palpable, it does not form the posterior point of the elbow.

The medial epicondyle is a prominent projection on the medial side of the distal humerus. It is the origin for the flexor muscles of the forearm and protects the ulnar nerve posteriorly. It is a key landmark but is medial to the elbow's posterior point.

The olecranon is the large, proximal process of the ulna that fits into the olecranon fossa of the humerus during extension. It is subcutaneous and forms the most prominent posterior part of the elbow. Its superficial nature makes it easily accessible for palpation and prone to bursitis.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior part of the proximal ulna. It is not palpable from the surface because it is deep to the brachialis muscle and other soft tissues. It forms the anterior boundary of the trochlear notch.

The radial head is located on the lateral aspect of the elbow, distal to the lateral epicondyle. It can be felt rotating during pronation and supination of the forearm. It does not contribute to the posterior 'point' of the elbow joint.
citation: The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Anatomic snuffbox boundaries and contents
statement: A patient presents with pain on the posterolateral side of the wrist after a fall. The clinician palpates a triangular depression bounded medially by the extensor pollicis longus. Which arterial pulse is most readily felt within this specific area?
options: Ulnar artery
Brachial artery
Radial artery
Anterior interosseous artery
Deep palmar arch
correctOption: Radial artery
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery is located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist, lateral to the pisiform bone. It does not pass through the anatomic snuffbox, which is located on the lateral (radial) side. Palpating the ulnar pulse requires assessment near the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon.

The brachial artery is found in the arm and cubital fossa, well proximal to the wrist. It terminates by dividing into the radial and ulnar arteries near the neck of the radius. It is not accessible for palpation at the posterolateral wrist.

The radial artery passes through the anatomic snuffbox as it moves from the anterior forearm to the dorsum of the hand. It lies deep to the tendons forming the snuffbox boundaries and can be compressed against the scaphoid and trapezium. This site is a secondary location for pulse assessment if the distal radius site is unavailable.

The anterior interosseous artery travels deep in the forearm along the interosseous membrane. It primarily supplies the deep muscles of the forearm and does not emerge superficially in the snuffbox. It is not a palpable artery in clinical practice.

The deep palmar arch is formed primarily by the terminal branch of the radial artery within the palm of the hand. While the radial artery contributes to it after passing through the snuffbox, the arch itself is deep within the hand. It cannot be palpated as a discrete pulse in the snuffbox region.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis (see Figs. 3.34 and 3.105B, C). Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here.

coreConcept: Palpation of the brachial artery
statement: To assess the systemic pulse in the mid-arm region, a clinician palpates the brachial artery. According to its topographical relations, where is this artery specifically located in the arm?
options: On the triceps brachii, overlapped medially by the coracobrachialis
On the brachialis, overlapped laterally by the biceps brachii
On the humerus, posterior to the medial intermuscular septum
In the deltopectoral groove, lateral to the cephalic vein
Deep to the pronator teres in the proximal forearm
correctOption: On the brachialis, overlapped laterally by the biceps brachii
optionExplanations: The triceps brachii is located in the posterior compartment of the arm, whereas the brachial artery travels in the anterior compartment. The coracobrachialis is located superiorly and does not overlap the artery in the mid-arm. This description incorrectly identifies the muscular relationships.

The brachial artery descends in the arm on the anterior surface of the brachialis muscle. It is positioned medially and is partially covered or overlapped by the medial border of the biceps brachii muscle. This relationship is essential for compressing the artery against the humerus to control bleeding.

The brachial artery is located anterior to the medial intermuscular septum, not posterior to it. The posterior compartment contains the profunda brachii artery and the radial nerve. Placing the artery posterior to the septum would place it in the wrong anatomical compartment.

The deltopectoral groove contains the cephalic vein and the deltoid branch of the thoracoacromial artery, but not the brachial artery. The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major, distal to the axilla. The groove is too superior and lateral for brachial artery palpation.

While the brachial artery does pass into the cubital fossa and eventually divides, the question specifies the mid-arm region. In the proximal forearm, the artery has already bifurcated into the radial and ulnar arteries. The pronator teres relates more to the ulnar artery's path.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Subclavian artery compression
statement: A patient suffers a severe laceration to the upper arm with profuse arterial bleeding. To stop the hemorrhage, a first responder applies pressure in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck. Against which structure is the artery being compressed?
options: Clavicle
First rib
Transverse process of C7
Scalenus anterior muscle
Body of the hyoid bone
correctOption: First rib
optionExplanations: The clavicle lies anterior to the subclavian artery, but it is not the primary surface against which the artery is compressed to stop hemorrhage. Compression is directed downward and backward toward the rib. The clavicle's mobility makes it a less stable base for effective arterial occlusion.

The subclavian artery passes over the superior surface of the first rib as it exits the thoracic outlet. By applying deep pressure in the supraclavicular fossa (posterior triangle), the artery can be pinned against the first rib. This maneuver is a vital emergency technique to control distal upper limb bleeding.

The transverse process of C7 is located more posteriorly and superiorly than the point where the subclavian artery crosses the first rib. While the vertebral artery enters the transverse foramen of C6, it is not the target for compression in this scenario. The first rib provides a much broader and more accessible shelf.

The scalenus anterior muscle actually lies anterior to the second part of the subclavian artery. Compressing the artery against a soft muscle would be ineffective for stopping high-pressure arterial flow. The goal is to use a hard bony surface like the first rib.

The hyoid bone is located in the anterior midline of the neck, far superior to the subclavian artery's path. It is associated with the larynx and tongue muscles rather than the major vessels of the upper limb. It serves no role in controlling limb hemorrhage.
citation: The subclavian artery, as it crosses the first rib to become the axillary artery, can be palpated in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck (see Fig. 3.66). The artery can be compressed here against the first rib to stop a catastrophic hemorrhage. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle.

coreConcept: Allen's test for ulnar artery patency
statement: During an Allen's test, the clinician compresses the radial artery and asks the patient to clench their fist. Upon opening the hand, the palm remains white (pallor) for an extended period. What does this specific finding indicate?
options: The radial artery is patent.
The ulnar artery is occluded or insufficient.
The superficial palmar arch is primarily supplied by the radial artery.
The median nerve is compressed at the wrist.
The brachial artery is bifurcating abnormally high.
correctOption: The ulnar artery is occluded or insufficient.
optionExplanations: The radial artery is being intentionally compressed by the clinician during this phase of the test. Therefore, its patency is not being assessed by the return of color; rather, its occlusion is the baseline for the test. The test evaluates the alternative supply.

If the palm remains white while the radial artery is compressed, it means the ulnar artery is failing to provide sufficient collateral blood flow to the palmar arches. A normal result would be a rapid return of pink color (rubor) within seconds. This indicates ulnar artery insufficiency or occlusion.

While the radial artery does contribute to the palmar arches, the Allen's test specifically checks if the ulnar artery can maintain perfusion alone. The failure of the palm to turn pink does not define the 'primary' source but rather the 'inadequacy' of the ulnar source. Most individuals have a dominant ulnar supply to the superficial arch.

Median nerve compression (Carpal Tunnel Syndrome) affects sensation and motor function in the hand but does not cause the vascular pallor observed in the Allen's test. The test is purely vascular and does not assess nerve integrity. Nerve issues would present with paresthesia or wasting.

A high bifurcation of the brachial artery is a common anatomical variation but would not typically cause a positive Allen's test if the distal ulnar artery is patent. The test assesses the functional flow at the wrist level regardless of where the parent vessel divided. It is not a diagnostic tool for bifurcation levels.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches. When the patient is asked to open the hands, the skin of the palms is at first white, and then normally the blood quickly flows into the arches through the ulnar arteries.

coreConcept: Median cubital vein and bicipital aponeurosis
statement: When performing venipuncture in the cubital fossa, which anatomical structure provides a protective barrier between the median cubital vein and the deeper brachial artery?
options: Brachialis muscle
Bicipital aponeurosis
Pronator teres muscle
Deep fascia of the arm
Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm
correctOption: Bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle lies deep to the brachial artery, forming part of the floor of the cubital fossa. It does not sit between the vein and the artery. Therefore, it cannot provide a protective barrier against needle penetration from the surface to the artery.

The bicipital aponeurosis (lacertus fibrosus) is a triangular band of fascia that runs medially from the biceps tendon. It passes superficial to the brachial artery and deep to the median cubital vein. This anatomical arrangement helps prevent accidental arterial puncture during routine blood draws.

The pronator teres muscle forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. While it is in the vicinity, it does not serve as a horizontal barrier between the superficial veins and the brachial artery. Its primary role is forearm pronation and elbow flexion.

The deep fascia of the arm (brachial fascia) invests the muscles but the specific structure separating the vein and artery in the fossa is the specialized bicipital aponeurosis. General deep fascia is present, but the aponeurosis is the clinically cited protective layer. It is a reinforcement of the deep fascia.

The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is a superficial structure that usually runs lateral to the biceps tendon. It does not lie between the median cubital vein and the brachial artery. Injury to this nerve during venipuncture would cause sensory loss, not arterial damage.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein. The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization.

coreConcept: Palpation of anterior (pectoral) axillary lymph nodes
statement: To specifically palpate the anterior (pectoral) group of axillary lymph nodes during a breast examination, in which direction and against which structure should the examiner's fingers press?
options: Backward against the subscapularis muscle
Laterally against the humerus
Forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
Superiorly toward the apex of the axilla
correctOption: Forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
optionExplanations: Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to palpate the posterior (subscapular) lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the posterior axillary fold. This maneuver would miss the pectoral group entirely.

Pressing laterally against the humerus or the medial side of the axillary vein is the method for palpating the lateral lymph nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. They are located along the lateral wall of the axilla.

The anterior or pectoral nodes are situated along the lower border of the pectoralis minor, behind the pectoralis major. By reaching into the axilla and pressing forward against the muscle wall, the examiner can trap these nodes against the pectoralis major. This is a standard part of an oncological breast exam.

Pressing medially against the thoracic wall (serratus anterior) is used to palpate the medial or central lymph nodes. These nodes are located deep in the axillary fat. They are not the specific target when assessing the anterior pectoral chain.

Superiorly directed palpation toward the apex of the axilla is intended to reach the apical (subclavicular) nodes. These nodes are located at the very top of the axilla, near the first rib. This is the final stage of the axillary nodal clearance assessment.
citation: The anterior (pectoral) nodes may be palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall of the axilla. The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein.

coreConcept: Bony landmarks of the elbow in flexion and extension
statement: A clinician is assessing the alignment of a patient's elbow after a traumatic injury. If the elbow is placed in full flexion, what is the expected normal topographical relationship between the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the olecranon?
options: They should form a straight horizontal line.
They should form an equilateral triangle.
The olecranon should be superior to the epicondyles.
The epicondyles should overlap the olecranon.
They should form a vertical line along the ulnar axis.
correctOption: They should form an equilateral triangle.
optionExplanations: A straight horizontal line is the expected relationship when the elbow is in full extension, not flexion. In this position, the olecranon moves superiorly into the olecranon fossa. Disruption of this line in extension suggests a fracture or dislocation.

In a normal elbow flexed to 90 degrees or more, the two epicondyles and the tip of the olecranon form the vertices of an equilateral triangle. This relationship is a key clinical sign used to differentiate between supracondylar fractures (where the triangle is preserved) and elbow dislocations (where the triangle is distorted).

While the olecranon does move relative to the humerus, describing it simply as 'superior' is anatomically imprecise compared to the 'equilateral triangle' configuration. The triangle describes the 3D relationship between the three points. In flexion, the olecranon actually moves inferiorly and posteriorly relative to the humeral shaft.

The epicondyles do not overlap the olecranon; they remain distinct palpable landmarks on the medial and lateral sides of the joint. The olecranon remains the most posterior point. Overlapping would imply a severe impaction fracture or gross deformity.

A vertical line along the ulnar axis does not describe the relationship between these three points in any standard anatomical position. The epicondyles are humeral landmarks, while the olecranon is ulnar. Their relationship is primarily horizontal or triangular depending on the degree of flexion.
citation: When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Palpation of the ulnar artery pulse
statement: Where is the most appropriate site to palpate the ulnar pulse at the wrist?
options: Medial to the pisiform bone, deep to the flexor carpi ulnaris
Lateral to the pisiform bone, superficial to the flexor retinaculum
In the anatomic snuffbox, medial to the extensor pollicis longus
On the anterior surface of the radius, lateral to the flexor carpi radialis
Between the tendons of the palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis
correctOption: Lateral to the pisiform bone, superficial to the flexor retinaculum
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery is not located medial to the pisiform; the pisiform is the most medial bone of the proximal carpal row. The artery and nerve pass lateral to it in Guyon's canal. Placing fingers medial to the pisiform would result in palpating the hypothenar muscles.

The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum, just lateral to the pisiform bone. In this location, it is covered only by the palmar carpal ligament, skin, and fascia. This superficial position makes it an ideal site for pulse assessment.

The anatomic snuffbox is on the lateral (radial) side of the wrist and contains the radial artery, not the ulnar artery. The ulnar artery is on the opposite side of the wrist. Palpating here would only yield a radial pulse.

The anterior surface of the radius is the site for palpating the radial pulse, not the ulnar pulse. The radial artery lies between the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis. This is the most common site for taking a peripheral pulse.

The space between the palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis typically overlies the median nerve. The ulnar artery is much more medial, near the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon. Palpating between these central tendons would not provide a reliable arterial pulse.
citation: The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse.

coreConcept: Coracoid Process Palpation
statement: During a physical examination of the shoulder, a clinician attempts to palpate the coracoid process. Which of the following describes the correct anatomical location for palpating this structure?
options: Deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
Superficial palpation at the most lateral point of the acromion process
Deep pressure in the center of the supraspinatus fossa
Palpation along the medial border of the scapula at the level of the spine
Superficial palpation at the junction of the medial and middle thirds of the clavicle
correctOption: Deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a prominent projection of the scapula that serves as an attachment point for several muscles and ligaments. It is located deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle and can be felt by applying firm pressure just below the lateral third of the clavicle. This landmark is essential for identifying the deltopectoral triangle and for performing certain regional nerve blocks.

The lateral point of the acromion forms the tip of the shoulder and is easily palpable superficially, but it is distinct from the coracoid process. The acromion is the lateral continuation of the scapular spine, whereas the coracoid projects anteriorly from the scapular neck. Palpating here would assess the acromioclavicular region rather than the coracoid.

The supraspinatus fossa is located on the posterior aspect of the scapula, superior to the scapular spine. It is filled by the supraspinatus muscle and is not the correct location for palpating the anteriorly-projecting coracoid process. Palpation here is typically used to assess for muscle wasting or trigger points.

The medial border of the scapula is located posteriorly and is used to identify the level of the thoracic vertebrae. The coracoid process is an anterior structure and cannot be reached from the medial border. This site is more relevant for assessing the rhomboid muscles or the serratus anterior.

The junction of the medial and middle thirds of the clavicle is far too medial for the coracoid process. This area is closer to the sternoclavicular joint and the attachment of the sternocleidomastoid muscle. The coracoid process is consistently found inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Medial Epicondyle and Ulnar Nerve Palpation
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the small finger after striking their elbow against a hard surface. The clinician decides to palpate the nerve most likely involved. Where should the clinician palpate to roll this nerve against a bony landmark?
options: Posterior surface of the medial epicondyle
Anterior surface of the lateral epicondyle
Directly over the olecranon process
Within the bicipital groove of the humerus
Lateral to the tendon of the biceps brachii in the cubital fossa
correctOption: Posterior surface of the medial epicondyle
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve travels in the ulnar sulcus (groove) located on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Because the nerve is superficial at this site, it can be easily palpated and rolled against the bone, which often produces a characteristic tingling sensation. This location makes the nerve highly vulnerable to compression or direct trauma.

The lateral epicondyle is the site of origin for the common extensor tendon and is related to the radial nerve, but the radial nerve does not pass posteriorly in a palpable sulcus here. Palpating the anterior lateral epicondyle would not allow for rolling the ulnar nerve. This site is more relevant for diagnosing lateral epicondylitis.

The olecranon process is the proximal end of the ulna and forms the point of the elbow. While the ulnar nerve passes between the olecranon and the medial epicondyle, it is specifically the posterior surface of the epicondyle that provides the hard surface for palpation. The olecranon itself is covered by the olecranon bursa and triceps tendon.

The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) is located on the proximal humerus between the greater and lesser tubercles. It contains the long head of the biceps brachii tendon and is far proximal to the elbow region where the ulnar nerve is typically palpated. Palpation here is used to assess for biceps tendonitis.

The area lateral to the biceps brachii tendon in the cubital fossa contains the radial nerve as it divides into its superficial and deep branches. The ulnar nerve does not pass through the cubital fossa; it remains posterior to the medial epicondyle as it enters the forearm. Palpating here would assess the radial nerve or the brachial artery.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture). The nerve can be palpated and rolled against the epicondyle.

coreConcept: Scapular Landmarks and Vertebral Levels
statement: A clinician is using surface landmarks to identify vertebral levels on a patient's back. The inferior angle of the scapula is palpated. This landmark most accurately corresponds to which of the following?
options: Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7)
Spine of the third thoracic vertebra (T3)
Spine of the second thoracic vertebra (T2)
Spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra (T10)
Spine of the twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12)
correctOption: Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7)
optionExplanations: The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that typically aligns with the spinous process of the T7 vertebra and the seventh rib when the arms are at the side. This relationship is frequently used in clinical practice to count ribs or locate specific thoracic levels for procedures like thoracocentesis. It marks the lowest point of the scapular body.

The spine of the third thoracic vertebra (T3) typically corresponds to the level of the medial end of the scapular spine (the root of the spine). This is a higher landmark than the inferior angle. Using T3 to identify the inferior angle would result in a significant anatomical error.

The spine of the second thoracic vertebra (T2) is generally level with the superior angle of the scapula. The superior angle is often covered by the levator scapulae muscle and is less easily palpated than the inferior angle. T2 is much too superior to represent the inferior angle.

The T10 vertebral level is located significantly lower than the scapula in the mid-thoracic region. By this level, the scapula has ended, and the clinician would be palpating the lower ribs or the origins of the latissimus dorsi. T10 is often used as a landmark for the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm.

The T12 vertebral level marks the transition between the thoracic and lumbar spine and the level of the 12th rib. This is far inferior to the scapula. Palpating at this level would be appropriate for assessing the kidneys or the costovertebral angles, not scapular landmarks.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Breast Examination and Clinical Signs
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a patient is asked to raise both arms above her head. The clinician observes a localized area of skin dimpling that was not apparent when the arms were at the side. What is the most likely anatomical explanation for this finding?
options: A carcinoma is pulling on the suspensory ligaments (of Cooper)
Normal physiological stretching of the pectoral fascia
Contraction of the serratus anterior pulling on the lateral thoracic wall
Rupture of a lactiferous duct due to benign cystic changes
Fat necrosis within the superficial fascia of the lower quadrant
correctOption: A carcinoma is pulling on the suspensory ligaments (of Cooper)
optionExplanations: Skin dimpling (peau d'orange or simple retraction) occurs when a malignant tumor invades and exerts traction on the suspensory ligaments of Cooper, which connect the dermis to the deep fascia. Raising the arms increases the tension on these ligaments, making the tethering effect of the tumor more visible. This is a classic clinical sign of breast carcinoma.

Normal physiological stretching of the pectoral fascia occurs during arm elevation but should result in a smooth, symmetrical contour of the breast. It does not cause localized dimpling or nipple retraction. Localized changes in skin tension during this maneuver are almost always pathological.

The serratus anterior muscle is located on the lateral thoracic wall and does not have direct attachments to the breast skin or suspensory ligaments.
