While it contracts during arm elevation to rotate the scapula, its action does not produce the specific skin dimpling associated with breast pathology. This muscle lies deep to the breast tissue.

Rupture of a lactiferous duct or benign cystic changes typically present as a palpable mass or inflammatory signs rather than localized skin dimpling upon arm elevation. While ductal ectasia can cause nipple retraction, the specific sign of skin dimpling during movement is more characteristic of ligamentous tethering by a malignancy. Benign cysts usually remain mobile.

Fat necrosis can cause skin changes that mimic carcinoma, but the specific maneuver of raising the arms to elicit dimpling is the standard clinical test for identifying tumors tethered to the suspensory apparatus. While fat necrosis is a differential, the anatomical mechanism described in the context specifically links this maneuver to carcinoma tethering. Carcinoma is the primary concern when this sign is elicited.
citation: Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple. Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts.

coreConcept: Upper Limb Dermatomes
statement: A patient reports a loss of sensation specifically on the skin of the middle finger following a neck injury. Based on the standard dermatomal map of the upper limb, which spinal cord segment is most likely affected?
options: C7
C5
C6
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C7 dermatome is classically mapped to the middle finger of the hand. This is a critical landmark for neurological examinations of the upper limb to assess the integrity of the seventh cervical spinal nerve. Sensory loss here often correlates with a C6-C7 disc herniation.

The C5 dermatome is located on the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid muscle and the lateral epicondyle. It does not extend to the fingers. Testing C5 involves checking sensation over the lateral shoulder and upper arm.

The C6 dermatome typically covers the lateral forearm and the thumb (first digit). While it is adjacent to C7, it does not provide the primary sensory innervation for the middle finger. C6 is often tested at the thumb or the radial side of the hand.

The C8 dermatome is situated on the medial side of the hand, specifically involving the little finger (fifth digit) and sometimes the ring finger. It represents the transition to the medial margin of the limb. It is distinct from the central position of the C7 dermatome.

The T1 dermatome is located on the medial aspect of the forearm and the distal part of the arm. It does not extend into the fingers of the hand. T1 is part of the medial margin distribution along with C8 and T2.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Deltoid Wasting and Bony Prominence
statement: Following an injury to the axillary nerve, a patient exhibits significant atrophy of the shoulder musculature. Which bony landmark becomes much more prominent and easily palpable due to the loss of muscle bulk in this region?
options: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
Lesser tuberosity of the humerus
Coracoid process of the scapula
Infraglenoid tubercle
Surgical neck of the humerus
correctOption: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
optionExplanations: The deltoid muscle normally provides the rounded contour of the shoulder, overlying the proximal humerus. When the deltoid atrophies due to axillary nerve paralysis, the greater tuberosity of the humerus loses its muscular covering and becomes a prominent, easily palpable landmark. This change in contour is a hallmark of chronic axillary nerve injury.

The lesser tuberosity is located more medially and anteriorly on the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis. While it may become slightly more accessible, it is not the primary landmark that defines the lateral shoulder contour like the greater tuberosity. The greater tuberosity is the most lateral bony point covered by the deltoid.

The coracoid process is an anterior structure and is already palpable via deep pressure even in healthy individuals. While muscle wasting might make the area appear more hollow, the coracoid is not the specific structure that becomes 'readily palpated' as a direct result of deltoid wasting. It is located medial to the deltoid's main bulk.

The infraglenoid tubercle is located deep on the scapula, serving as the origin for the long head of the triceps brachii. It is not a surface landmark and remains difficult to palpate regardless of deltoid atrophy. It is situated inferior to the glenoid cavity.

The surgical neck of the humerus is a region of the bone rather than a specific 'tuberosity' or projection. While the area is exposed by deltoid wasting, the greater tuberosity is the specific bony prominence that creates the new, sharper contour of the shoulder. The surgical neck is also the site where the axillary nerve itself is often injured.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse.

coreConcept: Cephalic Vein and Radial Styloid
statement: A clinician is attempting to establish peripheral venous access in a patient with severe hypotension and collapsed veins. Which anatomical landmark provides the most reliable location for finding the cephalic vein in the distal forearm?
options: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Immediately anterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
Medial to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
Directly over the dorsal venous arch of the hand
Lateral to the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is known for its relatively constant anatomical position at the wrist, where it passes in the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process. This constancy makes it a preferred site for a 'cut-down' procedure or emergency cannulation when other veins are not visible due to shock. It then continues up the lateral side of the forearm.

The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is associated with the basilic vein and the origin of the median cubital vein, but these are often less constant or more prone to collapsing in shock compared to the distal cephalic vein. The cephalic vein is lateral, not medial. This site is also near the ulnar nerve.

The area medial to the flexor carpi ulnaris is the territory of the ulnar artery and nerve. The cephalic vein is a lateral structure and would not be found in this medial location. Palpating here is used for the ulnar pulse, not for finding the cephalic vein.

While the cephalic vein begins from the dorsal venous arch, the arch itself is highly variable and often the first to collapse in a state of shock. The vein's position at the radial styloid is much more predictable for emergency access. The dorsal veins are also very mobile and difficult to stabilize.

The extensor pollicis longus forms the medial boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. While the cephalic vein passes near this region, its most 'fairly constant' landmark for clinical access in difficult cases is specifically described as being posterior to the radial styloid process. The snuffbox contains the radial artery, which must be avoided.
citation: When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis.

coreConcept: Posterior Axillary Lymph Node Palpation
statement: To properly examine the posterior (subscapular) group of axillary lymph nodes, how should the clinician position their fingers and in what direction should they apply pressure?
options: Press backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle
Press forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Press laterally against the medial side of the humerus
Press medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
Press superiorly toward the apex of the axilla and the first rib
correctOption: Press backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle
optionExplanations: The posterior or subscapular lymph nodes are located along the subscapular vessels on the posterior wall of the axilla. To palpate them, the examiner must reach into the axillary fossa and press posteriorly (backward) against the subscapularis muscle, which forms the posterior wall. This group drains the posterior thoracic wall and the scapular region.

Pressing forward against the pectoralis major muscle is the technique used to palpate the anterior (pectoral) group of lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor. They primarily drain the lateral and central parts of the breast.

Pressing laterally against the humerus or the medial side of the axillary vein is the technique for palpating the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. They are located along the lateral wall of the axilla.

Pressing medially against the thoracic wall (ribs and serratus anterior) is the technique for palpating the central lymph nodes, which are located deep to the pectoralis minor. These nodes receive lymph from the anterior, posterior, and lateral groups. They are often the most easily felt if enlarged.

Pressing superiorly toward the first rib is the technique used to palpate the apical (subclavicular) group of lymph nodes. These nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage before it enters the subclavian trunk. They are located at the very apex of the axilla.
citation: The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery.

coreConcept: Apical Axillary Lymph Node Palpation
statement: Which of the following describes the correct clinical technique for palpating the apical group of axillary lymph nodes?
options: Place finger tips high in the axilla reaching the outer border of the first rib
Press the fingers against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle
Press the fingers backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis
Palpate the center of the axilla while the patient adducts the arm against resistance
Palpate the medial side of the axillary vein against the humerus
correctOption: Place finger tips high in the axilla reaching the outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: The apical lymph nodes are situated at the apex of the axilla, medial to the coracoid process and superior to the pectoralis minor. To reach them, the clinician must advance their fingers as high as possible into the axillary vault toward the first rib while the patient's muscles are relaxed. These nodes receive lymph from all other axillary groups.

Pressing against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major is the specific maneuver for the anterior (pectoral) nodes, not the apical nodes. The anterior nodes are located along the medial axillary wall. This group is particularly important in breast cancer staging.

Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle targets the posterior (subscapular) nodes. These are located on the posterior axillary wall and drain the back and shoulder regions. They are distinct from the more superiorly located apical nodes.

Palpating the center of the axilla is used for the central nodes, and having the patient adduct the arm (pushing on the hip) is a technique to make the pectoralis major firm to help identify the anterior nodes. For apical nodes, the muscles must be relaxed, and the limb should hang freely. Adduction would block access to the apex.

Palpating against the humerus or the medial side of the axillary vein targets the lateral group of nodes. These nodes drain the upper limb and are located along the lateral wall of the axilla. The apical nodes are much more medial and superior, near the thoracic inlet.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt.

coreConcept: Radiology of the Distal Humerus
statement: When interpreting a lateral radiograph of the elbow, a clinician notes the relationship between the humeral shaft and the condyles. What is the normal anatomical forward angulation of the distal humerus relative to the long axis of the shaft?
options: 45 degrees
15 degrees
90 degrees
5 degrees
30 degrees
correctOption: 45 degrees
optionExplanations: The distal end of the humerus (the trochlea and capitulum) is normally tilted anteriorly at an angle of approximately 45 degrees relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This angulation is crucial for the full range of elbow flexion and is a key feature to assess on lateral X-rays. Displacement of this angle often indicates a supracondylar fracture.

An angulation of 15 degrees is far too shallow and would represent a significant posterior displacement or malunion of a distal humeral fracture. Normal anatomy requires a much more pronounced forward tilt to accommodate the coronoid and radial heads during flexion. This would severely limit the elbow's functional range.

A 90-degree angulation would be a gross deformity and is not found in normal human anatomy. Such an extreme angle would imply a complete disruption of the distal humerus or a severe congenital anomaly. The 45-degree tilt is the standard physiological orientation.

A 5-degree angulation is nearly straight and would be considered abnormal. The lack of the natural 45-degree anterior tilt would result in the olecranon hitting the humerus prematurely during extension or the forearm bones being blocked during flexion. It is a sign of poor fracture reduction.

While 30 degrees is closer to the correct value than other distractors, the standard anatomical and radiological teaching specifies a 45-degree forward angulation. This specific angle ensures that the articular surfaces are optimally positioned for the hinge-like movement of the elbow joint. Deviations from 45 degrees are clinically significant.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Acromion as the tip of the shoulder
statement: Which bony landmark forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder and represents the lateral extension of the scapular spine?
options: Coracoid process
Acromion
Greater tuberosity
Medial epicondyle
Lateral epicondyle
correctOption: Acromion
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a beaklike projection from the scapula that lies inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle and is not the tip of the shoulder. It requires deep palpation through the deltoid muscle to be felt. It serves as an attachment for the short head of the biceps and coracobrachialis.

The acromion is the broad, flat lateral extension of the scapular spine and constitutes the most lateral bony point of the shoulder. It is subcutaneous and easily palpable, articulating with the clavicle at the acromioclavicular joint. It serves as a primary landmark for clinical measurements of the upper limb.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus is located lateral to the acromion but is typically covered by the thick deltoid muscle. It only becomes easily palpable when the deltoid muscle undergoes significant wasting. It is the site of attachment for three of the four rotator cuff muscles.

The medial epicondyle is a prominent projection on the distal humerus, not the shoulder. It is easily palpable at the elbow and serves as the origin for the common flexor tendon. The ulnar nerve passes posterior to it in the ulnar sulcus.

The lateral epicondyle is a smaller projection on the distal lateral humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It serves as the origin for the common extensor tendon of the forearm. It is a landmark for the elbow joint, not the shoulder tip.
citation: The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder. The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula.

coreConcept: Elbow joint bony relations in flexion
statement: During a physical examination of the elbow, the clinician notes that the medial and lateral epicondyles and the olecranon process form an equilateral triangle. In which position is the elbow joint most likely held?
options: Full extension
90 degrees of flexion
Hyperextension
Full pronation
Full supination
correctOption: 90 degrees of flexion
optionExplanations: In full extension, the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the tip of the olecranon process normally align in a straight horizontal line. Deviation from this line in extension may indicate a fracture or dislocation. This relationship is a key diagnostic feature in orthopedic assessment.

When the elbow is flexed to approximately 90 degrees, the three bony landmarks (medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and olecranon) form the vertices of an equilateral triangle. This geometric relationship is preserved in normal joints but altered in cases of supracondylar fractures or elbow dislocations. It is a fundamental topographical feature used to assess joint integrity.

Hyperextension of the elbow would maintain the linear relationship of the three bony points, similar to normal extension. It does not result in the formation of an equilateral triangle. Excessive hyperextension may suggest ligamentous laxity or joint capsule injury.

Pronation is a rotational movement of the forearm that occurs at the proximal and distal radioulnar joints. It does not significantly alter the relationship between the humeral epicondyles and the ulnar olecranon. The triangle/line relationship is determined by the degree of flexion/extension at the humeroulnar joint.

Supination involves the rotation of the radius around the ulna to turn the palm anteriorly. Like pronation, it does not change the relative positions of the epicondyles and the olecranon. The equilateral triangle configuration is specifically a result of elbow flexion.
citation: The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon and the pulley-shaped trochlea of the humerus; it also has strong medial and lateral ligaments. When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Radial pulse location in the distal forearm
statement: A clinician is attempting to palpate the radial pulse in the distal forearm. Between which two tendons should the fingers be placed to most effectively feel the artery against the radius?
options: Flexor carpi ulnaris and Palmaris longus
Brachioradialis and Flexor carpi radialis
Flexor digitorum superficialis and Flexor carpi radialis
Abductor pollicis longus and Extensor pollicis brevis
Extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis
correctOption: Brachioradialis and Flexor carpi radialis
optionExplanations: The flexor carpi ulnaris and palmaris longus tendons are located on the medial (ulnar) side of the forearm. The ulnar artery, not the radial artery, is associated with the flexor carpi ulnaris. Palpating here would not yield a radial pulse.

The radial artery lies superficially on the anterior surface of the distal radius, specifically between the lateral brachioradialis tendon and the medial flexor carpi radialis tendon. Because it is covered only by skin and fascia here, it can be easily compressed against the bone to feel the pulse. This is the standard clinical site for heart rate assessment.

The flexor digitorum superficialis lies deeper and more medially than the ideal site for radial pulse palpation. While the flexor carpi radialis is one of the correct boundaries, the other boundary is the brachioradialis. Placing fingers between these two would be too medial.

The abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis form the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. While the radial artery can be felt in the snuffbox, it is less easily palpated there than in the distal forearm. These tendons are located on the posterolateral aspect of the wrist.

The extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis are located on the posterior and lateral aspects of the forearm. They are part of the extensor compartment and do not border the radial artery at the standard pulse-taking site on the anterior distal radius. Palpation here would be ineffective for pulse detection.
citation: In the distal forearm, the radial artery lies on the anterior surface of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis muscles, and is covered only by skin and fascia (see Figs. 3.31 and 3.38). This is the ideal site for taking the radial pulse. If the pulse cannot be felt, try feeling for the radial artery on the other wrist.

coreConcept: Allen's Test procedure and physiology
statement: During an Allen’s test, the clinician compresses both the radial and ulnar arteries and asks the patient to clench their fist. Upon releasing the pressure on the ulnar artery only, the palm remains white for more than 15 seconds. What does this finding specifically indicate?
options: Patency of the radial artery
Occlusion or insufficiency of the ulnar artery
Normal collateral circulation
Occlusion of the brachial artery
Hyperemia of the palmar arches
correctOption: Occlusion or insufficiency of the ulnar artery
optionExplanations: The patency of the radial artery is tested by releasing the radial artery while keeping the ulnar artery compressed. In this scenario, only the ulnar artery was released, so the result does not directly confirm radial artery patency. A white palm after releasing the radial artery would indicate radial occlusion.

A positive Allen's test occurs when the palm remains pale (white) after releasing pressure from one of the arteries, indicating that the released artery is not providing sufficient blood flow to the palmar arches. Since the ulnar artery was released and the palm did not turn pink, it suggests the ulnar artery is occluded or insufficient. This is critical before performing procedures like radial artery cannulation.

Normal collateral circulation would be indicated if the palm promptly turned pink within a few seconds of releasing the ulnar artery. The prompt return of color demonstrates that the ulnar artery can adequately supply the entire hand. A delay of 15 seconds is a clear sign of abnormal circulation.

While a brachial artery occlusion would affect blood flow to the entire hand, the Allen's test specifically isolates the distal contributions of the radial and ulnar arteries. If the brachial artery were occluded, the pulse would likely be absent at both sites initially. The test specifically evaluates the patency of the individual forearm arteries.

Hyperemia refers to an excess of blood in the vessels supplying an organ or other part, which would cause the palm to turn very red quickly. The scenario describes a persistent white palm (pallor), which is the opposite of hyperemia. Pallor indicates ischemia or lack of blood flow.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches. When the patient is asked to open the hands, the skin of the palms is at first white.

coreConcept: Subclavian artery compression site
statement: In a case of catastrophic hemorrhage from the upper limb, where can the subclavian artery be most effectively compressed against the first rib?
options: In the deltopectoral triangle
In the root of the posterior triangle of the neck
Posterior to the medial epicondyle
Deep to the pectoralis minor muscle
At the mid-shaft of the humerus
correctOption: In the root of the posterior triangle of the neck
optionExplanations: The deltopectoral triangle contains the cephalic vein and the deltoid branch of the thoracoacromial artery, but not the subclavian artery. Compressing this area would not stop a major arterial bleed from the subclavian or axillary arteries. It is located between the deltoid and pectoralis major muscles.

The subclavian artery passes over the first rib as it exits the thorax to enter the axilla. At the root of the posterior triangle of the neck, just superior to the clavicle, the artery is relatively superficial and can be pressed downward against the firm surface of the first rib. This maneuver is a life-saving technique to control distal hemorrhage.

The medial epicondyle is a landmark for the ulnar nerve and the origin of flexor muscles, not the subclavian artery. Palpating posterior to the medial epicondyle would allow one to feel the ulnar nerve, but it has no utility in stopping a catastrophic limb hemorrhage. The brachial artery is the main artery at the elbow level.

The second part of the axillary artery lies deep to the pectoralis minor muscle. While it can be compressed here, it is much deeper and more difficult to access effectively in an emergency compared to the subclavian artery at the first rib. The pectoralis minor muscle acts as a thick barrier to effective compression.

At the mid-shaft of the humerus, the brachial artery can be compressed against the bone. However, this would only control bleeding distal to the mid-arm. For a catastrophic hemorrhage involving the entire limb or axilla, a more proximal compression point like the subclavian artery is required.
citation: The subclavian artery, as it crosses the first rib to become the axillary artery, can be palpated in the root of the posterior triangle of the neck (see Fig. 3.66). The artery can be compressed here against the first rib to stop a catastrophic hemorrhage. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle.

coreConcept: Median cubital vein and bicipital aponeurosis
statement: When performing venipuncture in the cubital fossa, which anatomical structure provides a protective barrier between the median cubital vein and the underlying brachial artery?
options: Brachialis muscle
Bicipital aponeurosis
Pronator teres muscle
Deep fascia of the arm
Lateral intermuscular septum
correctOption: Bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle forms part of the floor of the cubital fossa and lies deep to the brachial artery. It does not sit between the vein and the artery, so it cannot provide protection during venipuncture. Its primary function is flexion of the elbow joint.

The bicipital aponeurosis (lacertus fibrosus) is a triangular band of fascia that runs medially from the biceps brachii tendon. It passes superficial to the brachial artery and median nerve but deep to the median cubital vein. This anatomical arrangement protects the deeper structures from accidental needle penetration during blood draws.

The pronator teres muscle forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. While it is in the vicinity, it does not serve as a horizontal barrier between the superficial veins and the deep brachial artery. It is primarily involved in forearm pronation and elbow flexion.

The deep fascia of the arm (brachial fascia) invests the muscles of the arm but is not the specific specialized structure that separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery. The bicipital aponeurosis is a specific thickening of this fascia that provides the protective function in the cubital fossa. General deep fascia is present throughout the limb.

The lateral intermuscular septum separates the anterior and posterior compartments of the arm. It is located on the lateral side of the humerus and is not involved in the anatomy of the cubital fossa where the median cubital vein and brachial artery are located. It provides an attachment point for muscles like the triceps and brachialis.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein. The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization.

coreConcept: Anterior axillary lymph node palpation
statement: To palpate the anterior (pectoral) group of axillary lymph nodes, in which direction should the examiner press their fingers?
options: Backward against the subscapularis muscle
Medially against the thoracic wall
Forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Laterally against the humerus
Upward toward the apex of the axilla
correctOption: Forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
optionExplanations: Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to palpate the posterior (subscapular) group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the subscapular vessels on the posterior wall of the axilla. This is distinct from the anterior group examination.

Pressing medially against the thoracic wall (serratus anterior) is generally used to palpate the central group of lymph nodes or to assess the medial wall of the axilla. The anterior nodes are specifically associated with the pectoral muscles, not the medial rib cage. The central nodes are often felt against the ribs.

The anterior or pectoral lymph nodes lie along the lower border of the pectoralis minor and behind the pectoralis major. To feel them, the examiner must reach into the axilla and press forward, trapping the nodes against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle. This is a standard part of a breast and axillary examination.

Pressing laterally against the humerus or the medial side of the axillary vein is the method for palpating the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. They are located along the lateral wall of the axilla.

Pressing upward toward the apex of the axilla, near the outer border of the first rib, is the technique for palpating the apical (subclavicular) lymph nodes. These nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage. They are the most superiorly located group in the axilla.
citation: The anterior (pectoral) nodes may be palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall of the axilla. The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein.

coreConcept: Ulnar artery pulse location
statement: Where is the most appropriate site to palpate the ulnar pulse as the artery enters the hand?
options: Medial to the pisiform bone
Lateral to the pisiform bone
In the anatomic snuffbox
Proximal to the medial epicondyle
Deep to the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon
correctOption: Lateral to the pisiform bone
optionExplanations: The pisiform bone is the most medial carpal bone in the proximal row. There is no major artery medial to the pisiform; the ulnar artery and nerve pass on its lateral side through Guyon's canal. Palpating medially would only reveal the bony prominence of the pisiform.

The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum, situated just lateral to the pisiform bone. In this location, it is covered only by skin and fascia, allowing the pulse to be easily detected. The ulnar nerve lies between the artery and the pisiform bone.

The anatomic snuffbox is located on the posterolateral aspect of the wrist and contains the radial artery. The ulnar artery is located on the anterior and medial (ulnar) side of the wrist. Palpating the snuffbox would only allow for detection of the radial pulse.

Proximal to the medial epicondyle, the ulnar nerve can be felt, but the ulnar artery is not yet formed; the brachial artery is the primary vessel in the arm. The ulnar artery originates in the cubital fossa. Palpating here would not provide an ulnar pulse.

While the ulnar artery travels deep to the flexor carpi ulnaris in the forearm, it becomes superficial at the wrist to pass over the flexor retinaculum. Palpating deep to the tendon in the mid-forearm is difficult due to the muscle bulk. The wrist, lateral to the pisiform, is the clinically preferred site.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Brachial artery palpation in the arm
statement: A clinician is palpating the brachial artery in the mid-arm region. Which muscle overlaps the artery from the lateral side at this location?
options: Coracobrachialis
Triceps brachii
Biceps brachii
Brachialis
Deltoid
correctOption: Biceps brachii
optionExplanations: The coracobrachialis muscle is located in the upper part of the arm and the artery lies medial to it. It does not overlap the artery from the lateral side in the mid-arm region. It serves as a landmark for the musculocutaneous nerve which pierces it.

The triceps brachii is the primary muscle of the posterior compartment of the arm. It is separated from the brachial artery by the humerus and the medial intermuscular septum. It does not overlap the brachial artery, which is located in the anterior compartment.

In the arm, the brachial artery travels in the medial bicipital groove. The biceps brachii muscle lies lateral to the artery and its medial border slightly overlaps the vessel. To palpate the pulse, the clinician often needs to displace the biceps laterally or palpate just medial to its belly.

The brachialis muscle lies directly deep (posterior) to the brachial artery in the lower half of the arm. The artery rests on the anterior surface of the brachialis. Therefore, the brachialis does not overlap it from the lateral side but rather forms its floor.

The deltoid muscle is located in the shoulder region and the proximal third of the arm. By the mid-arm region, the deltoid has inserted into the deltoid tuberosity of the humerus. It is too superior to overlap the brachial artery at the standard mid-arm palpation site.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Apical axillary lymph node palpation technique
statement: When examining the apical group of axillary lymph nodes, what is the correct positioning for the patient and the target landmark for the examiner's fingers?
options: Arm abducted 90 degrees; fingers at the lateral wall
Arm hanging at the side; fingers at the outer border of the first rib
Hand on hip; fingers against the pectoralis major
Arm flexed; fingers in the ulnar sulcus
Arm elevated; fingers at the inferior angle of the scapula
correctOption: Arm hanging at the side; fingers at the outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: Abducting the arm to 90 degrees tightens the axillary fascia, making it difficult to reach the deep apical nodes. This position is more suitable for inspecting the skin of the axilla rather than deep palpation. The lateral wall is the site for lateral nodes, not apical nodes.

To palpate the apical nodes, the patient must relax the shoulder muscles by letting the arm hang at the side, which loosens the axillary fascia. The examiner reaches high into the apex of the axilla toward the outer border of the first rib. These nodes are the most superior group and receive lymph from all other axillary groups.

Placing the hand on the hip and pushing medially contracts the pectoralis major, making it firm. This maneuver is specifically used to palpate the anterior (pectoral) nodes against the muscle's posterior surface. It would obstruct access to the high apical nodes.

The ulnar sulcus is located posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus at the elbow. This is the site for palpating the ulnar nerve, not axillary lymph nodes. There are no apical lymph nodes in the elbow region.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a landmark for the 7th rib and T7 spinous process, located on the posterior thoracic wall. It is far inferior to the axillary apex. Palpating here would not allow for the assessment of any axillary lymph node groups.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt. Examination of the axillary lymph nodes always forms part of the clinical examination of the breast.

coreConcept: Surface marking of the inferior angle of the scapula
statement: During a physical examination, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a landmark for surface marking. Which of the following structures is located at the same horizontal level as this palpable landmark?
options: The spine of the fourth thoracic vertebra
The spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra
The spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra
The second rib
The twelfth rib
correctOption: The spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra
optionExplanations: The spine of the fourth thoracic vertebra is located much higher in the thoracic region, typically corresponding to the level of the root of the scapular spine rather than the inferior angle. Palpating this level would place the examiner superior to the bulk of the scapular body. It is not the standard landmark for the inferior angle.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that consistently aligns with the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7). This relationship is clinically useful for identifying vertebral levels and rib counts during posterior thoracic examinations. It allows for rapid orientation during auscultation or procedures involving the mid-thoracic wall.

The spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra is located significantly inferior to the scapula, near the lower limit of the thoracic cage. Using the inferior angle of the scapula to find T10 would result in a significant vertical error in localization. T10 is more closely associated with the level of the xiphoid process anteriorly.

The second rib is located at the superior aspect of the thoracic cage and is typically marked by the sternal angle (Angle of Louis) anteriorly. Posteriorly, the second rib is found near the superior angle of the scapula, not the inferior angle. It is far too superior to be associated with the T7 level.

The twelfth rib is the most inferior rib and marks the lower boundary of the thoracic cavity, far below the scapula. It is often used as a landmark for the kidneys or the T12/L1 vertebral junction. The inferior angle of the scapula is five vertebral levels superior to this point.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Clinical signs of breast carcinoma during physical examination
statement: A 55-year-old woman presents for a routine breast examination. The clinician observes that when the patient raises her arms above her head, there is visible dimpling of the skin and slight retraction of the nipple. What is the primary anatomical mechanism responsible for these findings in the presence of a malignancy?
options: Invasion of the pectoralis major muscle fascia
Obstruction of the axillary lymph nodes
Traction on the lactiferous ducts and suspensory ligaments
Compression of the lateral thoracic artery
Hypertrophy of the retromammary space
correctOption: Traction on the lactiferous ducts and suspensory ligaments
optionExplanations: Invasion of the pectoralis major fascia can cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, but it is not the primary cause of skin dimpling or nipple retraction. Fixation is tested by asking the patient to press her hands against her hips to contract the muscle. Dimpling specifically relates to more superficial connective tissue involvement.

Obstruction of axillary lymph nodes typically leads to lymphedema of the upper limb or the breast tissue itself, often presenting as 'peau d'orange' (orange peel skin). While a sign of advanced cancer, it does not directly cause the mechanical pulling required for nipple retraction. Retraction is a result of direct fibrous tethering.

Malignant tumors within the breast can invade and shorten the lactiferous ducts and the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments). When these structures are pulled or tethered by the tumor, they cause the overlying skin to dimple or the nipple to retract, especially during maneuvers like arm elevation. This is a classic clinical sign of underlying carcinoma.

Compression of the lateral thoracic artery would lead to localized ischemia or reduced blood flow but does not have a mechanical effect on the skin or nipple position. Arterial supply is generally not the cause of the structural deformities seen in breast cancer. These deformities are almost exclusively due to fibrous tissue changes.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue that allows the breast to move freely over the pectoralis major; hypertrophy here is not a feature of malignancy. In fact, cancer usually obliterates this space if it invades deeply, leading to fixation rather than dimpling. Dimpling requires tension on the suspensory apparatus.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatome distribution
statement: A patient presents with localized sensory loss specifically involving the skin of the middle finger. Based on the standard dermatomal map of the upper limb, which spinal cord segment is most likely affected?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C5 dermatome is primarily located on the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid region and the proximal forearm. It does not extend to the digits of the hand. Sensory loss here would suggest a lesion in the upper roots of the brachial plexus.

The C6 dermatome covers the lateral forearm and the thumb (first digit). While it is adjacent to the middle finger, it does not typically provide the primary sensory innervation for the third digit. C6 is often tested at the thumb or the lateral aspect of the wrist.

The C7 dermatome is classically situated on the middle finger (third digit) of the hand. It occupies a central position in the hand's dermatomal map, separating the lateral C6 territory from the medial C8 territory. Testing sensation on the middle finger is the standard clinical method for assessing the C7 spinal segment.

The C8 dermatome is located on the medial side of the hand, specifically involving the little finger (fifth digit) and the medial half of the ring finger. It also extends along the medial border of the forearm. It does not encompass the middle finger.

The T1 dermatome is found along the medial aspect of the forearm and the distal arm. It does not extend into the hand or the fingers. T1 is typically tested on the medial side of the antecubital fossa or the mid-forearm.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Palpation of the greater tuberosity in deltoid atrophy
statement: Following a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus, a patient develops significant atrophy of the shoulder musculature, making a specific bony landmark much more prominent and easily palpable. Which landmark is now more accessible due to the wasting of the deltoid muscle?
options: The coracoid process
The greater tuberosity
The lesser tuberosity
The medial epicondyle
The bicipital groove
correctOption: The greater tuberosity
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is located anteriorly and is palpated through the anterior fibers of the deltoid, but it is not the primary structure covered by the bulk of the deltoid muscle. While it may become slightly more visible, it is not the landmark specifically noted for becoming 'readily palpable' due to deltoid wasting. It is a landmark of the scapula, not the humerus.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus is normally covered by the thick deltoid muscle, which gives the shoulder its rounded contour. When the axillary nerve is damaged (often by surgical neck fractures), the deltoid undergoes rapid atrophy, causing the shoulder to appear 'squared off.' This loss of muscle mass makes the underlying greater tuberosity much more prominent and easy to palpate.

The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis muscle. It is generally deeper and less affected by the superficial contour changes of the deltoid compared to the greater tuberosity. It is not the classic landmark revealed by deltoid wasting.

The medial epicondyle is located at the distal end of the humerus at the elbow joint. Its palpability is unaffected by the state of the deltoid muscle in the shoulder region. It is always a prominent landmark regardless of proximal muscle atrophy.

The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) lies between the greater and lesser tuberosities and contains the long head of the biceps tendon. While it is in the same region, the greater tuberosity is the broad lateral landmark that defines the shoulder's bony prominence once the deltoid wastes. The groove itself is a depression rather than a palpable 'point'.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the cephalic vein at the wrist
statement: In an emergency situation where a patient is in shock and superficial veins are collapsed, a clinician attempts to locate the cephalic vein for venipuncture. Where is this vein most consistently located at the level of the wrist?
options: Immediately anterior to the medial epicondyle
Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Lateral to the tendon of the flexor carpi radialis
Medial to the pisiform bone
Directly over the olecranon process
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The medial epicondyle is a landmark at the elbow, not the wrist. While the basilic vein passes near the medial epicondyle, the question specifically asks for the cephalic vein's location at the wrist. This option is anatomically incorrect for the site described.

The cephalic vein is known for its relatively constant anatomical position at the wrist, where it resides in the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process. This consistency makes it a valuable site for venous access when other veins are not visible due to hypotension or shock. It then continues proximally along the lateral aspect of the forearm.

The area lateral to the flexor carpi radialis tendon is the site where the radial artery pulse is typically palpated. While the cephalic vein is in the general lateral vicinity, its most reliable landmark for 'blind' or difficult access is its relationship to the bony styloid process. The tendon is a landmark for the artery, not the primary landmark for the vein.

The area medial to the pisiform bone is associated with the ulnar nerve and ulnar artery as they enter the hand. The cephalic vein is a lateral structure and would not be found on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist. This site is used for taking the ulnar pulse.

The olecranon process is the bony point of the elbow. No major superficial veins suitable for venipuncture are located directly over this subcutaneous bony prominence. The cephalic vein is located in the forearm and arm, far from the posterior elbow point.
citation: When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis.

coreConcept: Anatomical rationale for basilic vein catheterization
statement: Why is the basilic vein often preferred over the cephalic vein for central venous catheterization starting from the cubital fossa?
options: The basilic vein has more valves to prevent backflow
The basilic vein is more superficial and easier to see in shock
The basilic vein increases in diameter and is in a direct line with the axillary vein
The cephalic vein always joins the brachial vein at a right angle
The basilic vein is separated from the brachial artery by the deltoid muscle
correctOption: The basilic vein increases in diameter and is in a direct line with the axillary vein
optionExplanations: The number of valves is not the primary reason for choosing the basilic vein for catheterization. While valves can sometimes obstruct a catheter, the geometric path and vessel caliber are far more significant factors for successful central placement. The basilic vein's path is simply more anatomically favorable.

In many patients, the cephalic vein is actually more superficial and easier to visualize than the basilic vein, especially in the forearm. However, ease of initial puncture does not equate to ease of advancing a long catheter to the central circulation. The basilic vein's deeper, straighter course is the deciding factor.

The basilic vein is the preferred route because it increases in diameter as it moves proximally and maintains a relatively straight, direct line into the axillary vein. In contrast, the cephalic vein often narrows and enters the axillary vein at a sharp angle in the deltopectoral triangle, which can make advancing a catheter difficult. This direct alignment reduces the risk of catheter malposition.

The cephalic vein joins the axillary vein, not the brachial vein, and while it does often have a sharp 'T-junction' or 'S-bend' near its termination, it is not a consistent right angle to the brachial vein. The primary issue is the sharp turn into the axillary vein through the clavipectoral fascia. This makes the basilic route much smoother.

The basilic vein is not separated from the brachial artery by the deltoid muscle; in the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein (which joins the basilic) is separated from the artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. The deltoid is a shoulder muscle and does not intervene between these structures in the arm or fossa. This option misidentifies the regional anatomy.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein (see Fig. 3.68). The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Radiographic anatomy of the distal humerus
statement: When evaluating a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anatomical angulation of the distal end of the humerus relative to the humeral shaft?
options: Angulated backward 15°
Angulated forward 45°
Angulated medially 30°
Perfectly aligned at 0°
Angulated laterally 90°
correctOption: Angulated forward 45°
optionExplanations: A backward (posterior) angulation would be abnormal and might suggest a supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement. The normal anatomy of the humerus involves an anterior tilt to facilitate the range of motion in flexion. 15 degrees is also an incorrect value for this specific landmark.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward (anteriorly) at approximately 45° relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. Recognizing this normal angulation is critical when interpreting lateral X-rays to distinguish between normal anatomy and subtle fractures. This tilt allows for a greater degree of elbow flexion before the coronoid process hits the humerus.

Medial angulation refers to the 'carrying angle' seen in the AP view, not the lateral view. The question specifically asks about the lateral radiograph and the relationship of the distal end to the shaft. 30 degrees is not the standard measurement for the anterior tilt.

Perfect alignment (0°) would actually be an abnormal finding on a lateral radiograph. If the distal humerus were not tilted forward, the range of motion of the elbow joint would be significantly restricted. This tilt is a fundamental feature of the distal humeral architecture.

A 90° lateral angulation is not found in normal human anatomy and would represent a severe deformity or a completely displaced fracture. The distal humerus remains in the same general plane as the shaft, with only the 45° anterior tilt. Lateral angulation is not a standard descriptor for this relationship.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Surface landmarks of the shoulder (Acromion)
statement: Which palpable bony landmark forms the 'tip of the shoulder' and represents the lateral extension of the scapular spine?
options: The coracoid process
The acromion
The greater tuberosity
The medial epicondyle
The superior angle of the scapula
correctOption: The acromion
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is located anteriorly and inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. While it is palpable, it does not form the 'tip' of the shoulder; rather, it is felt deep to the deltoid muscle. It is often described as being 'like a crow's beak'.

The acromion is the broad, flat, lateral extension of the spine of the scapula and forms the highest point and the palpable 'tip' of the shoulder. It articulates with the clavicle at the acromioclavicular joint. Its name literally translates from Greek as the 'point of the shoulder'.

The greater tuberosity is a landmark on the humerus, not the scapula. While it contributes to the rounded contour of the shoulder, it lies inferior to the acromion and is covered by the deltoid muscle. It is not the lateral extension of the scapular spine.

The medial epicondyle is a landmark of the distal humerus at the elbow joint. It is located far from the shoulder region and serves as an origin for the forearm flexor muscles. It is easily palpable but irrelevant to the shoulder's tip.

The superior angle of the scapula is located at the junction of the superior and medial borders of the scapula. It is covered by the levator scapulae and trapezius muscles and is not the lateral-most point of the shoulder. It is situated much more medially than the acromion.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Palpation of the posterior border of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: The anterior border
The posterior aspect of the body
The interosseous border
The radial notch
The coronoid process
correctOption: The posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be easily palpated along its length. Palpation of the forearm usually targets the posterior side to feel the bone.

The posterior aspect (or posterior border) of the ulnar shaft is subcutaneous throughout its entire length. It can be felt by running a finger from the olecranon process down to the styloid process of the ulna. This makes it a useful landmark for assessing ulnar fractures or performing regional blocks.

The interosseous border of the ulna is the sharp lateral edge where the interosseous membrane attaches. It is located deep within the forearm between the radius and ulna and is completely inaccessible to surface palpation. It serves as a site for muscle attachment rather than a surface landmark.

The radial notch is a small articular surface on the lateral side of the coronoid process that receives the head of the radius. It is located deep within the elbow joint complex and is not palpable from the skin surface. It is a feature of the proximal ulna only.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior and proximal part of the ulna. It is located deep in the cubital fossa and is covered by the brachialis muscle and other soft tissues. It is not palpable along the length of the forearm and is only accessible via deep pressure in specific positions.
citation: The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand.

coreConcept: Palpation of the apical axillary lymph nodes
statement: To palpate the apical group of axillary lymph nodes, what is the correct patient positioning and the specific anatomical landmark the examiner must reach?
options: Arm abducted to 90°; reach the medial epicondyle
Hand on hip pushing medially; reach the pectoralis major
Arm hanging relaxed at the side; reach the outer border of the first rib
Arm raised above the head; reach the lower border of the teres major
Hand on the opposite shoulder; reach the coracoid process
correctOption: Arm hanging relaxed at the side; reach the outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: Abducting the arm to 90° actually tightens the axillary fascia and makes it more difficult to reach the deep apical nodes. Furthermore, the medial epicondyle is at the elbow and is not a landmark for axillary node palpation. This position is incorrect for this specific group of nodes.

Placing the hand on the hip and pushing medially is the maneuver used to contract the pectoralis major to palpate the anterior (pectoral) nodes. While useful for the anterior group, it does not allow the examiner to reach high enough into the apex of the axilla for the apical nodes. The apical nodes require muscle relaxation.

For the apical nodes, the patient must relax the shoulder muscles by letting the arm hang at the side, which loosens the axillary fascia. The examiner then reaches high into the apex of the axilla toward the outer border of the first rib. This is the most superior group of axillary nodes and represents the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage.

Raising the arm above the head is used during breast inspection to look for skin dimpling, but it stretches the axillary tissues, making deep palpation of the apex difficult. The teres major forms the posterior fold of the axilla and is a landmark for the posterior (subscapular) nodes, not the apical nodes.

Placing the hand on the opposite shoulder is a maneuver sometimes used in shoulder exams (like the Hawkin's test) but is not the standard position for axillary node palpation. The coracoid process is an anterior landmark, whereas the apical nodes are located much deeper and more superiorly against the thoracic cage.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the scapula and vertebral levels
statement: During a physical examination of the back, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a surface landmark. Which of the following vertebral levels is typically marked by this anatomical feature?
options: Spine of the third thoracic vertebra (T3)
Spine of the fifth thoracic vertebra (T5)
Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7)
Spine of the ninth thoracic vertebra (T9)
Spine of the twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12)
correctOption: Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7)
optionExplanations: The spine of the third thoracic vertebra (T3) is typically at the level of the root of the scapular spine, not the inferior angle. This landmark is useful for identifying the upper thoracic region but is superior to the target landmark. Palpation of this area helps in locating the superior border of the scapula.

The fifth thoracic vertebra (T5) lies between the scapular spine and the inferior angle. While it is in the interscapular region, it does not correspond to a specific, easily identifiable bony landmark of the scapula. It is often used as a reference point for auscultation of the lungs.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that corresponds to the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7). This relationship is clinically significant for counting ribs and identifying vertebral levels during a physical exam. It is easily palpable in most subjects, especially when the arm is at the side.

The ninth thoracic vertebra (T9) is located inferior to the scapula. The inferior angle of the scapula typically ends at T7 or T8, so T9 would be too low to serve as a direct landmark for the scapula. This level is closer to the lower margin of the thoracic cage.

The twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12) marks the transition between the thoracic and lumbar spine and the level of the last rib. It is far inferior to the scapula and is not used as a landmark for scapular positioning. This level is associated with the attachment of the diaphragm and the location of the kidneys.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Identification of the acromion as the tip of the shoulder
statement: A patient presents with a suspected shoulder dislocation. The clinician palpates the 'tip of the shoulder' to assess for bony alignment. Which anatomical structure forms this specific palpable landmark?
options: Coracoid process
Greater tuberosity of the humerus
Acromion of the scapula
Lesser tuberosity of the humerus
Surgical neck of the humerus
correctOption: Acromion of the scapula
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is located anterolaterally and is palpated via deep pressure inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. It does not form the lateral 'tip' of the shoulder but rather a projection on the anterior aspect. It serves as an attachment point for the short head of the biceps and coracobrachialis.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus is located lateral to the head of the humerus and is covered by the deltoid muscle. While it is palpable, especially if the deltoid is wasted, it is not the primary structure defining the 'tip' of the shoulder. It is the site of attachment for the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles.

The acromion is the broad, flat lateral extension of the scapular spine and is the most lateral bony point of the shoulder girdle. It is subcutaneous and forms the easily palpable 'tip' of the shoulder. It articulates with the clavicle at the acromioclavicular joint and provides origin to the deltoid muscle.

The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the proximal humerus, medial to the bicipital groove. It is not palpable as the tip of the shoulder and is situated much deeper than the acromion. It serves as the insertion site for the subscapularis muscle.

The surgical neck of the humerus is a common site of fracture located distal to the tuberosities. It is not a surface landmark and cannot be palpated as the tip of the shoulder. It is clinically important because the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap around it.
citation: The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder. The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula.

coreConcept: Palpability of the ulnar shaft
statement: Which border or surface of the ulna is subcutaneous and can be easily palpated along its entire length from the olecranon to the styloid process?
options: Anterior border
Lateral (interosseous) border
Posterior border
Medial surface
Anterior surface
correctOption: Posterior border
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor digitorum profundus and pronator quadratus muscles. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be palpated directly through the skin. This border separates the anterior and medial surfaces of the bone.

The lateral or interosseous border of the ulna provides attachment for the interosseous membrane. It is located deep within the forearm between the radius and ulna and is not accessible for surface palpation. It serves as a boundary for the deep compartments of the forearm.

The posterior border (or posterior aspect of the body) of the ulna is rounded and subcutaneous throughout its length. It can be easily felt by running a finger from the 'point' of the elbow (olecranon) down to the wrist. This makes it a common site for assessing ulnar fractures or bony tenderness.

The medial surface of the ulna is largely covered by the flexor digitorum profundus muscle in its proximal portion. While the distal part is more superficial, the entire length is not subcutaneous like the posterior border. It is located between the anterior and posterior borders.

The anterior surface of the ulna gives origin to the flexor digitorum profundus and is covered by the thick muscle mass of the anterior forearm. It is not palpable from the surface. This surface is bounded by the interosseous and anterior borders.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Mechanism of nipple retraction in breast carcinoma
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a clinician observes that the patient's nipple is retracted. Based on the anatomical relationships of the breast, this sign is most likely due to a tumor exerting traction on which of the following?
options: Suspensory ligaments of Cooper
Lactiferous ducts
Pectoralis major fascia
Retromammary space
Serratus anterior muscle
correctOption: Lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: Traction on the suspensory ligaments of Cooper (ligamenta suspensoria) typically results in dimpling of the skin, not necessarily retraction of the nipple itself. These ligaments connect the dermis to the deep fascia and maintain the breast's structural integrity. When a tumor invades these ligaments, they shorten and pull the skin inward.

Nipple retraction occurs when a carcinoma within the breast substance invades and causes fibrosis of the lactiferous ducts. As these ducts are pulled or shortened by the malignant process, they exert a direct inward pull on the nipple. This is a classic clinical sign of underlying breast malignancy.

Involvement of the pectoralis major fascia by a tumor may cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, which is assessed by asking the patient to contract the pectoral muscles. It does not directly cause nipple retraction. This finding indicates advanced local spread of the carcinoma.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue between the breast and the pectoral fascia that allows the breast some degree of movement. Obliteration of this space by a tumor leads to breast fixation rather than nipple retraction. It is a potential space often used for breast implants.

The serratus anterior muscle lies deep to the lateral part of the breast and is not involved in the mechanism of nipple retraction. While a very deep tumor might invade this muscle, the resulting clinical signs would relate to chest wall fixation. It is primarily responsible for protraction of the scapula.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Clinical utility and safety of mammography
statement: Which of the following best describes the radiographic technique of mammography and its clinical application?
options: It uses high-dose ionizing radiation to treat malignant tumors.
It is a low-dose X-ray technique used for screening tumors and cysts.
It is an ultrasound-based method used primarily for pregnant patients.
It is a magnetic resonance technique that avoids all radiation exposure.
It is used exclusively for the drainage of benign breast abscesses.
correctOption: It is a low-dose X-ray technique used for screening tumors and cysts.
optionExplanations: Mammography is a diagnostic and screening tool, not a treatment modality like radiotherapy. Radiotherapy uses high-dose radiation to kill cancer cells, whereas mammography uses minimal doses for imaging. High doses would be dangerous for routine screening purposes.

Mammography utilizes extremely low doses of X-rays to produce detailed images of breast tissue, making it safe for regular screening. It is highly effective at detecting both benign and malignant masses, as well as cysts, before they are palpable. Because the radiation dose is minimal, the examination can be repeated periodically as part of a screening program.

While ultrasound is used for breast imaging (especially to distinguish solid from cystic masses), mammography specifically refers to the X-ray-based technique. Ultrasound is often a secondary tool used when mammography results are inconclusive or in younger patients with dense breast tissue. It does not use ionizing radiation.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the breast is a different modality that uses magnetic fields rather than X-rays. While it avoids radiation, it is not called mammography and is typically reserved for high-risk patients or specific diagnostic dilemmas. Mammography remains the primary screening standard.

Mammography is an imaging technique, not a surgical procedure for drainage. While it can be used to guide a needle for biopsy or aspiration, its primary role is the detection and screening of lesions. Abscess drainage is typically performed under ultrasound guidance or clinical palpation.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatomes and sensory testing
statement: A patient reports a loss of sensation specifically on the skin of the middle finger following a neck injury. Which spinal cord segment is most likely associated with this specific dermatome?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C5 dermatome is typically located on the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid region. It does not extend down to the fingers. Sensory loss here would suggest an injury to the C5 spinal nerve or the upper trunk of the brachial plexus.

The C6 dermatome covers the lateral forearm and the thumb (first digit). It is part of the lateral margin of the upper limb sensory distribution. Injury to C6 often results in weakness of elbow flexion and loss of sensation in the thumb.

The C7 dermatome is specifically situated on the middle finger (third digit). Testing sensation on the middle finger is a standard clinical method for evaluating the integrity of the C7 spinal segment. This segment also provides the primary motor supply for elbow extension (triceps).

The C8 dermatome is located on the medial side of the hand, specifically involving the little finger (fifth digit) and sometimes the ring finger. It represents the transition to the medial margin of the limb. C8 is also critical for the motor function of the intrinsic hand muscles.

The T1 dermatome is found on the medial aspect of the forearm and the upper arm. It does not reach the fingers. T1 is the most inferior segment of the brachial plexus and is often tested by checking sensation along the medial elbow and forearm.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Consequences of deltoid atrophy on shoulder palpation
statement: A patient suffers an axillary nerve injury resulting in paralysis and rapid wasting of the deltoid muscle. Which bony landmark of the humerus becomes significantly more prominent and easier to palpate as a result of this atrophy?
options: Lesser tuberosity
Greater tuberosity
Deltoid tuberosity
Medial epicondyle
Anatomic neck
correctOption: Greater tuberosity
optionExplanations: The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and is not the primary structure that becomes prominent with deltoid wasting. It is situated medially to the intertubercular groove. Even with muscle atrophy, it remains relatively deep compared to the greater tuberosity.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus lies immediately deep to the deltoid muscle on the lateral aspect of the shoulder. When the deltoid wastes away due to axillary nerve paralysis, the protective muscular covering is lost, making the greater tuberosity much more prominent and easily palpable. This change alters the normal rounded contour of the shoulder.

The deltoid tuberosity is the site of insertion for the deltoid muscle on the lateral shaft of the humerus. While it is the attachment point, it is located further down the shaft and does not form the prominent 'corner' of the shoulder that becomes visible with deltoid atrophy. It is less distinct than the tuberosities of the head.

The medial epicondyle is a landmark at the distal end of the humerus near the elbow. Its palpability is unaffected by the state of the deltoid muscle, which is located at the proximal end of the arm. It is a key landmark for the ulnar nerve.

The anatomic neck of the humerus is the groove separating the head from the tuberosities. It is a deep structure and is not a surface landmark, even in cases of extreme muscle wasting. It is the site of attachment for the glenohumeral joint capsule.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse.

coreConcept: Surface landmark for the cephalic vein at the wrist
