While the deep fascia of the arm is present, the bicipital aponeurosis is the specific, thickened reinforcement of that fascia in the cubital region that provides the primary protection. General deep fascia is too thin and ubiquitous to be the specific landmark described for this clinical purpose.

The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. It does not lie between the median cubital vein and the brachial artery. The artery and vein are located more centrally within the fossa relative to the pronator teres.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein.

coreConcept: Palpation of anterior axillary lymph nodes
statement: To clinically examine the anterior (pectoral) group of axillary lymph nodes, in which direction should the examiner's fingers be pressed?
options: Backward against the subscapularis muscle
Laterally against the medial side of the humerus
Forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
Upward toward the apex of the axilla against the first rib
correctOption: Forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
optionExplanations: Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to palpate the posterior (subscapular) group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the posterior axillary fold. This would not allow for the assessment of the pectoral nodes.

Pressing laterally against the humerus is the technique used to palpate the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the medial side of the axillary vein. This group receives most of the lymph from the upper limb itself.

The anterior or pectoral nodes are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor, behind the pectoralis major. By pressing the fingers forward against the back of the pectoralis major muscle, the examiner can trap these nodes against the muscle wall. This is a standard part of a breast and axillary examination.

Pressing medially against the rib cage and serratus anterior is not the standard technique for any specific axillary node group. While the medial wall of the axilla is formed by these structures, the nodes are typically palpated against the muscular folds or the humerus. This maneuver would likely be uncomfortable and unproductive.

Pressing upward toward the apex of the axilla is the technique used to palpate the apical lymph nodes. This requires the patient to relax their shoulder completely to allow the fingers to reach the highest point of the axilla. It does not target the anterior pectoral group.
citation: The anterior (pectoral) nodes may be palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall of the axilla. The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla.

coreConcept: Palpation of apical axillary lymph nodes
statement: Which of the following describes the correct patient positioning and examiner technique for palpating the apical group of axillary lymph nodes?
options: Patient pushes hand hard against hip; examiner presses against pectoralis major
Patient's arm is abducted to 90 degrees; examiner presses against the humerus
Patient's arm hangs relaxed at the side; examiner reaches high toward the first rib
Patient's arm is flexed at the elbow; examiner palpates the medial epicondyle
Patient's arm is elevated above the head; examiner presses against the subscapularis
correctOption: Patient's arm hangs relaxed at the side; examiner reaches high toward the first rib
optionExplanations: Having the patient push their hand against their hip contracts the pectoralis major, which is useful for palpating the anterior (pectoral) nodes. However, for apical nodes, the muscles must be relaxed to allow the examiner's fingers to reach deep into the apex. Contraction would block access to the apical region.

Abducting the arm to 90 degrees and pressing against the humerus is the technique for palpating the lateral axillary nodes. The apical nodes are located much higher and more medially than the humeral shaft. This position would not allow the examiner to reach the apex of the axilla.

To reach the apical nodes, the axillary fascia and overlying muscles (pectoralis major and minor) must be as relaxed as possible. Letting the arm hang at the side achieves this relaxation. The examiner then reaches the highest point of the axilla, targeting the area where the axillary vessels pass over the first rib.

Palpating the medial epicondyle with the elbow flexed is used to find the ulnar nerve or assess the elbow joint's bony landmarks. It has no relevance to the examination of axillary lymph nodes. The apical nodes are located in the shoulder/axilla region, not the elbow.

Elevating the arm above the head actually tightens the skin and fascia of the axilla, making deep palpation of the apex more difficult. While this position is used for breast inspection, it is not ideal for palpating the apical lymph nodes. The subscapularis is the landmark for the posterior nodes, not the apical ones.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy for ulnar pulse palpation
statement: A clinician is attempting to palpate the ulnar pulse at the wrist. What is the correct anatomical relationship of the ulnar artery to the pisiform bone at this location?
options: The artery lies directly medial to the pisiform bone
The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone
The artery lies deep to the pisiform bone
The artery lies proximal to the pisiform bone in the snuffbox
The artery lies between the pisiform and the scaphoid
correctOption: The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone
optionExplanations: The medial side of the pisiform bone is the most ulnar point of the wrist and consists mostly of the insertion of the flexor carpi ulnaris. The neurovascular bundle (ulnar nerve and artery) does not pass medial to this bone. Palpating here would only reveal the bone and tendon.

The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum, specifically positioned just lateral to the pisiform bone. In this location, it is only covered by skin and fascia, making the pulse accessible. The ulnar nerve lies between the artery and the pisiform bone.

The ulnar artery does not pass deep to the pisiform bone; it passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum in Guyon's canal. The pisiform bone serves as the medial boundary of this canal. Deep structures at the wrist would include the carpal bones and the contents of the carpal tunnel.

The anatomic snuffbox is located on the radial (lateral) and posterior side of the wrist, containing the radial artery. The ulnar artery is on the opposite (medial) side of the wrist. Therefore, the ulnar pulse cannot be found in the snuffbox.

The scaphoid is located on the radial side of the wrist, while the pisiform is on the ulnar side. The distance between them spans the entire width of the wrist. The ulnar artery is specifically localized to the immediate lateral vicinity of the pisiform, not the mid-wrist area.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. ... The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Acromion as a surface landmark
statement: Which bony landmark of the scapula is described as a broad, flat lateral extension that forms the easily palpable 'tip of the shoulder'?
options: Coracoid process
Acromion
Infraglenoid tubercle
Superior angle
Medial border
correctOption: Acromion
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a beak-like projection that points anteriorly and is located inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. It is not the 'tip of the shoulder' and is felt through the deltoid muscle rather than being the most lateral point. It is much deeper than the acromion.

The acromion is the most lateral part of the scapula and articulates with the clavicle at the acromioclavicular joint. It is subcutaneous and forms the bony point of the shoulder. It is a direct continuation of the scapular spine and serves as a major attachment for the deltoid and trapezius.

The infraglenoid tubercle is located on the lateral border of the scapula, just below the glenoid cavity. It serves as the attachment for the long head of the triceps brachii. It is deep and not palpable from the surface, especially not at the tip of the shoulder.

The superior angle of the scapula is located at the junction of the superior and medial borders. It is covered by the levator scapulae and trapezius muscles. While it can be palpated, it is located much more medially and superiorly than the tip of the shoulder.

The medial border of the scapula (vertebral border) runs parallel to the vertebral column. It is easily palpable when the arm is moved, but it is located on the back rather than the shoulder tip. It serves as the insertion for the rhomboids and serratus anterior.
citation: The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder. ... The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length.

coreConcept: Brachial artery palpation in the arm
statement: A clinician needs to palpate the brachial artery in the middle of the arm. What is the correct anatomical relationship of the artery to the surrounding muscles at this level?
options: It lies on the triceps brachii and is overlapped medially by the coracobrachialis
It lies on the brachialis and is overlapped laterally by the biceps brachii
It lies superficial to the biceps brachii muscle
It lies between the brachioradialis and the pronator teres
It lies posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
correctOption: It lies on the brachialis and is overlapped laterally by the biceps brachii
optionExplanations: The triceps brachii is located in the posterior compartment of the arm, while the brachial artery is the main vessel of the anterior compartment. The coracobrachialis is in the anterior compartment but is proximal to the mid-arm palpation site. This description incorrectly places the artery in the posterior compartment.

In the mid-arm, the brachial artery travels in the medial bicipital groove. It rests directly on the brachialis muscle, which forms the floor of this region, and is partially covered or 'overlapped' by the medial edge of the biceps brachii. This relationship allows the clinician to palpate the pulse by pressing medially against the humerus.

The brachial artery is a deep structure relative to the biceps brachii; it is not superficial to it. To palpate the artery, one must move the biceps brachii laterally or palpate just medial to its margin. Placing fingers superficial to the biceps would only allow palpation of the muscle itself.

The space between the brachioradialis and the pronator teres is located in the cubital fossa and proximal forearm, not the mid-arm. While the brachial artery does enter the cubital fossa, the question specifically asks for its relationship in the middle of the arm. These muscles are too distal to be mid-arm landmarks.

The structure that lies posterior to the medial epicondyle is the ulnar nerve, not the brachial artery. The brachial artery passes anterior to the elbow joint in the cubital fossa. Palpating posterior to the medial epicondyle would allow one to 'roll' the ulnar nerve, but no pulse would be felt there.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis.

coreConcept: Scapular surface landmarks and vertebral levels
statement: During a physical examination of the back, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a reference point. Which of the following anatomical levels does this landmark typically represent?
options: The level of the second rib and T2 vertebra
The level of the fourth rib and T4 vertebra
The level of the seventh rib and T7 vertebra
The level of the tenth rib and T10 vertebra
The level of the twelfth rib and L1 vertebra
correctOption: The level of the seventh rib and T7 vertebra
optionExplanations: The T2 level corresponds to the superior angle of the scapula rather than the inferior angle. This landmark is useful for identifying the upper limits of the thoracic cage and the second rib. It is located significantly higher than the point used for lower thoracic marking.

The T4 level is associated with the sternal angle anteriorly and the spine of the fourth thoracic vertebra posteriorly. It serves as a critical landmark for the division of the mediastinum and the bifurcation of the trachea. It is too superior to be marked by the inferior angle of the scapula.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a primary surface landmark that consistently aligns with the seventh rib and the spinous process of the T7 vertebra. This relationship is utilized by clinicians to accurately count ribs and locate specific thoracic vertebral levels during physical exams. It is easily palpable in the living subject when the upper limb is in the neutral position.

The T10 level is located in the lower thoracic region, often corresponding to the xiphisternal joint or the dermatome of the umbilicus. The scapula does not reach this inferior level in a standard anatomical position. Using this as a landmark for the scapula would result in significant clinical error.

The L1 level is a lumbar vertebral landmark associated with the transpyloric plane and the termination of the spinal cord in adults. It is situated well below the inferior margin of the thoracic cage and the scapula. It has no direct topographical relationship with the scapular angles.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Clinical examination of the breast and nipple retraction
statement: A 55-year-old woman presents for a routine breast examination. The clinician notes that when the patient raises her arms above her head, there is visible retraction of the right nipple. What is the most likely anatomical mechanism for this finding?
options: Infiltration of the pectoral fascia by a tumor
Tension on the suspensory ligaments of Cooper
Carcinoma pulling on the lactiferous ducts
Obstruction of the superficial lymphatic vessels
Atrophy of the retromammary space
correctOption: Carcinoma pulling on the lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: Infiltration of the pectoral fascia by a tumor typically causes the breast to become fixed to the chest wall rather than causing isolated nipple retraction. This finding is best demonstrated by asking the patient to contract the pectoralis major muscles. It indicates deep invasion but is not the primary cause of nipple inversion.

Tension on the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) by a growing carcinoma results in dimpling of the overlying skin. While this is a sign of malignancy, it is distinct from the mechanism that specifically pulls the nipple inward. Skin dimpling occurs because these ligaments connect the dermis to the deep fascia.

Nipple retraction occurs when a carcinoma within the breast substance involves and pulls on the lactiferous ducts. These ducts converge at the nipple, and their shortening due to neoplastic infiltration draws the nipple toward the tumor. This is a classic clinical sign of an underlying malignancy that requires urgent investigation.

Obstruction of the superficial lymphatic vessels by cancer cells leads to lymphedema of the skin, known as peau d'orange. This gives the skin a thickened, pitted appearance similar to an orange peel. It is a sign of advanced disease but does not directly cause the mechanical retraction of the nipple.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue that allows the breast to move freely over the deep fascia. Atrophy or obliteration of this space might limit breast mobility but does not cause the specific pulling force required for nipple retraction. Nipple retraction is an active mechanical process caused by ductal involvement.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatomes and sensory testing
statement: A patient reports a loss of sensation specifically on the skin of the middle finger following a neck injury. Which spinal cord segment is most likely affected based on standard dermatomal mapping?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C5 dermatome is primarily located on the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid region. It does not extend to the digits of the hand. Sensory loss here would suggest a lesion at the C5 spinal level or the axillary nerve.

The C6 dermatome covers the lateral forearm and the thumb (first digit). It is part of the lateral margin of the upper limb sensory distribution. It does not provide the primary sensory innervation for the middle finger.

The C7 dermatome is classically situated on the middle finger and the central portion of the hand. Testing sensation on the middle finger is the standard clinical method for evaluating the integrity of the C7 spinal segment. This is a critical landmark in neurological examinations of the upper limb.

The C8 dermatome is located on the medial margin of the hand, specifically involving the little finger (fifth digit). It also covers the medial aspect of the wrist. It is distinct from the central sensory zone occupied by C7.

The T1 dermatome is found along the medial margin of the forearm and the upper arm. It does not extend into the hand or the fingers. Lesions at this level would result in sensory deficits proximal to the wrist on the ulnar side.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Palpation of apical axillary lymph nodes
statement: During a comprehensive axillary lymph node examination, a clinician attempts to palpate the apical group. Which of the following describes the correct technique and anatomical target for this specific group?
options: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis
Pressing laterally against the axillary vein and artery
Placing fingers high in the axilla toward the outer border of the first rib
Palpating the center of the axilla between the anterior and posterior walls
correctOption: Placing fingers high in the axilla toward the outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major is the technique used to palpate the anterior or pectoral group of lymph nodes. These nodes receive the majority of lymph from the breast. They are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.

Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle targets the posterior or subscapular group of lymph nodes. These nodes are situated along the subscapular vessels. They drain the posterior thoracic wall and the scapular region.

Pressing laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery is the method for palpating the lateral group of lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the medial side of the axillary vein. They receive most of the lymph from the upper limb.

The apical nodes are located at the apex of the axilla, medial to the pectoralis minor and superior to the other groups. To palpate them, the patient must relax the shoulder to allow the examiner's fingers to reach high up toward the outer border of the first rib. This group represents the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage.

Palpating the center of the axilla between the anterior and posterior walls targets the central group of lymph nodes. These nodes are embedded in the axillary fat and receive lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. They are often the most easily felt if enlarged.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt.

coreConcept: Anatomy of central venous catheterization via the basilic vein
statement: A clinician chooses the basilic vein for central venous catheterization. What anatomical characteristic makes the basilic vein more suitable for this procedure compared to the cephalic vein?
options: It is more superficial and easier to visualize in obese patients
It lacks a bicipital aponeurosis at the level of the cubital fossa
It increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
It always lies lateral to the styloid process of the radius
It is the only vein that communicates with the deep venous system
correctOption: It increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is actually deeper than the cephalic vein in many parts of its course and can be harder to visualize without ultrasound. The cephalic vein is often more superficial in the forearm. Ease of visualization is not the primary reason for choosing the basilic vein for central access.

The bicipital aponeurosis is a structure in the cubital fossa that separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery. It does not determine the suitability of the basilic vein for catheterization. Its primary clinical role is protecting the underlying artery during venipuncture.

The basilic vein is preferred because it follows a relatively straight, direct path into the axillary vein and increases in caliber as it ascends. This reduces the risk of the catheter getting caught in valves or sharp turns, which is a common problem with the cephalic vein. The cephalic vein often enters the axillary vein at a sharp angle in the deltopectoral triangle.

The cephalic vein, not the basilic vein, lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. This makes the cephalic vein a reliable site for peripheral venipuncture at the wrist. The basilic vein is located on the medial side of the forearm.

Both the basilic and cephalic veins communicate with the deep venous system through various perforating veins. This is not a unique feature of the basilic vein. The primary advantage for catheterization remains the linear anatomy and increasing diameter of the basilic-axillary transition.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis.

coreConcept: Radiological anatomy of the elbow joint
statement: When evaluating a lateral radiograph of the elbow in a trauma patient, what is the normal anterior angulation of the distal humerus relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft?
optionExplanations: An angulation of 15° is significantly less than the normal anatomical tilt of the distal humerus. Such a finding on a radiograph might suggest a supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement. Normal anatomy requires a more pronounced forward tilt for proper joint function.

While 30° is closer to the correct value, it still represents an underestimation of the normal humeral angulation. Proper alignment is essential for the full range of flexion and extension at the elbow. Deviations from the standard 45° angle are clinically significant in orthopedic assessments.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at approximately 45° relative to the shaft. This angulation is a critical feature to identify on lateral radiographs to ensure the distal humerus is not fractured or displaced. It allows for the coronoid process of the ulna to fit into the coronoid fossa during flexion.

An angulation of 60° would represent an excessive anterior tilt, which is not typical of normal humeral anatomy. This could indicate a malunited fracture or a congenital deformity. It would likely interfere with the normal mechanics of the olecranon during extension.

A 90° angle would mean the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is anatomically impossible in a healthy individual. This would only be seen in catastrophic fractures or severe dislocations. It is not a standard reference point for radiological evaluation.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Mammography principles and breast screening
statement: Which of the following statements best describes the clinical application and safety of mammography for breast screening?
options: It uses high-dose radiation and should only be performed once every five years
It is primarily used to distinguish between solid tumors and fluid-filled cysts
It uses extremely low doses of x-rays, allowing for frequent repeated examinations
It is only effective for detecting malignant carcinomas, not benign cysts
It requires the injection of contrast media into the lactiferous ducts
correctOption: It uses extremely low doses of x-rays, allowing for frequent repeated examinations
optionExplanations: Mammography does not use high-dose radiation; in fact, the doses are kept extremely low to minimize the risk of radiation-induced damage. This low-dose profile is what makes it suitable for regular screening programs. High-dose radiation would be counterproductive for a screening tool.

While mammography can identify masses, ultrasound is typically the preferred modality for distinguishing between solid tumors and fluid-filled cysts. Mammography identifies densities and calcifications that may suggest malignancy. It is a screening tool rather than a definitive diagnostic tool for fluid characterization.

Mammography is designed to use extremely low doses of x-rays, which minimizes the cumulative radiation risk to the patient. This safety profile allows the examination to be repeated annually or biennially as part of standard screening protocols. It is highly effective at detecting early-stage tumors and other abnormalities.

Mammography is used to screen for a wide range of breast pathologies, including benign tumors, malignant tumors, and cysts. It is not limited to the detection of carcinomas. Any abnormal density or swelling noted on the radiograph warrants further clinical investigation.

Standard screening mammography does not require the injection of contrast media. A procedure that involves injecting contrast into the ducts is called a galactogram or ductogram, which is a specialized diagnostic test. Screening mammography relies solely on the differential absorption of x-rays by breast tissues.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast. This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often. Any swelling should be noted. An underlying tumor, a cyst, or abscess formation can cause a swelling.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is characterized by being rounded, subcutaneous, and easily palpable along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: The anterior border of the body
The interosseous border
The posterior aspect of the body
The coronoid process
The radial notch
correctOption: The posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be easily palpated along its length. Palpation of the anterior forearm primarily reveals muscle bulk.

The interosseous border of the ulna is the sharp lateral edge where the interosseous membrane attaches. It is located deep within the forearm between the radius and ulna. It is not accessible for surface palpation in a living subject.

The posterior aspect (or posterior border) of the ulnar body is subcutaneous throughout its entire length. This allows a clinician to trace the bone from the olecranon down to the ulnar styloid process by simply sliding a finger along the back of the forearm. It is a key landmark for assessing ulnar fractures.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior and proximal part of the ulna that serves as an attachment for the brachialis muscle. It is located deep within the cubital fossa and is not subcutaneous. It cannot be palpated from the surface under normal conditions.

The radial notch is a small articular surface on the lateral side of the coronoid process that receives the head of the radius. It is a deep structure within the elbow joint complex. It is entirely covered by ligaments and muscles, making it impossible to palpate.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length.

coreConcept: Anatomical orientation of the medial epicondyle
statement: In the anatomical position, what is the specific orientation of the medial epicondyle of the humerus, and which structure is closely related to its posterior surface?
options: Directed anteriorly; related to the median nerve
Directed medially and posteriorly; related to the ulnar nerve
Directed laterally; related to the radial nerve
Directed posteriorly; related to the brachial artery
Directed medially and anteriorly; related to the musculocutaneous nerve
correctOption: Directed medially and posteriorly; related to the ulnar nerve
optionExplanations: The medial epicondyle is not directed anteriorly; it has a distinct posterior tilt in the anatomical position. Furthermore, the median nerve passes anterior to the elbow joint, not posterior to the medial epicondyle. The median nerve is separated from the epicondyle by the humeral head of the pronator teres.

In the anatomical position, the medial epicondyle is directed medially and slightly posteriorly. This orientation is significant because the ulnar nerve runs in a groove (the ulnar sulcus) on its posterior surface. This makes the nerve highly vulnerable to compression or trauma at this site, often referred to as the 'funny bone'.

The lateral epicondyle, not the medial, is directed laterally. The radial nerve is related to the lateral side of the humerus and passes anterior to the lateral epicondyle. The medial epicondyle is much larger and more prominent than its lateral counterpart.

While the medial epicondyle does have a posterior component to its orientation, it is primarily a medial structure. The brachial artery is located anteriorly in the cubital fossa, medial to the biceps tendon. It does not have a direct relationship with the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle.

The medial epicondyle faces posteriorly rather than anteriorly. The musculocutaneous nerve terminates as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm and is located on the lateral side of the elbow. It does not pass near the medial epicondyle or the ulnar sulcus.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here.

coreConcept: Palpation of the greater tuberosity in deltoid wasting
statement: A patient presents with a shoulder injury resulting in axillary nerve damage. As the deltoid muscle undergoes rapid atrophy, which bony landmark of the humerus becomes significantly more prominent and easier to palpate?
options: Lesser tuberosity
Greater tuberosity
Surgical neck
Deltoid tuberosity
Intertubercular sulcus
correctOption: Greater tuberosity
optionExplanations: The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus and is the insertion site for the subscapularis muscle. It is generally not the most prominent landmark revealed by deltoid wasting. It is situated medial to the greater tuberosity and the bicipital groove.

The greater tuberosity forms the lateral-most part of the proximal humerus and is normally covered by the thick deltoid muscle. When the deltoid wastes away due to axillary nerve paralysis, the rounded contour of the shoulder is lost, and the greater tuberosity becomes subcutaneous and easily palpable. This change in surface anatomy is a classic sign of deltoid atrophy.

The surgical neck of the humerus is the constricted region distal to the tuberosities. While it is the site where the axillary nerve itself is often injured (e.g., in fractures), it is not a prominent bony projection that becomes more palpable with muscle wasting. It is a common site for humeral fractures.

The deltoid tuberosity is a roughened area on the lateral surface of the humeral shaft where the deltoid muscle inserts. It is located much further down the arm than the shoulder joint. Wasting of the muscle would make the insertion point less distinct rather than more prominent.

The intertubercular sulcus (bicipital groove) lies between the greater and lesser tuberosities and contains the long head of the biceps tendon. It is a depression rather than a projection. While it might be easier to locate if the overlying muscle is gone, the greater tuberosity remains the most prominent palpable feature.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure.

coreConcept: Breast carcinoma and skin dimpling mechanism
statement: A 52-year-old woman presents for a routine physical examination. When she is asked to raise both arms above her head, the clinician observes a distinct dimpling of the skin in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. Which of the following anatomical structures is most likely being pulled by an underlying malignancy to cause this specific surface finding?
options: Suspensory ligaments
Pectoralis minor fascia
Serratus anterior digitations
Retromammary space
Clavipectoral fascia
correctOption: Suspensory ligaments
optionExplanations: The suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) connect the dermis of the skin to the deep fascia of the pectoralis major; when a carcinoma invades these ligaments, it causes them to shorten and pull the skin inward, resulting in dimpling. This maneuver is enhanced by raising the arms, which stretches the breast tissue against the thoracic wall. Other structures like the lactiferous ducts can also be tethered, leading to nipple retraction rather than generalized skin dimpling. Understanding this surface landmark is critical for early clinical detection of breast cancer.

The pectoralis minor fascia is located deep to the pectoralis major and does not have direct fibrous attachments to the skin of the breast. While a tumor may eventually invade the deep fascia, it is the superficial suspensory ligaments that are responsible for the characteristic skin dimpling. Clinical examination focuses on the mobility of the breast over the pectoralis major to assess deep invasion. Therefore, this fascia is not the primary structure causing the observed dimpling.

The serratus anterior digitations form the medial wall of the axilla and the lateral thoracic wall, lying deep to the lateral portion of the breast. While the breast overlaps this muscle, the muscle itself does not provide the fibrous scaffolding to the skin that would result in dimpling upon tumor contraction. Dimpling is specifically a result of tension on the superficial fascial system. Thus, the serratus anterior is not the correct anatomical correlate for this sign.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose areolar tissue between the breast and the pectoralis major fascia that allows the breast some degree of movement. Invasion of this space by a tumor may cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, but it does not directly cause skin dimpling. Skin dimpling requires a tethering effect between the tumor and the dermis. Consequently, the retromammary space is not the structure being pulled in this scenario.

The clavipectoral fascia is a deep fascial layer that invests the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles and is not involved in the superficial architecture of the breast. It is located superior and deep to the main glandular tissue of the breast. It does not possess the suspensory fibers necessary to transmit tension from a tumor to the overlying skin. Therefore, it cannot be the cause of the observed skin dimpling.
citation: With the patient undressed to the waist and sitting upright, first inspect the breasts for symmetry. Some degree of asymmetry is common and is the result of unequal breast development. Any swelling should be noted. An underlying tumor, a cyst, or abscess formation can cause a swelling. Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatome distribution (C7)
statement: During a neurological examination of the upper limb, a clinician tests for sensory perception to evaluate specific spinal cord segments. Which of the following areas is the primary site for testing the C7 dermatome?
options: Lateral aspect of the arm
Medial aspect of the forearm
The middle finger
The thumb and lateral forearm
The little finger
correctOption: The middle finger
optionExplanations: The C7 dermatome is classically represented by the skin over the middle finger, making it the standard site for testing this specific spinal segment. This central position in the hand separates the lateral dermatomes (C3-C6) from the medial dermatomes (C8-T2). Accurate mapping of these segments is essential for localizing spinal cord or nerve root injuries. Therefore, the middle finger is the correct anatomical landmark for C7.

The lateral aspect of the arm is primarily supplied by the C5 and C6 dermatomes, which follow the lateral margin of the upper limb. Testing this area would evaluate the upper cervical segments rather than C7. The transition from C6 to C7 occurs as one moves distally toward the hand. Thus, this site is incorrect for C7 testing.

The medial aspect of the forearm is supplied by the T1 dermatome, which follows the medial margin of the limb. Sensory loss in this area would suggest an injury to the lower brachial plexus or the T1 nerve root. It is distinct from the C7 distribution which is more central in the hand. Consequently, this option is incorrect.

The thumb and the lateral aspect of the forearm are primarily associated with the C6 dermatome. C6 typically covers the lateral forearm and the first digit (thumb), while C7 takes over at the second or third digit. Testing the thumb would specifically assess the C6 spinal segment. Therefore, this is not the correct site for C7.

The little finger (fifth digit) and the medial side of the hand are supplied by the C8 dermatome. This area is part of the medial margin distribution of the upper limb's sensory supply. C8 is adjacent to C7 but is clearly demarcated at the ulnar side of the hand. Thus, the little finger is used to test C8, not C7.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Deltoid atrophy and greater tuberosity palpation
statement: A patient presents with significant muscle wasting over the lateral aspect of the shoulder following a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. On palpation, the clinician can easily feel a prominent bony landmark that is normally obscured by the deltoid muscle. Which landmark is now readily palpable?
options: Lesser tuberosity of the humerus
Greater tuberosity of the humerus
Coracoid process
Acromion process
Infraglenoid tubercle
correctOption: Greater tuberosity of the humerus
optionExplanations: The greater tuberosity of the humerus is the most lateral bony projection of the proximal humerus and is normally covered by the thick belly of the deltoid muscle. When the axillary nerve is damaged (often by surgical neck fractures), the deltoid undergoes rapid atrophy, removing the muscular padding and making the greater tuberosity easily palpable. This change in surface anatomy is a hallmark of deltoid paralysis. Therefore, the greater tuberosity is the correct landmark.

The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the humerus, medial to the bicipital groove. While it may become slightly more accessible with muscle wasting, it is not the primary lateral landmark that becomes prominent when the deltoid wastes. The deltoid primarily covers the lateral and superior aspects of the humerus. Thus, the lesser tuberosity is not the best answer.

The coracoid process is an anterior projection of the scapula that can be palpated deep to the anterior deltoid even in healthy individuals. It is located inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. While deltoid wasting might make it slightly easier to feel, it is not the landmark specifically described as being 'underlying' the bulk of the deltoid that becomes prominent upon its wasting. Consequently, this is incorrect.

The acromion process is the subcutaneous 'tip of the shoulder' and is always easily palpable regardless of deltoid muscle mass. It forms the roof of the shoulder joint and serves as the origin for the deltoid. Because it is already subcutaneous, its palpability does not change significantly with muscle wasting. Therefore, it is not the landmark revealed by atrophy.

The infraglenoid tubercle is located on the lateral border of the scapula, inferior to the glenoid cavity, and serves as the origin for the long head of the triceps. It is situated deep within the axilla and posterior shoulder and is not readily palpable even with deltoid wasting. It is far removed from the surface anatomy of the lateral shoulder. Thus, this option is incorrect.
citation: The paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Cephalic vein landmark at the wrist
statement: In an emergency situation where a patient is in shock and superficial veins are not visible, a clinician attempts to locate the cephalic vein for urgent venous access. According to standard surface anatomy, where is the cephalic vein most consistently located at the level of the wrist?
options: Anterior to the medial epicondyle
Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Medial to the pisiform bone
Directly over the olecranon process
Lateral to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is a reliable site for venipuncture because of its constant anatomical position at the wrist, where it passes in the superficial fascia immediately posterior to the radial styloid process. This landmark is particularly useful when veins are collapsed due to shock, as the bony styloid process provides a fixed reference point. It then continues up the lateral side of the forearm. Therefore, this is the correct anatomical location.

The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is associated with the basilic vein and the median cubital vein in the cubital fossa, not the cephalic vein at the wrist. While these are common sites for venipuncture, they do not match the specific landmark of the radial styloid. The cephalic vein is lateral, while the medial epicondyle is medial. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The area medial to the pisiform bone is the site where the ulnar nerve and ulnar artery pass into the hand. There is no major superficial vein consistently located here for emergency access. This region is on the ulnar (medial) side of the wrist, whereas the cephalic vein is on the radial (lateral) side. Consequently, this is not the correct site.

The olecranon process is the bony point of the elbow and is subcutaneous, but it is not a site for the cephalic vein. The cephalic vein travels in the deltopectoral groove and the lateral arm/forearm, staying away from the posterior bony prominence of the elbow. Therefore, this landmark is irrelevant for locating the cephalic vein.

The area lateral to the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist. The cephalic vein is a lateral structure of the upper limb. The basilic vein would be more likely found on the medial side, but the specific landmark for the cephalic vein is the radial styloid. Thus, this option is incorrect.
citation: The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization. Every clinical professional, in an emergency, should know where to obtain blood from the arm. When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Radiographic angulation of the distal humerus
statement: When evaluating a lateral radiograph of the elbow joint following trauma, a radiologist assesses the alignment of the distal humerus. What is the normal anterior angulation of the lower end of the humerus relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft?
options: 15 degrees
30 degrees
45 degrees
60 degrees
90 degrees
correctOption: 45 degrees
optionExplanations: The distal end of the humerus, including the trochlea and capitulum, is normally angulated anteriorly at approximately 45 degrees relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This angulation is a critical radiographic landmark; a change in this angle on a lateral view often indicates a supracondylar fracture, especially in pediatric patients. Maintaining this relationship is essential for the normal range of elbow flexion. Therefore, 45 degrees is the correct anatomical value.

An angulation of 15 degrees is too shallow and would represent a significant posterior displacement or malalignment of the distal humeral epiphysis. Normal anatomy requires a much more pronounced anterior tilt to accommodate the coronoid process of the ulna during flexion. Thus, 15 degrees is incorrect.

While 30 degrees represents some anterior tilt, it is less than the standard 45-degree angulation described in anatomical and radiological texts. Using 30 degrees as a baseline might lead to underestimating the degree of displacement in a fracture. The standard anatomical tilt is more significant. Consequently, this option is incorrect.

An angulation of 60 degrees would be an excessive anterior tilt, potentially indicating an impacted fracture or a congenital deformity. Such a sharp angle would likely interfere with the mechanical stability and range of motion of the elbow joint. It exceeds the normal physiological range of 45 degrees. Therefore, this is not the correct standard.

A 90-degree angulation would mean the articular surface is perpendicular to the shaft, which is not the case in human humeral anatomy. This would be a gross deformity and would not allow for the normal hinge-like function of the elbow. The actual tilt is half of this value. Thus, 90 degrees is incorrect.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Apical axillary lymph node palpation technique
statement: During a clinical examination for suspected lymphadenopathy, the clinician specifically palps the apical group of axillary lymph nodes. Which of the following describes the correct anatomical landmark for locating these nodes?
options: Posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Anterior surface of the subscapularis
Medial side of the axillary vein
Outer border of the first rib
Center of the axilla between the walls
correctOption: Outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: The apical lymph nodes are located at the apex of the axilla, which is the highest point where the axillary sheath enters the neck. The specific landmark for palpating these nodes is the outer border of the first rib, reached by placing the fingers high up in the axilla while the patient's arm is relaxed. These nodes receive lymph from all other axillary groups, making them clinically significant in cancer staging. Therefore, the first rib is the correct landmark.

The posterior surface of the pectoralis major is the landmark used to palpate the anterior (pectoral) group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes lie along the lower border of the pectoralis minor and receive lymph primarily from the breast. They are much lower and more anterior than the apical nodes. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle is the landmark for palpating the posterior (subscapular) group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the subscapular vessels on the posterior wall of the axilla. They are distinct from the apical group located at the very top of the axillary space. Consequently, this is not the correct site.

The medial side of the axillary vein is the landmark for the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes, which receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. These nodes are palpated by pressing laterally against the humerus and the neurovascular bundle. They are not the apical nodes, which are situated more superiorly. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

The center of the axilla, between the anterior and posterior walls, is the location of the central lymph nodes. These nodes are embedded in axillary fat and receive lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. While they are central, they are not the 'apical' nodes located at the thoracic inlet. Thus, this is incorrect.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve palpation at the elbow
statement: A clinician is performing a physical exam on a patient with tingling in the 4th and 5th digits. To assess the ulnar nerve at the elbow, where should the clinician palpate to feel the nerve being 'rolled' against a bony prominence?
options: Anterior to the lateral epicondyle
Posterior to the medial epicondyle
Directly over the olecranon process
In the center of the cubital fossa
Superior to the coracoid process
correctOption: Posterior to the medial epicondyle
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve is uniquely accessible to palpation as it passes through the ulnar sulcus (groove) on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. In this location, the nerve is superficial and can be rolled against the bone, which often produces a characteristic tingling sensation (the 'funny bone' effect). This is a common site for entrapment or trauma. Therefore, posterior to the medial epicondyle is the correct location.

The area anterior to the lateral epicondyle is associated with the radial nerve as it divides into its superficial and deep branches. The ulnar nerve does not pass near the lateral epicondyle; it is strictly a medial structure at the level of the elbow. Palpating here would not allow for assessment of the ulnar nerve. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The olecranon process is the bony point of the elbow formed by the ulna. While the ulnar nerve passes between the medial epicondyle and the olecranon, it is specifically the posterior surface of the epicondyle that provides the hard surface against which the nerve is rolled during examination. The olecranon itself is not the primary landmark for this maneuver. Consequently, this is incorrect.

The center of the cubital fossa contains the brachial artery and the median nerve, which are protected by the bicipital aponeurosis. The ulnar nerve does not enter the cubital fossa; it passes posterior to the elbow joint. Therefore, palpation in the cubital fossa would not reach the ulnar nerve. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The coracoid process is a landmark of the scapula in the shoulder region, far proximal to the elbow joint. The ulnar nerve originates from the medial cord of the brachial plexus in the axilla but is not palpated against the coracoid process. This landmark is irrelevant for an elbow examination. Therefore, this is not the correct site.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture). The nerve can be palpated and rolled against the epicondyle.

coreConcept: Scaphoid palpation in the anatomic snuffbox
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls on an outstretched hand and complains of severe pain on the lateral side of the wrist. The clinician notes deep tenderness upon palpation within the triangular depression bounded medially by the extensor pollicis longus. Which bone is most likely fractured?
options: Lunate
Triquetrum
Scaphoid
Pisiform
Hamate
correctOption: Scaphoid
optionExplanations: The scaphoid bone forms the floor of the anatomic snuffbox, making it directly accessible to palpation in this region. Tenderness in the snuffbox following a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH) is a classic clinical sign of a scaphoid fracture, which is the most common carpal bone fracture. Because of its retrograde blood supply, missing this diagnosis can lead to avascular necrosis. Therefore, the scaphoid is the correct bone.

The lunate is located in the center of the proximal carpal row and is not palpated within the anatomic snuffbox. It is more commonly associated with dislocations rather than fractures from a FOOSH injury. Tenderness from a lunate injury would be felt more centrally on the dorsal or palmar aspect of the wrist. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The triquetrum is located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist, opposite the anatomic snuffbox. It is the third bone in the proximal row and is not involved in the lateral surface anatomy described. Palpation of the triquetrum occurs distal to the ulnar styloid. Consequently, this is not the correct bone.

The pisiform is a sesamoid bone located on the palmar, medial aspect of the wrist within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris. It is easily palpable on the anterior surface but is nowhere near the posterolateral anatomic snuffbox. It is not typically fractured in the mechanism described. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

The hamate is a distal carpal bone on the ulnar side, characterized by its palpable hook on the palmar surface. It does not contribute to the floor of the anatomic snuffbox and is not located on the lateral side of the wrist. Fractures of the hamate often involve the hook and occur from different mechanisms, such as swinging a golf club. Thus, this is incorrect.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis (see Figs. 3.34 and 3.105B, C). Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here (see Fig. 3.105B).

coreConcept: Elbow bony landmarks in flexion
statement: A clinician is examining a patient's elbow for potential displacement following a fall. When the elbow is placed in a position of full flexion, what is the normal spatial relationship between the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the tip of the olecranon?
options: They form a straight horizontal line
They form a straight vertical line
They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
The olecranon moves superior to both epicondyles
The epicondyles overlap the olecranon completely
correctOption: They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
optionExplanations: In a normal elbow, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the olecranon process maintain a specific geometric relationship that changes with movement. When the elbow is flexed to 90 degrees, these three points form an equilateral triangle (or isosceles triangle in some texts). Disruption of this triangle suggests a fracture or dislocation of the elbow components. Therefore, the equilateral triangle is the correct anatomical configuration in flexion.

A straight horizontal line is the normal relationship of these three bony landmarks when the elbow is in full extension. As the elbow flexes, the olecranon moves distally and anteriorly relative to the humerus, breaking the linear alignment. Finding a straight line in flexion would indicate a significant pathology. Thus, this option is incorrect.

A straight vertical line is not a normal anatomical relationship for these landmarks in any standard physiological position of the elbow. The epicondyles are always mediolateral to each other, preventing a vertical alignment with the olecranon. This description does not match elbow surface anatomy. Consequently, this is incorrect.

In flexion, the olecranon moves distally (away from the shoulder) as it rotates around the trochlea, not superiorly. The 'triangle' is formed because the olecranon moves to a plane lower than the horizontal line connecting the two epicondyles. Therefore, the suggestion that it moves superiorly is anatomically inaccurate.

The epicondyles and the olecranon are distinct bony prominences that do not overlap; they are used as landmarks precisely because they remain palpable and separate. Overlapping would imply a catastrophic crush injury or gross malformation. In normal surface anatomy, they remain distinct vertices of a geometric shape. Thus, this option is incorrect.
citation: The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon and the pulley-shaped trochlea of the humerus; it also has strong medial and lateral ligaments. When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Central axillary lymph node location
statement: During a systematic palpation of the axilla, the clinician places their fingers deep into the axillary fat, midway between the anterior and posterior muscular walls. Which group of lymph nodes is being evaluated in this specific location?
options: Pectoral nodes
Subscapular nodes
Lateral nodes
Central nodes
Apical nodes
correctOption: Central nodes
optionExplanations: The central axillary lymph nodes are located deep within the axillary fat in the center of the axilla. They are positioned between the pectoralis major (which forms the anterior wall) and the subscapularis (which forms the posterior wall). These nodes are often the most easily palpable if enlarged because they receive drainage from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. Therefore, central nodes is the correct answer.

The pectoral (anterior) nodes are located along the inferior border of the pectoralis minor, specifically against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major. They are not found in the 'center' of the axillary space but rather against the anterior wall. Palpation involves pressing forward, not deep into the center. Thus, this option is incorrect.

The subscapular (posterior) nodes are located along the posterior wall of the axilla, specifically against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle. They follow the subscapular vessels and are palpated by pressing backward against the posterior wall. They are distinct from the central nodes located in the middle of the axillary contents. Consequently, this is incorrect.

The lateral nodes are located along the medial side of the axillary vein and are palpated by pressing laterally against the upper part of the humerus. They receive lymph from the upper limb and are situated more laterally than the central group. They are not found 'midway' between the anterior and posterior walls. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

The apical nodes are the most superior group, located at the apex of the axilla near the first rib. They are reached by palpating high up in the axilla, far above the central fat pad where the central nodes reside. They represent the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage. Thus, this is not the correct group for the described location.
citation: The examiner then palpates the axillary nodes (see Fig. 3.69) as follows: The anterior (pectoral) nodes may be palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall of the axilla... The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall).

coreConcept: Palpation of the coracoid process
statement: A clinician is performing a physical examination of the shoulder and intends to palpate the coracoid process. Which of the following describes the correct technique and location for identifying this structure?
options: Superficial palpation at the medial end of the clavicle
Deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
Palpation of the most lateral point of the acromion process
Deep pressure in the center of the supraspinatus fossa
Superficial palpation along the posterior border of the scapular spine
correctOption: Deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
optionExplanations: The medial end of the clavicle is the site of the sternoclavicular joint and is not related to the coracoid process. Palpation here would assess the joint integrity rather than the scapular projections. The coracoid process is located much further laterally and deeper within the shoulder complex.

The coracoid process is a thick, beaklike projection of the scapula that serves as an attachment for several muscles and ligaments. It is located deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle and can be felt by applying deep pressure just below the lateral third of the clavicle. Other landmarks like the acromion or medial clavicle are more superficial or located in different planes, making them incorrect for this specific palpation.

The acromion process forms the tip of the shoulder and is a superficial landmark, unlike the deeper coracoid process. While both are parts of the scapula, the acromion is the lateral extension of the scapular spine. Palpating the acromion does not provide access to the coracoid process due to their distinct anatomical positions.

The supraspinatus fossa is located on the posterior aspect of the scapula, superior to the scapular spine. The coracoid process projects anterolaterally from the anterior aspect of the scapula. Therefore, deep pressure in the supraspinatus fossa would target the supraspinatus muscle rather than the coracoid.

The posterior border of the scapular spine is a superficial landmark easily felt on the back of the shoulder. It leads laterally to the acromion but is entirely separate from the coracoid process. The coracoid process must be accessed from the anterior side of the body through the deltoid muscle.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve palpation at the medial epicondyle
statement: During a routine physical exam, a physician rolls a cord-like structure against the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Which structure is being assessed?
options: Median nerve
Radial nerve
Ulnar nerve
Brachial artery
Basilic vein
correctOption: Ulnar nerve
optionExplanations: The median nerve typically travels in the anterior compartment of the arm and passes through the cubital fossa. It does not have a close anatomical relationship with the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle. Consequently, it cannot be rolled against this specific bony landmark during palpation.

The radial nerve travels in the radial groove on the posterior humerus but then moves laterally to pass anterior to the lateral epicondyle. It is not found near the medial epicondyle. Palpation at the medial epicondyle would never involve the radial nerve.

The medial epicondyle is a prominent bony landmark on the distal humerus. The ulnar nerve passes through the ulnar sulcus on its posterior surface, making it palpable against the bone. Blunt trauma to this area often results in the 'funny bone' sensation due to nerve compression. The radial and median nerves do not have this specific relationship with the medial epicondyle.

The brachial artery is located anteriorly in the arm and cubital fossa, medial to the biceps tendon. It is not found on the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle. Pulse assessment of the brachial artery occurs in the medial bicipital groove or cubital fossa.

The basilic vein is a superficial vein that travels along the medial aspect of the forearm and arm. While it is in the general medial region, it is located in the superficial fascia and is not rolled against the posterior medial epicondyle. It is primarily used for venipuncture rather than nerve assessment.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture).

coreConcept: Anatomic snuffbox boundaries
statement: A medical student is asked to identify the medial boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. Which of the following tendons should they locate?
options: Extensor pollicis brevis
Abductor pollicis longus
Extensor carpi radialis longus
Extensor pollicis longus
Flexor carpi radialis
correctOption: Extensor pollicis longus
optionExplanations: The extensor pollicis brevis tendon forms part of the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox, not the medial boundary. It travels alongside the abductor pollicis longus. Together, these two tendons define the lateral margin of the triangular depression.

The abductor pollicis longus is one of the two tendons forming the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. It is situated most laterally when the thumb is extended. It does not contribute to the medial border of this anatomical space.

The extensor carpi radialis longus tendon passes deep to the snuffbox but does not form its superficial boundaries. It is involved in wrist extension and radial deviation. It is not used as a surface landmark for the snuffbox margins.

The anatomic snuffbox is a depression on the posterolateral wrist. Its medial boundary is the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus, while the lateral boundary consists of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis. This area is clinically significant for palpating the scaphoid bone and the radial artery. Misidentifying these boundaries can lead to incorrect clinical assessment of wrist injuries.

The flexor carpi radialis is an anterior forearm muscle whose tendon is a landmark for the radial pulse at the wrist. It is located on the palmar side of the limb. It has no anatomical relationship with the posterolateral anatomic snuffbox.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis (see Figs. 3.34 and 3.105B, C). Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here.

coreConcept: Brachial artery palpation
statement: Where is the most appropriate location to palpate the brachial artery in the mid-arm region?
options: Lateral to the biceps brachii muscle
Posterior to the triceps brachii muscle
On the brachialis muscle, overlapped laterally by the biceps brachii
In the groove between the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
Superficial to the olecranon process
correctOption: On the brachialis muscle, overlapped laterally by the biceps brachii
optionExplanations: The lateral side of the biceps brachii does not contain the main trunk of the brachial artery. The artery travels along the medial aspect of the arm in the medial bicipital groove. Palpating laterally would likely only encounter the cephalic vein or deltoid fibers.

The posterior compartment of the arm contains the triceps brachii and the profunda brachii artery, not the main brachial artery. The brachial artery is strictly an anterior compartment structure. Palpating posteriorly would not allow for a brachial pulse assessment.

The brachial artery is the main arterial supply to the arm. It can be palpated as it lies on the brachialis muscle, where it is partially overlapped by the biceps brachii. This location is proximal to its bifurcation into the radial and ulnar arteries in the cubital fossa. Palpating it against the humerus is a standard technique for assessing the pulse or measuring blood pressure.

The groove between the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis is the specific site for palpating the radial pulse at the distal radius. This is located in the forearm, not the mid-arm. The brachial artery has already branched into the radial and ulnar arteries by this level.

The olecranon process is a bony landmark of the ulna at the elbow. No major artery is palpated superficially over this bone. The ulnar nerve is nearby, but it is posterior to the medial epicondyle, not the olecranon itself.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Physiological basis of Allen's test
statement: During the performance of Allen's test, why does the patient's palm initially appear white (pallor) after opening the clenched fist while the clinician maintains pressure on both the radial and ulnar arteries?
options: Venous congestion prevents arterial inflow
The clenching of the fist and arterial compression have emptied the palmar arches of blood
The sympathetic nervous system causes localized vasodilation
The radial artery provides the sole supply to the superficial palmar arch
The ulnar artery is naturally occluded by the flexor retinaculum during fist clenching
correctOption: The clenching of the fist and arterial compression have emptied the palmar arches of blood
optionExplanations: Venous congestion would typically cause cyanosis or redness (rubor) rather than pallor. Pallor indicates a lack of blood flow into the capillary beds. In Allen's test, the goal is to demonstrate the absence of arterial blood before testing collateral flow.

Allen's test assesses the collateral circulation of the hand. Clenching the fist forces blood out of the palmar arches, while compressing the radial and ulnar arteries prevents refill. Upon opening the hand, the initial pallor (white skin) confirms that the arterial supply is successfully occluded. The subsequent return of color when one artery is released demonstrates the patency of that specific vessel.

Sympathetic activation typically causes vasoconstriction, not vasodilation, in peripheral vessels. However, the pallor in Allen's test is a mechanical result of compression and fist clenching, not a neural reflex. Vasodilation would cause the palm to turn red more quickly.

The superficial palmar arch is primarily supplied by the ulnar artery, though the radial artery often contributes. Allen's test specifically proves that the hand has a dual supply. If the radial artery were the sole supply, releasing the ulnar artery would not result in the return of color.

The flexor retinaculum does not occlude the ulnar artery during normal fist clenching; the artery and nerve pass superficial to it in Guyon's canal. The occlusion during the test is performed manually by the clinician's fingers. The fist clenching serves to squeeze blood out of the small vessels of the palm.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches. When the patient is asked to open the hands, the skin of the palms is at first white.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the elbow in flexion
statement: A clinician is assessing a patient for a suspected elbow dislocation. Which of the following configurations of the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and olecranon process is considered normal when the elbow is flexed?
options: A straight horizontal line
A vertical line along the axis of the humerus
An equilateral triangle
A right-angled triangle with the apex at the lateral epicondyle
A T-shaped configuration
correctOption: An equilateral triangle
optionExplanations: A straight horizontal line is the normal relationship of these three bony landmarks only when the elbow is in full extension. If this configuration persists during flexion, it suggests a fracture or dislocation. The movement of the olecranon during flexion normally breaks this linear alignment.

The bony landmarks of the elbow never form a vertical line along the humeral axis in any normal physiological position. The epicondyles are fixed on the distal humerus, while the olecranon moves in the sagittal plane. This description does not match standard surface anatomy.

The relationship between the epicondyles and the olecranon changes with elbow position. In full extension, these three bony landmarks align in a straight horizontal line. When the elbow is flexed to 90 degrees, they shift to form the vertices of an equilateral triangle. This change is a key diagnostic feature used to assess the integrity of the elbow joint and detect dislocations or fractures.

While the landmarks form a triangle, it is specifically described as an equilateral triangle in standard anatomical texts. A right-angled triangle would imply an asymmetrical displacement of one of the epicondyles or the olecranon. Such a finding would be highly suspicious for pathology.

A T-shaped configuration is not a recognized anatomical description for the relationship between the epicondyles and the olecranon. This term does not accurately reflect the geometric shift that occurs during elbow flexion. The triangle description is the clinical standard for physical examination.
citation: When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle. The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon and the pulley-shaped trochlea.

coreConcept: Palpation of the third part of the axillary artery
