statement: To verify the position of the axillary sheath, a clinician palpates the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. Against which muscle is this part of the artery felt?
options: Pectoralis minor
Coracobrachialis
Teres major
Subscapularis
Serratus anterior
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor muscle is the landmark used to divide the axillary artery into three parts. The second part of the artery lies directly posterior to the pectoralis minor. The third part, which is being palpated, is located distal to the lower border of this muscle.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery in the axilla. While it is a useful landmark for the neurovascular bundle, the third part of the artery is specifically described as lying in front of the teres major. Palpating against the coracobrachialis would not be the standard technique for this pulse.

The third part of the axillary artery is the segment located distal to the pectoralis minor muscle. It can be palpated within the axilla, specifically where it lies anterior to the teres major muscle. This site is useful for verifying the position of the axillary sheath during regional anesthesia. Other parts of the artery are deeper or covered by the pectoralis major, making them less accessible for direct palpation.

The subscapularis muscle forms the majority of the posterior wall of the axilla. While the axillary artery passes anterior to it, the specific clinical landmark for palpating the third part is the teres major muscle, which forms the inferior border of the posterior wall. The subscapularis is located more superiorly.

The serratus anterior muscle forms the medial wall of the axilla. The axillary artery travels along the lateral wall/apex area, not against the medial wall. Palpating against the serratus anterior would likely encounter the long thoracic nerve rather than the axillary artery pulse.
citation: The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. ... The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis.

coreConcept: Palpation of pectoral axillary lymph nodes
statement: Which of the following maneuvers is correct for palpating the anterior (pectoral) group of axillary lymph nodes?
options: Pressing laterally against the humerus
Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle
Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Pressing medially against the upper ribs
Pressing superiorly toward the apex of the axilla
correctOption: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
optionExplanations: Pressing laterally against the humerus is the technique used to palpate the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes, which lie along the axillary vein. This group receives drainage from most of the upper limb. It is not the correct maneuver for the pectoral nodes.

Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle is the technique for palpating the posterior (subscapular) lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the subscapular vessels on the posterior axillary wall. They primarily drain the posterior thoracic wall and scapular region.

Pectoral lymph nodes are part of the axillary lymphatic system and receive drainage from the breast. They are located along the inferior border of the pectoralis major muscle. To palpate them, the examiner must press forward against the posterior surface of this muscle on the anterior axillary wall. This technique ensures the nodes are compressed against a firm surface for easier detection of enlargement.

Pressing medially against the upper ribs/serratus anterior is not the standard technique for any specific axillary node group. While the central nodes are in the middle of the axilla, they are usually palpated by reaching high and pressing against the chest wall, but the pectoral nodes specifically require the anterior wall maneuver.

Pressing superiorly toward the apex of the axilla is the technique used to identify the apical lymph nodes. This requires the patient to relax their shoulder muscles so the examiner can reach the outer border of the first rib. It is distinct from the palpation of the more superficial pectoral nodes.
citation: The anterior (pectoral) nodes may be palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall of the axilla. The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein.

coreConcept: Clinical significance of the anatomic snuffbox
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls on an outstretched hand and presents with localized pain and deep tenderness in the triangular depression at the base of the thumb. Which bone is most likely injured?
options: Lunate
Pisiform
Scaphoid
Trapezium
Hamate
correctOption: Scaphoid
optionExplanations: The lunate is located in the center of the proximal carpal row and is more commonly associated with dislocations than fractures. Tenderness from a lunate injury is typically felt in the center of the wrist, not in the anatomic snuffbox. It does not form the floor of the snuffbox.

The pisiform is a sesamoid bone located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris. It is easily palpable on the palmar surface. It is anatomically distant from the anatomic snuffbox on the radial side.

The scaphoid is the most commonly fractured carpal bone, often resulting from a fall on an outstretched hand. Because it forms the floor of the anatomic snuffbox, localized tenderness in this depression is a hallmark clinical sign. Failure to recognize this can lead to complications like avascular necrosis due to the bone's retrograde blood supply. Other carpal bones like the lunate or triquetrum are not primarily palpated in this specific triangular space.

The trapezium is located distal to the scaphoid and forms the base of the first metacarpal joint. While it is on the radial side, the 'deep tenderness' in the snuffbox specifically points to the scaphoid, which lies more proximally in the floor of that space. Trapezium fractures are significantly less common.

The hamate is located on the ulnar side of the distal carpal row. It is known for its hook, which can be fractured by direct impact (e.g., swinging a golf club). It has no relationship with the anatomic snuffbox, which is a radial-sided landmark.
citation: Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid. ... Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here (see Fig. 3.105B).

coreConcept: Ulnar artery palpation at the wrist
statement: When palpating the ulnar pulse at the wrist, what is the relationship of the ulnar artery to the pisiform bone and the ulnar nerve?
options: The artery is medial to the pisiform bone
The artery is lateral to the pisiform and lateral to the ulnar nerve
The artery is deep to the flexor retinaculum
The artery is medial to the ulnar nerve
The artery is posterior to the pisiform bone
correctOption: The artery is lateral to the pisiform and lateral to the ulnar nerve
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery is not medial to the pisiform; the pisiform is the most medial structure in this region of the wrist. The artery must be lateral to the bone to enter the palm via Guyon's canal. Palpating medially would only encounter the medial edge of the hand.

The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum. At this point, it is positioned just lateral to the pisiform bone, which serves as a protective bony landmark. The ulnar nerve lies medial to the artery, separating it from the pisiform itself. This superficial location makes the ulnar pulse easily accessible for clinical evaluation of hand circulation.

Unlike the structures in the carpal tunnel, the ulnar artery and nerve pass superficial to the flexor retinaculum. They are contained within a separate fibro-osseous space called the ulnar canal (Guyon's canal). This superficial position is why the pulse is palpable at the wrist.

The ulnar nerve is actually the structure located between the ulnar artery and the pisiform bone. Therefore, the artery is lateral to the nerve. Reversing this relationship would be anatomically incorrect and could lead to confusion during surgical procedures or nerve blocks.

The ulnar artery passes anterior (superficial) to the plane of the carpal bones as it crosses the wrist. It does not travel posterior to the pisiform. Its superficial path is what allows it to be covered only by skin and fascia at the palpation site.
citation: The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site.

coreConcept: Coracoid process palpation
statement: A clinician is performing a surface anatomy examination of the shoulder. To palpate the coracoid process, where should deep pressure be applied?
options: Superior to the medial third of the clavicle
Inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle through the deltoid
Directly over the acromioclavicular joint
Posterior to the spine of the scapula
Medial to the bicipital groove on the humerus
correctOption: Inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle through the deltoid
optionExplanations: The medial third of the clavicle is associated with the sternoclavicular joint and the attachment of the sternocleidomastoid, not the coracoid process. Palpation here would not reach any scapular projections. The coracoid process is located much further laterally and inferiorly.

The coracoid process is a beaklike projection of the scapula that serves as an attachment for the short head of the biceps and coracobrachialis. It is situated deep to the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle, just below the lateral aspect of the clavicle. Deep pressure in this specific region allows the clinician to identify its firm, bony resistance.

The acromioclavicular joint is located at the very lateral tip of the clavicle where it meets the acromion. While it is a palpable landmark, it is superior and lateral to the coracoid process. This joint is often assessed for separation rather than for scapular beak projections.

The spine of the scapula is a posterior feature that leads to the acromion. The coracoid process projects anterolaterally from the anterior aspect of the scapular neck. Therefore, it cannot be palpated from a posterior approach through the scapular spine.

The bicipital groove is a feature of the humerus that houses the long head of the biceps tendon. While the coracoid process is nearby, it is a scapular landmark, not a humeral one. Palpating medial to the groove would likely involve the lesser tubercle of the humerus instead.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve palpation at the medial epicondyle
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the medial one and a half fingers after hitting their elbow. During physical examination, the clinician attempts to palpate the nerve responsible. Where is this nerve most easily rolled against a bony landmark?
options: Anterior to the lateral epicondyle of the humerus
Posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
Within the bicipital groove of the humerus
Superficial to the olecranon process
Lateral to the radial tuberosity
correctOption: Posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is the origin for the extensor muscles of the forearm and is associated with the radial nerve, not the ulnar nerve. The radial nerve passes anterior to the lateral epicondyle as it enters the forearm. Injury here would affect the lateral aspect of the hand.

The ulnar nerve travels in the ulnar sulcus (groove) on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. This location makes the nerve highly superficial and vulnerable to compression or 'funny bone' sensations. It can be easily palpated and rolled against the bone by a clinician in this specific groove.

The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) is located on the proximal humerus and contains the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii. It does not house the ulnar nerve. Palpation here is used to assess for bicipital tendonitis rather than ulnar nerve entrapment.

The olecranon process is the proximal end of the ulna and forms the point of the elbow. While the ulnar nerve is nearby, it is specifically located in the groove between the olecranon and the medial epicondyle. The nerve is not superficial to the olecranon itself, which is covered by a bursa.

The radial tuberosity is a landmark on the radius for the insertion of the biceps brachii muscle. It is located deep in the cubital fossa and is not a site for ulnar nerve palpation. The ulnar nerve remains on the medial side of the forearm, far from the radial tuberosity.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture).

coreConcept: Posterior border of the ulna palpability
statement: During a physical examination of the forearm, which bony feature can be palpated subcutaneously along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: The anterior border of the radius
The lateral border of the humerus
The posterior border of the ulna
The medial border of the radius
The anterior border of the ulna
correctOption: The posterior border of the ulna
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the radius is covered by the thick muscle bellies of the flexor compartment, such as the flexor pollicis longus. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be easily palpated along its length. Only the distal end of the radius is easily accessible near the wrist.

The lateral border of the humerus is covered by the deltoid and brachioradialis muscles. While the epicondyles are palpable, the entire length of the humeral shaft is not subcutaneous. Deep palpation is required to feel the bone through the muscle layers.

The posterior border of the ulna is unique because it is not covered by muscles throughout its length. It lies directly beneath the skin, extending from the olecranon to the styloid process. This makes it a critical landmark for identifying forearm fractures or performing surgical approaches.

The medial border of the radius faces the ulna and serves as the attachment for the interosseous membrane. It is located deep within the forearm and is completely inaccessible to surface palpation. It is surrounded by the deep muscles of the forearm.

The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor digitorum profundus and other flexor muscles. Unlike the posterior border, it is not subcutaneous. Palpating the anterior aspect of the forearm reveals soft tissue rather than a continuous bony ridge.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Scaphoid palpation in the anatomic snuffbox
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls on an outstretched hand and complains of wrist pain. The clinician notes deep tenderness in the triangular depression on the posterolateral wrist. Which bone is most likely injured based on this surface landmark?
options: Lunate
Pisiform
Scaphoid
Trapezium
Triquetrum
correctOption: Scaphoid
optionExplanations: The lunate is located in the center of the proximal carpal row and is not found within the anatomic snuffbox. Tenderness of the lunate is usually felt on the mid-dorsum of the wrist. It is more commonly associated with dislocations than fractures from a fall.

The pisiform is a sesamoid bone located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris. It is nowhere near the anatomic snuffbox, which is a lateral (radial) structure. It is palpated on the anterior-medial aspect of the wrist.

The scaphoid forms the floor of the anatomic snuffbox, making it the most accessible carpal bone for palpation in this region. Tenderness here after a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH) is a classic clinical sign of a scaphoid fracture. Due to its retrograde blood supply, identifying this injury is vital to prevent avascular necrosis.

The trapezium is located distal to the scaphoid and forms the base of the first metacarpal. While it is near the snuffbox, the scaphoid is the primary bone forming the floor where tenderness is most diagnostic. The trapezium is usually palpated more distally during thumb movements.

The triquetrum is located on the medial side of the wrist, proximal to the hamate and distal to the articular disc of the radioulnar joint. It is an ulnar-sided bone and is not related to the anatomic snuffbox. Injuries to the triquetrum would present with medial wrist pain.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis. Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here.

coreConcept: Nipple retraction mechanism in carcinoma
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a clinician observes that one nipple is pulled inward. What is the primary anatomical mechanism by which an underlying carcinoma causes this retraction?
options: Invasion of the pectoralis major muscle
Tension placed on the lactiferous ducts
Compression of the retromammary space
Obstruction of the axillary lymph nodes
Atrophy of the suspensory ligaments of Cooper
correctOption: Tension placed on the lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: Invasion of the pectoralis major muscle by a tumor typically causes the breast to become fixed to the chest wall. While this is a sign of advanced cancer, it causes immobility of the breast mass rather than specific retraction of the nipple. Nipple changes are more related to internal ductal structures.

The lactiferous ducts converge at the nipple to allow for the passage of milk. When a malignant tumor grows within the breast tissue, it can involve these ducts and cause fibrosis or shortening. This mechanical pulling on the ducts results in the characteristic inward retraction of the nipple seen on inspection.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue between the breast and the pectoral fascia. Compression or obliteration of this space might occur with a large posterior tumor, but it does not directly pull the nipple inward. Its primary clinical relevance is allowing the breast to move freely over the chest wall.

Obstruction of axillary lymph nodes leads to lymphedema of the breast tissue, which can cause 'peau d'orange' (skin thickening and pitting). It does not cause focal retraction of the nipple. Lymph node involvement is a staging factor rather than a direct cause of nipple inversion.

The suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) connect the dermis to the deep fascia. If a tumor pulls on these ligaments, it causes skin dimpling, not nipple retraction. Nipple retraction specifically requires involvement of the ductal system that terminates at the nipple itself.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Cephalic vein location at the wrist
statement: In an emergency situation where superficial veins are not visible due to shock, where can the cephalic vein be reliably located for venipuncture at the wrist?
options: Directly over the pisiform bone
Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Medial to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
Anterior to the medial epicondyle
Deep to the bicipital aponeurosis
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The pisiform bone is on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist. The cephalic vein is a lateral (radial) structure, so it would not be found near the pisiform. The ulnar artery and nerve are the primary structures of interest near the pisiform.

The cephalic vein begins in the dorsal venous network of the hand and winds around the lateral aspect of the wrist. It consistently passes through the superficial fascia just posterior to the radial styloid process. This anatomical constancy makes it a 'lifeline' site for venous access when other veins have collapsed.

The area medial to the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is the territory of the ulnar neurovascular bundle. The cephalic vein is located on the opposite (lateral) side of the limb. Palpating or attempting venipuncture here would risk hitting the ulnar nerve or artery.

The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is the site of the basilic vein and the origin of the flexor muscles. While the basilic vein is used for catheterization, the question specifically asks for the cephalic vein's location at the wrist. The medial epicondyle is at the elbow, not the wrist.

The bicipital aponeurosis is located in the cubital fossa (elbow region), where it protects the brachial artery from the median cubital vein. It is not a structure found at the wrist. The cephalic vein at the wrist is superficial to all deep fascial structures.
citation: The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization. Every clinical professional, in an emergency, should know where to obtain blood from the arm. When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

coreConcept: Lateral epicondyle anatomical position
statement: When identifying landmarks on the distal humerus, the lateral epicondyle is found immediately proximal to which articular feature?
options: Trochlea
Coronoid fossa
Capitulum
Olecranon fossa
Radial tuberosity
correctOption: Capitulum
optionExplanations: The trochlea is the pulley-shaped medial articular surface of the humerus that articulates with the ulna. It is located distal to the medial epicondyle, not the lateral one. The lateral epicondyle is associated with the radial side of the joint.

The coronoid fossa is a depression on the anterior surface of the humerus, superior to the trochlea, that receives the coronoid process of the ulna during flexion. It is a medial structure and is not the articular surface immediately distal to the lateral epicondyle.

The capitulum is the rounded, lateral articular surface of the humerus that articulates with the head of the radius. The lateral epicondyle sits just proximal to this structure on the lateral side of the humerus. This relationship is a key feature in both surface anatomy and radiographic interpretation of the elbow.

The olecranon fossa is a large deep pit on the posterior surface of the humerus that accommodates the olecranon of the ulna during extension. While it is a distal humeral feature, it is a posterior midline structure rather than a lateral articular surface associated with the lateral epicondyle.

The radial tuberosity is a landmark on the radius bone itself, serving as the insertion point for the biceps brachii. It is not part of the humerus. The question asks for a feature on the distal humerus that is distal to the lateral epicondyle.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea.

coreConcept: Dermatomes of the medial margin of the upper limb
statement: A clinician is testing sensory perception along the medial margin of the upper limb. Which spinal cord segments are primarily responsible for the dermatomes in this region?
options: C3, C4, and C5
C5 and C6
C7 only
C8, T1, and T2
T3 and T4
correctOption: C8, T1, and T2
optionExplanations: C3, C4, and C5 dermatomes are located much higher, covering the base of the neck and the lateral aspect of the upper arm (shoulder area). They do not extend to the medial margin of the distal limb. These segments are tested near the clavicle and acromion.

C5 and C6 dermatomes are situated along the lateral margin of the upper limb, including the thumb and lateral forearm. They are the counterparts to the medial dermatomes. Testing these involves the lateral aspect of the arm and the first digit.

The C7 dermatome is specifically localized to the middle finger and the central portion of the posterior hand and forearm. It acts as a transition zone between the lateral (C5-C6) and medial (C8-T1) dermatomes. It does not cover the medial margin of the limb.

The medial margin of the upper limb, including the little finger and the medial side of the forearm and arm, is supplied by the C8, T1, and T2 spinal segments. C8 covers the medial hand and little finger, while T1 and T2 cover the medial forearm and arm respectively. This is a standard area for testing lower brachial plexus integrity.

T3 and T4 dermatomes are located on the anterior and posterior trunk, with T4 typically at the level of the nipples. While T2 extends into the axilla and medial arm, T3 and T4 do not contribute significantly to the upper limb's sensory margins. They are primarily thoracic wall segments.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Elbow bony relations in flexion
statement: A clinician is assessing a patient for a possible elbow dislocation. When the elbow is placed in a flexed position, what is the normal topographical relationship between the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the olecranon?
options: They form a straight horizontal line
They form a straight vertical line
They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
The olecranon moves superior to both epicondyles
The epicondyles overlap in a cross-sectional plane
correctOption: They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
optionExplanations: A straight horizontal line is the normal relationship of these three landmarks only when the elbow is in full extension. In this position, the olecranon sits in the olecranon fossa between the two epicondyles. Flexion changes this alignment significantly.

The landmarks never form a straight vertical line in any standard anatomical position. The epicondyles are always medial and lateral to each other. A vertical alignment would suggest a severe, non-anatomical deformity or fracture.

In the flexed elbow, the olecranon process moves distally and anteriorly relative to the humeral shaft, while the epicondyles remain fixed. This displacement results in the three points forming an equilateral triangle. Disruption of this triangle is a key clinical sign of dislocation or supracondylar fracture.

The olecranon does not move superior to the epicondyles during flexion; rather, it moves away from the humerus as the ulna rotates around the trochlea. In a flexed position, the olecranon forms the apex of a triangle pointing distally. Superior movement would be highly abnormal.

The epicondyles are on opposite sides of the distal humerus and do not overlap. In a cross-sectional model, they would be seen as distinct medial and lateral projections. Their relative positions are used to orient the humerus in imaging and physical exams.
citation: When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Palpation of posterior (subscapular) axillary lymph nodes
statement: To palpate the posterior (subscapular) group of axillary lymph nodes, in which direction and against which structure should the examiner apply pressure?
options: Forward against the pectoralis major muscle
Laterally against the humerus
Backward against the subscapularis muscle
Medially against the rib cage
Superiorly toward the apex of the axilla
correctOption: Backward against the subscapularis muscle
optionExplanations: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major is the technique used to palpate the anterior (pectoral) lymph nodes. These nodes drain the anterior chest wall and the lateral breast. They are located on the anterior wall of the axilla.

Pressing laterally against the humerus or the axillary vein is the technique used to palpate the lateral axillary nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. They are located along the lateral wall of the axilla.

The posterior (subscapular) nodes are located along the subscapular vessels on the posterior wall of the axilla. To feel them, the clinician must reach into the axillary fold and press backward against the subscapularis muscle. This group drains the posterior thoracic wall and the scapular region.

Pressing medially against the rib cage (serratus anterior muscle) is used to palpate the central nodes or to assess the medial wall of the axilla. The subscapular nodes are specifically associated with the posterior wall, not the medial wall. The medial wall contains the long thoracic nerve.

Superior pressure toward the apex of the axilla is used to palpate the apical nodes, which are located at the very top of the axillary space near the first rib. This requires the patient to relax their shoulder completely. It is not the specific maneuver for the subscapular group.
citation: The posterior (subscapular) nodes may be palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein.

coreConcept: Coracoid process palpation
statement: A clinician is attempting to palpate the coracoid process of the scapula during a physical examination. Which of the following techniques is most appropriate to identify this landmark?
options: Applying superficial pressure over the medial third of the clavicle
Applying deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle
Palpating the posterior aspect of the acromion process
Applying deep pressure in the center of the axillary vault
Palpating the superior border of the scapula near the vertebral column
correctOption: Applying deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a deep, beaklike projection of the scapula located inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle. Because it is covered by the thick fibers of the anterior deltoid, it cannot be felt with superficial touch and requires deep pressure to be identified. This landmark is essential for localizing the origin of the short head of the biceps and the coracobrachialis muscles.
Superficial pressure over the medial clavicle would identify the sternoclavicular joint or the clavicular head of the pectoralis major, not the coracoid.
The posterior acromion is a separate landmark forming the tip of the shoulder and is not related to the anterolateral projection of the coracoid.
Deep pressure in the axillary vault is used to palpate central lymph nodes rather than the bony coracoid process.
The superior border of the scapula near the vertebral column is covered by the trapezius and is not the location of the coracoid process.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve palpation at the medial epicondyle
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the small finger after hitting their elbow against a table. To assess the integrity of the nerve most likely involved, where should the clinician attempt to palpate and roll the nerve against a bony landmark?
options: Anterior to the lateral epicondyle
Posterior to the medial epicondyle
Within the bicipital groove of the humerus
Directly over the olecranon process
Lateral to the tendon of the biceps brachii
correctOption: Posterior to the medial epicondyle
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve is uniquely accessible for palpation as it passes through the ulnar sulcus on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle. In this location, the nerve is superficial and can be rolled against the bone, which often produces a characteristic tingling sensation. This site is also a common point of entrapment or injury due to its exposed position.
The lateral epicondyle is the origin of the extensor muscles and is not associated with the path of the ulnar nerve.
The bicipital groove (intertubercular sulcus) contains the long head of the biceps tendon and the ascending branch of the anterior circumflex humeral artery, not the ulnar nerve.
The olecranon process is the point of the elbow and serves as the insertion for the triceps, but the ulnar nerve lies medial to it.
The area lateral to the biceps tendon in the cubital fossa is where the radial nerve or its branches are located, not the ulnar nerve.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture). The nerve can be palpated and rolled against the epicondyle.

coreConcept: Posterior border of the ulna palpation
statement: During a forearm examination, which specific part of the ulna can be easily palpated along its entire length because it is subcutaneous?
options: The anterior border of the shaft
The lateral border of the shaft
The posterior aspect of the body
The coronoid process
The interosseous border
correctOption: The posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The posterior aspect of the ulnar body is subcutaneous from the olecranon to the styloid process, making it easily palpable throughout its length. This feature is clinically useful for identifying fractures or assessing bony alignment without the interference of thick muscle layers. Most other borders of the ulna are deeply buried under the forearm flexors or extensors.
The anterior border of the ulnar shaft is covered by the flexor digitorum profundus and pronator quadratus, preventing direct palpation.
The lateral border of the ulna serves as the attachment for the interosseous membrane and is situated deep between the radius and ulna.
The coronoid process is a deep projection on the proximal ulna that is covered by the brachialis muscle and cannot be palpated along the shaft.
The interosseous border is the sharp lateral edge of the bone where the interosseous membrane attaches, making it inaccessible to surface palpation.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Lateral axillary lymph node palpation
statement: When performing a clinical examination of the axillary lymph nodes, how should the examiner position their fingers to specifically palpate the lateral group of nodes?
options: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis
Pressing laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein
Pressing medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
Pressing superiorly toward the outer border of the first rib
correctOption: Pressing laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein
optionExplanations: The lateral axillary lymph nodes are located along the medial side of the axillary vein and receive the majority of the lymphatic drainage from the upper limb. To palpate them, the examiner must press their fingers laterally against the neurovascular bundle and the humerus. This group is distinct from the pectoral and subscapular groups which are associated with the axillary walls.
Pressing forward against the pectoralis major targets the anterior (pectoral) lymph nodes, which drain the breast and anterior thoracic wall.
Pressing backward against the subscapularis targets the posterior (subscapular) lymph nodes located on the posterior axillary wall.
Pressing medially against the ribs would target the medial wall of the axilla, but the lateral nodes are specifically associated with the axillary vein on the lateral aspect.
Pressing superiorly toward the first rib is the technique used to identify the apical lymph nodes, which represent the final common pathway for axillary drainage.
citation: The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery. The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall).

coreConcept: Central axillary lymph node palpation
statement: Which of the following best describes the anatomical location for palpating the central group of axillary lymph nodes?
options: Deep to the pectoralis minor near the coracoid process
In the center of the axilla between the anterior and posterior walls
Along the lateral border of the scapula
In the root of the neck posterior to the clavicle
Directly beneath the skin of the floor of the axilla
correctOption: In the center of the axilla between the anterior and posterior walls
optionExplanations: The central axillary lymph nodes are situated deep within the axillary fat, positioned between the pectoralis major (forming the anterior wall) and the subscapularis (forming the posterior wall). They are often the most easily palpable nodes if enlarged because they receive drainage from the lateral, pectoral, and subscapular groups. Palpation requires the examiner to reach high into the center of the axillary space.
The nodes near the coracoid process are typically part of the apical or infraclavicular groups, not the central group.
The lateral border of the scapula is the site for the subscapular (posterior) nodes, which are distinct from the central nodes.
Nodes in the root of the neck are supraclavicular nodes, which are separate from the axillary lymph node classification.
While the central nodes are in the axillary vault, they are deep to the axillary fascia and fat, not directly beneath the skin of the axillary floor.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side.

coreConcept: Medial epicondyle orientation
statement: In the standard anatomic position, what is the normal orientation of the medial epicondyle of the humerus relative to the humeral shaft?
options: Directed medially and anteriorly
Directed medially and posteriorly
Directed directly laterally
Directed directly anteriorly
Directed laterally and posteriorly
correctOption: Directed medially and posteriorly
optionExplanations: In the anatomic position, the medial epicondyle is not a simple medial projection; it is oriented medially and slightly posteriorly. This orientation is significant because it aligns with the direction of the head of the humerus, reflecting the natural internal torsion of the humeral shaft. Understanding this posterior tilt is crucial for the correct interpretation of elbow radiographs and clinical assessment of alignment.
An anterior orientation would be incorrect, as the epicondyle tilts backward to match the humeral head's retroversion.
The medial epicondyle is, by definition, on the medial side of the humerus, so a lateral direction is anatomically impossible.
A purely anterior direction would ignore the posterior tilt that is characteristic of the distal humerus in the anatomic position.
The epicondyle is located on the medial side of the distal humerus, making any lateral orientation incorrect.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Clavicle palpability
statement: Which of the following statements regarding the surface anatomy of the clavicle is correct?
options: It is only palpable at its medial and lateral extremities
It is entirely subcutaneous and palpable along its whole length
It is covered by the subclavius muscle throughout its length
It can only be palpated when the arm is fully abducted
It is palpable only in the posterior triangle of the neck
correctOption: It is entirely subcutaneous and palpable along its whole length
optionExplanations: The clavicle is a subcutaneous bone that lies just beneath the skin and fascia, allowing it to be easily palpated from the sternoclavicular joint to the acromioclavicular joint. This accessibility makes it a primary landmark for clinical procedures and a common site for identifying fractures. Unlike many other bones, it lacks thick muscular coverage on its anterior and superior surfaces.
The clavicle is not limited to palpation at its ends; the entire shaft is accessible due to its subcutaneous position.
While the subclavius muscle is located inferior to the clavicle, it does not cover the bone's superior or anterior surfaces, which remain palpable.
Palpation of the clavicle does not require arm abduction, as it is easily felt in the neutral anatomic position.
The clavicle forms the base of the posterior triangle, but its palpability extends across the entire anterior chest wall to the shoulder.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

coreConcept: Breast asymmetry
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a clinician notes a slight difference in size between the left and right breasts. What is the most common cause of this finding in a healthy individual?
options: Early-stage ductal carcinoma
Chronic mastitis
Unequal breast development
Obstruction of the lactiferous ducts
Atrophy of the pectoralis major
correctOption: Unequal breast development
optionExplanations: A degree of breast asymmetry is a normal finding in many women and is typically the result of unequal development of the mammary glands during puberty. Clinicians must recognize this as a common variation to avoid unnecessary alarm, while still remaining vigilant for pathological changes. Asymmetry is only concerning if it is a new development or associated with other clinical signs like masses or skin changes.
While carcinoma can cause swelling and change the shape of the breast, it is not the most common cause of baseline asymmetry in the general population.
Chronic mastitis usually presents with pain, redness, and localized changes rather than simple developmental asymmetry.
Ductal obstruction typically leads to localized swelling or cysts rather than a generalized difference in breast size.
Pectoralis major atrophy would affect the chest wall contour but is not a standard cause of developmental breast asymmetry.
citation: With the patient undressed to the waist and sitting upright, first inspect the breasts for symmetry. Some degree of asymmetry is common and is the result of unequal breast development. Any swelling should be noted. An underlying tumor, a cyst, or abscess formation can cause a swelling.

coreConcept: Breast carcinoma tethering
statement: A patient is asked to raise both arms above her head during a breast examination. The clinician observes a small area of skin dimpling on the lateral aspect of the right breast. This sign is most likely caused by a tumor tethered to which of the following structures?
options: The pectoralis minor muscle
The suspensory ligaments
The serratus anterior fascia
The retromammary space
The deep pectoral fascia
correctOption: The suspensory ligaments
optionExplanations: Skin dimpling occurs when a breast carcinoma invades and contracts the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) that connect the dermis to the deep fascia. When the patient raises their arms, these tethered ligaments pull on the skin, creating a visible depression. This maneuver is a critical part of the physical exam for detecting non-palpable or deep-seated malignancies.
The pectoralis minor is deep to the breast and pectoralis major; while a tumor can invade it, skin dimpling is specifically mediated by the suspensory ligaments.
The serratus anterior is located on the lateral thoracic wall, and while it forms part of the breast bed, it is not the primary structure responsible for skin dimpling.

The retromammary space is a plane of loose connective tissue that normally allows the breast to move freely; obliteration of this space by a tumor limits mobility but does not directly cause skin dimpling.

Tethering to the deep pectoral fascia can cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, but the specific sign of skin dimpling is caused by the pull on the suspensory ligaments reaching the dermis.
citation: Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple.

coreConcept: Dermatomes C3-C6
statement: A clinician is testing the sensory integrity of the C3 to C6 spinal segments. In which region of the upper limb should the clinician apply stimuli to test these specific dermatomes?
options: Along the medial margin of the limb
On the middle finger
Along the lateral margin of the limb
On the palmar surface of the small finger
Over the olecranon process
correctOption: Along the lateral margin of the limb
optionExplanations: The dermatomes for the C3 through C6 spinal segments are located along the lateral (pre-axial) margin of the upper limb. This distribution follows the developmental pattern of the limb buds and is essential for localizing nerve root injuries. Testing these areas helps differentiate between high cervical and lower cervical or thoracic nerve involvement.
The medial margin of the limb is supplied by the C8, T1, and T2 dermatomes, representing the post-axial border.
The middle finger is the classic landmark for the C7 dermatome, which sits between the lateral and medial groups.
The small finger is primarily supplied by the C8 dermatome, which is part of the medial margin group.
The skin over the olecranon process is typically associated with the C7 or C8 dermatomes, depending on the specific map used, but it is not part of the C3-C6 lateral margin group.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Palpation of the acromion as the tip of the shoulder
statement: During a physical examination of the shoulder, which bony landmark is identified as the broad, flat lateral extension of the scapular spine that forms the palpable tip of the shoulder?
options: Coracoid process
Acromion
Greater tuberosity
Lesser tuberosity
Sternal end of the clavicle
correctOption: Acromion
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is a beaklike structure located inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle and requires deep palpation through the deltoid muscle. It does not form the lateral tip of the shoulder but rather projects anterolaterally from the scapular neck. It serves as an attachment point for muscles like the short head of the biceps brachii.

The acromion is the lateral-most extension of the scapular spine and is easily palpable as the bony point of the shoulder. It articulates with the clavicle at the acromioclavicular joint and provides a site for the origin of the deltoid muscle. Its subcutaneous position makes it a primary landmark for shoulder assessments.

The greater tuberosity of the humerus is located lateral to the acromion but is typically covered by the thick deltoid muscle. It only becomes easily palpable if the deltoid muscle undergoes significant wasting or atrophy. It serves as the insertion site for several rotator cuff muscles.

The lesser tuberosity is located on the anterior aspect of the proximal humerus, medial to the bicipital groove. It is not the lateral tip of the shoulder and is generally difficult to palpate through the overlying musculature. It provides the insertion point for the subscapularis muscle.

The sternal end of the clavicle is located medially at the root of the neck where it articulates with the manubrium of the sternum. It is far removed from the lateral tip of the shoulder. This landmark is easily palpable but is part of the sternoclavicular joint.
citation: The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder. The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula.

coreConcept: Palpation of the lateral epicondyle and its relation to the capitulum
statement: A clinician is palpating the distal humerus to identify landmarks for an injection. Which of the following correctly describes the lateral epicondyle?
options: A large, knoblike projection proximal to the trochlea
A small, roughened projection proximal to the capitulum
The proximal end of the ulna forming the point of the elbow
A subcutaneous bone palpable only during elbow flexion
A projection located distal to the radial head
correctOption: A small, roughened projection proximal to the capitulum
optionExplanations: The large, knoblike projection proximal to the trochlea is the medial epicondyle, not the lateral epicondyle. The medial epicondyle is more prominent and serves as the origin for the common flexor tendon. It also has a close relationship with the ulnar nerve on its posterior surface.

The lateral epicondyle is a smaller, roughened projection located on the lateral side of the distal humerus, immediately proximal to the capitulum. It is easily palpable and serves as the common origin for the extensor muscles of the forearm. Its position is critical for diagnosing conditions like lateral epicondylitis.

The proximal end of the ulna that forms the point of the elbow is the olecranon process, not a humeral epicondyle. The olecranon fits into the olecranon fossa of the humerus during elbow extension. It is a distinct bony landmark from the humeral epicondyles.

The lateral epicondyle is subcutaneous and palpable in both flexion and extension of the elbow joint. It does not disappear or become hidden during specific joint movements. Its relationship with the olecranon changes from a straight line to a triangle during flexion.

The lateral epicondyle is part of the humerus and is therefore proximal to the radial head, not distal. The radial head articulates with the capitulum, which is just distal to the lateral epicondyle. Palpating distal to the radial head would lead to the shaft of the radius.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm.

coreConcept: Subcutaneous nature of the posterior border of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is described as being rounded, subcutaneous, and easily palpable along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: Anterior border
Lateral border
Posterior aspect of the body
Coronoid process
Radial notch
correctOption: Posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor muscles of the forearm and is not subcutaneous or easily palpable. Palpation of the anterior forearm primarily reveals muscle bulk rather than the underlying bone. This border serves as an attachment for the deep flexor muscles.

The lateral border of the ulna, also known as the interosseous border, is deep within the forearm and provides attachment for the interosseous membrane. It is not accessible for surface palpation because it faces the radius. It is surrounded by the deep muscles of the forearm.

The posterior aspect of the ulnar body is subcutaneous throughout its length, allowing it to be felt from the olecranon down to the ulnar styloid process. This makes it a useful landmark for assessing ulnar fractures or deformities. It separates the extensor and flexor compartments of the forearm.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior part of the proximal ulna that articulates with the humerus. It is located deep within the cubital fossa and is not palpable from the surface. It forms the inferior boundary of the trochlear notch.

The radial notch is a depression on the lateral side of the coronoid process that articulates with the head of the radius. It is a deep articular feature and cannot be palpated. It is essential for the proximal radioulnar joint's pivot movement.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon.

coreConcept: Mechanism of nipple retraction in breast carcinoma
statement: A 55-year-old woman presents with a newly discovered retraction of the right nipple. Pathologically, this clinical sign is most likely caused by a tumor exerting tension on which of the following structures?
options: Suspensory ligaments of Cooper
Lactiferous ducts
Pectoralis major fascia
Retromammary space
Axillary tail of Spence
correctOption: Lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: While tension on the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) causes dimpling of the skin, it is not the primary mechanism for nipple retraction. These ligaments connect the dermis to the deep fascia. Nipple retraction specifically involves structures directly connected to the nipple itself.

Nipple retraction occurs when a carcinoma within the breast tissue invades or pulls upon the lactiferous ducts, which converge at the nipple. As the tumor grows and causes fibrosis, it shortens these ducts, drawing the nipple inward. This is a classic sign of underlying malignancy.

Involvement of the pectoralis major fascia by a tumor can cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, limiting its mobility. However, this fixation does not directly cause the localized pulling of the nipple. Fixation is tested by asking the patient to contract the pectoral muscles.

The retromammary space is a layer of loose connective tissue between the breast and the deep fascia that allows for breast movement. Obliteration of this space by a tumor leads to breast immobility rather than nipple retraction. It is a plane used in surgical dissections.

The axillary tail of Spence is an extension of breast tissue into the axilla. While tumors can arise here, their presence would typically cause an axillary mass rather than nipple retraction. Nipple changes are usually associated with tumors in the central or subareolar regions.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Palpation of the third part of the axillary artery
statement: To verify the position of the axillary sheath, a clinician palpates the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. Against which muscle is this part of the artery located in the axilla?
options: Pectoralis minor
Coracobrachialis
Teres major
Subscapularis
Serratus anterior
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor muscle is used to divide the axillary artery into three parts, with the second part lying posterior to it. It does not serve as the posterior relation for palpating the third part of the artery. The third part begins at the lateral border of the pectoralis minor.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery in the axilla and serves as a guide for finding the neurovascular bundle. It is not the muscle directly behind the third part of the artery where pulsations are best felt. It is often used as a landmark during axillary surgery.

The third part of the axillary artery lies directly in front of the teres major muscle in the axilla, making this the site where its pulsations are most accessible. Palpating against this muscle allows the clinician to confirm the artery's position within the axillary sheath. This part of the artery extends to the lower border of the teres major.

The subscapularis muscle forms the majority of the posterior wall of the axilla and lies posterior to the second part of the axillary artery. While the third part is also in the axilla, the teres major is the specific muscle it lies in front of as it exits the axilla. Subscapular lymph nodes are palpated against this muscle.

The serratus anterior muscle forms the medial wall of the axilla and is not a posterior relation of the axillary artery. It is located on the thoracic cage and is separated from the artery by the axillary fat and lymph nodes. It is innervated by the long thoracic nerve.
citation: The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25).

coreConcept: Dermatome distribution of the upper limb (C3-C6)
statement: When testing for sensory loss in a patient with a suspected cervical spine injury, where would a clinician expect to find the dermatomes for the C3 through C6 spinal segments?
options: Along the medial margin of the upper limb
Specifically on the middle finger
Along the lateral margin of the upper limb
Only on the palmar surface of the hand
Exclusively on the posterior aspect of the scapula
correctOption: Along the lateral margin of the upper limb
optionExplanations: The medial margin of the upper limb is supplied by the lower cervical and upper thoracic segments, specifically C8, T1, and T2. Sensory loss in this area would suggest an injury lower in the brachial plexus or spinal cord. This region includes the medial forearm and arm.

The middle finger is the specific landmark for the C7 dermatome. It is a critical point for testing the middle segment of the brachial plexus. Testing the middle finger helps differentiate C7 involvement from C6 (thumb) or C8 (little finger) injuries.

The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to C6 are distributed along the lateral margin of the upper limb. This includes the shoulder area and the lateral aspect of the arm and forearm. Assessing sensation here helps evaluate the integrity of the upper roots of the brachial plexus.

The palmar surface of the hand is supplied by multiple dermatomes, including C6 (thumb side), C7 (middle), and C8 (medial side). It is not the exclusive location for C3-C6. The C3 and C4 segments are actually located much higher, near the base of the neck and shoulder.

While the posterior scapular region receives some sensory innervation from cervical nerves, the C3-C6 dermatomes are primarily mapped along the lateral margin of the limb. The scapular skin is also supplied by dorsal rami of spinal nerves. The lateral limb margin is the standard clinical testing site for these segments.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Palpation of central axillary lymph nodes
statement: During a clinical examination of the axilla, the central group of lymph nodes is palpated by placing the fingers in which specific location?
options: Against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Against the anterior surface of the subscapularis
Against the medial side of the axillary vein
In the center of the axilla between the anterior and posterior walls
At the outer border of the first rib
correctOption: In the center of the axilla between the anterior and posterior walls
optionExplanations: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major is the technique used to palpate the anterior (pectoral) lymph nodes. These nodes receive the majority of lymph from the breast. They are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.

Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to palpate the posterior (subscapular) lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the subscapular vessels. They drain the posterior thoracic wall and scapular region.

Palpating against the medial side of the axillary vein targets the lateral lymph nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. They are located along the distal part of the axillary vein.

The central lymph nodes are located deep in the axillary fat and are palpated in the center of the axillary space, between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). They receive lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. They are often the most easily felt nodes if enlarged.

The outer border of the first rib is the landmark for the apical lymph nodes, which are the most superior group in the axilla. Palpating these requires the patient to relax the shoulder completely. They receive lymph from all other axillary node groups.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla.

coreConcept: Palpation of apical axillary lymph nodes
statement: To palpate the apical group of axillary lymph nodes, what is the required patient position and the specific anatomical landmark for the examiner's fingers?
options: Hand on hip with shoulder adducted; palpate the pectoralis major
Arm abducted to 90 degrees; palpate the medial side of the humerus
Shoulder muscles relaxed and limb hanging; palpate the outer border of the first rib
Hand on the opposite shoulder; palpate the floor of the axilla
Shoulder shrugged upward; palpate the apex of the posterior triangle
correctOption: Shoulder muscles relaxed and limb hanging; palpate the outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: Placing the hand on the hip and adducting the shoulder is used to contract the pectoralis major for palpating the anterior (pectoral) nodes. This maneuver makes the anterior wall firm, allowing the nodes to be pressed against it. It is not used for the apical nodes, which require muscle relaxation.

Abducting the arm to 90 degrees might expose the axilla but does not facilitate the deep palpation required for the apical nodes. The medial side of the humerus is the site for palpating lateral nodes, not apical ones. Apical nodes are much more superior and medial.

Palpating the apical nodes requires the patient to relax the shoulder muscles completely with the arm hanging at the side, allowing the examiner to reach the highest point of the axilla. The fingers are directed toward the outer border of the first rib. This is the final destination of lymph before it enters the subclavian trunk.

Placing the hand on the opposite shoulder is not a standard maneuver for axillary node palpation. The floor of the axilla consists of skin and fascia and does not contain a specific node group. Palpation must be directed against the bony or muscular walls.

Shrugging the shoulder upward would tighten the muscles and close the apex of the axilla, making palpation of the apical nodes impossible. The posterior triangle of the neck contains different lymph node chains, such as the supraclavicular nodes. Apical nodes are strictly within the axillary apex.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt.

coreConcept: Subcutaneous nature of the clavicle
statement: Which bone of the pectoral girdle is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length?
options: Scapula
Clavicle
Humerus
First rib
Coracoid process
correctOption: Clavicle
optionExplanations: The scapula is largely covered by thick muscles like the trapezius, infraspinatus, and latissimus dorsi, making only certain parts like the spine, acromion, and inferior angle palpable. Its body is not subcutaneous. It is a flat bone that moves extensively on the thoracic wall.

The clavicle is a long bone that lies immediately beneath the skin, making it palpable from its sternal end to its acromial end. This subcutaneous position makes it one of the most frequently fractured bones in the body. It serves as a strut that holds the upper limb away from the trunk.

The humerus is the bone of the arm and is surrounded by large muscle groups such as the deltoid, biceps, and triceps. Only its distal epicondyles and the greater tuberosity (if the deltoid is wasted) are easily palpable. The shaft of the humerus is not subcutaneous.

The first rib is located deep in the root of the neck and is covered by the clavicle and the scalene muscles. It is not easily palpable along its length and is generally only accessible where the subclavian artery crosses it. It forms the boundary of the superior thoracic aperture.

The coracoid process is a projection of the scapula that can be felt only with deep pressure through the deltoid muscle. It is not a bone palpable along its 'entire length' as it is a relatively small process. It is located inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Safety and utility of mammography
statement: Which of the following statements correctly describes the radiographic technique of mammography used for breast cancer screening?
options: It uses high-dose radiation to ensure clear visualization of deep cysts
It is only effective for detecting malignant tumors, not benign cysts
It utilizes extremely low doses of x-rays to minimize risks during repeated exams
It is a thermal imaging technique that detects heat from hypervascular tumors
It requires the injection of radioactive contrast into the lactiferous ducts
correctOption: It utilizes extremely low doses of x-rays to minimize risks during repeated exams
optionExplanations: Mammography does not use high-dose radiation; instead, it relies on low-energy x-rays to provide high-contrast images of soft tissue. High doses would increase the risk of radiation-induced mutations, which is counterproductive for a screening tool. The goal is to balance image quality with patient safety.

Mammography is effective for detecting a wide range of breast abnormalities, including both malignant tumors and benign conditions like cysts or fibroadenomas. It can identify microcalcifications that are often the first sign of cancer. It is the gold standard for early detection in asymptomatic women.

The safety of mammography stems from the use of extremely low doses of ionizing radiation (x-rays). This allows the procedure to be performed annually or biennially for screening purposes with minimal cumulative risk to the patient. It is a highly regulated radiographic procedure.

Mammography is a radiographic (x-ray) technique, not a thermal imaging (thermography) technique. Thermography measures skin temperature and is not a standard or reliable method for breast cancer screening. Mammography provides anatomical detail that thermal imaging lacks.

Standard screening mammography does not involve the injection of radioactive contrast into the ducts. A procedure called a galactogram or ductogram involves injecting contrast into a duct to investigate nipple discharge, but this is a specialized diagnostic test, not a routine mammogram. Mammography is non-invasive.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: Palpation of the coracoid process
statement: A clinician is attempting to palpate the coracoid process of the scapula on a patient. Which of the following describes the correct anatomical approach to locate this structure?
options: Deep pressure through the posterior part of the deltoid, superior to the spine of the scapula
Superficial palpation at the most lateral point of the shoulder, lateral to the acromion
Deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
Palpation along the medial border of the scapula at the level of the second rib
Deep pressure in the center of the axilla against the humerus
correctOption: Deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
optionExplanations: The posterior deltoid covers the infraspinatus and teres minor muscles, and the spine of the scapula is a superficial landmark, not the site for the coracoid process. Palpating here would likely reach the supraspinatus or the scapular spine itself. The coracoid process is located anteriorly, not posteriorly.

The most lateral point of the shoulder is formed by the acromion, which is a flat extension of the scapular spine. The coracoid process is situated more medially and anteriorly than the acromion. Superficial palpation at the lateral tip would identify the acromion rather than the deeper coracoid process.

The coracoid process is a beaklike projection that can be felt by applying deep pressure through the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle. It is located just inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle. This landmark is essential for identifying the origin of the short head of the biceps brachii and coracobrachialis.

The medial border of the scapula is easily palpable but is located on the posterior thoracic wall. The second rib level corresponds to the superior angle of the scapula, not the coracoid process. The coracoid process is an anteriorly projecting structure and cannot be reached from the medial border.

Deep pressure in the center of the axilla is used to palpate the central axillary lymph nodes against the thoracic wall or humerus. The coracoid process is located superior to the axillary floor. This maneuver would not allow for the identification of the coracoid process due to the intervening soft tissues and neurovascular bundle.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Ulnar nerve palpation at the medial epicondyle
statement: A patient presents with tingling in the ring and little fingers after a fall on the elbow. The clinician decides to palpate the ulnar nerve. Where is this nerve most easily rolled against a bony landmark?
options: Anterior to the lateral epicondyle of the humerus
Posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
Directly over the olecranon process
In the center of the cubital fossa, medial to the biceps tendon
Along the lateral border of the brachioradialis muscle
correctOption: Posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is the site of origin for the common extensor tendon and is related to the radial nerve, not the ulnar nerve. The radial nerve passes anterior to the lateral epicondyle before dividing into its terminal branches. Palpating here would not assess the ulnar nerve.

The ulnar nerve passes through the ulnar sulcus on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. In this location, the nerve is superficial and can be easily palpated or rolled against the bone. This site is a common area for nerve compression or trauma, often referred to as the 'funny bone.'

The olecranon process is the proximal end of the ulna and forms the point of the elbow. While the ulnar nerve is nearby, it is specifically located in the groove between the olecranon and the medial epicondyle. Palpating directly over the olecranon would only reveal the bone and the triceps tendon insertion.

The cubital fossa contains the median nerve and the brachial artery, which are located medial to the biceps brachii tendon. The ulnar nerve does not pass through the cubital fossa; it enters the forearm by passing behind the medial epicondyle. Therefore, it cannot be palpated in the cubital fossa.

The lateral border of the brachioradialis is associated with the superficial branch of the radial nerve in the forearm. The ulnar nerve travels down the medial side of the forearm, deep to the flexor carpi ulnaris. Palpating the lateral forearm would not allow for assessment of the ulnar nerve.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture).

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the ulna
statement: During a physical examination of the forearm, which part of the ulna is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length?
options: The anterior border of the shaft
The lateral border of the shaft
The posterior aspect of the body
The coronoid process
The radial notch
correctOption: The posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the deep flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. It is not subcutaneous and cannot be palpated directly. Only the posterior border of the ulna is accessible to surface palpation.

The lateral border of the ulna serves as the attachment for the interosseous membrane and is buried deep between the flexor and extensor muscle groups. It is not accessible for palpation from the skin surface. This border faces the radius and is situated deep within the forearm.

The posterior border or aspect of the ulnar shaft is subcutaneous throughout its length, from the olecranon to the styloid process. This makes it an important landmark for assessing fractures or bony abnormalities. Because it is not covered by muscles, it can be easily traced with a finger.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior surface of the proximal ulna that articulates with the humerus. It is located deep within the cubital fossa and is covered by the brachialis muscle. It is not palpable during a standard surface examination.

The radial notch is a small articular surface on the lateral side of the coronoid process that receives the head of the radius. It is a deep structure within the proximal radioulnar joint. It is entirely obscured by the surrounding ligaments and muscles, making it impossible to palpate.
