citation: The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length.

coreConcept: Breast examination - dimpling/retraction mechanism
statement: A 55-year-old woman is undergoing a clinical breast examination. When she is asked to raise her arms above her head, a small area of skin dimpling is observed in the upper outer quadrant. What is the anatomical basis for this finding?
options: Contraction of the pectoralis major pulling on the tumor
A carcinoma tethered to the suspensory ligaments or skin
Normal stretching of the lactiferous ducts during arm elevation
Rupture of a benign cyst causing localized skin collapse
Compression of the axillary lymph nodes against the chest wall
correctOption: A carcinoma tethered to the suspensory ligaments or skin
optionExplanations: While the pectoralis major contracts during certain maneuvers, skin dimpling is specifically caused by the tumor's effect on the connective tissue framework of the breast. The muscle itself does not directly pull on the skin to cause dimpling unless the tumor has invaded the pectoral fascia. The primary mechanism involves the suspensory ligaments.

Skin dimpling or nipple retraction occurs when a malignant tumor invades and shortens the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) or the lactiferous ducts. Raising the arms increases the tension on these structures, making the tethering effect more visible as the skin is pulled inward. This is a classic clinical sign of breast carcinoma.

Normal lactiferous ducts are flexible and do not cause skin dimpling when the arms are raised. Stretching of healthy breast tissue during movement is uniform and does not produce localized indentations. Dimpling is an abnormal finding that suggests underlying pathology.

Benign cysts typically do not tether to the skin or suspensory ligaments; they are usually mobile and well-circumscribed. A ruptured cyst might cause inflammation or a mass, but it does not typically produce the characteristic dimpling seen with invasive carcinoma. Dimpling is more indicative of a fibrotic or malignant process.

Axillary lymph node enlargement may be palpable in the axilla but does not cause dimpling of the breast skin. The lymph nodes are located in the axillary fat, separate from the suspensory ligaments of the breast. Skin dimpling is a localized phenomenon within the breast tissue itself.
citation: Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head. With this maneuver, a carcinoma tethered to the skin, the suspensory ligaments, or the lactiferous ducts produces dimpling of the skin or retraction of the nipple.

coreConcept: Radial artery palpation in the distal forearm
statement: To take a radial pulse at the wrist, the clinician should palpate between which two anatomical structures?
options: Tendons of flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum superficialis
Tendons of brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
Tendon of palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis
Styloid process of the ulna and the pisiform bone
Tendons of abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
correctOption: Tendons of brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
optionExplanations: The flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum superficialis are located on the medial (ulnar) side of the forearm. The ulnar artery, not the radial artery, is found in this general vicinity, specifically lateral to the flexor carpi ulnaris. This is not the site for a radial pulse.

The radial artery is most superficial in the distal forearm, where it lies directly on the anterior surface of the radius. It is positioned between the tendon of the brachioradialis (laterally) and the tendon of the flexor carpi radialis (medially). This accessibility makes it the standard site for heart rate assessment.

The palmaris longus tendon, when present, is located medial to the flexor carpi radialis. The median nerve lies between these two tendons or just deep to the palmaris longus. The radial artery is located lateral to the flexor carpi radialis, not between it and the palmaris longus.

The area between the ulnar styloid and the pisiform bone is on the medial side of the wrist. This region is associated with the ulnar nerve and artery as they enter Guyon's canal. It is not the correct location for palpating the radial pulse.

The abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons form the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. While the radial artery can be felt in the snuffbox, the standard site for the radial pulse is in the distal forearm, proximal to these tendons. The snuffbox pulse is generally harder to detect.
citation: In the distal forearm, the radial artery lies on the anterior surface of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis muscles, and is covered only by skin and fascia (see Figs. 3.31 and 3.38). This is the ideal site for taking the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Cephalic vein location at the wrist
statement: A nurse is looking for a reliable site for venipuncture at the wrist. Which vein is described as lying fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the radial styloid process?
options: Basilic vein
Median cubital vein
Cephalic vein
Ulnar vein
Brachial vein
correctOption: Cephalic vein
optionExplanations: The basilic vein originates on the medial side of the dorsal venous network of the hand and travels up the medial aspect of the forearm. It is not located near the radial styloid process. At the wrist, it is found on the ulnar side.

The median cubital vein is a superficial vein located in the cubital fossa, connecting the cephalic and basilic veins. It is a common site for venipuncture in the elbow region but is not found at the level of the wrist. It is much more proximal than the radial styloid.

The cephalic vein begins at the lateral side of the dorsal venous arch of the hand. It consistently passes over the anatomical snuffbox and then runs immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius as it ascends the lateral forearm. This constancy makes it a useful landmark for clinical procedures.

The ulnar veins are deep veins (venae comitantes) that accompany the ulnar artery in the forearm. They are not superficial veins and are not typically used for standard venipuncture. Superficial drainage on the ulnar side is handled by the basilic vein.

The brachial veins are deep veins of the arm that accompany the brachial artery. They are formed by the union of the radial and ulnar veins at the elbow. They are not superficial structures and are not located at the wrist level.
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery.

coreConcept: Dermatomes of the upper limb (C7)
statement: During a neurological examination of the upper limb, a clinician tests for sensation on the middle finger. Which spinal cord segment is primarily being evaluated?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C5 dermatome is typically located on the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid muscle. It does not extend to the fingers. Testing the middle finger would not provide information about the C5 segment.

The C6 dermatome covers the lateral forearm and the thumb (first digit). While it is adjacent to the C7 area, it does not typically include the middle finger. Sensation in the thumb is the classic test for C6 integrity.

The C7 dermatome is consistently mapped to the middle finger of the hand. This makes the middle finger a critical landmark for assessing the seventh cervical spinal nerve. Loss of sensation here suggests a lesion at the C7 level.

The C8 dermatome covers the medial side of the hand, specifically the little finger (fifth digit) and often the ring finger. It does not include the middle finger. The little finger is the primary testing site for C8.

The T1 dermatome is located on the medial aspect of the forearm and the distal part of the arm. It does not extend into the hand or fingers. Testing the middle finger would not assess the T1 spinal segment.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Lateral axillary lymph node palpation
statement: When performing a systematic palpation of the axillary lymph nodes, how should the clinician position their fingers to specifically assess the lateral group of nodes?
options: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis
Pressing laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein and humerus
Placing finger tips high in the axilla against the first rib
Pressing medially against the serratus anterior on the thoracic wall
correctOption: Pressing laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein and humerus
optionExplanations: Pressing forward against the pectoralis major is the technique used to palpate the anterior or pectoral group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the breast. This maneuver does not target the lateral group.

Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla is used to palpate the posterior or subscapular group of nodes. These nodes drain the posterior thoracic wall and scapular region. It is not the correct technique for lateral nodes.

The lateral axillary lymph nodes are located along the medial side of the axillary vein. To palpate them, the examiner must press their fingers laterally against the upper part of the humerus and the neurovascular bundle. This group receives lymph from almost the entire upper limb.

Placing the fingers high in the axilla toward the outer border of the first rib is the technique for palpating the apical lymph nodes. These nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage. This is a deeper palpation than that required for the lateral nodes.

Pressing medially against the thoracic wall (serratus anterior) is used to palpate the central axillary lymph nodes, which are embedded in the axillary fat. The lateral nodes are located more peripherally against the limb itself. This maneuver would miss the lateral group.
citation: The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery. The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall).

coreConcept: Scapular inferior angle as a landmark
statement: A clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a surface landmark during a physical exam. This structure typically corresponds to which vertebral level?
options: Spine of the second thoracic vertebra (T2)
Spine of the fourth thoracic vertebra (T4)
Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7)
Spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra (T10)
Spine of the twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12)
correctOption: Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7)
optionExplanations: The spine of the second thoracic vertebra (T2) corresponds roughly to the superior angle of the scapula. This is the uppermost point of the medial border. The inferior angle is located much lower on the thoracic cage.

The spine of the third or fourth thoracic vertebra (T3/T4) typically aligns with the root of the scapular spine. This is where the spine of the scapula meets the medial border. It is an important landmark but is superior to the inferior angle.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7). This is useful for counting ribs or identifying vertebral levels in the mid-back. It is easily palpable in most individuals.

The spine of the tenth thoracic vertebra (T10) is located well below the scapula. The scapula typically ends at the level of the eighth rib or T7/T8 vertebra. T10 would be closer to the level of the lower ribs and the kidneys.

The spine of the twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12) marks the end of the thoracic spine and the beginning of the lumbar region. It is far inferior to the scapula. The scapula is confined to the upper and middle portions of the posterior thorax.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Radiology of the humerus (angulation)
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anterior angulation of the distal humerus relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft?
optionExplanations: An angulation of 15° is too shallow for the distal humerus. The normal anatomy requires a more significant forward tilt to allow for a full range of flexion at the elbow joint. This value would be considered abnormal on a radiograph.

While 30° is a significant angle, it is still less than the standard anatomical norm for the distal humerus. A 30° angle might suggest a malunited fracture or a developmental variation. The standard reference for clinical radiology is higher.

The distal end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at approximately 45° relative to the shaft. This angulation is critical for the mechanics of the elbow joint, particularly for achieving maximum flexion. Recognizing this angle is essential for identifying supracondylar fractures.

An angulation of 60° would be excessive and would likely interfere with the stability or extension of the elbow joint. Such a sharp angle is not found in normal humeral anatomy. It would likely represent a significant deformity or traumatic displacement.

A 90° angulation would mean the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is anatomically impossible in a healthy individual. This would represent a catastrophic fracture-dislocation. Normal anatomy maintains a 45° relationship.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Palpation of the ulnar nerve at the medial epicondyle
statement: A 24-year-old athlete presents with tingling and numbness in the small finger after striking the medial aspect of their elbow against a hard surface. Upon examination, the clinician palpates a cord-like structure in a shallow groove on the posterior aspect of the most prominent medial bony landmark of the distal humerus. Which structure is being palpated?
options: Ulnar nerve
Median nerve
Radial nerve
Brachial artery
Basilic vein
correctOption: Ulnar nerve
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve is uniquely positioned in the ulnar sulcus on the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. This superficial location allows the nerve to be easily palpated and rolled against the bone, but also makes it highly vulnerable to blunt trauma. Compression or injury at this site typically results in paresthesia along the ulnar distribution of the hand, including the fifth digit.

The median nerve is located deep within the cubital fossa, situated medial to the brachial artery and protected by the bicipital aponeurosis. It does not pass behind the medial epicondyle and is not typically palpable as a cord-like structure in that specific region. Clinical signs of median nerve injury usually involve the lateral palm and the first three and a half digits.

The radial nerve travels in the spiral groove of the humerus and passes anterior to the lateral epicondyle before dividing into superficial and deep branches. It is not found on the medial side of the elbow and would not be affected by trauma to the posterior medial epicondyle. Radial nerve injury is more commonly associated with midshaft humeral fractures and results in wrist drop.

The brachial artery is the main arterial supply of the arm and is palpated on the anterior aspect of the distal humerus, medial to the biceps brachii muscle. It enters the cubital fossa anteriorly and is not found in the posterior ulnar sulcus. Pulsations would be the primary finding during palpation of this vessel rather than a cord-like neural structure.

The basilic vein is a superficial vessel that ascends along the medial side of the forearm and arm, but it lies in the superficial fascia rather than a bony sulcus. While it is medial, it does not have the firm, cord-like consistency of a nerve when rolled against bone. It eventually pierces the deep fascia in the middle of the arm to join the deep venous system.
citation: The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm. The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here (e.g., via blunt trauma or bony fracture).

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the scaphoid in the anatomic snuffbox
statement: A 19-year-old skateboarder falls on an outstretched hand and presents with localized pain on the posterolateral aspect of the wrist. The clinician identifies a triangular depression distal to the radial styloid and finds significant tenderness upon deep palpation between the tendons of the extensor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis. Which bone is most likely fractured?
options: Scaphoid
Lunate
Triquetrum
Pisiform
Trapezium
correctOption: Scaphoid
optionExplanations: The scaphoid bone forms the floor of the anatomic snuffbox, making it the most accessible carpal bone for palpation in this region. Deep tenderness in this specific triangular space following a fall on an outstretched hand is a classic clinical indicator of a scaphoid fracture. Because of its retrograde blood supply, unrecognized fractures here carry a high risk of avascular necrosis.

The lunate is located in the proximal row of carpal bones, medial to the scaphoid, and is best palpated on the dorsal aspect of the wrist distal to the radius. It does not reside within the boundaries of the anatomic snuffbox. Lunate injuries are more commonly associated with dislocations rather than localized tenderness in the snuffbox.

The triquetrum is situated on the medial (ulnar) side of the proximal carpal row, distal to the articular disc of the distal radioulnar joint. It is far removed from the lateral boundaries of the anatomic snuffbox. Tenderness associated with the triquetrum would be found on the ulnar side of the wrist, often distal to the ulnar styloid.

The pisiform is a sesamoid bone located within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris on the anterior (palmar) surface of the wrist. It is easily palpable as a pea-shaped projection on the medial side of the wrist crease. It has no anatomical relationship with the posterolateral anatomic snuffbox.

The trapezium is the carpal bone that articulates with the first metacarpal and is located distal to the scaphoid. While it can be palpated near the base of the thumb, it is not the primary bone forming the floor of the snuffbox where scaphoid tenderness is most acute. The scaphoid is more proximal and directly receives the force of a fall on the radial side.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis (see Figs. 3.34 and 3.105B, C). Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here.

coreConcept: Palpation of the coracoid process
statement: To identify the attachment site for the short head of the biceps brachii and the coracobrachialis, a clinician must palpate a specific scapular projection. Where is this landmark most effectively palpated on a living subject?
options: Deep to the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
Superficial to the acromion, at the tip of the shoulder
In the floor of the intertubercular groove of the humerus
At the medial border of the scapula, level with the T2 spine
Posterior to the surgical neck of the humerus
correctOption: Deep to the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is an essential landmark that can be felt by applying deep pressure through the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle. It is located just inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle in the infraclavicular fossa. This process serves as the origin for the coracobrachialis and the short head of the biceps brachii, as well as the insertion for the pectoralis minor.

The acromion is the lateral continuation of the scapular spine and forms the subcutaneous tip of the shoulder. It is easily palpable without deep pressure and articulates with the clavicle at the acromioclavicular joint. It does not serve as the attachment point for the coracobrachialis or the short head of the biceps.

The intertubercular (bicipital) groove is a feature of the humerus, not the scapula, and it houses the long head of the biceps brachii tendon. While it is a palpable landmark on the anterior humerus, it is distinct from the coracoid process. The floor of this groove provides the insertion for the latissimus dorsi muscle.

The medial border of the scapula is easily palpable along the back, but the coracoid process is an anteriorly projecting structure. The level of the T2 spine corresponds more closely to the superior angle of the scapula. Palpating the medial border is useful for assessing scapular winging or muscle atrophy, but not for locating the coracoid.

The surgical neck of the humerus is located distal to the greater and lesser tubercles and is a common site for fractures. It is not a scapular process and is located deeper and more laterally than the coracoid. The axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap around the surgical neck, making them vulnerable in fractures at this site.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula. It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the radial artery in the distal forearm
statement: When taking a patient's radial pulse at the wrist, the clinician's fingers should be placed on the anterior surface of the distal radius between which two tendons?
options: Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
Flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus
Palmaris longus and flexor carpi ulnaris
Brachioradialis and abductor pollicis longus
Flexor digitorum superficialis and flexor carpi radialis
correctOption: Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
optionExplanations: The radial artery is most superficial in the distal forearm, where it lies directly on the flat anterior surface of the radius. It is bounded laterally by the tendon of the brachioradialis and medially by the tendon of the flexor carpi radialis. This anatomical arrangement makes it the standard site for pulse assessment because the artery can be easily compressed against the underlying bone.

The space between the flexor carpi radialis and the palmaris longus contains the median nerve, which is deeper and does not produce a palpable pulse. Palpating in this area would not yield the radial pulse and might cause discomfort if the nerve is compressed. The palmaris longus is a central tendon that is absent in a small percentage of the population.

The flexor carpi ulnaris and palmaris longus are located more medially in the forearm. The ulnar artery is associated with the flexor carpi ulnaris but is deeper and more difficult to palpate than the radial artery. This region is not used for routine pulse checks unless the radial pulse is unavailable or Allen's test is being performed.

The abductor pollicis longus forms the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox on the dorsal/lateral aspect of the wrist. While the radial artery does pass through the snuffbox, the standard site for taking the pulse is on the anterior (volar) surface of the radius. Palpating between the brachioradialis and abductor pollicis longus would be too lateral for the standard pulse site.

The flexor digitorum superficialis tendons are located deep to the palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis in the distal forearm. They do not serve as the primary surface landmarks for the radial artery. The radial artery remains lateral to the flexor carpi radialis throughout its course in the distal forearm.
citation: In the distal forearm, the radial artery lies on the anterior surface of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis muscles, and is covered only by skin and fascia (see Figs. 3.31 and 3.38). This is the ideal site for taking the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Palpation of the central axillary lymph nodes
statement: During a systematic examination of the axillary lymph nodes, the clinician palpates deep within the center of the axillary fossa, midway between the anterior and posterior axillary walls. Which specific group of nodes is being evaluated in this position?
options: Central nodes
Pectoral (anterior) nodes
Subscapular (posterior) nodes
Lateral nodes
Apical nodes
correctOption: Central nodes
optionExplanations: The central axillary lymph nodes are located deep in the adipose tissue of the axilla, receiving lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. They are best palpated by pressing the fingers toward the apex of the axilla and then sliding them down against the thoracic wall. This group is often the first to be palpated when screening for generalized axillary lymphadenopathy.

The pectoral or anterior nodes are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor, behind the pectoralis major. They are palpated by pressing the fingers forward against the posterior surface of the anterior axillary wall. These nodes primarily drain the lateral and central parts of the breast and the anterior thoracic wall.

The subscapular or posterior nodes lie along the subscapular vessels on the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle. They are palpated by pressing the fingers backward against the posterior axillary wall. These nodes receive lymph from the posterior aspect of the thoracic wall and the scapular region.

The lateral nodes are situated along the medial side of the axillary vein and receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. They are palpated by pressing the fingers laterally against the upper part of the humerus. They are distinct from the central nodes, which are located more medially within the axillary fat.

The apical nodes are the most superior group, located at the apex of the axilla near the outer border of the first rib. They receive lymph from all other axillary groups and are palpated by reaching high into the axilla while the patient's shoulder muscles are relaxed. They represent the final common pathway for axillary lymph drainage before entering the subclavian trunk.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla.

coreConcept: Radiology of the distal humerus angulation
statement: A clinician is evaluating a lateral radiograph of the elbow following a traumatic injury. To determine if there is a subtle displacement of the distal humerus, the clinician assesses the normal anterior angulation of the humeral condyles. What is the standard degree of this forward angulation relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft?
options: 45 degrees
15 degrees
30 degrees
60 degrees
90 degrees
correctOption: 45 degrees
optionExplanations: The distal end of the humerus is naturally tilted anteriorly at an angle of approximately 45 degrees relative to the shaft. This angulation is critical for the full range of flexion at the elbow joint, as it allows the coronoid and radial heads to clear the humeral shaft. On a lateral radiograph, a line drawn down the anterior surface of the humeral shaft (the anterior humeral line) should normally pass through the middle third of the capitulum.

An angulation of 15 degrees would be significantly less than normal and might suggest a supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement of the distal fragment. Such a reduction in the normal tilt would severely limit the patient's ability to achieve full flexion. Radiologists look for the preservation of the 45-degree tilt to confirm anatomical alignment.

While 30 degrees is an anterior tilt, it is still less than the physiological 45 degrees found in a healthy humerus. Deviations from the 45-degree norm can indicate malunion of a previous fracture or an acute injury. Maintaining this specific geometry is essential for the mechanical stability and kinematics of the humero-ulnar and humero-radial joints.

A 60-degree angulation would represent an excessive anterior tilt, which is not the standard anatomical configuration. Such an extreme angle would likely be the result of a significant fracture-dislocation or a congenital deformity. It would alter the tension of the collateral ligaments and the efficiency of the brachialis and biceps muscles.

A 90-degree angulation would mean the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is anatomically impossible in a normal state. This would represent a catastrophic failure of the humeral structure. The 45-degree angle is the specific evolutionary adaptation that balances the stability of extension with the range of motion required for flexion.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the ulnar artery at the wrist
statement: To assess the patency of the ulnar artery during an Allen's test, the clinician must palpate the artery at the wrist. What is the correct anatomical relationship of the ulnar artery to the pisiform bone at this level?
options: Just lateral to the pisiform
Just medial to the pisiform
Directly superficial to the pisiform
Deep to the pisiform bone
2 cm proximal to the pisiform in the midline
correctOption: Just lateral to the pisiform
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum, where it is located immediately lateral to the pisiform bone. In this position, it is accompanied by the ulnar nerve, which lies between the artery and the bone. This superficial location allows the artery to be compressed against the retinaculum to check for a pulse or to perform vascular tests.

The medial side of the pisiform bone is the extreme ulnar edge of the wrist and consists primarily of the insertion of the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon and the skin. The ulnar artery and nerve are always found on the radial (lateral) side of the pisiform as they enter Guyon's canal. Palpating medially would not allow for pulse detection.

The ulnar artery does not pass directly over the superficial surface of the pisiform bone; rather, it passes lateral to it. The pisiform is a subcutaneous bone that can be felt as a hard prominence, but the artery is located in the soft tissue depression just lateral to this prominence. Placing fingers directly on the bone would obscure the pulse.

The ulnar artery is a superficial structure at the wrist and does not pass deep to the carpal bones. It travels superficial to the flexor retinaculum, unlike the median nerve and flexor tendons which pass deep to it in the carpal tunnel. This superficiality is what makes the ulnar pulse accessible to clinicians.

While the ulnar artery can be found proximal to the wrist, the standard landmark for palpation is at the level of the pisiform. The midline of the wrist contains the palmaris longus tendon and the median nerve, not the ulnar artery. Moving 2 cm proximal would place the palpation site over the muscle belly of the flexor carpi ulnaris.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Palpation of the third part of the axillary artery
statement: A clinician needs to verify the position of the axillary sheath by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. Against which muscle should the artery be palpated to most easily detect these pulsations?
options: Teres major
Pectoralis minor
Coracobrachialis
Latissimus dorsi
Subscapularis
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The third part of the axillary artery extends from the lower border of the pectoralis minor to the lower border of the teres major. At the distal end of the axilla, the artery lies directly anterior to the teres major muscle. This provides a relatively firm surface against which the artery can be compressed to feel the pulse.

The pectoralis minor muscle serves as the landmark that divides the axillary artery into three parts. The second part of the artery lies deep to the pectoralis minor, making it difficult to palpate directly. The third part, which is the most accessible for palpation, begins only after the artery has passed the lower border of this muscle.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery in the axilla. While it is a useful landmark for the neurovascular bundle, the artery does not lie directly in front of it in a way that facilitates palpation against the muscle. The artery is more medial, following the course of the humerus.

The latissimus dorsi muscle forms the posterior axillary fold along with the teres major, but the artery is specifically described as lying in front of the teres major in the distal axilla. While they are close, the teres major is the more direct posterior relation for the third part of the artery. Palpation is most effective when targeting the teres major.

The subscapularis muscle forms the majority of the posterior wall of the axilla and is a posterior relation to the second part of the axillary artery. By the time the artery reaches its third part, it has moved more distally toward the arm, where it lies in front of the teres major and latissimus dorsi tendons. The subscapularis is too proximal for the third part's pulse.
citation: The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the cephalic vein at the radial styloid
statement: A patient is brought to the emergency department in profound hypovolemic shock with collapsed peripheral veins. The medical team needs to establish venous access quickly. Based on surface anatomy, where is the most reliable location to find the cephalic vein, even when it is not visible?
options: Immediately posterior to the radial styloid process
Immediately anterior to the medial epicondyle
In the groove between the olecranon and medial epicondyle
Superficial to the bicipital aponeurosis in the cubital fossa
Just medial to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the radial styloid process
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein has a very consistent anatomical course as it wraps from the dorsal venous network of the hand to the lateral aspect of the forearm. It passes immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. This constancy makes it a 'lifeline' for clinicians attempting venipuncture or a venous cutdown in patients whose veins are otherwise invisible due to shock.

The area anterior to the medial epicondyle is where the basilic vein and the median cubital vein are typically found. While these are common sites for venipuncture, they can be more variable or harder to locate when collapsed compared to the cephalic vein at the radial styloid. The basilic vein also tends to be more mobile in the superficial fascia.

The groove between the olecranon and the medial epicondyle contains the ulnar nerve (the ulnar sulcus). There are no major superficial veins in this specific groove that are used for emergency access. Attempting venipuncture here would risk significant nerve injury and would not yield a reliable venous target.

The median cubital vein lies superficial to the bicipital aponeurosis in the cubital fossa, but it is often the first vein to collapse in shock. While it is a preferred site for routine blood draws, the cephalic vein at the wrist is often more 'fixed' and easier to find by palpating the bony landmark of the radial styloid.

The area medial to the flexor carpi ulnaris is the territory of the ulnar artery and nerve, not the cephalic vein. The cephalic vein is a lateral (radial) structure. Placing a needle in the medial distal forearm would likely result in an arterial puncture or nerve damage rather than successful venous access.
citation: When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the inferior angle of the scapula
statement: During a physical examination, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a landmark to identify vertebral levels. Which thoracic vertebra's spinous process is typically at the same horizontal level as the inferior angle?
options: T7
T3
T2
L1
correctOption: T7
optionExplanations: The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that corresponds to the level of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7) and the seventh rib. This is useful for clinicians when counting ribs or identifying vertebral levels for procedures like thoracocentesis. It is easily palpated even in muscular individuals at the lowest point of the scapular body.

The T3 vertebral level corresponds to the root of the spine of the scapula, not the inferior angle. The spine of the scapula is the prominent ridge on the posterior surface that ends laterally as the acromion. Identifying T3 is helpful for locating the upper lobes of the lungs during auscultation.

The T2 vertebral level is generally associated with the superior angle of the scapula. This is the highest point of the scapula where the levator scapulae muscle inserts. It is much higher than the inferior angle and is often covered by the trapezius muscle, making it slightly harder to palpate than the inferior angle.

The T10 vertebral level is significantly lower than the scapula. By this level, the scapula has ended, and the clinician would be palpating the lower thoracic wall near the floating ribs. T10 is a landmark often used to approximate the level of the xiphisternal joint or the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm.

The L1 vertebral level marks the beginning of the lumbar spine and the transpyloric plane. It is located well below the thoracic cage and the scapulae. Landmarks at this level include the end of the spinal cord (conus medullaris) and the origin of the superior mesenteric artery, but it has no relationship to scapular anatomy.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Inferior angle of the scapula as a vertebral landmark
statement: During a physical examination of the posterior thorax, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a surface landmark. Which vertebral level and rib are typically identified by this structure?
options: Third thoracic vertebra and third rib
Fifth thoracic vertebra and fifth rib
Seventh thoracic vertebra and seventh rib
Ninth thoracic vertebra and ninth rib
Twelfth thoracic vertebra and twelfth rib
correctOption: Seventh thoracic vertebra and seventh rib
optionExplanations: The third thoracic vertebra (T3) is typically aligned with the medial end of the spine of the scapula, not the inferior angle. This landmark is useful for identifying the upper thoracic region but is too superior for the inferior angle. Palpation of the T3 level is often used to locate the beginning of the oblique fissure of the lung.

The fifth thoracic vertebra (T5) lies between the spine and the inferior angle of the scapula. While it is a significant vertebral level, it does not correspond to a primary palpable landmark of the scapula in the neutral anatomical position. It is generally located midway down the medial border of the scapula.

The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable surface landmark that corresponds to the level of the seventh rib and the spinous process of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7). This relationship is clinically significant for counting ribs and identifying vertebral levels during procedures like thoracocentesis or spinal anesthesia. It is easily palpable in most subjects, even those with significant muscular development.

The ninth thoracic vertebra (T9) is located well below the inferior angle of the scapula. Using the scapula to identify T9 would result in an inaccurate assessment of the lower thoracic anatomy. Landmarks for T9 are usually found by counting down from the T7 level established by the scapular angle.

The twelfth thoracic vertebra (T12) marks the transition to the lumbar spine and is associated with the floating ribs. It is located much lower in the back than the scapula. The inferior angle of the scapula is far too superior to serve as a landmark for the T12 vertebral level.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula. This forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder.

coreConcept: Anatomical location of the lateral epicondyle
statement: A clinician is palpating the bony landmarks of the distal humerus. Which of the following correctly describes the anatomical position of the lateral epicondyle relative to other humeral structures?
options: Distal to the capitulum on the medial side
Proximal to the capitulum on the lateral side
Proximal to the trochlea on the medial side
Distal to the trochlea on the lateral side
Posterior to the ulnar sulcus
correctOption: Proximal to the capitulum on the lateral side
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is located on the lateral side of the humerus, not the medial side. Furthermore, it is proximal to the articular surfaces, not distal. The medial side of the distal humerus is dominated by the much larger medial epicondyle.

The lateral epicondyle is a small, roughened projection situated on the distal, lateral aspect of the humerus, specifically proximal to the capitulum. It serves as the common origin for the superficial extensor muscles of the forearm. Its palpability is essential for diagnosing conditions like lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow).

The structure located proximal to the trochlea on the medial side is the medial epicondyle, not the lateral epicondyle. The medial epicondyle is more prominent and is associated with the ulnar nerve. These two epicondyles are the primary palpable landmarks of the elbow region.

The lateral epicondyle is proximal to the articular surfaces of the humerus, and the trochlea is the medial part of the humeral condyle. Therefore, the lateral epicondyle cannot be distal to the trochlea. The trochlea articulates with the ulna, while the capitulum articulates with the radius.

The ulnar sulcus is located on the posterior surface of the medial epicondyle, where the ulnar nerve resides. The lateral epicondyle does not have a similar sulcus for a major nerve on its posterior aspect. This landmark is on the opposite side of the humerus from the lateral epicondyle.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm.

coreConcept: Palpability of the ulnar shaft
statement: Which part of the ulna is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: Anterior border of the body
Lateral border of the body
Medial border of the body
Posterior aspect of the body
Interosseous border of the body
correctOption: Posterior aspect of the body
optionExplanations: The anterior border of the ulna is covered by the flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. This muscular coverage prevents the anterior border from being easily palpable along its length. It is not considered a subcutaneous landmark.

The lateral border of the ulna is also known as the interosseous border and serves as the attachment site for the interosseous membrane. It is located deep within the forearm between the radius and ulna. Consequently, it is not accessible for surface palpation.

The medial border of the ulna is surrounded by muscle mass and is not as consistently subcutaneous as the posterior aspect. While parts of the medial surface may be felt, it does not form a continuous palpable ridge from the elbow to the wrist. The posterior aspect is the primary subcutaneous landmark.

The posterior aspect of the body of the ulna is rounded, subcutaneous, and easily palpable along its entire length. It extends from the olecranon at the elbow to the styloid process at the wrist. This makes it a critical landmark for assessing ulnar fractures or performing clinical examinations of the forearm.

The interosseous border is the sharp lateral edge of the ulna where the interosseous membrane attaches. It is positioned deeply between the two forearm bones and is completely covered by muscles. It cannot be palpated on the surface of the living subject.
citation: The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox after a fall on the outstretched hand is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Mammography and radiation safety
statement: Mammography is a primary tool for breast cancer screening. Which of the following statements correctly describes the radiological nature of this procedure?
options: It uses high-frequency sound waves to visualize cysts
It utilizes high-dose ionizing radiation for maximum contrast
It employs extremely low doses of x-rays to minimize risk
It relies on magnetic resonance to detect metabolic changes
It is only effective for detecting malignant tumors, not benign cysts
correctOption: It employs extremely low doses of x-rays to minimize risk
optionExplanations: The use of high-frequency sound waves describes ultrasonography, not mammography. While ultrasound is often used as an adjunct to mammography to differentiate between solid masses and fluid-filled cysts, it is not the modality used in a standard mammogram. Mammography specifically refers to x-ray imaging.

Mammography does not use high-dose radiation; in fact, the goal is to use the lowest dose possible to achieve diagnostic quality. High-dose radiation would increase the risk of secondary cancers, making it unsuitable for frequent screening. Modern mammography equipment is designed to optimize contrast while minimizing exposure.

Mammography utilizes extremely low doses of x-rays, which ensures that the risks associated with radiation exposure are minimal. This safety profile allows the examination to be performed regularly as a screening tool for early detection of breast pathology. It is highly effective for identifying both benign and malignant lesions.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a different modality that uses magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses, not x-rays. While breast MRI is used in specific high-risk cases, it is not the standard 'mammography' procedure. Mammography is strictly a radiographic (x-ray) technique.

Mammography is extensively used to screen for a wide range of breast abnormalities, including benign tumors, malignant tumors, and cysts. It is not limited to the detection of malignancy. The ability to visualize microcalcifications and architectural distortions makes it a versatile screening tool.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often. With the patient undressed to the waist and sitting upright, first inspect the breasts for symmetry.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the cephalic vein at the wrist
statement: In an emergency situation where superficial veins are not visible due to shock, where can the cephalic vein be reliably located at the wrist for venipuncture?
options: Immediately anterior to the styloid process of the radius
Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
Medial to the pisiform bone
Lateral to the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris
Deep to the bicipital aponeurosis
correctOption: Immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius
optionExplanations: The area anterior to the radial styloid process is occupied by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis, as well as the radial artery. The cephalic vein is located on the dorsal/posterior aspect of this region. Placing a needle anteriorly would risk arterial puncture.

The cephalic vein is a constant landmark located in the superficial fascia immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. This predictable anatomical position makes it an ideal site for venous access when other peripheral veins are collapsed or invisible. It is often referred to as the 'intern's vein' due to its reliability.

The area medial to the pisiform bone is associated with the ulnar nerve and ulnar artery as they enter the hand. The cephalic vein is a lateral structure of the upper limb and is not found in the medial wrist region. The basilic vein would be the more likely venous structure on the medial side.

The flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist. The cephalic vein is located on the lateral (radial) side. Therefore, it cannot be found lateral to the flexor carpi ulnaris, which is a medial landmark.

The bicipital aponeurosis is located in the cubital fossa of the elbow, not at the wrist. It separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery. At the wrist, the cephalic vein is superficial and is not covered by any such aponeurotic structure.
citation: When a patient is in a state of shock, the superficial veins are not always visible. The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius. In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery.

coreConcept: Dermatomes of the lateral margin of the upper limb
statement: A patient presents with sensory loss specifically along the lateral margin of the upper limb. Which spinal cord segments are most likely involved based on this dermatomal distribution?
options: C3 to C6
C7 only
C8 to T2
T3 to T5
C1 to C2
correctOption: C3 to C6
optionExplanations: The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 through C6 are specifically located along the lateral margin of the upper limb. Sensory testing in this region is used to evaluate the integrity of these specific spinal cord segments. Loss of sensation here suggests a lesion affecting the upper portion of the brachial plexus or the corresponding cervical roots.

The C7 dermatome is localized to the middle finger and does not cover the entire lateral margin of the limb. While C7 is part of the brachial plexus, its sensory distribution is more central in the hand. It is a key landmark for isolating the middle trunk of the plexus.

The segments C8, T1, and T2 provide sensory innervation to the medial margin of the upper limb, not the lateral margin. Testing the medial aspect of the arm and forearm evaluates these lower brachial plexus components. This area is often affected in Klumpke's palsy or lower cervical disc herniations.

The T3 to T5 dermatomes are located on the thorax and do not extend down the upper limb. T2 is the lowest segment typically associated with the medial arm (via the intercostobrachial nerve). These thoracic segments are used to identify the level of spinal cord injuries in the chest region.

The C1 and C2 spinal segments do not have dermatomes on the upper limb. C1 has no sensory root, and C2 provides sensation to the posterior scalp and upper neck. The upper limb dermatomes begin at C3 and extend through T2.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Bony relations of the elbow in flexion
statement: When a clinician examines a flexed elbow joint, what is the expected geometric relationship between the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the tip of the olecranon?
options: They form a straight horizontal line
They form a straight vertical line
They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
They form a right-angled triangle with the olecranon at the 90° vertex
The olecranon becomes impalpable and cannot be used as a landmark
correctOption: They form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle
optionExplanations: A straight horizontal line is the normal relationship between these three landmarks when the elbow is in full extension, not flexion. Disruption of this line in extension can indicate a fracture or dislocation. In flexion, the olecranon moves distally relative to the epicondyles.

The bony landmarks of the elbow do not form a vertical line in any standard anatomical position. The epicondyles are always medial and lateral to each other. A vertical alignment would imply a severe and impossible rotational deformity of the humerus or ulna.

In the flexed position, the medial epicondyle, lateral epicondyle, and the tip of the olecranon process normally form the vertices of an equilateral triangle. This relationship is a fundamental clinical sign used to confirm the integrity of the elbow joint. If this triangle is distorted, it suggests a displacement such as a supracondylar fracture or elbow dislocation.

While the landmarks form a triangle, it is specifically described as equilateral rather than right-angled. In an equilateral triangle, all sides and angles are equal, providing a symmetrical reference for the clinician. A right-angled triangle would imply an asymmetrical displacement of one of the landmarks.

The olecranon remains the most prominent and easily palpable landmark of the elbow regardless of the degree of flexion. In fact, it becomes even more prominent as the elbow is flexed, forming the 'point' of the elbow. It is never impalpable in a normal joint.
citation: The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon and the pulley-shaped trochlea of the humerus; it also has strong medial and lateral ligaments. When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line; in flexion, the bony points form the boundaries of an equilateral triangle.

coreConcept: Radiological angulation of the distal humerus
statement: On a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anterior angulation of the distal end of the humerus relative to its shaft?
optionExplanations: An angulation of 15° is too shallow and does not represent the normal anatomy of the distal humerus. Such a finding on a radiograph might suggest a malunited fracture or a developmental abnormality. The normal forward tilt is significantly more pronounced to allow for adequate flexion.

While 30° is a significant angle, it is still less than the standard anatomical norm for the humeral head-to-shaft relationship at the elbow. Accurate assessment of this angle is vital during the surgical reduction of supracondylar fractures. The correct angle ensures the coronoid process can fit into its fossa during flexion.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at 45° relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This anterior tilt is essential for the mechanical function of the elbow joint, particularly for achieving a full range of flexion. Clinicians must verify this angle on lateral radiographs to ensure proper alignment after injury.

An angulation of 60° would be excessive and would likely interfere with the stability and range of motion of the elbow joint. This degree of tilt is not found in normal human anatomy. It would result in the articular surfaces being oriented too far anteriorly.

A 90° angulation would mean the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is anatomically impossible in a healthy individual. This would represent a catastrophic fracture-dislocation. The normal 45° angle provides the optimal balance between stability and mobility.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Mechanism of nipple retraction in breast carcinoma
statement: During a clinical breast examination, a physician notes retraction of the nipple. What is the underlying anatomical mechanism by which a carcinoma causes this finding?
options: Invasion and shortening of the suspensory ligaments
Compression of the pectoral fascia
Pulling on the lactiferous ducts
Obstruction of the axillary lymph nodes
Hypertrophy of the mammary glands
correctOption: Pulling on the lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: Invasion of the suspensory (Cooper's) ligaments by a tumor typically causes dimpling of the overlying skin, not necessarily retraction of the nipple itself. These ligaments anchor the breast tissue to the dermis. While related to malignancy, the mechanism for nipple retraction is distinct.

Compression or invasion of the pectoral fascia may cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, which is tested by having the patient contract the pectoralis major. It does not directly cause the nipple to pull inward. Nipple retraction is specifically linked to the ductal system.

A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause nipple retraction by invading and pulling on the lactiferous ducts. These ducts converge at the nipple, and their tethering or shortening by a malignant mass results in the nipple being drawn inward. This is a classic clinical sign of breast cancer.

Obstruction of axillary lymph nodes leads to lymphedema of the breast, which may manifest as 'peau d'orange' (orange peel skin) due to skin thickening around hair follicles. It does not cause mechanical retraction of the nipple. Lymphadenopathy is a sign of metastasis rather than the cause of nipple inversion.

Hypertrophy of the mammary glands, such as during pregnancy or puberty, generally causes breast enlargement and may make the nipple more prominent. It does not cause the pathological pulling or retraction seen in malignancy. Retraction is an asymmetrical, often fixed change associated with tissue destruction or fibrosis.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Clinical suspicion of scaphoid fracture
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls on an outstretched hand and presents with deep tenderness in the triangular depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist. Which injury should the clinician most strongly suspect?
options: Dislocation of the lunate bone
Fracture of the scaphoid bone
Rupture of the extensor pollicis longus tendon
Fracture of the radial styloid process
Compression of the ulnar nerve in Guyon's canal
correctOption: Fracture of the scaphoid bone
optionExplanations: Lunate dislocation typically presents with pain and swelling in the central wrist and may cause median nerve compression symptoms. It does not specifically cause localized tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox. The mechanism of injury is similar, but the site of maximal tenderness differs.

Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox following a fall on an outstretched hand is a classic indicator of a scaphoid fracture. The scaphoid is the most frequently fractured carpal bone, and its location in the floor of the snuffbox makes it directly palpable there. Failure to diagnose this can lead to avascular necrosis due to its retrograde blood supply.

Rupture of the extensor pollicis longus (EPL) tendon would result in an inability to extend the thumb at the interphalangeal joint and a loss of the medial boundary of the snuffbox. While it can occur after wrist fractures, it does not present with deep bony tenderness in the snuffbox floor. EPL rupture is often a late complication of distal radius fractures.

A fracture of the radial styloid (Chauffeur's fracture) causes tenderness more proximally and laterally than the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. While the snuffbox is near the styloid, the 'deep tenderness' described is pathognomonic for the scaphoid bone itself. Radiographs are needed to differentiate the two.

Guyon's canal is located on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist, near the pisiform and hamate bones. Compression of the ulnar nerve here would cause sensory loss in the pinky and half of the ring finger. It is anatomically distant from the posterolateral anatomic snuffbox.
citation: Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid. The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus.

coreConcept: Radial artery palpation landmarks in the distal forearm
statement: When palpating the radial pulse in the distal forearm, between which two specific muscle tendons should the clinician place their fingers?
options: Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
Flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus
Flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum superficialis
Abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
Brachioradialis and pronator teres
correctOption: Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
optionExplanations: The radial artery is most accessible in the distal forearm where it lies directly on the anterior surface of the radius. It is specifically positioned between the tendon of the brachioradialis laterally and the tendon of the flexor carpi radialis medially. This superficial location, covered only by skin and fascia, makes it the primary site for pulse assessment and arterial blood gas sampling.

The flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus tendons are both located in the anterior forearm, but the radial artery lies lateral to the flexor carpi radialis. Palmaris longus is more medial and is often used as a landmark for the median nerve rather than the radial artery. In many individuals, the palmaris longus may even be congenitally absent.

The flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum superficialis are located on the medial aspect of the forearm. These structures are associated with the ulnar artery and ulnar nerve rather than the radial artery. Palpating in this region would yield an ulnar pulse if performed correctly near the pisiform bone.

The abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons form the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. While the radial artery can be palpated within the snuffbox, this is not the standard distal forearm site described between the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis. The snuffbox site is typically used when the distal forearm pulse is difficult to appreciate.

The pronator teres is a muscle of the proximal forearm and does not extend to the distal region where the radial pulse is typically taken. The brachioradialis does form the lateral boundary, but the medial boundary at the wrist is the flexor carpi radialis, not the pronator teres. Therefore, this combination is anatomically incorrect for distal palpation.
citation: In the distal forearm, the radial artery lies on the anterior surface of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis muscles, and is covered only by skin and fascia (see Figs. 3.31 and 3.38). This is the ideal site for taking the radial pulse. If the pulse cannot be felt, try feeling for the radial artery on the other wrist.

coreConcept: Ulnar artery palpation and its relationship to the pisiform bone
statement: A clinician is attempting to palpate the ulnar pulse at the wrist. Which of the following best describes the anatomical relationship of the ulnar artery at this site?
options: It lies medial to the pisiform bone and deep to the flexor retinaculum.
It lies lateral to the pisiform bone and superficial to the flexor retinaculum.
It lies lateral to the radial artery and deep to the palmaris longus.
It lies medial to the ulnar nerve and lateral to the flexor carpi ulnaris.
It lies posterior to the ulnar nerve and medial to the scaphoid bone.
correctOption: It lies lateral to the pisiform bone and superficial to the flexor retinaculum.
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery enters the hand by passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum, unlike the structures in the carpal tunnel. It is positioned just lateral to the pisiform bone, which serves as a reliable bony landmark for palpation. At this location, it is only covered by skin and fascia, allowing the pulse to be easily detected.

The ulnar artery does not lie medial to the pisiform bone; the pisiform is the most medial carpal bone in the proximal row. Furthermore, the ulnar artery and nerve pass superficial to the flexor retinaculum through Guyon's canal, not deep to it. Placing fingers medial to the pisiform would result in palpating soft tissue away from the arterial path.

The ulnar artery is located on the medial side of the wrist, while the radial artery is on the lateral side. They are separated by the width of the forearm and several muscle tendons. The palmaris longus is a midline structure and does not cover the ulnar artery at the level of the pisiform.

The ulnar artery actually lies lateral to the ulnar nerve at the wrist, not medial to it. The ulnar nerve is situated between the artery and the pisiform bone. The flexor carpi ulnaris tendon is the most medial structure, and the nerve and artery lie lateral to its insertion on the pisiform.

The ulnar artery is superficial to the nerve in some contexts but generally lies lateral to it at the wrist level. The scaphoid bone is located on the lateral (radial) side of the wrist, far from the ulnar artery's medial path. This description confuses the medial and lateral landmarks of the carpus.
citation: As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse. The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve.

coreConcept: Protective function of the bicipital aponeurosis in the cubital fossa
statement: During an emergency venipuncture in the cubital fossa, a trainee expresses concern about accidentally puncturing the brachial artery. Which anatomical structure provides a natural barrier between the median cubital vein and the underlying brachial artery?
options: Brachialis muscle
Bicipital aponeurosis
Pronator teres muscle
Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm
Flexor retinaculum
correctOption: Bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis is a flat expansion of the biceps brachii tendon that passes medially across the cubital fossa. It specifically separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deeper brachial artery and median nerve. This anatomical arrangement is clinically significant as it provides a layer of protection during intravenous injections or blood draws.

The brachialis muscle forms part of the floor of the cubital fossa and lies deep to the brachial artery. It does not separate the median cubital vein from the artery; rather, the artery rests upon the brachialis. Therefore, it cannot protect the artery from a needle entering from the skin surface.

The pronator teres muscle forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. While it is a key landmark for the region, it does not serve as a horizontal barrier between the superficial veins and the deep brachial artery. Its primary role is forearm pronation and elbow flexion.

The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is a superficial structure that provides sensation to the lateral forearm. It is located in the superficial fascia near the cephalic vein, but it does not form a protective fibrous barrier over the brachial artery. Its injury during venipuncture would cause sensory loss, not arterial damage.

The flexor retinaculum is located at the wrist, where it forms the roof of the carpal tunnel. It has no anatomical presence in the cubital fossa and therefore cannot provide any protection to the brachial artery. This structure is involved in stabilizing the flexor tendons and the median nerve at the carpus.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein. The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization.

coreConcept: Anatomical advantages of the basilic vein for central catheterization
statement: Why is the basilic vein preferred over the cephalic vein for the insertion of a peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) starting from the cubital fossa?
options: The basilic vein has a smaller diameter, reducing the risk of thrombosis.
The basilic vein is more superficial and easier to visualize in obese patients.
The basilic vein increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein.
The cephalic vein joins the axillary vein at a more acute, favorable angle.
The basilic vein is less likely to be accompanied by cutaneous nerves.
correctOption: The basilic vein increases in diameter and maintains a direct line with the axillary vein.
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is anatomically advantageous for central catheterization because it gradually increases in diameter as it ascends the arm. Furthermore, it follows a relatively straight, direct path into the axillary vein, which facilitates the advancement of long catheters toward the superior vena cava. This reduces the likelihood of the catheter getting stuck or causing vessel wall trauma.

A smaller diameter is actually a disadvantage for central catheterization, as it increases the risk of vessel wall irritation and thrombosis. The basilic vein is preferred specifically because its diameter increases, allowing for better hemodynamics around the catheter. Cephalic veins often narrow significantly before joining the axillary vein.

The basilic vein is actually deeper than the cephalic vein in the mid-arm, as it pierces the deep fascia to join the brachial veins. The cephalic vein remains superficial until the deltopectoral triangle. Therefore, ease of visualization is not the primary reason for its preference in central lines.

The cephalic vein often joins the axillary vein at a very sharp, nearly 90-degree angle within the deltopectoral triangle. This 'T-junction' can make it extremely difficult to navigate a catheter into the axillary and subclavian veins. In contrast, the basilic vein's transition into the axillary vein is much smoother and more linear.

Both the basilic and cephalic veins are accompanied by cutaneous nerves in the superficial fascia (medial and lateral cutaneous nerves of the forearm, respectively). The presence of these nerves is a consideration for both vessels during venipuncture. The preference for the basilic vein is based on its vascular geometry and size, not the absence of nerves.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein (see Fig. 3.68). The superficial veins are clinically important and are used for venipuncture, transfusion, and cardiac catheterization.

coreConcept: Palpation technique for apical axillary lymph nodes
statement: To properly palpate the apical group of axillary lymph nodes, what is the required patient position and the specific anatomical landmark for the examiner's fingers?
options: Arm abducted to 90 degrees; fingers at the lateral border of the scapula.
Hand on hip with medial pressure; fingers against the pectoralis major.
Arm hanging relaxed at the side; fingers high in the axilla at the first rib.
Arm fully elevated above the head; fingers at the apex of the coracoid process.
Hand on the opposite shoulder; fingers against the subscapularis muscle.
correctOption: Arm hanging relaxed at the side; fingers high in the axilla at the first rib.
optionExplanations: Palpation of the apical nodes requires the patient to relax the shoulder girdle and pectoralis muscles by letting the arm hang naturally at the side. The examiner reaches the highest point of the axillary vault, aiming for the outer border of the first rib where these nodes are located. This group receives lymph from all other axillary nodes and is the final station before the subclavian trunk.

Abducting the arm to 90 degrees actually tightens the axillary fascia and makes deep palpation of the apex more difficult. The lateral border of the scapula is the site for palpating the posterior (subscapular) nodes, not the apical nodes. Relaxation is key to reaching the deep apical structures.

Placing the hand on the hip and pushing medially is the maneuver used to contract the pectoralis major. This is specifically done to palpate the anterior (pectoral) nodes against the muscle's posterior surface. It would hinder the examination of the apical nodes by closing off the axillary space.

Full elevation of the arm stretches the skin and fascia of the axilla, which can obscure deep nodal masses. The coracoid process is a landmark for the attachment of muscles like the pectoralis minor, but it is not the primary landmark for the apical lymph node group, which sits more medially near the rib cage.

Placing the hand on the opposite shoulder is sometimes used for other shoulder exams, but it is not the standard position for apical node palpation. Pressing against the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to identify the posterior (subscapular) nodes. The apical nodes are much higher and more medial than the subscapularis muscle belly.
citation: For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib. If the nodes are enlarged, they can be felt.

coreConcept: Radiographic landmarks and angulation of the distal humerus
statement: When interpreting a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anatomical angulation of the distal humerus relative to its shaft?
options: Angulated posteriorly at 15 degrees
Angulated forward at 45 degrees
Angulated medially at 30 degrees
Perfectly aligned with the long axis (0 degrees)
Angulated laterally at 45 degrees
correctOption: Angulated forward at 45 degrees
optionExplanations: The distal end of the humerus, including the trochlea and capitulum, is normally tilted anteriorly (forward) at an angle of approximately 45 degrees relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This angulation is crucial for the normal range of elbow flexion and is a key feature to assess on lateral radiographs following trauma. Loss of this angulation often indicates a supracondylar fracture.

Posterior angulation of the distal humerus is abnormal and typically suggests a fracture displacement, such as an extension-type supracondylar fracture.
