The normal anatomy is designed with an anterior tilt to accommodate the coronoid process of the ulna during full flexion. A 15-degree posterior tilt would significantly impair joint mechanics.

Medial angulation refers to the 'carrying angle' seen in the AP view, not the lateral view. The distal humerus does have a slight medial tilt in the frontal plane, but the 45-degree forward tilt is the defining characteristic of the lateral radiographic profile. Medial angulation is not measured as 30 degrees in a standard lateral view.

Perfect alignment (0 degrees) between the distal articular surface and the shaft is not the normal state. If a radiograph shows the distal humerus perfectly in line with the shaft, it may indicate a subtle fracture or remodeling after injury. The 45-degree anterior tilt is a consistent finding in healthy adults.

Lateral angulation is not a standard feature of the distal humerus in the lateral plane. While the humerus has complex geometry, the primary tilt of the condyles is anterior. Lateral deviation would be described as cubitus valgus or varus in an anterior-posterior clinical assessment, not a lateral radiograph.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Palpation of the brachial artery in the arm
statement: Where is the most appropriate location to palpate the brachial artery in the mid-arm region?
options: On the lateral side of the biceps brachii, resting on the humerus
On the medial side of the arm, resting on the brachialis muscle
Posterior to the triceps brachii in the radial groove
Superficial to the deltoid muscle insertion
In the groove between the brachialis and brachioradialis
correctOption: On the medial side of the arm, resting on the brachialis muscle
optionExplanations: In the arm, the brachial artery travels along the medial aspect of the humerus. It lies directly on the brachialis muscle and is partially overlapped by the medial border of the biceps brachii. This medial position allows the artery to be compressed against the humerus to control bleeding or to palpate the pulse.

The lateral side of the biceps brachii does not contain the brachial artery; the cephalic vein is the primary vascular structure in the lateral bicipital groove. Palpating here would not reveal the brachial pulse. The artery stays medial to the biceps throughout most of its course in the arm.

The radial groove on the posterior aspect of the humerus contains the radial nerve and the profunda brachii artery, not the main brachial artery. The brachial artery remains in the anterior compartment of the arm. Palpating posterior to the triceps would only allow assessment of the posterior compartment structures.

The deltoid muscle insertion is on the lateral aspect of the humerus (deltoid tuberosity). The brachial artery is located medially and much deeper than the superficial fibers of the deltoid. There is no significant arterial pulse to be felt superficial to the deltoid insertion.

The groove between the brachialis and brachioradialis is located in the lateral cubital region. While the radial nerve passes through this space, the brachial artery is located more medially in the cubital fossa. This lateral groove is not the standard site for brachial artery palpation.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis (see Fig. 3.31). This is where the clinician takes the radial pulse.

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatome distribution (C3-T2)
statement: A patient presents with sensory loss specifically along the medial margin of the upper limb. Which spinal cord segments are most likely involved based on dermatomal distribution?
options: C3, C4, and C5
C5 and C6
C7 only
C8, T1, and T2
C6 and C7
correctOption: C8, T1, and T2
optionExplanations: The dermatomes of the upper limb follow a specific pattern where the lower cervical and upper thoracic nerves (C8, T1, T2) supply the medial aspect. C8 typically covers the medial hand and little finger, while T1 and T2 cover the medial forearm and arm, respectively. Sensory loss in this 'medial margin' distribution strongly suggests a lesion affecting these specific spinal segments or the medial cord of the brachial plexus.

C3, C4, and C5 dermatomes are located much higher, covering the base of the neck, the shoulder (acromion), and the lateral aspect of the upper arm. They do not extend to the medial margin of the limb. C5 specifically provides sensation to the lateral aspect of the arm near the deltoid.

C5 and C6 dermatomes are situated along the lateral margin of the upper limb. C6 specifically covers the lateral forearm and the thumb. A lesion here would cause sensory deficits on the opposite side of the limb compared to the medial margin.

The C7 dermatome is uniquely situated on the middle finger and the central portion of the hand. It acts as a transition zone between the lateral (C3-C6) and medial (C8-T2) dermatomes. It does not primarily cover the medial margin of the limb.

C6 covers the lateral forearm and thumb, while C7 covers the middle finger. Neither of these segments is responsible for the medial margin of the limb. The medial margin is exclusively the domain of the C8 through T2 segments.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Mammography utility and safety in breast screening
statement: Which of the following statements regarding mammography is correct according to standard clinical practice?
options: It uses high-dose radiation to ensure deep tissue penetration.
It is only useful for detecting malignant tumors, not benign cysts.
It uses extremely low doses of x-rays, allowing for frequent repeated examinations.
It has largely been replaced by MRI as the primary screening tool.
It cannot detect nipple retraction caused by underlying carcinomas.
correctOption: It uses extremely low doses of x-rays, allowing for frequent repeated examinations.
optionExplanations: Mammography is designed to use the lowest possible dose of ionizing radiation that still provides high-quality diagnostic images. Because the risk of radiation-induced injury is minimal at these levels, the procedure is safe for routine, periodic screening in asymptomatic women. This allows for early detection of changes that might not be palpable during a physical exam.

High-dose radiation would increase the risk of secondary cancers and is not used for screening purposes. Modern mammographic equipment is optimized for soft tissue contrast using low-energy x-ray beams. The goal is to maximize diagnostic yield while minimizing the absorbed dose to the breast tissue.

Mammography is highly effective at identifying a wide range of breast pathologies, including benign cysts, fibroadenomas, and malignant carcinomas. It can often distinguish between solid masses and fluid-filled cysts, although ultrasound is frequently used as a follow-up for further characterization. It is not limited to malignancy detection.

While MRI is a powerful tool for breast imaging, especially in high-risk patients or for staging known cancer, mammography remains the gold standard for primary population-wide screening. Mammography is more cost-effective, faster, and has a well-established track record for reducing breast cancer mortality. MRI is typically an adjunct, not a replacement.

Mammography is excellent at detecting the underlying masses or architectural distortions that cause clinical signs like nipple retraction. While nipple retraction is a physical finding observed during inspection, the mammogram provides the internal visualization of the lactiferous duct pulling or the tumor mass responsible for the sign. It is a complementary tool to the physical examination.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often. Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction.

coreConcept: Palpation of the third part of the axillary artery
statement: The third part of the axillary artery is a key landmark for verifying the position of the axillary sheath. Against which muscle can its pulsations be felt in the axilla?
options: Pectoralis minor
Teres major
Coracobrachialis
Serratus anterior
Latissimus dorsi
correctOption: Teres major
optionExplanations: The third part of the axillary artery extends from the lower border of the pectoralis minor to the lower border of the teres major. At its distal end, it lies anterior to the teres major muscle within the axilla. This relationship allows a clinician to palpate the arterial pulsations against the firm background of the muscle belly.

The pectoralis minor muscle is the landmark used to divide the axillary artery into its three parts. The first part is proximal to it, the second part is posterior to it, and the third part is distal to it. Therefore, the third part is not felt 'in front of' the pectoralis minor, but rather distal to its lower border.

The coracobrachialis muscle lies lateral to the axillary artery and serves as a guide for finding the neurovascular bundle in the arm. While the artery is close to it, the specific landmark for palpating the third part's pulsations in the axillary vault is the teres major, which forms part of the posterior wall.

The serratus anterior muscle forms the medial wall of the axilla. The axillary artery and the cords of the brachial plexus lie lateral to this muscle, separated by the axillary fat and lymph nodes. It does not provide a direct surface for palpating the third part of the artery.

The latissimus dorsi muscle also forms part of the posterior wall of the axilla, alongside the teres major. However, the teres major is the more specific anatomical landmark cited for the position of the third part of the axillary artery. The artery lies anterior to the combined tendon/muscle mass of these two structures as they approach the humerus.
citation: The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis.

coreConcept: Lateral Epicondyle Palpation
statement: During a physical examination of the elbow, a clinician identifies a small, roughened projection on the distal lateral aspect of the humerus. Which bony landmark is being palpated, and what is its relationship to the humeral articular surfaces?
options: Medial epicondyle, located proximal to the trochlea
Lateral epicondyle, located proximal to the capitulum
Olecranon process, located proximal to the trochlea
Coronoid process, located proximal to the capitulum
Radial head, located distal to the capitulum
correctOption: Lateral epicondyle, located proximal to the capitulum
optionExplanations: The medial epicondyle is a much larger, knoblike projection compared to the lateral epicondyle and is situated on the medial side of the humerus. It is located proximal to the trochlea, not the capitulum, and serves as the origin for the common flexor tendon. Palpation of this area is also used to locate the ulnar nerve in the ulnar sulcus.

The lateral epicondyle is correctly identified as the small, roughened projection on the distal lateral side of the humerus. It is positioned immediately proximal to the capitulum, which articulates with the head of the radius. This landmark is easily palpable and serves as the attachment site for the radial collateral ligament and the common extensor tendon.

The olecranon process is part of the proximal ulna, not the humerus, and forms the prominent point of the elbow. While it is easily palpable, it is not a projection on the lateral side of the humerus. It articulates with the olecranon fossa of the humerus during full extension of the forearm.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior surface of the proximal ulna that fits into the coronoid fossa of the humerus during flexion. It is not located on the lateral side of the humerus nor is it proximal to the capitulum. It provides an attachment point for the brachialis muscle and the ulnar collateral ligament.

The radial head is a part of the radius bone, not the humerus, and is located distal to the capitulum of the humerus. While it can be palpated just distal to the lateral epicondyle during pronation and supination, it is not the roughened projection on the humerus itself. It articulates with both the capitulum and the radial notch of the ulna.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm.

coreConcept: Anatomic Snuffbox and Scaphoid Palpation
statement: A 22-year-old athlete presents with wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand. On examination, there is deep tenderness within the triangular depression bounded medially by the extensor pollicis longus tendon. Which bone is most likely injured, and where is it most easily palpated?
options: Lunate, palpated distal to the radial styloid
Triquetrum, palpated in the floor of the snuffbox
Scaphoid, palpated in the anatomic snuffbox
Pisiform, palpated at the base of the thumb
Trapezium, palpated proximal to the ulnar styloid
correctOption: Scaphoid, palpated in the anatomic snuffbox
optionExplanations: The lunate is located in the proximal row of carpal bones but is typically palpated on the dorsum of the wrist, distal to the radius, especially during wrist flexion. It is not the primary bone found in the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. Tenderness here is more often associated with Kienböck's disease or perilunate dislocations rather than snuffbox tenderness.

The triquetrum is located on the ulnar side of the proximal carpal row and is not accessible via the anatomic snuffbox, which is a radial-sided landmark. It is best palpated distal to the ulnar styloid process on the medial aspect of the wrist. Injuries to the triquetrum usually result from direct blows or extreme wrist motions.

The scaphoid is the most commonly fractured carpal bone and is located in the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. Deep tenderness in this specific triangular depression after a fall on an outstretched hand is a classic clinical sign of a scaphoid fracture. The snuffbox is bounded by the extensor pollicis longus medially and the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis laterally.

The pisiform is a sesamoid bone located on the palmar aspect of the wrist within the tendon of the flexor carpi ulnaris. It is easily palpable on the medial (ulnar) side of the wrist, not at the base of the thumb or within the snuffbox. It serves as a landmark for the ulnar nerve and artery as they enter the hand.

The trapezium is located in the distal carpal row and articulates with the first metacarpal, but it is situated distal to the scaphoid. While it can be felt in the distal part of the snuffbox, the scaphoid is the primary bone occupying the floor where tenderness is most diagnostic. The ulnar styloid is on the opposite side of the wrist from the trapezium.
citation: Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid. ... The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis.

coreConcept: Axillary Sheath and Axillary Artery Pulsations
statement: A clinician intends to perform a brachial plexus block and needs to verify the position of the axillary sheath. Which arterial pulsation should be palpated, and what is its primary anatomical relation in the axilla?
options: First part of the axillary artery, posterior to the pectoralis minor
Second part of the axillary artery, medial to the humerus
Third part of the axillary artery, in front of the teres major
Brachial artery, lateral to the biceps brachii
Subclavian artery, posterior to the clavicle
correctOption: Third part of the axillary artery, in front of the teres major
optionExplanations: The first part of the axillary artery is located proximal to the pectoralis minor muscle and is generally too deep to palpate easily for the purpose of identifying the axillary sheath. It extends from the lateral border of the first rib to the medial border of the pectoralis minor. This segment is enclosed within the axillary sheath but is not the standard site for palpation.

The second part of the axillary artery lies directly posterior to the pectoralis minor muscle, which obscures its pulsations from direct surface palpation. It is surrounded by the cords of the brachial plexus, making it a critical area for regional anesthesia, but the pulsations are not felt 'medial to the humerus' as a primary landmark. The pectoralis minor is the defining relation for this segment.

The third part of the axillary artery is the most accessible portion for palpation within the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle. Feeling these pulsations allows the clinician to accurately locate the axillary sheath, which contains the artery and the terminal branches of the brachial plexus. This is the standard landmark used before performing an axillary brachial plexus block.

The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major and is the continuation of the axillary artery into the arm. While its pulsations are easily felt in the medial bicipital groove, it is distal to the axillary sheath's main body in the axilla. It is overlapped by the biceps brachii from the lateral side, not located lateral to it.

The subclavian artery is located in the root of the neck and becomes the axillary artery at the lateral border of the first rib. While it can be compressed against the first rib in the posterior triangle of the neck, its pulsations are not used to verify the position of the axillary sheath within the axilla itself. It is superior and medial to the axillary region.
citation: The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. ... The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65).

coreConcept: Lateral Axillary Lymph Node Palpation
statement: During a comprehensive axillary lymph node examination, the clinician attempts to palpate the lateral group of nodes. What is the correct technique and anatomical landmark used to identify these specific nodes?
options: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major
Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis
Pressing laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein
Pressing medially against the lateral thoracic wall and serratus anterior
Pressing superiorly toward the outer border of the first rib
correctOption: Pressing laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein
optionExplanations: Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major is the technique used to palpate the anterior or pectoral group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes receive the majority of the lymph from the breast. This maneuver requires the patient to press their hand against their hip to contract the pectoralis major.

Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla is the method for palpating the posterior or subscapular lymph nodes. These nodes drain the posterior thoracic wall and the scapular region. They are located along the subscapular vessels.

The lateral axillary lymph nodes are specifically palpated by pressing the examiner's fingers laterally against the medial side of the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery. These nodes receive most of the lymph from the upper limb. This group is situated along the distal part of the axillary vein.

Pressing medially against the lateral thoracic wall is the technique used to palpate the central axillary lymph nodes, which are located deep to the axillary fascia in the fat of the axilla. They receive lymph from the anterior, posterior, and lateral groups. They are not specifically associated with the medial side of the axillary vein in the same way the lateral nodes are.

Pressing superiorly toward the outer border of the first rib is the technique used to palpate the apical axillary lymph nodes. These are the most superior group and receive lymph from all other axillary node groups. To facilitate this, the patient must relax their shoulder muscles to allow the examiner's fingers to reach high into the apex of the axilla.
citation: The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery. The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall).

coreConcept: Central Axillary Lymph Node Palpation
statement: A physician is performing a physical exam on a patient with suspected lymphadenopathy. To palpate the central axillary lymph nodes, where should the examiner's fingers be positioned relative to the axillary walls?
options: Against the medial wall of the axilla, over the serratus anterior
In the center of the axilla, between the anterior and posterior walls
High in the apex of the axilla, against the first rib
Against the lateral wall of the axilla, near the bicipital groove
Deep to the pectoralis minor, near the coracoid process
correctOption: In the center of the axilla, between the anterior and posterior walls
optionExplanations: The medial wall of the axilla is formed by the upper ribs and the serratus anterior muscle. While lymph nodes can be found in this general vicinity, the 'central' group is specifically defined by its location in the fat of the axillary floor, rather than being pressed directly against the serratus anterior. The lateral nodes are more closely associated with the humerus and axillary vein.

The central axillary lymph nodes are located in the center of the axilla, situated between the pectoralis major (which forms the anterior wall) and the subscapularis (which forms the posterior wall). They are often the most easily palpable nodes if enlarged because they lie in the base of the axilla. They receive drainage from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral node groups.

The apex of the axilla, near the outer border of the first rib, is the site for palpating the apical lymph nodes. This requires the patient to relax the shoulder completely to allow the examiner's hand to reach the highest point of the axillary space. The apical nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary lymphatic drainage before entering the subclavian trunk.

The lateral wall of the axilla is formed by the intertubercular (bicipital) groove of the humerus. The lateral group of lymph nodes is located here, along the medial side of the axillary vein. Palpating this area involves pressing laterally against the humerus, which is distinct from the technique used for the central nodes located in the axillary fat.

The pectoralis minor forms part of the anterior wall of the axilla, and the second part of the axillary artery lies deep to it. While the apical nodes are located superior to the pectoralis minor, the central nodes are located inferior and more superficial to it within the axillary fossa. The coracoid process is a bony landmark used for other purposes, such as identifying the origin of the short head of the biceps.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla.

coreConcept: Elbow Joint Bony Relations in Extension
statement: When a clinician examines a patient's fully extended elbow, what is the expected topographical relationship between the medial epicondyle, the lateral epicondyle, and the olecranon process?
options: They form an equilateral triangle
They form a right-angled triangle
They lie in a straight horizontal line
The olecranon is significantly superior to the epicondyles
The epicondyles are obscured by the olecranon
correctOption: They lie in a straight horizontal line
optionExplanations: An equilateral triangle is the configuration formed by these three bony landmarks when the elbow is in a state of flexion. This change in orientation is a critical diagnostic feature; if the triangle is distorted in flexion, it may indicate a fracture or dislocation. In extension, however, this triangular relationship is lost as the olecranon moves into the olecranon fossa.

A right-angled triangle is not a standard anatomical description for the relationship between the epicondyles and the olecranon in any normal position of the elbow. Clinical anatomy focuses on the transition from a straight line in extension to an equilateral triangle in flexion. Deviations from these patterns suggest underlying pathology such as a supracondylar fracture.

In the position of full extension, the medial epicondyle, the lateral epicondyle, and the proximal tip of the olecranon process normally lie in a straight horizontal line. This alignment is used by clinicians to assess the integrity of the elbow joint. If the olecranon is displaced above or below this line while the arm is extended, it suggests a joint abnormality.

The olecranon process does not sit superior to the epicondyles in a normal extended elbow; rather, it aligns with them. If the olecranon were significantly superior, it would indicate a posterior dislocation of the ulna relative to the humerus. In extension, the olecranon is tucked into the olecranon fossa of the humerus, bringing it into line with the epicondyles.

The epicondyles are not obscured by the olecranon; they are distinct bony projections on the medial and lateral sides of the distal humerus. The olecranon is the central point between them. All three landmarks should be easily palpable in a healthy individual, and their relative positions are essential for surface marking and physical diagnosis.
citation: The elbow joint is stable because of the wrench-shaped articular surface of the olecranon and the pulley-shaped trochlea of the humerus; it also has strong medial and lateral ligaments. When examining the elbow joint, clinicians must remember the normal relations of the bony points. In extension, the medial and lateral epicondyles and the top of the olecranon process are in a straight line.

coreConcept: Clavicle Subcutaneous Nature
statement: Which of the following statements best describes the surface anatomy and palpability of the clavicle in a living subject?
options: It is covered by the pectoralis major and is only palpable at its ends
It is subcutaneous and can be felt along its entire length
It is deep to the trapezius muscle throughout its medial two-thirds
It can only be palpated when the shoulder is fully abducted
It is entirely obscured by the platysma muscle in all individuals
correctOption: It is subcutaneous and can be felt along its entire length
optionExplanations: While the pectoralis major muscle does originate from the medial half of the clavicle, the bone itself remains subcutaneous and is not completely covered by the muscle. The entire length of the clavicle, from the sternoclavicular joint to the acromioclavicular joint, can be traced by touch. It serves as a vital landmark for various clinical procedures, including subclavian vein catheterization.

The clavicle is a subcutaneous bone, meaning it lies just beneath the skin and superficial fascia. Because of this, it is easily palpable along its entire length in almost all individuals. This accessibility makes it a common site for fractures and an important reference point for identifying other structures in the root of the neck and the thoracic wall.

The trapezius muscle attaches to the lateral third of the clavicle, not the medial two-thirds. Even at its attachment site, the superior surface of the clavicle remains palpable. The medial portion of the clavicle is actually the most prominent part and is not covered by any thick muscle layer that would prevent palpation.

Palpation of the clavicle does not require any specific movement of the shoulder joint, such as abduction. It is a fixed bony landmark that is visible and palpable in the neutral anatomical position. In fact, shoulder movements like shrugging or protraction make the clavicle even more prominent as it moves with the scapula.

The platysma is a thin, superficial muscle of the neck that crosses over the clavicle to reach the upper thorax. However, it is not thick enough to obscure the bone. The clavicle remains the most easily identifiable bony structure in the transition zone between the neck and the chest, regardless of the platysma's presence.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Olecranon as the Point of the Elbow
statement: A patient presents with a 'bursitis' at the very tip or 'point' of the elbow. Which bony structure forms this landmark and is the proximal end of the ulna?
options: Coronoid process
Radial head
Olecranon
Styloid process of the ulna
Trochlea
correctOption: Olecranon
optionExplanations: The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior part of the proximal ulna. It is not palpable from the surface because it is covered by the brachialis muscle and other soft tissues of the cubital fossa. It serves to stabilize the elbow in flexion by fitting into the coronoid fossa of the humerus.

The radial head is the proximal end of the radius, not the ulna. It is located on the lateral side of the elbow joint, distal to the lateral epicondyle. While it can be palpated and felt rotating during forearm movements, it does not form the posterior 'point' of the elbow.

The olecranon is the large, thick, bony projection at the proximal end of the ulna. It forms the prominent 'point' of the elbow that is easily felt when the arm is flexed. It serves as the insertion site for the triceps brachii muscle and is the location of the olecranon bursa, which can become inflamed (olecranon bursitis).

The styloid process of the ulna is located at the distal end of the bone, near the wrist. It is a small, pointed projection on the posteromedial aspect of the ulnar head. While it is palpable, it is nowhere near the elbow joint and does not contribute to the 'point' of the elbow.

The trochlea is a pulley-shaped articular surface on the distal humerus, not the ulna. It articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna to form the hinge part of the elbow joint. It is a deep structure and is not palpable as a surface landmark like the olecranon.
citation: The olecranon (olecran- is Greek for “elbow”) is the easily palpable proximal end of the ulna that forms the “point” of the elbow. The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand.

coreConcept: Radial Artery in the Anatomic Snuffbox
statement: While the radial pulse is most commonly taken at the distal radius, it can also be felt in the anatomic snuffbox. In this location, what is the relationship of the radial artery to the carpal bones?
options: It lies deep to the lunate bone
It crosses the superficial surface of the scaphoid
It passes medial to the pisiform bone
It runs between the hamate and the capitate
It is located posterior to the ulnar styloid
correctOption: It crosses the superficial surface of the scaphoid
optionExplanations: The lunate bone is located in the center of the proximal carpal row and does not form the floor of the anatomic snuffbox. The radial artery passes much further laterally (radially) than the lunate. The lunate is primarily related to the distal end of the radius and the capitate bone.

In the anatomic snuffbox, the radial artery runs across the floor, which is primarily formed by the scaphoid and trapezium bones. Pulsations can be felt here as the artery winds around the lateral aspect of the wrist to reach the dorsum of the hand. This is a secondary site for pulse palpation if the distal forearm site is unavailable.

The pisiform bone is located on the ulnar (medial) side of the wrist. The ulnar artery, not the radial artery, is the vessel associated with the pisiform. The radial artery is strictly a lateral structure at the level of the wrist and hand.

The hamate and capitate are bones of the distal carpal row located toward the center and ulnar side of the wrist. The radial artery does not pass between them; instead, it enters the palm by passing between the two heads of the first dorsal interosseous muscle after leaving the snuffbox. Its path is much more lateral.

The ulnar styloid is the distal-most point of the ulna on the medial side of the wrist. The radial artery is located on the opposite (lateral) side of the wrist. Any vessel found posterior to the ulnar styloid would be a branch of the ulnar artery or a dorsal carpal network component, not the main radial artery.
citation: The anatomic snuffbox is a small triangular skin depression on the posterolateral side of the wrist that is bounded medially by the tendon of the extensor pollicis longus and laterally by the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis. Its clinical importance lies in the fact that the scaphoid bone is most easily palpated here and that the pulsations of the radial artery can be felt here.

coreConcept: Radiographic Angulation of the Distal Humerus
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow, a radiologist notes the angulation of the distal humerus relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. What is the normal physiological forward angulation of the lower end of the humerus?
options: 15 degrees
30 degrees
45 degrees
60 degrees
90 degrees
correctOption: 45 degrees
optionExplanations: An angulation of 15 degrees is too shallow for the normal distal humerus. While the 'carrying angle' of the forearm in extension is approximately 5 to 15 degrees, this refers to the lateral deviation of the ulna, not the forward tilt of the humeral condyles seen on a lateral X-ray. Correct identification of humeral angulation is vital for diagnosing supracondylar fractures.

A 30-degree angle is an underestimate of the normal forward tilt of the distal humerus. The articular surfaces (trochlea and capitulum) are projected anteriorly to allow for a greater range of flexion before the coronoid process of the ulna hits the humerus. Misalignment at this angle might suggest a subtle malunion or remodeling after injury.

The lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward at approximately 45 degrees relative to the shaft. This anatomical tilt is clearly visible on a true lateral radiograph and is essential for normal elbow mechanics and range of motion. If this angle is altered, as often seen in supracondylar fractures, it can significantly limit the patient's ability to flex the elbow.

A 60-degree forward angulation would be excessive and likely represent a pathological state or a severe fracture deformity. The normal 45-degree tilt provides the optimal balance between joint stability and the space required for the forearm bones during full flexion. Excessive anterior tilting would disrupt the alignment of the olecranon and coronoid fossae.

A 90-degree angulation would mean the articular surface is perpendicular to the shaft, which is not the case in human anatomy. Such an extreme angle would be functionally impossible for the hinge mechanism of the elbow. The distal humerus maintains a specific 45-degree orientation to facilitate the pulley-like action of the trochlea.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Central Axillary Lymph Node Palpation
statement: During a clinical examination of the axilla in a patient with suspected lymphadenopathy, a physician attempts to palpate the central group of lymph nodes. Which of the following describes the correct anatomical location for palpating these specific nodes?
options: In the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major and the subscapularis
Against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle on the anterior wall
Against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall
Against the medial side of the axillary vein and the humerus
High up in the axilla at the outer border of the first rib
correctOption: In the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major and the subscapularis
optionExplanations: The central axillary nodes are located deep within the axillary fat, situated between the anterior wall (pectoralis major) and the posterior wall (subscapularis). Palpation requires the examiner to reach into the center of the axillary vault while the patient's muscles are relaxed. This group receives lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral nodes, making it a critical site for detecting metastatic spread.

The anterior or pectoral nodes are located along the lower border of the pectoralis minor and are palpated by pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major. These nodes primarily drain the lateral quadrants of the breast and the integument above the umbilicus.

The posterior or subscapular nodes are found along the subscapular vessels and are palpated by pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle. They receive lymph from the back of the trunk as far down as the iliac crest.

The lateral nodes are situated along the medial side of the axillary vein and are palpated against the humerus. These nodes receive the majority of the lymph from the upper limb, excluding the vessels following the cephalic vein.

The apical nodes are located at the apex of the axilla, medial to the pectoralis minor, and are reached by palpating high up toward the outer border of the first rib. They receive lymph from all other axillary node groups and drain into the subclavian lymph trunk.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib.

coreConcept: Clavicle Palpation
statement: A medical student is practicing surface anatomy on a peer. Which of the following bones is described as being subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length, serving as a key landmark for the upper limb?
options: Humerus
Clavicle
Scapula
Radius
Ulna
correctOption: Clavicle
optionExplanations: The humerus is largely surrounded by thick musculature, such as the deltoid and biceps, making only specific parts like the epicondyles and greater tuberosity easily palpable. Its shaft is not subcutaneous along its entire length.

The clavicle is a unique long bone that lies horizontally and is subcutaneous throughout its entire extent. This makes it easily accessible for palpation from the sternoclavicular joint medially to the acromioclavicular joint laterally. It serves as a vital landmark for identifying the borders of the neck and the axilla.
The scapula is mostly covered by the trapezius, infraspinatus, and other muscles. While the spine, acromion, and inferior angle are palpable, the entire bone is not subcutaneous.
The radius is covered by the brachioradialis and other forearm muscles. Only its distal end and the radial head are easily palpable, rather than its entire length.

While the posterior border of the ulna is subcutaneous and palpable, the entire bone is not as accessible as the clavicle. The proximal and distal ends are prominent, but the anterior and lateral surfaces are deep to muscle.
citation: The clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra. The acromion (acromi- is Greek for “point of the shoulder”) is the broad, flat lateral extension of the spine of the scapula.

coreConcept: Radial Artery Palpation (Distal Forearm)
statement: A clinician is attempting to locate the radial pulse in the distal forearm of a patient. Between which two tendons should the clinician place their fingers to most effectively palpate the radial artery against the radius?
options: Flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum superficialis
Palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis
Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
Abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
Extensor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
correctOption: Brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis
optionExplanations: The flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum superficialis are located on the medial side of the forearm. The ulnar artery, not the radial artery, is associated with the flexor carpi ulnaris at the wrist.

The palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis tendons are located more medially in the central forearm. While the radial artery is lateral to the flexor carpi radialis, it does not lie between these two specific tendons.

In the distal forearm, the radial artery is situated superficially on the anterior surface of the radius. It is specifically positioned between the tendon of the brachioradialis (laterally) and the tendon of the flexor carpi radialis (medially). This anatomical arrangement allows the artery to be easily compressed against the bone to feel the pulse.

The abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis form the lateral boundary of the anatomic snuffbox. While the radial artery passes through the snuffbox, this is not the primary site described for palpation in the distal forearm between tendons.

The extensor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis bound the anatomic snuffbox on the posterior aspect of the wrist. The radial artery can be felt here, but it is less easily palpated than at the distal radius between the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis.
citation: In the distal forearm, the radial artery lies on the anterior surface of the radius, between the tendons of the brachioradialis and flexor carpi radialis muscles, and is covered only by skin and fascia (see Figs. 3.31 and 3.38). This is the ideal site for taking the radial pulse. If the pulse cannot be felt, try feeling for the radial artery on the other wrist.

coreConcept: Ulnar Artery Palpation (Pisiform relation)
statement: When palpating the ulnar pulse at the wrist, the clinician identifies the pisiform bone as a landmark. What is the correct anatomical relationship of the ulnar artery to the pisiform bone and the ulnar nerve at this location?
options: The artery lies medial to the pisiform bone and medial to the ulnar nerve
The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone and lateral to the ulnar nerve
The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone and medial to the ulnar nerve
The artery lies medial to the pisiform bone and lateral to the ulnar nerve
The artery lies deep to the pisiform bone and the ulnar nerve
correctOption: The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone and lateral to the ulnar nerve
optionExplanations: The ulnar artery does not lie medial to the pisiform bone; the pisiform is the most medial landmark in this region. Placing fingers medial to the pisiform would not allow for arterial palpation.

At the wrist, the ulnar artery and ulnar nerve pass superficial to the flexor retinaculum. The artery is positioned lateral to the pisiform bone and is also lateral to the ulnar nerve, which sits between the artery and the bone. This superficial position makes the ulnar pulse palpable just lateral to the pisiform.
The ulnar nerve is actually the structure located between the pisiform bone and the ulnar artery. Therefore, the artery is lateral to both the bone and the nerve, not medial to the nerve.
The artery cannot be medial to the pisiform as the pisiform is the medial boundary of the ulnar canal (Guyon's canal). The artery and nerve are lateral to this bony prominence.
The ulnar artery and nerve are superficial to the flexor retinaculum, not deep to the bones. Their superficial location is what allows the pulse to be felt through only skin and fascia.
citation: The ulnar artery can be palpated as it crosses superficial to the flexor retinaculum in company with the ulnar nerve. The artery lies lateral to the pisiform bone, separated from it by the ulnar nerve. As the [ulnar] artery crosses over the flexor retinaculum, it lies just lateral to the pisiform bone and is covered only by skin and fascia, making this a good site for taking the ulnar pulse.

coreConcept: Axillary Artery (Third Part) Palpation
statement: In an emergency situation requiring the palpation of the axillary artery within the axilla, where is the third part of this artery most reliably felt in relation to the surrounding musculature?
options: Behind the pectoralis minor muscle
In front of the teres major muscle
Medial to the coracobrachialis muscle
Lateral to the long head of the triceps
Deep to the subscapularis muscle
correctOption: In front of the teres major muscle
optionExplanations: The second part of the axillary artery lies behind the pectoralis minor muscle. This part is not easily palpable because it is covered by the thick muscle belly of the pectoralis minor.

The third part of the axillary artery extends from the lower border of the pectoralis minor to the lower border of the teres major. It can be palpated in the axilla where it lies directly in front of the teres major muscle, making it accessible for pulse checks or compression.
While the coracobrachialis is a lateral relation to the axillary artery, the most reliable posterior landmark for palpating the third part in the axillary vault is the teres major.
The long head of the triceps is a posterior relation but is not the primary muscle against which the third part of the axillary artery is palpated in the axilla.

The subscapularis muscle forms the posterior wall of the axilla, but the third part of the artery has already passed distal to the majority of this muscle's surface by the time it reaches the palpable area in front of the teres major.
citation: The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65). The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii (see Fig. 3.25). The radial artery lies superficially in front of the distal end of the radius.

coreConcept: Dermatomes (C7/Middle Finger)
statement: A patient presents with sensory loss specifically localized to the middle finger following a suspected spinal cord injury. Which spinal cord segment is most likely associated with the dermatome covering this specific area?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C5 dermatome typically covers the lateral aspect of the arm, near the deltoid region and extending down toward the elbow. It does not provide primary sensory innervation to the fingers.
The C6 dermatome is located along the lateral margin of the forearm and typically includes the thumb and the lateral side of the index finger. It is not the primary dermatome for the middle finger.

The C7 dermatome is specifically situated on the middle finger. Testing sensation in this digit is a standard clinical method for evaluating the integrity of the seventh cervical spinal cord segment or its corresponding nerve root.
The C8 dermatome covers the medial margin of the hand, including the little finger and the medial side of the ring finger. It does not extend to the middle finger.
The T1 dermatome is located along the medial margin of the forearm and the upper arm. It is situated proximal to the hand and does not involve the sensory innervation of the middle finger.
citation: The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb. Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary.

coreConcept: Allen's Test (Radial Patency)
statement: To specifically assess the patency of the radial artery using the Allen’s test, which of the following procedural steps must be performed after the patient clenches their fist to blanch the palm?
options: Release the radial artery while maintaining compression on the ulnar artery
Release the ulnar artery while maintaining compression on the radial artery
Release both the radial and ulnar arteries simultaneously
Maintain compression on both arteries for at least sixty seconds
Compress the brachial artery in the mid-arm region
correctOption: Release the ulnar artery while maintaining compression on the radial artery
optionExplanations: Releasing the radial artery while compressing the ulnar artery is the procedure used to test the patency of the ulnar artery, as it checks if the ulnar artery can supply the hand alone. This is the opposite of what is required to test radial patency.

To establish the patency of the radial artery, the clinician must compress the ulnar artery (lateral to the pisiform) while the patient clenches their fist. Upon opening the hand, the ulnar compression is maintained, and the radial artery is released; if the palm turns pink, the radial artery is patent.

Releasing both arteries simultaneously would not allow the clinician to isolate the blood flow from a single artery, which is the primary goal of the Allen's test. This would result in immediate flushing regardless of individual arterial patency.

Maintaining compression on both arteries without release would keep the palm blanched and provide no information about the patency of either the radial or ulnar systems. The test relies on the observation of reperfusion.

Compressing the brachial artery is not part of the Allen's test. The Allen's test specifically evaluates the collateral circulation of the hand provided by the radial and ulnar arteries through the palmar arches.
citation: The Allen’s test is used to determine the patency of the ulnar and radial arteries. With the patient’s hands resting in the lap, compress the radial arteries against the anterior surface of each radius and ask the patient to tightly clench the fists. The clenching of the fists closes off the superficial and deep palmar arterial arches. When the patient is asked to open the hands, the skin of the palms is at first white.

coreConcept: Breast Examination (Asymmetry/Swelling)
statement: During the initial inspection phase of a clinical breast examination with the patient sitting upright, which of the following findings is correctly identified as a potential cause of localized swelling?
options: Normal unequal breast development
Lactiferous duct shortening
An underlying tumor or abscess
Suspensory ligament contraction
Pectoralis major hypertrophy
correctOption: An underlying tumor or abscess
optionExplanations: Unequal breast development is a common cause of breast asymmetry, but it is generally a global difference in size rather than a localized 'swelling' or mass. It is considered a normal developmental variation.
Shortening or pulling on the lactiferous ducts by a carcinoma typically results in nipple retraction rather than a localized swelling. This is a sign of tissue tethering rather than mass effect.

Localized swelling in the breast tissue can be caused by several pathological entities, including underlying tumors (benign or malignant), cysts, or abscess formation. These conditions create a mass effect that is visible or palpable during examination.

Contraction or tethering of the suspensory ligaments (of Cooper) by a tumor typically causes dimpling of the skin. While associated with a tumor, the ligamentous change itself is a sign of retraction, not the primary cause of the swelling.

Pectoralis major hypertrophy would occur deep to the breast tissue and would likely present as a more generalized increase in the chest wall prominence rather than a localized breast swelling. It is not a common finding in a standard breast exam for masses.
citation: With the patient undressed to the waist and sitting upright, first inspect the breasts for symmetry. Some degree of asymmetry is common and is the result of unequal breast development. Any swelling should be noted. An underlying tumor, a cyst, or abscess formation can cause a swelling. Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple.

coreConcept: Elbow Radiology (Humerus Angulation)
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow following a traumatic injury, what is the normal anatomical angulation of the distal end of the humerus relative to its shaft?
options: Angulated backward 15°
Angulated forward 45°
Angulated medially 30°
Angulated laterally 45°
Parallel to the shaft (0°)
correctOption: Angulated forward 45°
optionExplanations: The distal humerus is not angulated backward; a posterior angulation would be indicative of a fracture, such as a supracondylar fracture with posterior displacement. Normal anatomy features an anterior tilt.

In a normal lateral radiograph of the elbow, the lower end of the humerus (the condyle) is angulated forward at approximately 45° relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. Recognizing this normal angulation is crucial for identifying subtle fractures or displacements.

Medial angulation is not the standard feature observed on a lateral radiograph. Medial or lateral deviations are better assessed on an anteroposterior (AP) view and would suggest a varus or valgus deformity.

While 45° is the correct magnitude, the direction is forward (anterior), not lateral. Lateral angulation would not be visible as a primary feature of the distal humerus on a standard lateral projection.

The distal humerus is not parallel to the shaft. If the 45° forward angulation is lost and the distal end appears parallel or posteriorly tilted, it is a strong radiographic sign of a supracondylar fracture.
citation: Radiology of Elbow Region after Injury: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.

coreConcept: Bicipital Aponeurosis Protection
statement: During venipuncture of the median cubital vein, which anatomical structure provides a protective barrier between the vein and the underlying brachial artery, reducing the risk of accidental intra-arterial injection?
options: Brachialis muscle
Bicipital aponeurosis
Deep fascia of the arm
Pronator teres muscle
Biceps brachii tendon
correctOption: Bicipital aponeurosis
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle lies deep to the brachial artery in the floor of the cubital fossa. It does not provide a barrier between the superficial veins and the artery.

The bicipital aponeurosis (lacertus fibrosus) is a flat expansion of the biceps tendon that passes medially across the cubital fossa. It specifically separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deeper brachial artery, acting as a surgical and clinical shield.

While the deep fascia covers the muscles of the arm, the bicipital aponeurosis is the specific specialized structure within the cubital fossa that provides the described protection for the brachial artery.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa. While it is in the vicinity, it does not lie between the median cubital vein and the brachial artery.

The biceps brachii tendon is the lateral structure from which the bicipital aponeurosis originates. The tendon itself is lateral to the brachial artery and does not serve as the primary medial protective sheet.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis. This is important because the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs that should have been injected into the vein. The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization.

coreConcept: Palpation of central axillary lymph nodes
statement: During a comprehensive clinical examination of the axilla, where should the examiner specifically palpate to assess the central group of lymph nodes?
options: In the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major and subscapularis
Against the medial side of the axillary vein and artery
High up in the axilla against the outer border of the first rib
Against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle
Against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle
correctOption: In the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major and subscapularis
optionExplanations: The central axillary lymph nodes are located deep within the axillary fat and are best palpated by pressing toward the center of the axillary fossa. This position is anatomically bounded by the pectoralis major forming the anterior wall and the subscapularis forming the posterior wall. Clinicians use this landmark to identify lymphadenopathy that may suggest systemic disease or localized breast pathology.

Palpating against the medial side of the axillary vein and artery targets the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes, not the central group. The lateral nodes receive most of the lymphatic drainage from the upper limb. This maneuver requires the examiner to press laterally against the humerus and neurovascular bundle.

Placing the fingers high up in the axilla toward the outer border of the first rib is the specific technique for palpating the apical lymph nodes. This requires the patient to relax their shoulder muscles to allow the examiner's hand to reach the apex of the axilla. The apical nodes receive drainage from all other axillary node groups.

Pressing forward against the posterior surface of the pectoralis major muscle is the technique used to palpate the anterior or pectoral group of lymph nodes. These nodes are situated along the lower border of the pectoralis minor. They primarily drain the lateral and central parts of the breast.

Pressing backward against the anterior surface of the subscapularis muscle is the technique used to palpate the posterior or subscapular group of lymph nodes. These nodes are located along the subscapular vessels and the posterior axillary fold. They drain the posterior aspect of the thoracic wall and the scapular region.
citation: The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall). For the apical nodes, the patient is asked to relax the shoulder muscles and let the upper limb hang down at the side. The examiner then gently places the tips of the fingers of the examining hand high up in the axilla to the outer border of the first rib.

coreConcept: Surface anatomy of the lateral epicondyle
statement: Which bony landmark is located on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, immediately proximal to the capitulum?
options: Lateral epicondyle
Medial epicondyle
Olecranon
Coronoid process
Radial tuberosity
correctOption: Lateral epicondyle
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is a small, roughened projection that serves as the common origin for the extensor muscles of the forearm. It is situated on the lateral aspect of the distal humerus, just above the capitulum, which articulates with the head of the radius. It is easily palpable and is a key landmark for diagnosing lateral epicondylitis.

The medial epicondyle is a much larger and more prominent projection located on the medial side of the distal humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It serves as the origin for the flexor muscles of the forearm and protects the ulnar nerve. It is not associated with the capitulum.

The olecranon is the proximal part of the ulna that forms the point of the elbow and articulates with the olecranon fossa of the humerus. It is not a part of the humerus itself. It is easily palpable on the posterior aspect of the elbow joint.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior part of the proximal ulna that fits into the coronoid fossa of the humerus during flexion. It is not located on the lateral side of the humerus. It is generally not as easily palpable as the epicondyles.

The radial tuberosity is a projection on the medial side of the neck of the radius, serving as the insertion point for the biceps brachii muscle. It is located on the radius, not the humerus. It is deep and difficult to palpate directly.
citation: The lateral epicondyle is the small, roughened projection on the distal, lateral side of the humerus, proximal to the capitulum. It is readily palpable. The medial epicondyle is the large, knoblike projection on the distal, medial side of the humerus, proximal to the trochlea. It is easily palpable and forms an important surface landmark in the arm.

coreConcept: Palpation of the posterior border of the ulna
statement: Which part of the ulna is subcutaneous and can be palpated along its entire length from the elbow to the wrist?
options: Posterior border of the body
Anterior border of the body
Interosseous border
Coronoid process
Radial notch
correctOption: Posterior border of the body
optionExplanations: The posterior border of the ulnar shaft is subcutaneous throughout its length, extending from the olecranon to the ulnar styloid process. This makes it an important landmark for assessing fractures or bony irregularities along the forearm. Because it is not covered by thick muscle, it is easily accessible to the clinician's fingers.

The anterior border of the ulnar body is covered by the deep flexor muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor digitorum profundus. This makes it impossible to palpate directly through the skin. It serves as an attachment point for various muscular structures.

The interosseous border of the ulna is the sharp lateral edge that provides attachment for the interosseous membrane. It is located deep between the radius and ulna and is surrounded by the deep muscles of the forearm. It cannot be palpated on the surface of the limb.

The coronoid process is a projection on the anterior aspect of the proximal ulna that participates in the elbow joint. It is covered by the brachialis muscle and other soft tissues in the cubital fossa. It is not palpable along the length of the forearm.

The radial notch is a small articular surface on the lateral side of the coronoid process that receives the head of the radius. It is a deep articular feature of the proximal radioulnar joint. It is entirely internal and cannot be felt on the surface.
citation: The posterior aspect of the body [of the ulna] is rounded and subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length. Deep tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox (see the following discussion of the wrist) after a fall on the outstretched hand in a young adult is suspicious for a fractured scaphoid.

coreConcept: Mechanism of nipple retraction in breast carcinoma
statement: What is the specific anatomical mechanism by which a carcinoma within the breast substance causes retraction of the nipple?
options: Pulling on the lactiferous ducts
Tension on the suspensory ligaments
Invasion of the pectoralis major fascia
Obstruction of the subareolar lymphatic plexus
Compression of the retromammary space
correctOption: Pulling on the lactiferous ducts
optionExplanations: Nipple retraction occurs when a malignant tumor invades and causes fibrosis of the lactiferous ducts, which normally open onto the nipple. As these ducts shorten due to the cancerous process, they pull the nipple inward toward the breast substance. This is a classic clinical sign of an underlying breast carcinoma.

Tension on the suspensory ligaments (Cooper's ligaments) typically causes dimpling of the skin overlying the breast, rather than retraction of the nipple itself. These ligaments connect the dermis to the deep fascia. When a tumor involves these ligaments, it pulls the skin inward during movement or palpation.

Invasion of the pectoralis major fascia by a tumor can cause the breast to become fixed to the chest wall, limiting its mobility. While this is a sign of advanced malignancy, it does not directly cause the specific phenomenon of nipple retraction. Nipple retraction is specifically related to the ductal system.

Obstruction of the subareolar lymphatic plexus or other lymphatic vessels leads to lymphedema of the skin, resulting in the 'peau d'orange' appearance. This causes the skin to become thickened and pitted around the hair follicles. It is a distinct clinical finding from nipple retraction.

Compression or obliteration of the retromammary space occurs when a tumor grows posteriorly and attaches to the deep fascia. This space normally allows the breast to move freely over the pectoralis major muscle. Its loss indicates deep invasion but is not the mechanism for nipple inversion.
citation: Carefully examine the nipples for evidence of retraction. A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts. Next, ask the patient to lie down so that the breasts can be palpated against the underlying thoracic wall. Finally, ask the patient to sit up again and raise both arms above her head.

coreConcept: Clinical utility of mammography
statement: Which of the following statements correctly describes the clinical application and safety of mammography?
options: It uses low-dose x-rays allowing for frequent screening of tumors and cysts
It is primarily used for therapeutic destruction of malignant cysts
It utilizes high-frequency ultrasound waves to visualize deep fascia
It requires high-dose radiation that limits its use to once every five years
It is only effective for detecting malignant tumors, not benign cysts
correctOption: It uses low-dose x-rays allowing for frequent screening of tumors and cysts
optionExplanations: Mammography is a specialized radiographic technique that employs low-energy X-rays to create detailed images of breast tissue. Because the radiation dose is extremely low, the risk to the patient is minimal, making it an ideal tool for regular screening. It is effective at identifying various pathologies, including both benign cysts and malignant masses.

Mammography is a diagnostic and screening tool, not a therapeutic modality. It is used to detect and characterize lesions but does not provide treatment or destruction of cysts. Therapeutic interventions for breast cancer involve surgery, chemotherapy, or targeted radiation therapy.

Mammography uses ionizing radiation (X-rays), not ultrasound waves. While breast ultrasound is a valuable adjunctive tool for distinguishing between solid masses and fluid-filled cysts, it is a different imaging modality. Mammography remains the gold standard for initial population-based screening.

The radiation dose in modern mammography is very low, which is why it can be performed annually or biennially in screening programs without significant cumulative risk. High-dose radiation would be counterproductive for a screening tool. The benefits of early cancer detection far outweigh the minimal risks of the low-dose exposure.

Mammography is highly effective at detecting a wide range of breast abnormalities, including benign conditions like fibroadenomas and simple cysts. It is not limited to malignant findings. Radiologists look for specific patterns, such as microcalcifications or architectural distortion, to differentiate between benign and malignant processes.
citation: Mammography is a radiographic examination of the breast (Fig. 3.17). This technique is extensively used for screening the breasts for benign and malignant tumors and cysts. Extremely low doses of x-rays are used so that the dangers are minimal, and the examination can be repeated often.

coreConcept: Verification of the axillary sheath position
statement: How can a clinician verify the anatomical position of the axillary sheath during a physical examination?
options: By palpating the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery
By identifying the insertion of the pectoralis minor on the coracoid
By feeling the pulse of the subclavian artery in the posterior triangle
By palpating the medial cord of the brachial plexus against the humerus
By locating the cephalic vein in the deltopectoral groove
correctOption: By palpating the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery
optionExplanations: The axillary sheath is a fascial extension of the prevertebral layer of cervical fascia that encloses the axillary artery and the brachial plexus. Since the third part of the axillary artery is the most accessible portion within the axilla, its pulsations serve as a reliable landmark for the sheath's location. This is particularly useful when performing regional anesthesia blocks.

The pectoralis minor is an important landmark that divides the axillary artery into three parts, but its insertion on the coracoid process does not directly verify the position of the sheath. The sheath lies deep to the pectoralis major and minor muscles. Palpation of the muscle itself is less precise than feeling the arterial pulse.

The subclavian artery pulse can be felt in the root of the neck, but this is proximal to the formation of the axillary sheath. The axillary sheath begins as the subclavian vessels cross the first rib to enter the axilla. Therefore, the subclavian pulse does not verify the position of the sheath within the axilla itself.

While the cords of the brachial plexus are contained within the axillary sheath, they do not produce a palpable pulse and are difficult to distinguish from other soft tissues by touch. The artery is the only structure within the sheath that provides a clear, rhythmic physical signal. Clinicians rely on the artery to guide them to the plexus.

The cephalic vein is a superficial structure that travels in the deltopectoral groove and pierces the clavipectoral fascia to join the axillary vein. It is located outside the axillary sheath. Therefore, its position cannot be used to verify the location of the sheath or its contents.
citation: The position of the [axillary] sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery. The third part of the axillary artery can be felt in the axilla as it lies in front of the teres major muscle (see Figs. 3.12 and 3.65).

coreConcept: Upper limb dermatomes (C7)
statement: A patient presents with a loss of sensation localized specifically to the middle finger. Which spinal cord segment is most likely affected?
options: C7
C5
C6
C8
T1
correctOption: C7
optionExplanations: The C7 dermatome is classically mapped to the middle finger of the hand. Testing sensation in this specific area is a standard part of a neurological exam to assess the integrity of the seventh cervical spinal nerve root. It occupies a central position between the lateral (C6) and medial (C8) dermatomes of the hand.

The C5 dermatome is located on the lateral aspect of the upper arm, near the deltoid muscle. It does not extend down to the fingers. Damage to the C5 nerve root would typically result in sensory loss over the lateral shoulder and weakness in shoulder abduction.

The C6 dermatome covers the lateral margin of the forearm and the thumb (radial side of the hand). It is situated adjacent to the C7 dermatome. Sensory testing of the thumb is used to specifically evaluate the C6 spinal segment.

The C8 dermatome is located on the medial margin of the hand, specifically involving the little finger and the ulnar half of the ring finger. It is situated medial to the C7 dermatome. Loss of sensation in the pinky finger would suggest an issue with the C8 nerve root.

The T1 dermatome is found along the medial aspect of the forearm and the upper arm. It does not reach the hand or the fingers. It is tested by checking sensation on the inner aspect of the forearm, proximal to the wrist.
citation: Testing the integrity of the C3 to T1 segments of the spinal cord may become necessary. The dermatomes for the upper cervical segments C3 to 6 are located along the lateral margin of the upper limb; the C7 dermatome is situated on the middle finger; and the dermatomes for C8, T1, and T2 are along the medial margin of the limb.

coreConcept: Palpation of lateral axillary lymph nodes
statement: To palpate the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes, in which direction should the examiner press their fingers within the axilla?
options: Laterally against the axillary vein and humerus
Forward against the pectoralis major muscle
Backward against the subscapularis muscle
Medially against the upper ribs and serratus anterior
Superiorly toward the apex of the axilla
correctOption: Laterally against the axillary vein and humerus
optionExplanations: The lateral axillary lymph nodes are situated along the medial side of the axillary vein, which lies against the humerus. To palpate them, the examiner must press their fingers laterally toward the upper part of the humerus. These nodes are responsible for draining the majority of the lymph from the upper limb.

Pressing forward against the pectoralis major muscle is the technique for palpating the anterior (pectoral) nodes. These nodes are located along the inferior border of the pectoralis minor. They are not the lateral group, which is located deeper and more laterally in the axilla.

Pressing backward against the subscapularis muscle targets the posterior (subscapular) nodes. These nodes are found along the posterior axillary fold and the subscapular vessels. They drain the posterior thoracic wall and the scapular region, not the upper limb primarily.

Pressing medially against the upper ribs and the serratus anterior muscle would target the medial wall of the axilla. While some lymphatics pass through this area, it is not the standard location for palpating the lateral group of nodes. The lateral nodes are specifically associated with the neurovascular bundle.

Pressing superiorly toward the apex of the axilla is the method used to find the apical lymph nodes. These nodes are located at the very top of the axillary space, near the first rib. They represent the final common pathway for all axillary lymphatic drainage before entering the neck.
citation: The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein. The examiner’s fingers are pressed laterally against the axillary vein and the pulsating axillary artery. The central nodes may be palpated in the center of the axilla between the pectoralis major (anterior wall) and the subscapularis (posterior wall).

coreConcept: Radiographic anatomy of the distal humerus
statement: When reviewing a lateral radiograph of the elbow, what is the normal anatomical forward angulation of the distal end of the humerus relative to its shaft?
options: 45 degrees
15 degrees
30 degrees
60 degrees
90 degrees
correctOption: 45 degrees
optionExplanations: In a normal lateral radiographic view, the distal articular portion of the humerus (the condyle) is tilted anteriorly at an angle of approximately 45 degrees relative to the long axis of the humeral shaft. This angulation is crucial for the full range of elbow flexion. Displacement of this angle is a key diagnostic sign in supracondylar fractures.

An angulation of 15 degrees is significantly less than the normal 45-degree tilt found in the distal humerus. Such a finding on a radiograph might suggest a malunited fracture or a developmental abnormality. It would likely result in a significant reduction in the functional range of motion of the elbow joint.

While 30 degrees is closer to the normal range, it still represents a deviation from the standard 45-degree anterior tilt. Precise measurement of this angle helps orthopedic surgeons determine if a fracture requires surgical reduction. Maintaining the correct anterior tilt is essential for joint stability and mechanics.

A 60-degree forward angulation would be an excessive anterior tilt, which is not typical of normal human anatomy. Such an extreme angle would likely interfere with the articulation of the olecranon in the olecranon fossa during extension. It could be the result of a severe traumatic injury or significant bony remodeling.

A 90-degree angle would mean the distal humerus is perpendicular to the shaft, which is anatomically incorrect. The 45-degree tilt is a specific evolutionary adaptation that allows the forearm to clear the humerus during deep flexion. A 90-degree orientation would completely disrupt the hinge mechanism of the elbow.
citation: In examining lateral radiographs of the elbow region, remember that the lower end of the humerus is normally angulated forward 45° on the shaft; when examining a patient, the clinician should see that the medial epicondyle, in the anatomic position, is directed medially and posteriorly and faces in the same direction as the head of the humerus.
